CQC Statistical Guidance
Statistical Methods for Regulatory Analysis

title: CQC Statistical Guidance
text:
Overview
Practical statistical guidance for CQC analysts. Methods, worked examples, and decision criteria, with complete R and Python code throughout.
This book teaches judgement, not rules: every section explains the reasoning behind analytical choices so you can adapt to your specific context.







TipStart Here




First time here? → How to Use This Book for orientation and reading strategies
Know your question? → Quick Reference maps analytical problems directly to methods
Need QA guidance? → QA Principles for the quality framework underpinning all analysis






Part I: Foundations
Data types, descriptive statistics, variation, distributions, unusual observations, missing data concepts, statistical inference, and QA principles.
View Foundations →


Part II: Core Methods
Z-scoring, SPC, t-tests, correlation, outlier detection, and missing data handling – each with step-by-step guides and dual R/Python code.
View Methods →


Part III: Worked Examples
Complete analytical workflows using realistic CQC data, from data preparation through to stakeholder reporting.
View Examples →









NoteAbout This Version



Version 1.0 (2026) covers 8 foundation chapters, 6 core methods, and 10 worked examples with dual R/Python code. For feedback or suggestions, contact the Practice Lead for Quantitative Analytics and Statistics.
Saving a page as PDF: Use your browser’s Print function (Ctrl+P / Cmd+P) and choose “Save as PDF”. For best results, set margins to “Minimum” and enable “Background graphics”.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
What It Is, and What It Isn’t
This page orients you to the guidance – what it covers, how it’s built, and how to find what you need. Read it once; after that, the Quick Reference is your day-to-day entry point.
This book is practical statistical guidance for CQC analysts, whether you’re working to a deadline or building confidence with quantitative methods. Use it to choose an appropriate method for a regulatory question, execute it correctly, recognise the edge cases that need specialist input, and document your decisions to meet quality assurance (QA) requirements.
It is not a statistics textbook. It won’t teach theory from first principles – external courses do that – nor cover cutting-edge methods that need specialist expertise, nor stand in for software tutorials, which other technical guides can provide. Most importantly, it does not hand you mechanical rules that remove the need for judgement. Statistical decisions in a regulatory context ask you to balance technical correctness, operational feasibility, and stakeholder trust; this guidance helps you make those judgements, but it does not make them for you.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
NoteProfessional judgement is the point



You will meet situations where several methods are each defensible but point to different interpretations; where technical correctness pulls against operational feasibility; where stakeholder trust calls for different communication than statistical precision; and where fairness to providers needs different thresholds than significance alone would set. This guidance helps you navigate those trade-offs – explaining CQC’s reasoning, showing methods in regulatory context, and being explicit about the limits of current knowledge – but the call is yours.
This is not a CQC idiosyncrasy. The UK Code of Practice for Statistics rests on three pillars – Trustworthiness, Quality, and Value – and none of them reduces to a formula. Quality means choosing sound methods for the question in front of you; Value means producing something that genuinely informs a decision; Trustworthiness means being honest about limitations and uncertainty. Each is a matter of judgement, exercised well. This guidance is written to help you meet the Code in everyday analytical work, and flags throughout where a particular method engages a particular principle.
When in doubt, document your reasoning and consult. Good judgement comes from experience, and experience comes from making decisions and learning from them.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
The Three Layers
You will find boxes alongside the text throughout this guidance that borrow a device from Richard McElreath’s Statistical Rethinking, separating the mechanics of a method from two deeper kinds of engagement with it:

Main text – the minimum knowledge you need to apply the method correctly in standard cases.
Rethinking boxes (🤔) – the conceptual layer: common misconceptions, what a result does and does not mean, how methods connect to one another, and the reasoning behind CQC’s choices. They are about thinking clearly, not extra difficulty.
Overthinking boxes (⚙️) – the technical layer: the mathematics, edge cases, and assumption violations for when the standard approach breaks down.

The main text stands on its own; both kinds of box can be skipped without losing the thread.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
Three Ways to Read It
There is no single right way through – it depends on what you need right now.

Building confidence? Work through Part I: Foundations in order, including the Rethinking boxes, then move into Core Methods and Worked Examples as your work requires. Return to the Overthinking boxes when you’re ready to go deeper.
Under deadline? Go straight to the method via the Quick Reference or a similar Worked Example, skim the main text to refresh your memory, and check the Overthinking boxes only if you hit an edge case.
Reviewing or going deep? Read in full, including every Overthinking box, and use the Rethinking boxes to understand why CQC made the methodological choices it did.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
How the Guidance Is Organised
The substantive content falls into three parts, with this page and the Quick Reference as orientation up front.
Part I: Foundations covers the ideas the rest of the discipline rests on – how data behaves, how to describe it, how variation and uncertainty work, and what quality assurance demands. It closes with the computational context: the tools CQC analysts use, why the function is moving toward cloud-based scripted analysis, and what you need to get started in Microsoft Fabric. Code examples appear throughout the book from Part I onwards; if you encounter one before reaching that chapter, it tells you what environment to use and how to access it. If the later methods are the tools, Foundations is the understanding that tells you when and why to reach for each one.
Part II: Core Methods is the working toolkit: the techniques CQC analysts reach for most often. Each chapter is self-contained – you don’t need to read them in order, and they lean on the Foundations rather than on each other. Every one carries its quality-assurance scaffolding (what to check before you start, what assumptions to test, what to record) so the audit trail is built as you work, not bolted on afterwards.
Part III: Worked Examples shows the methods in motion: complete analyses on realistic synthetic data, from preparing the data through to reporting it to stakeholders.
The Appendices hold the Glossary and a list of further resources. If a term or acronym is ever unfamiliar, or you suspect it’s being used in a precise sense, look it up there – acronyms are expanded on first use, but the Glossary is the backstop.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
Start With Your Question
The most common mistake is to start from the method – “should I use a t-test?” – rather than from the question. Begin instead with what you are actually trying to find out, and the method usually follows.
Suppose you want to know whether a provider’s referral rate is unusually high. Framed that way, the shape of the answer is already visible: you are comparing one provider against all the others, so you need a way to express how far from typical it sits – which can, for example, point to z-scoring. Now change the question slightly: “has this provider’s rate been drifting upward over the past year?” You are no longer comparing against peers but watching a single series over time, which is the territory of statistical process control. Same provider, same data – but a different question leads to a different tool.
So when you’re unsure where to begin, sharpen the question first. Once it’s clear what you want to find out, the Quick Reference maps common analytical questions to the method that fits.

title: How to Use This Book
text:
When to Seek Specialist Support
This guidance goes well beyond the bare mechanics – the Rethinking and Overthinking boxes are there precisely to help with the awkward cases you’ll meet in practice, but they can’t cover every possible scenario. Seeking specialist support isn’t an admission that the guidance failed you. It’s a judgement you make when a problem starts to press against the edges of what you know and the tools you feel confident using.
That judgement is the skill worth developing, and the signs are recognisable once you look for them. A method’s stated assumptions don’t hold, and the relevant Overthinking box doesn’t cover your particular violation. You’ve followed the steps correctly, but the results don’t make sense and you can’t see why. You’re working on something genuinely new for CQC, with no established pattern to follow. Or the stakes are simply high enough – a major regulatory decision, a public-facing report – that a second pair of expert eyes is the prudent thing before you commit. None of these is a box to tick; each is a prompt to ask whether you’ve reached the limit of what you can stand behind.
The aim is not to offload the thinking, but to recognise when you’ve done enough of it alone. Whatever your situation, don’t wait until you’re stuck too deep: early consultation prevents wasted effort, and a five-minute conversation at the right moment often saves days.

How to Get Help
The Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) offers a space where analysts can ask questions, share approaches, and learn from each other – through Teams channels, drop-in sessions, and wider networking.
When reaching out, please keep in mind that the Guild is a community, not a delivery team. If your request is time-sensitive, or concerns a complex methodological question requiring formal review, escalate to the Practice Lead for Quantitative Analytics and Statistics, who will help identify the best source of support.

title: Quick Reference
text:
Find Your Method
Find your way to the right method – then go and think.
This page maps common analytical questions to the chapter that handles them, so an experienced analyst under time pressure can get moving quickly. What it can’t do is make the decision for you: most real situations have wrinkles these tables can’t see. Treat a row as “start reading here”, not “here’s your answer”. If you’re new to the guidance, start with How to Use This Book.
Two questions decide the method: what you’re trying to do, and what kind of data you have. Use the first to find the right chapter; let the second guide the specifics once you’re there. This is a signpost – the chapter you land on carries the assumptions, sample-size guidance, and worked examples.

title: Quick Reference
text:
The Shape of an Analysis
It’s tempting to read analysis as a pipeline – pick a method, run it, report it – but in practice it’s a loop. A typical path runs question → check the data → choose and run a method → interpret in context → document, but the honest version has arrows pointing backwards too. The data often sends you back to the question (what you wanted to measure isn’t quite what was collected); interpretation sends you back to the method (an assumption doesn’t hold); and the whole process shapes your documentation.
So treat these as a rhythm, not a checklist:

Question – sharpen what you’re actually trying to find out before reaching for a method.
Data – know your data type, check data quality, look at the distribution and summary statistics, and check for unusual values and missingness.
Method – follow the relevant chapter, and check its assumptions before trusting the output.
Interpret – read results in context: distance, statistical significance, and practical importance are different things, and significance is never the whole story.
Document – record methods, assumptions, limitations, and interpretation as you go, not afterwards (see QA Principles).

Expect to revisit earlier steps. Going back is not a sign you did it wrong; it’s how analysis actually works.

title: 1  Data Types & Implications
text:
1.1 Why Data Type Matters
Suppose someone hands you a column of CQC ratings – Inadequate, Requires Improvement, Good, Outstanding – and asks for the average. It is tempting to code them 1 to 4, take the mean, and report, say, 2.7. But what is 2.7? It lands between Requires Improvement and Good, yet nothing in the data says a provider is “seven-tenths of the way to Good”, and nothing says the gap from Inadequate to Requires Improvement is the same size as the gap from Good to Outstanding. The figure is arithmetically neat and analytically empty. The mistake was not in the calculation – it was in choosing a method the data could not bear.
That is what this chapter is about. Before you can pick a sound method, you have to know what kind of data you are holding, because the same regulatory question – is this provider unusual? – calls for different tools depending on whether you are looking at waiting times, incident counts, or ratings. Get the type right and the method usually follows. Get it wrong and the analysis can be invalid before you have computed a single number.
Data type decides:
It is the first thing to settle and the cheapest mistake to avoid.

title: 1  Data Types & Implications
text:
which statistical methods are valid,
which distributions might plausibly describe the data,
how to summarise it honestly,
which charts make sense,
and how to read the result.

title: 1  Data Types & Implications
section: 1.2 Two Questions That Sort Any Variable
text:
1.2 Two Questions That Sort Any Variable
A variable is any characteristic that can take different values from one case to the next – a provider’s rating, its waiting time, the number of incidents it reported last quarter. Almost anything you measure at CQC is a variable, and it can be sorted into a type with two questions.
The first is the big one: is this a quantity, or a label? A quantity is a number you can do arithmetic on – waiting times, counts, rates – where sums and means are meaningful. A label sorts cases into groups – region, rating, incident category – where arithmetic is nonsense, however numeric the codes look. This split, quantitative versus qualitative, governs everything downstream.
The second question depends on the answer to the first, and it is what separates four data sub-types.

Quantitative: numbers you can calculate with
For quantitative data the second question is: can it take any value in a range, or only whole steps?
Continuous data can take any value, decimals included. Waiting times (2.5 hours), length of stay (4.2 days), rates per 1,000 bed-days, staff-to-patient ratios. The tell is that between any two values you can always imagine another: between 1.5 and 1.6 metres there is 1.55, and between 1.55 and 1.56 there is 1.555, without end. Percentages belong here too – though penned between 0 and 100, a percentage can in principle be 23.4% or 23.456%.
Discrete data can only take separated values, almost always whole-number counts: the number of incidents, complaints, registered nurses, beds. You can record 5 incidents or 6, never 5.3. The distinction is about how you measure, not what you measure – days counted whole (1, 2, 3) are discrete, but the moment you record 1.5 days they become continuous.


Qualitative: labels and categories
For qualitative data the second question is: do the categories have a natural order?
Nominal data has none. Provider type, region, service type, incident category – “North East” is not more or less than “South West”, merely different. The categories are names, and names do not line up.
Ordinal data does line up. CQC ratings run Inadequate < Requires Improvement < Good < Outstanding; severity runs Low < Medium < High; a Likert item runs Strongly Disagree through to Strongly Agree. The order is genuine – but, as the rating average example hinted, the steps between categories are not guaranteed to be equal in size. That single fact is why ordinal data should not be treated as though it were quantitative without making that assumption explicit, and it is probably the most common type error in statistical analysis.
Put together, that is the whole map:

Quantitative – numbers it makes sense to apply direct calculations to

Continuous – any value in a range: waiting times, rates, percentages, ratios
Discrete – whole-number counts: incidents, complaints, staff, beds

Qualitative – labels and categories

Nominal – no natural order: region, provider type, incident category
Ordinal – ordered, with possibly unequal gaps: CQC ratings, severity, Likert scales

title: 1  Data Types & Implications
text:
1.3 A Ladder, Not Four Boxes
Those four types are not four equal boxes side by side. They form a ladder – what statisticians call the levels of measurement – and each rung up adds mathematical structure, unlocking things the rungs below cannot do. Read it from the bottom:

Nominal is the ground floor: all you can do is tell categories apart and count how often each occurs. Equality and frequency, nothing more.
Ordinal adds order: you can now rank cases and ask which is greater, on top of counting them.
Quantitative adds arithmetic: the gaps between values are real, so you can add, subtract, and take a mean.

Each rung keeps everything below it and gains something new. That is the single most useful idea in this chapter, because it tells you – before you pick any method – what you are allowed to do. You can count at every level, so the mode works anywhere. Ordering arrives at the ordinal rung, so the median and quantiles open up there. Arithmetic waits until the top, which is why the mean, the standard deviation, and most of the methods in this book need quantitative data. Climbing the ladder is the same as climbing the next chapter on describing data: mode, then median, then mean.
Two footnotes keep this honest. The continuous-versus-discrete split lives inside the top rung – it does not change what arithmetic is allowed, but it does decide which distribution to expect and which chart to reach for, since smooth measurements and whole-number counts behave differently. And the formal names for the top rung are interval and ratio (the difference is whether zero is a true zero); almost all CQC data is ratio – counts, times, and rates all have real zeros – so that distinction rarely earns its keep here.






Note🤔 Rethinking: Data type is sometimes a choice



CQC does not always inherit a data type; sometimes it chooses one. Should days to assessment be counted as discrete whole days or measured as continuous time? Should a rating be kept as an ordinal category or converted to a numeric score? These are not purely statistical decisions, and they are not made on statistical grounds alone:

Interpretability – stakeholders grasp “3 days” more readily than “2.7 days”.
Fairness – whole days may be fairer where providers cannot control exact timing.
Consistency – using the same approach across indicators makes them comparable.
Analytical power – continuous data generally carries more statistical information.

When you have a choice, document the reasoning: why you measured it this way, what alternatives you weighed, and what trade-offs you accepted. The “right” data type is the one that best serves the analytical purpose while remaining defensible to the people who will act on it – which is the Code of Practice’s Quality and Value pillars in everyday form.

title: 1  Data Types & Implications
text:
1.4 From Type to Method
The reason all of this earns its place is that a variable’s rung on the ladder points straight at the methods that fit and, just as usefully, at the ones that do not – the higher it sits, the more the table opens up. The table below is a bridge from this chapter to the rest of the book: find your type, and it tells you where to go.

Data types and the methods that suit them







Data type
Methods that fit
Where in this book




Continuous
Mean and standard deviation, z-scoring, t-tests, Pearson correlation, I-charts
Describing Data, z-scoring, t-tests, Correlation, SPC


Discrete – counts
z-scoring (with caution, Poisson), C- and U-charts
z-scoring, SPC


Discrete – proportions
z-scoring, P-charts, tests of proportions
z-scoring, SPC


Nominal
Counts and cross-tabulations, chi-squared tests of association
Established methods, on the roadmap; the Guild can advise meanwhile


Ordinal
Quantiles and IQR, Spearman correlation, rank-based tests
Describing Data; Spearman in Correlation



Two things in that table are worth pausing on. The first is that qualitative data is not a dead end. Nominal and ordinal variables have their own honest methods – cross-tabulations, chi-squared, quantiles, Spearman’s correlation – and the right move is to use them, not to force a category into a quantitative tool because the quantitative tools are more familiar.
The second is the proportions row. A proportion – the share of cases meeting some standard – can be though of as a percentage, and so continuous in principle. But because it is built from a count out of a total, the methods that suit it model that underlying count, which is why it sits among the discrete methods here rather than the continuous ones. The boundary cases below return to exactly this kind of double life.

title: 1  Data Types & Implications
text:
1.5 When the Type Isn’t Obvious
Most variables sort themselves in seconds. The ones that cause trouble are the handful that seem to sit in two places at once – and they cause trouble precisely because the method you reach for hangs on which type you decide they are.
“My data could be two types at once.” Usually this means the raw measurement is being confused with something derived from it. The number of incidents is discrete; the incident rate per 1,000 bed-days, calculated from it, is continuous. The raw type governs what you can do first; once you compute a rate or a percentage, you have created continuous data and a different set of methods opens up.
Percentages built on small counts. A percentage is continuous in principle, but something out of 10 can only ever be 0%, 10%, 20%, and so on – effectively discrete, and it misbehaves in methods that assume a smooth continuum. With small denominators, give a percentage the same caution you would give the bare count beneath it (this is one of the traps the z-scoring chapter handles directly).
Time, measured how? It depends on the ruler. Whole days (1, 2, 3) are discrete; days with fractions (1.5, 2.3) are continuous; banded time (0–7 days, 8–14, 15+) is ordinal. The same underlying quantity yields three different types and three different method sets, depending only on how it was recorded.
Likert scales. Strictly ordinal: 1 to 5, Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree. With many responses, analysts sometimes average them and treat the result as continuous. That is convenient and occasionally defensible, but it quietly assumes the steps are evenly spaced – the one thing ordinal data does not promise. The Overthinking box below weighs when it is and is not safe.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Treating ordinal data as if it were numbers



The most contested boundary case is whether ordinal scales – Likert items above all – can be averaged and treated as continuous. The argument in favour: with enough responses, the mean of many ordinal values approximates a continuous measure and behaves well enough. The argument against: this assumes equal spacing between categories, which is rarely true and never guaranteed.
In a CQC context, the safe default is to avoid treating ratings as quantitative – a mean rating of 2.7 is something that can invite subjective interpretation from stakeholders, and it imports an equal-spacing assumption the ratings might not support. The more defensible route is to count the proportion of providers in each category and analyse those proportions, which are themselves quantitative and continuous.
A related technical wrinkle: a percentage built on a small denominator is mathematically continuous but behaves discretely. With 10 patients you can only land on 0%, 10%, 20%, and so on, which breaks methods that assume a continuous distribution. The same caution applies to rates from very small counts (say 0–5 incidents), where the rate will not behave like smooth continuous data.
If a variable simply will not settle into one type, and the choice changes which method is valid, that is a good opportunity for a consultation with the Guild to discuss what the most defensible position might be.

title: 2  Visualising Data
text:
2.1 Why Look Before You Summarise?
Two hospital wards each reported exactly 60 incidents over the past year. That single total is the kind of figure that lands in a report, and on the strength of it the two wards look identical: same count, same year, nothing to choose between them.
But a yearly total only tells you how many, not when. To see that, you have to look at the fuller pattern – how those 60 incidents fall across the twelve months. That pattern, the complete picture of which values occur and how often (here, in which months the incidents landed), is what we will call a distribution. A total is a single figure squeezed out of it; the distribution is everything the figure left behind.
So plot the distribution – the same 60 incidents, month by month.
Figure 2.1: Two wards with the identical annual total of 60 reported incidents, shown month by month (illustrative figures). The total alone says the wards are the same. The picture says they are not: Ward A is steady at about five a month, while Ward B was quiet all year until a single month of 45 – the kind of spike a regulator would want to look into. The summary hid the very thing that mattered.
Now the two wards could hardly look less alike. Ward A is steady at about five a month; Ward B was quiet for eleven months and then had one catastrophic one. That spike is exactly what a regulator would want to see, and the total – a perfectly honest number – buried it completely. Boiling a distribution down to a single figure is something we do constantly, because one number is compact and easy to compare; the catch is that every such figure throws away the shape it came from, and sometimes the shape was the story. No number here was wrong; the number was simply not enough.
This is the habit the chapter is built around: look at the distribution first, then reduce it to numbers. Drawing the picture is cheap, and it is the surest guard against being misled by your own figures.
This chapter sits early, right after Data Types, for a simple reason: the picture you should draw is decided by the level of measurement. So that is where we start.

title: 2  Visualising Data
section: 2.2 A Plot for Each Rung of the Ladder
text:
2.2 A Plot for Each Rung of the Ladder
Visualisation climbs the same ladder of measurement the data types chapter set out. Each rung adds structure, and that structure decides which plot does the most honest work with the least effort. There are many charts to choose from, but for everyday work one workhorse fits each rung. Learn these three first; reach for the rest when the job asks for them.

Nominal: the bar chart
At the ground floor all you can do is sort cases into categories and count them, so the natural plot draws one bar per category, its height the count. Nothing about the categories is ordered, which leaves you free to sort the bars by size – tallest first – and that makes the chart far easier to read: the eye lands on the biggest group at once.








Figure 2.2: A bar chart of provider counts by type in one region (illustrative figures). Because the categories have no natural order, the bars are sorted from most to least frequent, so the largest group reads first. Heights are counts; the gaps between bars signal that the categories are separate, unconnected labels.





It is tempting to reach for a pie chart here, but the eye compares lengths far more accurately than angles – a pie with five near-equal slices defeats the reader, where a bar chart settles the ranking at a glance. When in doubt, a bar chart is the safe default for counting categories.


Ordinal: the same bar chart, but the order is sacred
Add an ordering and the bar chart still fits – but one freedom is now forbidden. Because the categories run in a genuine sequence (Inadequate, Requires Improvement, Good, Outstanding), you must keep them in that order along the axis, even when it puts a short bar between two tall ones. Sorting by size, the right move for nominal data, would destroy the very thing that makes the data ordinal.








Figure 2.3: A bar chart of CQC ratings across providers in one region (illustrative figures). The categories are ordinal, so the bars stay in their natural worst-to-best order rather than being sorted by height – the climb to a Good-dominated distribution is the story. A single neutral colour keeps the focus on the shape; the order is carried by position, not by colour.





Keep one thing honest: the bars sit at equal distances along the axis, but that even spacing is just a layout convenience, not a claim that the step from Inadequate to Requires Improvement is the same size as the step from Good to Outstanding. The order is real; the spacing is not – exactly the caution the data types chapter raised about treating ratings as numbers.


Quantitative: the histogram
At the top rung the categories dissolve into a true number line, and the plot changes character. A histogram slices that line into equal-width intervals – bins – counts how many values fall in each, and draws the counts as bars that touch. The touching is the point: it signals that the axis is a continuous quantity rather than a set of separate labels. For the first time the plot shows the shape of the data – where the bulk of the values sit, whether they trail off further on one side, whether there is one peak or two.








Figure 2.4: A histogram of provider waiting times in hours (synthetic data, n = 400). The continuous axis is sliced into one-hour bins, and the touching bars show the shape: most providers cluster at short waits, with a long tail of slower ones reaching to the right. A single average could not show that lopsidedness; the picture shows it at once.





A histogram is, in effect, what a bar chart becomes once the axis is a real number line: pushing the bars together turns a count of labels into a picture of a distribution. But it hides a decision the bar charts above did not have to make – how wide should the bins be? – and that decision deserves its own look.


The bin width is a dial, not a given
The bars of a histogram do not exist in the data; you create them by choosing how finely to slice the axis. Slice it coarsely and you get a few wide bins; slice it finely and you get many narrow ones. That choice is not cosmetic – it can change the story the chart appears to tell. The two panels below show the same measurements binned two ways.








Figure 2.5: The same 320 length-of-stay measurements, binned two ways. With wide bins (left) the data looks like a single hump – one typical length of stay. With narrow bins (right) the truth appears: two distinct groups, a short-stay cluster and a long-stay cluster, with a clear gap between them. Nothing about the data changed; only the bin width did. Had this been a real analysis, the wide-binned version would have hidden the fact that two different kinds of patient are mixed together.





Left, the data looks like one group with a single typical value. Right, it splits cleanly into two – a short-stay cluster and a long-stay cluster – which almost certainly means two different kinds of patient have been pooled into one chart. Same numbers, opposite conclusion, decided entirely by the width of a bin.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Don’t trust the default number of bins



Every plotting tool picks a bin width for you if you do not, and it is easy to accept that default as if it were the answer. It is not. The common rules – names you may meet, such as Sturges’, Scott’s, or the Freedman–Diaconis rule – are heuristics: sensible opening guesses derived from the number of values and their spread, nothing more. Each can mislead. Sturges’ rule tends to use too few bins for large datasets and can smooth a real second peak into a single hump, exactly as the left panel above does.
So treat the first histogram your software draws as a question, not an answer. Redraw it a few times – try half the bin width and twice it – and watch what survives. If a second peak appears and disappears as you nudge the width, you have learnt something either way: a peak that holds up across several widths is probably real, and one that vanishes the moment you change the bin is not yet to be trusted. Playing with the dial is where the insight is.
There is a sharper edge to this. Because the bin width is a free choice that changes the apparent message, it is also a lever for deception – a histogram can be tuned to make a problem vanish or a difference loom, whether through carelessness or intent. A visualisation is one of the most persuasive things you can put in front of a decision-maker, which is precisely why it is one of the easiest ways to mislead one. Choosing the bin width honestly – and being ready to show the chart at other widths – is part of doing the analysis in good faith.




Continuous or discrete: match the mark to the data
The top rung holds two kinds of data, and the data types chapter flagged that the split decides which chart to reach for. Continuous data – waiting times, rates, lengths of stay – can fall anywhere on the line, so the histogram’s touching bars are exactly right: each bar owns a stretch of the axis, and the unbroken row says the axis is a continuum. Discrete data – counts that can only land on whole numbers, like serious incidents per provider or beds per home – does not live in stretches. A provider has 2 incidents or 3, never 2.6, so the value sits at a single point on the line with nothing on either side of it.
Drawing counts as touching, filled bars quietly contradicts that: a bar spanning the width from 1.5 to 2.5 implies the value could have been anything in between, which it could not. A stick (or lollipop) plot says it straight – a thin stem rising to a dot at each whole-number count, and bare axis between – so the chart matches the data: discrete values at discrete points.








Figure 2.6: The same distribution of a count – serious incidents per provider (synthetic data, n = 300) – drawn two ways. Left, as touching bars: the unbroken row implies a continuum, as if a provider could have had 2.6 incidents. Right, as a lollipop plot: a dot on a thin stem at each whole number, with empty axis between, which is what a count actually is. The lollipop also spends far less ink for the same information – an echo of the chartjunk principle below.





None of this makes the bar version wrong – with many possible count values a histogram is often the practical choice, and separated bars are a perfectly honest middle ground. The point is to choose the mark on purpose: reserve touching bars for data that really is continuous, and let sticks or lollipops carry counts, especially when the counts are few or when a lighter, less ink-heavy chart would read more clearly. It is the same instinct as the rest of the chapter – let the form of the plot match the form of the data.

title: 2  Visualising Data
section: 2.3 The Workhorse Is Just the Start
text:
2.3 The Workhorse Is Just the Start
One plot per rung is the safe default, but it is not the whole story. The right chart depends on three questions answered together, and the data type is only the first:

What is the data type? This rules plots in and out. You cannot put nominal data on a number line, and you should not sort ordinal data by height. The rung sets the boundaries.
What is the analytical job? Showing one variable’s shape, comparing several groups, tracking a value over time, or revealing how two quantities move together are different jobs, and each favours a different plot.
What do you need to communicate, and to whom? A chart you use to explore your own data is not the same as one a board sees on a slide. Exploration rewards detail; communication rewards a single clear message.

Quantitative data shows this most clearly. The histogram is the default for one variable’s shape, but if the job is to compare several groups a box plot is often better: it draws each group as a compact summary – a box covering the central bulk of the values, a line for the middle, and whiskers reaching toward the extremes – so a dozen groups line up side by side in the space one histogram would take. (The exact recipe for that box is the business of later chapters; here it is enough that it summarises a distribution at a glance.) You give up the fine detail of the shape to gain the comparison. Which trade is right is not a property of the data alone – it is a property of the question you are asking and the person you are answering.






Note🤔 Rethinking: The same data, more than one honest plot



A plot is not just a faithful record of the data; it is an argument about what matters in it. The same column of waiting times can honestly become several different charts depending on the job. An analyst hunting for problems wants the histogram – the full shape, every bump and gap on show. A report comparing provider types wants box plots stacked side by side – less detail per group, but the comparison made instantly. A board paper might want a single headline figure with a small strip of dots behind it – almost no detail, one message that lands in a second.
None of these is the “correct” plot and the others wrong. They are tuned to different readers and different decisions, and all three can tell the truth. The skill is not memorising one plot per data type; it is matching the chart to the question being asked and the person asking it. That judgement – and the much wider catalogue of plots it draws on – is the craft the Data Visualisation Hub and Team (the Hub) exists to support.



Beyond one variable: pairs, matrices, and maps
Everything so far has shown a single variable at a time. Step up to two and a new family of plots opens, far too large to survey here – this is where the “so many options” really begins, and where the Hub’s deeper guidance earns its place. Three signposts are enough for now.
When you have two quantitative variables and want to see whether they move together – do longer waits go with more complaints? – the natural plot is the scatter plot: one variable on each axis, a dot for each case, and the pattern of the cloud tells the story. When you have a grid of values – every region crossed with every provider type, say – a heatmap colours each cell by its value, turning a dense table into something the eye can scan for hot spots. And when the unit is a place – providers by local authority, say – a map puts the data where it belongs geographically, revealing clusters a bar chart never could.
The scatter plot also carries a warning worth meeting now, because it shows how far a plot can outrun a summary. The four clouds below come from four datasets that share the same standard summary measures – the same centre, the same spread, and the same strength of straight-line relationship between the two variables (all quantities made precise in later chapters; take them on trust for the moment). By those numbers the four are interchangeable.








Figure 2.7: Anscombe’s quartet: four datasets that agree on every common summary measure – the average of each variable, how spread out each is, and how tightly the two track together – yet look nothing alike once plotted. Only the first is the simple cloud those numbers suggest. The second bends in a smooth curve, the third is a straight line dragged off course by a single high point, and the fourth invents a relationship out of one lone point far to the right. A summary agreed; the pictures did not.





Only the first is the tidy relationship those numbers imply. One bends, one is a clean line knocked askew by a single outlier, and one conjures a trend out of a lone far-off point. A regulator who reported only the summary for the fourth would be claiming a relationship that rests entirely on one case. It is the same lesson the two wards taught at the start of the chapter, now at the top of the ladder: the summary can agree while the shape disagrees, so look first.

title: 2  Visualising Data
section: 2.4 Principles for an Honest, Readable Chart
text:
2.4 Principles for an Honest, Readable Chart
Whatever plot you choose, a handful of principles keep it honest. The bin-width trap was one of them; here are four more. Each is simple to state and easy to breach by accident – and, as the binning example warned, easy to breach on purpose too.
Scales tell the truth. The fastest way to mislead with a truthful dataset is to clip the axis. A bar encodes its value as length from a baseline, so a bar chart’s axis must start at zero. Truncate it and you turn a small difference into a dramatic one.








Figure 2.8: The misleading version. Four regions’ share of providers rated Good or better (illustrative figures: 88%, 90%, 91%, 93%). With the axis clipped to start near 85%, the East’s bar towers over the North’s and the differences look dramatic – yet they span just five percentage points.













Figure 2.9: The honest version of the same four numbers. With the axis starting at zero, the bars are nearly level – the regions are much alike, and the small lead the East holds is shown at its true size. Same data, opposite impression, decided entirely by where the axis begins.





Show the data, not just a summary. Where you reasonably can, prefer plots that reveal the distribution over those that hide it behind a single mark. A bar showing a group’s average, with a small error bar on top, collapses a whole distribution to two numbers and can conceal a long tail, a stray extreme value, or even two distinct clusters hiding inside one average – the very thing the binning example exposed. When the data is few enough to show, show it.
Minimise chartjunk. Every drop of ink should carry information. Strip out the decoration – heavy gridlines, three-dimensional effects, redundant colour – that adds nothing. In the rung charts above, colour does no work because the category is already named on the axis, so a single colour is the honest choice; bring colour in only when it encodes something the reader needs.






Note🤔 Rethinking: The title is part of the chart



Most charts are labelled with their subject – “Waiting times by provider type” – which tells the reader what they are looking at but not what to see. For a plot whose job is to communicate a finding, the title can do far more work if it states the finding itself: “Mental health trusts wait twice as long as care homes.” An assertive title like that is sometimes the only line a busy reader takes in, so it should carry the message, with the chart standing as the evidence beneath it.
This applies to plots meant for an audience. A quick chart you draw to explore your own data, mid-way through a larger piece of work, needs no such polish – a plain label is fine, and over-styling exploratory plots is wasted effort. The distinction is the same one the three questions drew: a plot for thinking and a plot for telling are different objects, and the title is where the difference shows most.


Finally, and least negotiable of all: accessibility is a requirement, not a finishing touch.
A chart that some of your readers cannot read has failed, however elegant it is. Designing for accessibility from the start – not bolting it on at the end – means: choosing colourblind-safe palettes; never relying on colour alone to carry meaning, but doubling it up with labels, shapes, or position; ensuring text and lines have enough contrast against the background; and writing a short text description (a fig-alt, as every figure in this book carries) so that a reader using a screen reader gets the same information as everyone else, or should the image break for some reason, the user would still get the description in its stead.
This is a craft in its own right, and one with real consequences for who can act on your analysis. The Data Visualisation Team maintain dedicated accessibility guidance that goes well beyond this paragraph – consult it whenever you are producing a chart for others to read and reach out to them when in doubt. (Signpost to the Hub’s accessibility guidance to follow.)

title: 2  Visualising Data
text:
2.5 Where Visualisation Leads
The three workhorses – bar, ordered bar, histogram – will carry most of your everyday work, and the principles above will keep them honest. But they are only a doorway. Even within this chapter you have stepped past them: a stick plot to keep whole-number counts honest, and a scatter plot to see whether two quantities move together. And the chapter has named more plots than it had room to draw – the box plot for comparing many groups at once, the heatmap for a grid of values, the map for data tied to a place – each a craft of its own.
The lesson is that choosing a plot rewards as much thought as the analysis it illustrates. Whenever the data, the analytical question, or the audience asks for more than a workhorse can give, the wider catalogue – and the people who tend it – are a conversation with the Hub away. Whatever you reach for, the habit that opened this chapter is the one to keep: look at the distribution first, then reduce it to numbers.

title: 3  Central Tendency and Position
text:
3.1 Where Is the Middle?
Before you can compare providers or flag an outlier, you have to boil a variable down to a number or two: where does it sit, what is typical? Is the typical waiting time 2 hours or 20? Is the average incident rate 5 a month or 50? That looks like one question with one answer. It is not.
How much you can say depends on how high your data sits on the ladder of measurement. On the bottom rung you can only say what is most common; add ordering and you can speak of a middle; add arithmetic and you can take a mean. So this chapter climbs the ladder rung by rung – the mode, then the median and its quantile family, then the mean – defining each measure and working it through on a handful of real numbers along the way. The arithmetic stays light; the code and the heavier methods come in Part II.
One word needs pinning down first, because everyday speech blurs it. An average is any single value standing in for the typical one – and there are several, so the mode, the median, and the mean are all averages, one for each rung. A mean is one particular kind (and even “the mean” is a small family: arithmetic, geometric, harmonic, …). In conversation “the average” almost always means the arithmetic mean, but this book keeps the words apart: mean for that calculation, median and mode for the other two, and average only as the umbrella over all three.
A note on symbols, while we are pinning down words. When the numbers in front of you are the whole story – every provider in a region this month – you are simply describing them, and this book writes such descriptions with Greek letters: \(\mu\) (mu) for a mean, and \(\sigma\) (sigma, in the next chapter) for a standard deviation. When instead the data is only a sample standing in for a larger population you cannot see in full, the convention switches to Roman letters – \(\bar{x}\) for the sample mean, \(s\) for its standard deviation – and a small correction enters the arithmetic. Throughout the foundations we take the first view, describing the data in hand; the switch to samples, and why it changes the sums, is the business of Statistical Inference. (The count stays \(n\) throughout.)

title: 3  Central Tendency and Position
text:
3.2 Counting: The Mode
The one summary you can always compute is the plainest: which value turns up most often. The mode asks what is most common?, and it asks nothing of the data but the ability to tell categories apart – so it is the only location measure that works on nominal data, where there is no order to exploit and no arithmetic to do. There is nothing to calculate: you count how often each value occurs and report the most frequent one. Take last month’s incident counts at five care homes – 2, 3, 3, 4, 18 – and the mode is 3, the only value that occurs more than once. The most common CQC rating (“Good”) is a mode too.
Its limits are real: the mode need not be unique (a dataset can be bimodal or multimodal, with two or more values tied), it uses little of the data, and for continuous measurements it depends on how you bin (group) the values. For quantitative metrics it rarely leads. But for qualitative outcomes – ratings, compliance status, risk category – it is often the only honest summary of “typical”, and it has one trick the grander measures lack.






Note🤔 Rethinking: When the average describes no one



Ask for the centre of a two-humped distribution and the mean will dutifully hand back a single number sitting in the valley between the humps – an “average” that matches no actual case. Picture provider staffing ratios that fall into two clusters, small rural homes and large urban ones: the mean ratio describes a provider that does not exist, and the median is little better. The mode – modes, plural – is the one summary that says there are two populations here, stop averaging them.
That is why the mode earns a place of its own rather than trailing along behind the mean and median: it is the measure that can tell you the centre is the wrong question. When you find two modes, the usual next move is to split the data and describe each group on its own terms.

title: 3  Central Tendency and Position
text:
3.3 Ordering: The Median and Quantiles
Climb one rung – to data you can put in order – and a richer summary unlocks. A quantile names a position in the sorted data: the value with a given fraction of the data below it. Because that needs only ordering and no arithmetic, quantiles reach onto the ordinal rung, where means cannot follow. You can read the median CQC rating (Inadequate < Requires Improvement < Good < Outstanding) even though a “mean rating” is meaningless.
The median is the most-used quantile of all – the value with half the data below and half above. To find it, sort the values and take the middle one. With an odd number of values that is a single observation; with an even number it is the arithmetic mean of the two middle ones. Return to the five homes – 2, 3, 3, 4, 18, already in order – and with five values the middle one is the third: the median is 3.
The wider family is just the same idea at different resolutions: divide the sorted data into equal-sized groups. Percentiles make 100 groups, vigintiles 20, deciles 10, quintiles 5, quartiles 4 (Q1, Q2, Q3 at the 25th, 50th, and 75th). The median is simply the 50th percentile, the second quartile, the fifth decile – the same point named at different resolutions.
The median earns its keep because it is robust: one monstrous value barely moves it, so when a few values lie far from the rest it often describes the typical experience better than the mean. “Half of providers have shorter waits, half longer” is also easy to say and hard to misread.
Beyond the median, the wider family is what lets you rank and set thresholds. “This provider is in the top 10% for incident rates” lands with a non-technical audience far better than “1.5 standard deviations above the mean”, and “inspect everyone above the 95th percentile” is a clear, defensible rule. Because quantiles assume nothing about the shape of the distribution, they stay meaningful whether the data is normal, lopsided, or lumpy – and, again, they work on ordinal data where means cannot go.






Note🤔 Rethinking: Percentiles aren’t percentages



“This provider is at the 90th percentile” does not mean 90% of their patients experience some outcome. It means the provider ranks above 90% of other providers. The percentile is a position in the distribution of providers, not a rate within any one of them.
A provider at the 90th percentile for waiting times might have a mean wait of 4 hours – the percentile says 90% of providers have shorter mean waits, and tells you nothing about the spread of waits inside that provider, where some patients wait 2 hours and some 6. For regulatory communication, spell it out: “this provider’s incident rate is higher than 90% of similar providers,” not “this provider is at the 90th percentile.”

title: 3  Central Tendency and Position
text:
3.4 Arithmetic: The Mean
At the top rung, where the gaps between values are real numbers, arithmetic unlocks the most powerful summaries in the book – and the most demanding. The mean uses every value, which is what makes it informative and also what makes it fragile: a single extreme value drags it, and it needs genuinely quantitative data beneath it.
And the mean itself is not one thing – several calculations share the name, each combining numbers differently to suit a different kind of data. Write the values as \(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\), where \(n\) is how many there are, and give each value a weight \(w_i\) when some should count for more than others. The symbol \(\sum\) means “add up over all the values” and \(\prod\) “multiply them all together”. Then:

Arithmetic mean \(\displaystyle \mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\) – add the values up and divide by the count.
Geometric mean \(\displaystyle \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right)^{1/n}\) – multiply the values together and take the \(n\)th root (it needs positive values).
Harmonic mean \(\displaystyle \frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} 1/x_i}\) – divide the count by the sum of the reciprocals.
Weighted (arithmetic) mean \(\displaystyle \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}\) – multiply each value by its weight, add those up, and divide by the total weight.

On the five homes – 2, 3, 3, 4, 18 – the arithmetic mean is \((2+3+3+4+18)/5 = 6\). Notice that the mean (6) sits well above the mode and median (both 3): the single home with 18 incidents has pulled it up, while the median has not budged. That one comparison is the whole case for robustness, and it takes no special vocabulary to see.
The other means are for data the arithmetic mean handles badly. Three annual growth factors of 1.05, 1.10 and 1.03 multiply to 1.189, whose cube root, 1.059, is the growth rate that compounds correctly across the three years – the arithmetic mean would overstate it. And for rates built on different denominators – incidents per bed-day across a 100-bed and a 5-bed home – the harmonic mean respects those denominators, where a plain mean of the two rates would not. The table says which to reach for:

Which mean to reach for







Mean
Best for
CQC example




Arithmetic
Additive quantities, roughly symmetric data
Mean waiting time, incident counts


Geometric
Multiplicative processes, growth rates, ratios
Year-on-year percentage changes


Harmonic
Rates whose denominators vary
Incidents per 1,000 bed-days across providers of different sizes


Weighted arithmetic
Aggregating across different sizes or precisions
National benchmark weighted by provider size




Whose mean? Weighting and why it matters
Any mean can be weighted so some observations count for more than others, and the choice is rarely neutral. An unweighted national mean treats every provider the same and answers “what is typical for a provider?” – the right lens for fairness across providers. Weighting by size lets a 100-bed home count for ten times a 10-bed home and answers a different question, “what is typical for a patient?” – the right lens for system-level and resource questions. (Weights can also reflect precision, so more reliable estimates count for more, or recency, so newer data counts for more.) Neither is more correct; they answer different questions, so pick the one that matches your purpose and say which you used.
This matters most where providers differ wildly in size. A hospital with 10,000 admissions and 500 readmissions (5%) carries far more information than a unit with 20 admissions and 2 readmissions (10%); the plain arithmetic mean of the two rates is 7.5%, but that treats them as equals, where a weighted or harmonic mean respects how much each one actually tells you. A good habit is to compute more than one: if the arithmetic and harmonic means diverge sharply, that is itself a finding – your denominators are heterogeneous, and you must decide, deliberately, which mean answers your question.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: The generalised mean



The arithmetic, geometric, and harmonic means are not three unrelated tricks; they are one formula seen at three settings. The generalised mean (or power mean) is
\[M_p(x_1, \dots, x_n) = \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{\,p} \right)^{1/p}\]
that is: raise each value to a power p, take the ordinary mean, then take the pth root. The familiar means are particular values of p: \(p = 1\) gives the arithmetic mean, \(p \to 0\) the geometric, \(p = -1\) the harmonic, and \(p = 2\) the quadratic mean (root mean square). Larger p leans towards the big values, smaller p towards the small ones.
Weighting is a separate dial layered on top: replace the simple mean with a weighted one (\(w_i\) on each \(x_i\)) and you can have a weighted arithmetic, geometric, or harmonic mean. Every mean you have ever used is some choice of these two dials – a combination rule (p) and an importance rule (w). For CQC work you almost never touch p directly: additive data wants arithmetic, multiplicative data geometric, rates with varying denominators harmonic. A power mean with p outside {−1, 0, 1, 2}, or a weighted geometric or harmonic mean, is worth raising with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) – valid, but in need of justifying.




When the mean is fragile: robust alternatives
The mean’s habit of using every value is also its weakness: a single extreme case drags it, as the 18 just did. When that worries you, three robust options keep the description honest:

the median you have already met – it cares only how many values lie on each side, not how far the extremes reach;
a trimmed mean drops a fixed fraction from each end before averaging – a 10% trimmed mean discards the lowest and highest 10% of values, then takes the arithmetic mean of what remains;
a winsorised mean keeps every observation but caps the extremes, replacing the lowest and highest few with the nearest surviving value before averaging.

For most CQC work, reporting the mean and the median together and explaining any gap between them is clearer than reaching for these – and a large gap is a finding worth stating plainly (the formal language for that lopsidedness, skew, comes in Distribution Shape). For a high-stakes output, or when extreme values need investigating rather than taming, take it to the Guild and to Outlier Methods.

title: 3  Central Tendency and Position
text:
3.5 Choosing the Right Measure
It is tempting to read the ladder as “always climb as high as you can”. That is the wrong lesson. The ladder is a gate, not a ranking: it tells you which measures are valid – you cannot take a mean of nominal categories – not which is best. Once a measure is permitted, the question decides, and a lower-rung measure can be the better choice on higher-rung data.

What each rung makes available






If your data is…
the valid summaries are…




Nominal (categories)
the mode


Ordinal (ordered)
the mode, plus the median and quantiles


Quantitative (numbers)
the mode, the median and quantiles, plus the mean



For quantitative data – the top rung – all three are on the table, and which serves you best depends on the question. Reach for the mean when every value should count and when the result will feed further arithmetic (standard deviations, z-scores, tests). Reach for the median when extremes would distort the mean, or when “half are above, half below” communicates more honestly. Reach for the mode when the single most common value is the point – the typical rating, the usual length of stay. Often the right answer is more than one: reporting the mean alongside the median says more than either alone, precisely because the gap between them is informative.
Audience nudges you along the same lines: technical readers are comfortable with means, standard deviations, and confidence intervals; non-technical readers follow medians and percentiles more easily; and regulatory communication often shows both – the median for the typical experience, the mean for the system-level picture.

title: 3  Central Tendency and Position
text:
3.6 Position Isn’t Everything
Everything so far describes where the data sits. But location is only half a description. Two providers can share a mean incident rate of 10 a month while one runs steadily at 9–11 and the other lurches between 0 and 20 – same centre, completely different stories. The missing half is spread, and it is the essential complement to location: turn to Variation and Uncertainty next, and to Distribution Shape to see how the shape of a distribution decides whether the mean or the median tells the truth.

title: 4  Measuring Variation
text:
4.1 How Spread Out Is Your Data?
The previous chapter found the middle; this one asks how far the data spreads around it. The two questions are inseparable, because the middle on its own can hide everything that matters.
Two providers might both average 10 incidents a month. One records 9, 10, 11, 10, 10 – steady, predictable. The other records 0, 2, 18, 20, 10 – the same mean, a completely different operation. Location alone cannot tell them apart; spread can. And spread is what makes a single value unusual: a provider five incidents above the mean is barely worth a glance where the typical swing is ±10, but a klaxon where it is ±2.
Spread, like location, climbs the ladder of measurement. Each rung unlocks a richer way to describe it: at the bottom you can say only how diverse the categories are; add ordering and you can measure a range; add arithmetic and you can measure the typical distance from the mean. This chapter climbs those rungs, working a single scenario as it goes – one region’s providers, looked at three ways: their type (nominal), their rating (ordinal), and their monthly incident counts (quantitative).

title: 4  Measuring Variation
text:
4.2 Spread Without Order: Diversity
Even where you cannot order the values or do arithmetic on them, data still varies – it can pile into one category or scatter across many. That is diversity (or heterogeneity), and it is the only kind of spread nominal data admits.
The plainest measure is the proportion in the largest category: if 90% of providers are one type, the mix is homogeneous; if no category dominates, it is diverse. Two summaries make this precise. Writing \(p_i\) for the proportion in category \(i\) and \(k\) for the number of categories (and \(\sum\) for “add up over all the categories”):

Gini’s heterogeneity index \(\displaystyle D = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i^{\,2}\) – the chance that two cases picked at random fall in different categories. \(D = 0\) when everything is in one category; it rises towards \((k-1)/k\) as the categories even out. (The same quantity travels under several names: the Gini–Simpson index, or Simpson’s diversity index. They are one formula.)
Shannon’s entropy \(\displaystyle H = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \log_2 p_i\) – a measure from information theory, in bits. \(H = 0\) for one category, rising to \(\log_2 k\) for a perfectly even split. It is more sensitive to rare categories than Gini’s index.

Take the region’s ten providers: seven care homes, two GP practices, one hospital. Gini’s index works out at \(D = 1 - (0.7^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.1^2) = 1 - 0.54 = 0.46\), Shannon’s entropy at \(H = 1.16\) bits.
Two numbers are hard to feel in the abstract, so it helps to see the scale they sit on. The panels below hold the same ten providers but vary the mix – all in one type, the region’s actual spread, and as even a split as three types allow. Diversity is, quite literally, how level the bars are: one tall bar means a single type dominates and both indices collapse to zero; bars of equal height push both indices to their maximum.








Figure 4.1: The same ten providers sorted into three types, shown at three points on the diversity scale. Left: all ten share one type, so there is no diversity and both indices are zero. Middle: the region’s actual mix (seven care homes, two GP practices, one hospital), giving Gini D = 0.46 and Shannon H = 1.16 bits. Right: as even a three-way split as ten providers allow, close to the maxima (D = 0.67, H = 1.58 bits). The more level the bars, the higher both indices climb.





Only the first of those two numbers speaks for itself. Gini’s index is a probability: \(D = 0.46\) means that if you pick two of these providers at random, there is a 46% chance they are different types – a statement anyone can act on. Shannon’s \(1.16\) bits, by contrast, tells you almost nothing on its own. Bits of what, measured against what? The figure becomes interpretable only beside a yardstick, and the natural yardstick is the most diverse this mix could be: with three categories, the perfectly even split, worth \(\log_2 3 \approx 1.58\) bits. Now \(1.16 / 1.58 \approx 0.73\) says something real – this region is about three-quarters of the way to a perfectly even spread of provider types.
That same yardstick sharpens even the readable Gini index. Is \(0.46\) “a lot” of diversity? Set against its own maximum of \((k-1)/k = 0.67\) for three categories, it sits about two-thirds of the way to maximal – aligned with the verdict the entropy gave, as it should be. The lesson is general: a diversity index read in isolation is hard to judge, so always pin it to something. Report it either normalised to its 0–1 maximum (the dedicated Overthinking box in this chapter gives the recipe for both indices) or as a comparison – this region against another, or this month against last.






Note🤔 Rethinking: Why diversity matters for regulation



Heterogeneity is not just bookkeeping – it changes what a finding means.
A region’s incident categories are a case in point. If 90% of incidents are medication errors, the problem is concentrated and targetable; if they spread evenly across ten categories, that points to a systemic quality issue with no single lever to pull. The same logic reads provider types in a region (a homogeneous mix is simpler to benchmark; a diverse one needs stratified, like-for-like comparison) and ratings over time (rising diversity means the system is pulling apart, some improving while others slip; falling diversity means it is converging). Measuring diversity tells you whether variation is concentrated where you can act on it, or diffuse in a way that signals something structural.

title: 4  Measuring Variation
section: 4.3 Spread You Can Order: Range and the IQR
text:
4.3 Spread You Can Order: Range and the IQR
Add an ordering and a new idea becomes available: you can mark off where the data starts and ends. Two measures matter here, and both carry a subtlety worth stating plainly – the difference between naming an interval and measuring its length.
Start with the range. The word does double duty. It can mean the interval the data covers – “ratings run from Requires Improvement to Outstanding”, “waits run from 1 to 19 hours” – which needs only an ordering: name the smallest and largest values and you are done. Or it can mean the length of that interval, the single number \(\text{max} - \text{min}\), which is the form most textbooks call “the range”. That subtraction needs real numeric gaps, so it sits on the top rung – you cannot subtract Requires Improvement from Outstanding. Either way the range is pinned entirely to the two extremes, so a single freak value sets it.
Because it rides on those two extremes, the range means quite different things depending on whether your data is a whole population or a sample drawn from one – the distinction the previous chapter drew between describing the data in hand and estimating from a larger group. For a whole population the range is a fixed fact: by definition every value already lies inside it, so it states the outer limits and nothing more. That is occasionally what you want – a bounds check, “no wait exceeded 19 hours” – but it says nothing about how the bulk of providers behave, and adding more of the same population cannot change it. For a sample the range is no longer fixed but random, and that cuts both ways. On the one hand it now carries genuine information about the particular cases you drew. On the other, every extra case is another chance to catch a new extreme, so the observed range tends to grow with the sample size and, on average, falls short of the population’s true span. “Waits ran from 1 to 19 hours” can therefore say almost as much about how many providers you happened to sample as about the underlying variation. Either way the moral is the same: lean on the interquartile range, which ignores the extremes entirely.
The same interval-versus-length distinction runs through the far steadier interquartile range:
\[\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\]
from the 25th percentile (\(Q_1\)) to the 75th (\(Q_3\)). Finding those quartiles is a matter of position: sort the data and read off the values a quarter and three-quarters of the way through, so the pair \((Q_1, Q_3)\) marks out the central 50% of the distribution with only an ordering required. Turning that pair into the single number \(Q_3 - Q_1\) is again a subtraction, so the IQR as a width needs quantitative data, just as the range does.
This is exactly how far the region’s ratings reach. The ten providers are rated one Requires Improvement, six Good, three Outstanding; the median rating is Good, and the central 50% are all Good – so as an interval the IQR collapses onto a single grade. You can say that honestly without computing any width: the ratings barely spread. What you cannot do on ordinal data is put a number on the spread – no IQR width, no standard deviation – because the gaps between grades are undefined.
The IQR’s real virtue, which comes into its own once the data is numeric, is robustness: because it looks only at the central 50%, no extreme value can inflate it. Hold that thought – it is about to matter.

title: 4  Measuring Variation
section: 4.4 Spread You Can Compute: Variance, Standard Deviation, and CV
text:
4.4 Spread You Can Compute: Variance, Standard Deviation, and CV
At the top rung, where the gaps between values are real numbers, you can measure the typical distance from the mean. Take each value’s deviation from the mean, square it (so that values above and below do not cancel), average the squares, and you have the variance; its square root, back in the original units, is the standard deviation, \(\sigma\). Writing the values \(x_1, \dots, x_n\) with mean \(\mu\):
\[\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2, \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}\]
That is the recipe in symbols: square the distances from the mean, take their mean. If you want the standard deviation, “undo” the squaring. Variance (\(\sigma^2\)) lives in squared units (incidents², hours²) and is what many methods use internally; the standard deviation (\(\sigma\)) is in the original units and is the one to report – “the typical distance from the mean is about 6 incidents.” They are the same information, related by \(\text{variance} = \sigma^2\).
That word distance is the heart of it, and it is worth seeing rather than just reading. Both clouds below share the same centre; the standard deviation is, near enough, the typical length of the spokes running from each point to that centre – short on the left, long on the right.








Figure 4.2: Two synthetic point clouds with the same mean (the dark central point), each spoke joining an observation to that mean. Left: low variation, points sit close to the mean, so the typical spoke is short. Right: high variation, the same number of points scatter much further, so the typical spoke is long. The standard deviation summarises that typical spoke length.





You may have seen this formula with \(n-1\) in the denominator rather than \(n\). That is the sample version: when the data is not the whole story but a sample standing in for a larger population you cannot fully see, dividing by \(n-1\) corrects a slight bias in estimating that population’s spread, and the symbols switch to \(s^2\) and \(s\) to mark the change. It is the same idea; the reasons are inferential, and we return to them in Statistical Inference. For the sake of this chapter and the point we are trying to make, it does not matter which version we use.
The five care homes’ incident counts – 2, 3, 3, 4, 18, mean 6 – show both the machinery and its weakness. The deviations from the mean are −4, −3, −3, −2, 12; their squares 16, 9, 9, 4, 144 sum to 182, so \(\sigma^2 = 182/5 = 36.4\) and \(\sigma \approx 6.0\) incidents. Notice that the single home at 18 contributes 144 of those 182 – almost the entire spread – exactly as it dragged the mean in the last chapter. The standard deviation inherits the mean’s fragility.
The IQR does not. The central 50% of those five values runs from 3 to 4, so the IQR is just 1: the 18 lies outside it and leaves it untouched. This is the spread-side echo of mean versus median. The standard deviation uses every value and feeds the methods in Part II – z-scores, control limits in SPC, t-tests – but pays for it by bending to outliers; the IQR shrugs them off, and as a pair of quartile positions it can be read even on ordinal data. When the data is lopsided, report both.
One more measure earns its place when you need to compare across indicators. A standard deviation carries units, so you cannot ask whether waiting times “vary more” than incident rates by comparing their standard deviations directly. The coefficient of variation strips the units out by measuring spread relative to the mean:
\[\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \quad (\text{often as a percentage})\]
Read it as a question: how big is the typical wobble compared with the typical level? A CV of 0.2 says the standard deviation is one-fifth of the mean, and that statement holds whatever the units – which is exactly why it travels where a raw \(\sigma\) cannot. Expressing spread in proportion to location also matches how variation is felt: a swing of ±2 hours is alarming around a mean wait of 3 hours and trivial around a mean of 50. So waiting times with a mean of 3 hours and \(\sigma\) of 0.6 (CV = 20%) and incident rates with a mean of 15 and \(\sigma\) of 3 (CV = 20%) are equally variable in relative terms, however different their raw standard deviations. For the five homes the CV is \(\sigma / \mu = 6.0 / 6.0 \approx 100\%\) – the typical distance from the mean is about as large as the mean itself, a number that screams “outlier” on its own. Because it divides by the mean, the CV needs a non-zero mean and a meaningful zero on the scale, so it suits rates and counts but breaks down when the mean nears zero or the scale has no true zero (temperature in °C, say).
The CV is really the first of a small family of normalised spread measures – rescalings that let two quantities on different scales be compared. The Overthinking box gives the general recipe and two more members.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Normalising a spread measure



Normalising means rescaling a measure so that values from different scales can be set side by side. The coefficient of variation does it by dividing by the mean; the most general recipe does it by reference to a known range,
\[\text{normalised value} = \frac{\text{value} - \text{min}}{\text{max} - \text{min}}\]
which maps anything onto a 0–1 scale once you know the smallest and largest values it can take. This is the move behind normalised entropy, \(H / \log_2 k\), which divides Shannon’s entropy by its maximum \(\log_2 k\) so the diversity of incident types (say ten categories) and of provider types (four) sit on the same 0–1 footing however many categories each has. Gini’s index normalises in exactly the same way – divide by its own maximum, giving \(D_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{D}{(k-1)/k} = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\,D\). That is the calculation behind calling the region’s \(D = 0.46\) “two-thirds of the way to maximal” earlier: \(0.46 / 0.67 \approx 0.69\). The same rescaling is occasionally applied to a variance whose theoretical maximum is known.
Two cautions. First, min–max normalisation leans on the extremes, so a single outlier sitting at the maximum quietly compresses everything below it. Second, normalising a number does not lower its data-type requirements: rescaling an IQR or a variance still needs quantitative data, whereas normalised entropy works all the way down on nominal data. When you want a robust, unit-free spread measure for skewed numeric data – where the CV would mislead – reach instead for the quartile coefficient of dispersion, \(\text{QCD} = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{Q_3 + Q_1}\). Its numerator is just the IQR; its denominator, \(Q_3 + Q_1\), is twice the midhinge \((Q_1 + Q_3)/2\) – a robust measure of centre, the quartiles’ counterpart to the mean. So the QCD is spread divided by centre, exactly the logic of the CV, but built from quartiles instead of the mean and standard deviation: an outlier-resistant CV for skewed data.
Reach for a normalised measure whenever the thing you are comparing sits on a different scale: different indicators, different baseline levels across regions, or the same indicator over time as its mean drifts. If you are unsure which normalisation fits, or stakeholders find them opaque, raise it with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild).

title: 4  Measuring Variation
text:
4.5 Seeing the Spread
Summary numbers are indispensable, but they can agree on the headline and hide the story. A box plot turns the five-number summary into a picture – the median, the quartiles (the IQR as the box), the reach of the whiskers, and any outliers as separate points – so the spreads of several groups can be set side by side at a glance. Always look before you summarise. (Choosing and building these plots well is a craft in its own right, taken up in Data Visualisation.)
The plots below make the case. Four provider types have been given similar typical waiting times, yet their spreads could hardly be more different.








Figure 4.3: Box plots comparing synthetic waiting times (hours) across four CQC-regulated provider types (n = 100 per group, values are illustrative). Care homes show the tightest spread; GP practices and mental health trusts show substantially higher variation, with outliers visible in the mental health trust group.





The box plot earns its keep for comparison, but it still shows only those five numbers: two groups can share a median, a box, and whiskers yet be shaped quite differently inside them.
