If you want to pack in more – the full distributional shape, where the data actually piles up, whether it is single-peaked or hiding a second mode beneath a long tail – a ridgeline plot draws each group’s whole density as its own curve.








Figure 4.4: Ridgeline plot of the same synthetic data: each row is the distribution’s shape for one provider type, drawn as a smoothed density. Where the box plot reports five numbers, this shows the whole shape. Care homes are narrow and symmetric; hospital trusts moderately wide; GP practices wide and flat; mental health trusts right-skewed with a long upper tail.





The care homes are tightly clustered; the GP practices and mental health trusts spread wide, the latter with a long tail of high outliers. No table of means would have told you that – and the choice between \(\sigma\) and the IQR for these groups follows straight from what the plots reveal.

title: 4  Measuring Variation
text:
4.6 Choosing the Right Measure
As with location, the ladder is a gate, not a ranking. It rules out what makes no sense – there is no standard deviation of unordered categories, no arithmetic spread on a rating scale – but among the measures it permits, the question decides.

What each rung makes available






If your data is…
the valid spread measures are…




Nominal (categories)
diversity – Gini’s heterogeneity index, entropy, proportion in the largest category


Ordinal (ordered)
the above, plus the range and the IQR as intervals (lowest-to-highest, central-50% span)


Quantitative (numbers)
the above turned into widths (range and IQR as single numbers), plus variance, standard deviation, and the CV



As you climb the ladder you can often compute several of these, but they are not interchangeable – some carry more information than others, and which is most helpful depends on the question, so there is no absolute decision tree. On quantitative data a lower-rung measure is frequently the wiser pick. Reach for the standard deviation when the data is roughly symmetric and the result will feed a method that needs it (z-scores, control limits, t-tests). Reach for the IQR when outliers or skew would inflate \(\sigma\), or when “the central half spans this range” communicates more honestly – and, as ever, reporting both is frequently the most truthful answer, since the gap between them is itself a signal. When you are comparing across indicators, scales, or time, switch to a normalised measure (the CV, or the QCD for robust comparison).






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Robust spread beyond the IQR



The IQR is the workhorse robust measure of spread, but it is not the only one, and at least two more are worth knowing because of their popularity.
The first is the mean absolute deviation: rather than squaring each distance from the mean and then taking a square root, simply average the distances themselves, \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|x_i - \mu|\). Using the absolute value instead of the square stops large deviations from dominating as heavily as they do in the standard deviation, so it is a little more forgiving of outliers, and preserves the same measurement unit.
The second goes further. The median absolute deviation (typically referred to as MAD - although the previous measure would abbreviate to the same thing) replaces both the mean and the averaging with medians: take the median of the absolute distances from the median, \(\text{median}\bigl(|x_i - \tilde{x}|\bigr)\). Because every step uses a median, no single extreme value can shift it – it is as robust as the IQR. It has statistical properties that, under the right conditions, let it stand in for the standard deviation, which makes it a popular robust replacement for \(\sigma\) in the outlier rules of Outlier Methods.
For most CQC work the standard deviation and IQR are enough, and reporting both already signals when robustness matters. Reach for these only when you need a robust spread expressed on the standard deviation’s own scale – and if the choice is consequential, raise it with the Guild.

title: 4  Measuring Variation
text:
4.7 Spread Isn’t the Whole Story
Location and spread are the first two questions to ask of any distribution, and together they already say far more than either alone. But notice how often this chapter reached past them for a third idea to settle a choice: the long tail that made \(\sigma\) misleading, the lone value at 18 that the mean could not survive but the IQR shrugged off. That third idea is the distribution’s shape, and it is what ultimately adjudicates between \(\sigma\) and the IQR. Distribution Shape takes it up directly.
Spread is also the engine of the comparison methods still to come. Every time SPC draws a control limit, or z-scoring measures how far a provider sits from its peers, it is the standard deviation from this chapter doing the work.

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
5.1 Beyond Centre and Spread
The last two chapters gave you two numbers for a distribution: a centre, from Central Tendency and Position, and a spread, from Measuring Variation. It is tempting to think that pins a distribution down. It does not. Two distributions can share the same mean and the same standard deviation and still look nothing alike.
Figure 5.1: Two distributions with the identical mean (10 days) and the identical standard deviation (2 days), yet plainly different shapes. Left: symmetric, balanced about the centre. Right: lopsided, with a long tail of slow cases pulling to the right. Centre and spread agree; the shape disagrees. A report quoting only the mean and the standard deviation would treat these two services as the same.
That gap is what this chapter is about. Shape is everything about a distribution that survives once you have accounted for where it sits and how wide it is: read only the two numbers and you would call these services identical, when one runs steady and the other hides a tail of slow cases.
It helps to have words for the parts of a distribution before naming its shapes. Picture the histogram as a landscape. Most cases pile up in a central bulk around the high point, the peak; from there the counts fall away on each side into the tails – the sparse stretches at the far left and right where values grow rare. A distribution is symmetric when its two tails mirror each other, and shape is simply the form of this landscape: where the bulk sits, how the tails behave, and how many peaks there are.
Shape climbs the ladder of measurement along with everything else: the higher the rung, the more precisely you can pin it down, until at the top real numeric gaps let you put an exact figure on it. Whether one side of a distribution carries more of the cases than the other – the lean – and how much of the data sits out in the extremes – the tail weight or thickness – are matters of order and counting, as much at home on an ordinal scale as a quantitative one. Only a tail’s length truly needs numbers, since length is a distance, and distance wants a scale with real gaps and, usually, no fixed ceiling. So the questions below are sharpest for quantitative data, but you can put them to ordinal data too:
At the very bottom of the ladder, nominal categories carry no order at all, and with no line to lay them along there is no side to lean toward and no far edge for a tail to reach – so skew and tail weight fall away entirely. All that remains is counting: whether the cases concentrate in one category or spread across many. That is worth knowing, but it is the diversity of Measuring Variation rather than shape, and even calling a tall category a “peak” is shaky, since nominal bars can be reordered at will. Shape, in the sense this chapter means, begins where order does.

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
Which way does it lean? Its skew – does more of the data bank up at one end, with the opposite tail drawn out?
How many peaks does it have? Its modality – one bulk, or more than one?
How heavy are the tails? Its tail weight – how much of the data sits far out in the extremes, rather than near the bulk?

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
5.2 Which Way It Leans: Skew
The lopsided distribution from the opening figure has a name. When one tail is drawn out longer than the other, the distribution is skewed, and it is named for the side the long tail is on:

Right-skewed (or positively skewed): a long tail reaching to the right, toward the high values. This is the common case in regulatory data, because so many quantities are bounded below by zero but unbounded above. Waiting times, length of stay, incident and complaint counts, rates per 1,000 bed-days – most cases sit low, a few run very high.
Left-skewed (or negatively skewed): a long tail reaching to the left, toward the low values, with the bulk pressed up to the right.

Left skew deserves more attention than its “rarer” reputation suggests, because so many CQC indicators measure performance, and performance is often bounded above. The share of patients seen within a target, the percentage of records complete, a compliance score out of 100 – none can exceed its ceiling, so when most providers do well the distribution banks up against the top and trails away below. Pushed far enough this becomes a ceiling effect: a pile-up of providers at the maximum value (100%, or 1 for a proportion) that show little to no spread. A floor effect is its mirror image at the bottom. Ceilings are worth reading carefully, because very different things produce them – genuinely high performance, an indicator too easy to max out, or the pull of a financial or contractual incentive that turns the target itself into the goal – and the distribution alone will not tell you which. The shape flags the question; it does not answer it.
The figure below lays the three skewness cases side by side, and marks on each the two centres you already know.








Figure 5.2: The same idea in three shapes, each with a vertical line for the mean (magenta) and the median (teal). Symmetric (centre): the two coincide. Right-skewed (left): the long right tail pulls the mean above the median. Left-skewed (right): the long left tail pulls the mean below the median. The mean is drawn toward the longer tail; the median, counting cases rather than weighing values, stays nearer the bulk. As the main text notes, this neat ordering is the typical case, not a guarantee.





You have two dependable ways to read the lean, and a third to handle with care. Plot it: a histogram shows the asymmetry at a glance, and the look-first habit means you never skip this. Compare the mean with the median: the median splits the cases into two equal halves, so a long right tail of large values drags the mean above it – which is why mean above median points to a right lean, mean below median to a left one. It is a handy check, but only a heuristic, and it fails more often than textbooks admit: in discrete data where the halves either side of the median are uneven, in distributions with more than one peak, and where one tail is long while the other is heavy, the mean and median can sit the “wrong” way round. So let the gap suggest the lean and the plot settle it. The third way is a skewness statistic – but there is no single such number, the family of them is the business of the box below, and like any one-number summary each can mislead. Use one to confirm the picture, never to replace it.
Reading the lean pays off at once, because it settles choices the last two chapters left open. On a skewed distribution the median describes a typical case better than the mean, which the long tail drags away from the bulk – the familiar fact that most people earn below the “average” wage is this effect. The IQR likewise serves better than the standard deviation, which the tail inflates. And the handful of values out in the long tail are the expected shape of the data, not anomalies to flag – a distinction the chapter on unusual observations takes up. Quoting the mean and the median together, and naming any gap, is often the single clearest thing you can do.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Putting a number on skew



There is no single “skewness”; there is a family of measures, and they do not always agree.

The one most software returns is the moment coefficient (Fisher’s, also called Pearson’s): the average cubed standardised distance from the mean. Cubing keeps the sign – which way it leans – and weights far values heavily, which makes it sensitive and easily thrown by one outlier. It needs real numeric distances, so it belongs to quantitative data.
Pearson’s median coefficient, \(3(\text{mean} - \text{median})/\sigma\), is just the mean–median gap of the main text put on a scale. The “mean above median means right skew” rule is this measure with its sign read off, which is exactly why, like the rule, it is only a heuristic.
Bowley’s quantile coefficient, \((Q_3 + Q_1 - 2Q_2)/(Q_3 - Q_1)\), is built from the central half, so it shrugs off outliers (at the cost of ignoring the tails entirely). It is robust, but note it still does arithmetic on the quartile values, so it needs genuine numeric distances; robustness is not the same as working on ordinal data.

Two cautions close this off. First, even on numeric data no single number captures a shape: the measures can disagree, and the comfortable rule that positive skew means mean above median can fail. Second, none of these measures honestly fit ordinal data such as Likert scales, because every one of them needs real distances between the categories, and scoring the rungs 1 to 5 just assumes equal spacing. You can still read an ordinal lean by eye – from the bar chart and, if you have only one mode, from where this lies with respect to the median. Purpose-built ordinal measures do exist that work from cumulative counts alone, but they are specialist. So for ratings, plot the distribution and describe what you see, and take any attempt to put a number on the shape to the Guild – which is the right instinct whenever measuring shape starts to feel high-stakes.

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
5.3 How Many Peaks: Modality
The second question is the simplest to ask and one of the most consequential to answer: how many peaks does the distribution have? One clear peak makes it unimodal; two makes it bimodal; more, multimodal. It matters because a second peak often means two different groups have been pooled into one chart – and that is a finding, not a nuisance.








Figure 5.3: A bimodal distribution, deliberately less tidy than a textbook one: length of stay for a pooled set of patients, with a shorter-stay group around 4–5 days and a longer-stay group around 9–10 that overlap in the middle. The mean (magenta) and median (teal) both fall in the shallow valley between the humps, where comparatively few patients lie – describing neither group well. The honest response is to split the two groups and describe each on its own.





This is where centre and spread fail most plainly. The mean and the median sit close together in the dip between the groups, describing the quick day-cases and the long admissions about equally badly – the “typical” six-and-a-half-day stay belongs to neither. No measure of skew or tail weight rescues this; only noticing the second peak does. It is the same warning the data visualisation chapter raised when a bin width too coarse hid two groups inside one hump – and real data rarely separates as cleanly as a textbook picture, which is exactly why you have to look. When you see a second peak, the response is not a cleverer summary but to disaggregate: find what splits the humps – two services, two sites, two cohorts – and describe each on its own.

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
5.4 How Heavy the Tails: Extremes
The third question is about the far values: setting aside which way a distribution leans, how often does it throw up a value a long way from the centre? This is its tail weight (the formal name is kurtosis). Two distributions can share a centre, a spread, and even perfect symmetry, and still differ in how often they reach the extremes.








Figure 5.4: Three symmetric distributions with the same mean and the same standard deviation, drawn as density curves and differing only in their tails. The platykurtic curve (gold) is a flat block that ends abruptly near ±1.7 standard deviations – its tails are not merely light but absent. The mesokurtic curve (navy) is the familiar bell. The leptokurtic curve (magenta) has a taller, narrower peak and, crucially, rides above the bell out in the tails: it produces extreme values more often. Same spread, very different tails.





Tail weight is the quiet one of the three, but it carries a real regulatory message: it is about the risk of the rare, serious event. To see why, it helps to have a concrete benchmark. Under the bell – the mesokurtic reference – roughly 68% of values fall within one standard deviation of the mean, about 95% within two, and about 99.7% within three. This is the empirical rule, and it gives the standard deviation a meaning you can act on: a value more than two standard deviations from the centre is a one-in-twenty event; one more than three standard deviations is a one-in-three-hundred event. A heavy-tailed distribution undermines those odds: more than 5% of values land beyond two standard deviations, so the extreme values the bell says should be rare turn up more often than expected. A heavy-tailed quantity – the severity of incidents, say – therefore throws up extremes more often than its centre and spread would suggest, so a mean and a standard deviation that look reassuring can still sit over a distribution that occasionally spikes hard. It also reframes outliers: under heavy tails a far-out value may be the normal product of the shape rather than an error or a true anomaly – a thread the chapter on unusual observations picks up.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Leptokurtic, mesokurtic, platykurtic



Tail weight has a name, kurtosis, and the three cases have names too: a heavy-tailed distribution is leptokurtic, a light-tailed one platykurtic, and the bell itself is mesokurtic. The measure behind them is the average fourth standardised distance from the mean. The normal bell scores exactly 3 on it – that value is not arbitrary, it is simply what the bell works out to – so kurtosis is almost always quoted as excess kurtosis, kurtosis minus 3, which puts the bell at 0, leptokurtic above it, platykurtic below.
Two cautions and a consequence:

It is named for the tails, not the peak. The tall central spike of a leptokurtic curve is a side effect of holding the spread fixed while pushing weight into the tails, not the thing being measured.
It is even less robust than skew: built on the fourth power of distance, a single extreme value can swing it wildly.
It changes which inferential tools are valid – just as skew does. Many standard tests and intervals assume the bell and use its tail probabilities to turn a result into a p-value or a margin of error. Hand them a leptokurtic distribution, which throws up extreme values far more often than the bell predicts, and those probabilities come out wrong: the test understates how often a large value arises by chance, so the p-value is too small and you over-call significance. Light tails bias it the other way. When the tails matter and you are leaning on normal-theory inference, this is probably the time to look for some support.

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
5.5 What to Do About Shape
Shape is information, not a defect to scrub out. But it steers what you do next, in two arenas.
Describing the data. This part you have already met: when a distribution is skewed or heavy-tailed, quote the median and the IQR rather than the mean and standard deviation; when it is bimodal, split it; and say which you did, in plain words. No special machinery is needed.
Analysing it. The moment you move from describing data to drawing inferences from it – testing whether two services differ, estimating a value with a margin of error, fitting a model – shape stops being cosmetic. Most of the standard toolbox (the t-test, ordinary linear regression, the usual confidence interval) is built on a roughly symmetric, bell-like shape and leans on it to turn results into probabilities; symmetry is also the mathematically simplest case, which is why so much theory assumes it. Feed those tools a strongly skewed or heavy-tailed quantity and the probabilities can be wrong, as the boxes above describe. You then have three honest routes:

Make the data fit the tool. Re-express the quantity on a scale that pulls it toward symmetry – a logarithm for a long right tail, a square root for counts, exponentiating for a left tail, or a more general transform – then use the standard methods on the transformed values. (The mechanics, and the catch on the way back, are in the box below.)
Make the tool fit the data. Reach for methods that expect the shape: generalised linear models for skewed or count data, and richer location-scale-shape families that let the shape itself vary. These ask more statistical background but do not distort the data.
Lean on the shape as little as possible. Rank-based and other nonparametric methods assume almost nothing about the distribution, trading a little power for robustness – often the quickest safe option.

Which route fits is a method-by-method judgement that the later chapters take up, and Statistical Inference is where it begins. The one thing never to do is hand a strongly non-symmetric quantity to a tool that assumes symmetry and report the result as if the shape were not there.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: The mechanics of a log transform



The most common way to “make the data fit the tool” is to work with the logarithm of a strictly positive, right-skewed quantity – incident rates, waiting times, lengths of stay. A log scale compresses large values far more than small ones, so a long right tail is pulled in and the distribution often comes out close to symmetric, ready for the standard methods. Three strings are attached:

It changes the question. Differences on a log scale are multiplicative, not additive: a step of the same size will mean “twice as long”, not “two hours longer”. That is sometimes exactly the right way to think about a rate, and sometimes a quiet distortion of it.
It cannot take a zero. The logarithm of zero is undefined, so data with genuine zeros (no incidents this month) needs care – a small offset is the usual fix, but it is a choice to declare, not hide.
You must translate back. Stakeholders cannot act on log-days. Convert results to the original scale before reporting, and note that the back-transformed centre is a geometric mean (the natural average on a multiplicative scale, met in Central Tendency and Position), which is always not greater than the ordinary mean.

If a log does not straighten the distribution, or the data has meaningful zeros, do not force it – fall back on robust summaries, or on the shape-aware and nonparametric methods above. When the choice changes a published conclusion, it is worth raising with the Guild.

title: 5  Distribution Shape
text:
5.6 Where Shape Leads
Shape is the third and last of the everyday descriptors. Centre says where a distribution sits, spread says how wide it is, and shape – which way it leans, how many peaks it has, how much weight rides in its tails – says whether the first two can be trusted to speak for it, and whether the tools you are about to use will hold. That reach is what makes the chapter a hinge. It points back to the summaries and measures of spread it helps you choose between; it reaches across the ladder of measurement to ordinal scales, which have a lean and a tail-weight of their own; and it points forward twice – to the near question of when a value in the tail is merely the shape and when it is genuinely unusual, which the chapter on unusual observations takes up, and to the deeper one of how shape governs the validity of statistical inference, further on. The habit that carries through all of it is the one this part of the book keeps repeating: plot the distribution and read its shape before you trust any number drawn from it.

title: 6  Unusual Observations
text:
6.1 When a Value Stands Out
The chapter on distribution shape closed on a question it could raise but not settle: when is a value out in the tail simply the shape of the data, and when is it genuinely unusual? A right-skewed waiting-time distribution is meant to throw up the occasional very long wait; a heavy-tailed measure of incident severity is meant to spike now and then. Knowing the shape tells you that extremes will occur. It does not tell you which extreme, sitting in front of you now, has earned a second look.
That is this chapter’s work. An unusual observation – the everyday word is outlier – is a value that stands apart from the rest of the data: a waiting time far longer than the others, an incident rate well above a provider’s peers, a staffing level far below comparators. The single most important thing to understand about such values is also the most easily forgotten: there is no universal definition of “unusual.” What counts as extreme depends on the question you are asking, the data you are asking it of, and the context you are asking it in. The same number can be a glaring anomaly in one analysis and an unremarkable case in another.
Figure 6.1: The same 30-day wait (magenta line) against two services with the identical mean (14 days) and the identical standard deviation. Service A runs steady and symmetric, so a 30-day wait sits far beyond anything the service produces – a glaring anomaly. Service B has a long right tail of slow cases, so the very same 30-day wait is an ordinary, if unwelcome, member of the tail. The value did not change; the distribution behind it did. “Unusual” is a statement about a value and its backdrop, never a property of the number alone.
The figure makes the point sharper than words can. Both services share a mean of 14 days and the same standard deviation, the two numbers the last few chapters taught you to lead with – yet a 30-day wait means utterly different things in each. In the steady service it is off the map, a value the process never otherwise reaches, and it demands an explanation. In the service with a long tail it is exactly the kind of slow case the shape predicts. Read only the centre and the spread and you would treat the two waits the same. The shape, and the context it stands for, is what separates an anomaly from an ordinary extreme.
This is also where the ladder of measurement reappears. “Far from the rest” is sharpest on a quantitative scale, where distance is a real number you can put a figure on. One rung down, on an ordinal scale, you can still call a value unusual – a single Inadequate rating among a provider’s otherwise Good ones stands out by its position even though “how far” has no exact size. At the bottom, on a nominal scale, all that remains is rarity: a category that hardly any cases fall into. The higher the rung, the more precisely you can say not just that a value is unusual but how unusual – which is exactly the precision the line-drawing later in this chapter trades on.

title: 6  Unusual Observations
text:
6.2 Why a Value Stands Out
Before you decide what to do with an unusual value, find out why it is unusual – because the common reasons call for opposite responses, and the most damaging mistake in this whole area is to treat them as one. There are three to keep in mind.
A data error is the most common cause in practice, and the cheapest to fix. A decimal point goes missing and 23.4 becomes 234; days are entered where the field wanted hours, so a 3-day stay is logged as 72; a system writes 999 where a value is missing; a copy-and-paste lands one provider’s figures under another’s name. These values are not extreme so much as impossible, and many an alarming outlier simply dissolves once you check the source. The response is to verify, then correct the value if you can or set it aside with a documented note if you cannot. Before you ever investigate a provider for extreme performance, make sure you are not investigating a typo.
A genuine extreme is real, rare, and accurately recorded – the value is right, and it is often the whole point of looking. What lies behind it can be one of two things, and the distinction matters more than it first appears. Some genuine extremes are structural, a standing feature of the provider: a specialist unit that takes the sickest patients, a home serving a uniquely high-dependency population, a service whose casemix simply differs. Others are events – something that genuinely happened and may yet pass: a care home in the middle of an outbreak with high mortality this month, a staffing crisis that drives complaints up, a new infection-control protocol that drives incidents down. The first kind is about who the provider is; the second about what happened to it – and because an event plays out over time, telling it from ordinary fluctuation is the territory of statistical process control (one unusual month may be a blip; six in a row is a different story). Either way the response is the same in spirit: not to fix or remove anything, but to understand the cause and report it. An unusual value is not the same as a bad provider, but it is frequently the signal that sends an analyst to the door.
Mixed populations produce values that are not extreme for their own group but only look extreme once groups are pooled. A hospice quietly included in an analysis of acute hospitals will sit at the far end of almost any mortality measure – not because anything is wrong, but because it is being compared with the wrong things. A 20-bed care home dropped into a comparison of 200-bed nursing homes will scatter far more widely than its neighbours. The response is to compare like with like: stratify by provider type, size, or setting before flagging anyone.
Notice that the three reasons fan out into three different responses – correct the error, understand the genuine extreme, stratify the mixed comparison. That is why “what is causing this?” must come before “what shall I do about it?”

title: 6  Unusual Observations
text:
6.3 Where to Draw the Line
Once you accept that “unusual” is relative, the next question is where to put the line – and the honest answer is unsettling: you put it there. There is no threshold handed down by nature, only several reasonable rules that disagree.
The most familiar rule measures distance from the centre: how many standard deviations a value sits from the mean, \(z = (x - \mu)/\sigma\), with the line drawn at a \(z\) beyond 2 or beyond 3. It is intuitive and quantitative, but it leans on the mean and the standard deviation, both of which a large outlier inflates – so an extreme value can quietly drag the line out far enough to hide itself. This is really a family of rules rather than one, and the mean-and-standard-deviation version is simply its most common member: a robust cousin measures distance from the median instead, scaled by the median absolute deviation in place of the standard deviation – the basis of the modified z-score taken up in outlier detection methods – precisely so the outlier cannot inflate the yardstick meant to catch it. A position-based rule sidesteps the centre altogether by counting rather than measuring: flag the top 5%, or use Tukey’s fences and flag anything beyond 1.5 × the IQR from the quartiles. Because it rests on quantiles rather than the mean, it shrugs off the very values it is hunting and copes with skew. A model-based rule asks how poorly a value fits an expectation built from a provider’s characteristics – a residual from a model that already accounts for casemix, size, or acuity – and so flags values that are unusual for their circumstances, not merely large. And a domain rule sets the line from clinical or regulatory knowledge: a mortality figure, a staffing minimum, or a waiting time past which the value warrants attention whatever the statistics say. Each of these is defensible. Each draws the line in a different place.
Before reaching for any of them, though, the cheapest and most often skipped move is simply to look at the data – the same look-first habit the data visualisation chapter pressed. A histogram shows the shape; a box plot turns the five-number summary you met under variation into a compact picture built for spotting stragglers. The box spans the middle 50% – the IQR from \(Q_1\) to \(Q_3\) – with the median marked inside; each whisker reaches to the most extreme value still within 1.5 × IQR of the quartiles; and anything past that is drawn as its own point. Those 1.5 × IQR limits are Tukey’s fences, and the points beyond them are precisely the position-based flags from a moment ago, made visible.








Figure 6.2: A box plot of provider incident rates (the same data as the next figure). The box is the middle 50% – the IQR from Q1 to Q3 – with the median inside; the whisker reaches the furthest value still within 1.5 × IQR of the quartiles; the points past it lie beyond Tukey’s upper fence (dashed) and are the box plot’s flagged stragglers. The rule a box plot quietly applies is one position-based line among the several drawn in the comparison that follows.





The box plot, and the fences, come from John Tukey’s Exploratory Data Analysis (1977), the work that gave the technique its name. Tukey called the 1.5 × IQR limits the inner fences and a wider 3 × IQR pair the outer fences, beyond which a value is “far out”. Why 1.5? There is no deep theory behind it – it is a convention, and Tukey’s own defence amounted to saying that 1 flags too much ordinary data and 2 misses too many genuine extremes. A box-plot flag is therefore a prompt to look, not a verdict – even faultless data throws the occasional point past the fence.
One refinement matters for regulatory work, and it sharpens the precision argument still to come. The fraction of observations Tukey’s approach flags is not fixed: it drifts with how many you have. A larger dataset will, by the rule alone, produce more flagged points – the mirror image of the small-provider trap below, and another reminder that one fixed rule does not mean the same thing across providers of different sizes. The plain fences also over-flag skewed data, since they ignore the asymmetry the last chapter dwelt on (although, skew-aware versions exist). Set that single fence beside the other rules and the disagreement is plain:








Figure 6.3: One set of provider incident rates, three defensible rules, three different verdicts. Each provider is a dot; the vertical lines are the upper cut-offs from three common rules. On this right-skewed data the mean-plus-two-standard-deviations rule and Tukey’s fence flag noticeably more providers than the stricter three-standard-deviations rule. No rule is wrong – but which providers land on your investigation list depends on a choice you made, not on the data alone.





This is a researcher degree of freedom in plain sight: the rule and the threshold you pick shape the answer, even though each pick is defensible on its own. The remedy is not to pretend one rule is the “correct” one – it does not exist – but to choose the rule before you look at the data, to say why you chose it, and, when the stakes are high, to show how the verdict shifts under the alternatives. That last move, varying the choice and reporting what changes, is a sensitivity analysis, and it is the antidote to quietly trying thresholds until the flagged list looks the way you hoped. The chapter on QA principles makes a discipline of it.
Not every line is the analyst’s to choose, though. In regulation many thresholds are set externally – a national target for desired performance, a contractual minimum, a published standard – and these do real work: they split providers into meeting or missing the mark, and they feed methods such as z-scores measured against an external target and the banding that sorts providers into performance groups (taken up in z-scoring). An external threshold spares you the choice but not the responsibility. A cut-off still has consequences – who is investigated, who is judged to have fallen short – so it needs a justification, and the justification worth trusting is rarely the tidiness of the number. It rests on domain and clinical expertise about what level genuinely matters, not on the fact that 2, or 95%, or 10 makes for a round figure.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: the extreme that drifts back on its own



Take the most extreme provider on some measure this period – the very top or the very bottom of the ranking, on incident counts, on a satisfaction score, on a performance target, on anything – look again next period, and it will tend to sit closer to the middle of the group, even when nothing about it has changed. This is regression to the mean, a property of measurement rather than of management, and it is blind to whether the extreme was “good” or “bad”.
Any single figure is part signal and part noise: a true underlying level plus the luck of the period. The providers at the extreme of a ranking are disproportionately those whose noise happened to push the same way as their signal this time. Noise does not persist, so next period it tends to fall back and the figure drifts toward the bulk of the group on its own. The regulatory hazard is mis-attribution, in both directions: act on the worst performers and watch them “improve”, or single out the best and watch them “slip”, and it is tempting to credit – or blame – the action when much of the movement was always going to happen. The pull is strongest exactly where it does most harm: small providers, whose figures are the noisiest of all, as the next section takes up.
The guard is not to abandon flagging but to ask for a sustained signal rather than a single extreme reading – several periods, or several indicators pointing the same way – which is one reason monitoring over time, in statistical process control, and benchmarking across providers, in z-scoring, are built around patterns rather than one-off extremes.

title: 6  Unusual Observations
section: 6.4 Small Providers and the Precision Trap
text:
6.4 Small Providers and the Precision Trap
One mixed-population case is so common, and so often misread, that it deserves its own warning: the small provider. Small denominators make rates swing wildly for reasons that have nothing to do with quality or exceptional circumstances. One incident in a 20-bed home is a 5% rate; one incident in a 200-bed home is a 0.5% rate. The smaller home looks ten times worse, yet both had a single incident. The difference is the denominator, not the care.








Figure 6.4: Two hundred providers that all share the same true incident rate of 5 per 100, plotted against size (log scale). Small providers rest on small denominators, so their observed rates scatter widely – and a flat threshold (magenta, 8 per 100) flags a whole cluster of them (gold triangles) for nothing more than being small. The size-aware funnel limits (grey band, ±3 standard errors around the centre) widen for small providers and flag almost none. Spread is not the same as quality.





The width of natural variation depends on size, so a single flat line drawn across every provider flags the small ones simply for being small. The fix is to let the limits breathe: widen them for small denominators to match the precision the data can actually support. That is the idea behind a funnel plot, which the chapter on z-scoring develops into a working method – limits that flare out for small providers and close in for large ones, so that a provider is flagged only when it strays beyond what its own size would lead you to expect.
Widening the limits is one route; standardising the measure is the other. Rather than judge raw rates against a moving boundary, you can convert each provider’s value into how far it sits from what its size leads you to expect – a z-score, or a standardised ratio of observed to expected – so that size is folded into the number itself and every provider lands on one comparable scale. The funnel plot and the standardised score are two views of the same idea, and z-scoring is where the book works it through. The detail can wait; the instinct should not – never read a small provider’s raw rate as if it were as solid as a large one’s.
The trap cuts both ways, and the second edge is easy to miss. Small providers are not only prone to false alarms; they are also where a real problem is hardest to detect, because the same wide variation that throws up spurious highs can swallow a genuine signal. A small provider that clears no threshold has not been shown to be safe – it may simply be too small for any statistical rule to be sure. Absence of a signal is not the same as absence of a problem, and “statistical outlier” and “quality outlier” are not synonyms in either direction.

title: 6  Unusual Observations
section: 6.5 What to Do About an Unusual Value
text:
6.5 What to Do About an Unusual Value
The cardinal rule is short: never silently delete an unusual value. Investigate first, then decide – and let the decision follow the evidence rather than the convenience.
Investigation runs along the grain of the three origins above. Verify the data: go back to the source, check the units and the definition, make sure the value belongs to the provider it is filed under. Understand the context: what kind of provider is this, what period does the value cover, is it a one-off event or a standing feature? Look at the pattern: is the value unusual against its peers, against the provider’s own history, or both, and does it stand alone or is it echoed across related indicators? Weigh the regulatory significance: does the value point to possible harm, does it meet a threshold that triggers action, and what is the cost of acting against the cost of doing nothing? Only once you can say why the value is unusual are you in a position to decide what to do with it.
Four responses are worth knowing, and they belong in a deliberate order, default first.
Keep it and document it is the default, and for most regulatory work it is also the finish. When a value is verified and real, the honest thing is to retain it, report it plainly, and explain why it stands out. This matters most when you are describing what exists rather than estimating some underlying parameter: drop the high performers to tidy the picture and you have not cleaned the data, you have biased it, and you have hidden the very providers a regulator exists to notice.
Use robust summaries when a genuine extreme distorts the conventional statistics but you do not want to lose it. The median in place of the mean, the IQR in place of the standard deviation, Spearman’s correlation in place of Pearson’s – each describes the bulk of the data without letting one far-out value set the tone, so you can characterise the typical case and keep the extreme one in plain view.
Transform the scale when the issue is the shape the extremes create rather than any single point – most often a long right tail. Re-expressing a strictly positive, right-skewed quantity on a logarithmic scale pulls the tail in and often restores the symmetry that standard methods assume. The mechanics, and the catch on the way back to the original scale, are set out in the chapter on distribution shape; the thing to carry here is that a transformation reshapes all the data to tame a few values, which is sometimes exactly right and sometimes a quiet distortion.
Remove it is the last resort, justified only when a value is a confirmed, uncorrectable error or belongs to a fundamentally different population – the hospice mistakenly filed among acute hospitals, not the provider whose figure you simply dislike. “It made the results cleaner,” “it was more than three standard deviations out,” and “it did not fit the hypothesis” are not reasons; they are the warning signs of a result being shaped to taste. If you do remove a value, you owe the reader the sample size before and after, a stated rationale, and a sensitivity analysis showing whether the conclusions survive the exclusion. If the flagged list barely moves, say so and proceed with confidence; if it lurches, report both versions and lower your confidence accordingly.






Note🤔 Rethinking: in regulation, the outlier is often the point



Textbooks tend to present outliers as a nuisance: detect them, delete them, analyse the clean remainder. In a regulatory setting that instinct is close to backwards.
In academic estimation, an outlier can genuinely distort your picture of a typical case, and removing a confirmed contaminant sharpens the estimate. But CQC is frequently not trying to estimate the typical case at all – it is trying to find the exceptions. The provider with the extreme rate, the service that broke from its own history, the home whose figure no comparator explains: these are not noise getting in the way of the analysis, they are the analysis. Strip them out for the sake of a tidy distribution and you have deleted the findings.
Two consequences follow. First, the burden of proof runs the other way from the textbook default: keep an unusual value unless you can show it is an error or a different population, rather than removing it unless you can show it is real. Second, the asymmetry of mistakes matters – missing a genuinely failing provider (a false negative) is a graver regulatory failure than investigating one that turns out to be fine (a false positive), so a habit of over-removal quietly tilts the system toward the more dangerous error. Flag, investigate, document, and keep. Reserve removal for the cases you can defend out loud.


The methods above cover almost everything you will meet. Two further techniques are worth knowing in their own right – genuinely useful in the right place, hazardous in the wrong one.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: winsorising and trimming



Two long-standing techniques limit the influence of extreme values without investigating them case by case. Trimming discards a fixed fraction from each tail – drop the top and bottom 5% and work with the middle 90%. Winsorising instead pulls the extremes in rather than dropping them: anything above the 95th percentile is set to the 95th-percentile value, anything below the 5th to the 5th, so the number of observations is unchanged.
These are legitimate, familiar tools, and CQC already uses them: the current z-scoring guidance winsorises the most extreme z-scores before estimating overdispersion, so that a few outlying providers do not inflate the spread the method is calibrated against (following Spiegelhalter and colleagues). That is just the kind of job they are good for – stabilising an estimate, or reshaping a quantity so a tool that assumes a particular shape can be used at all (the “make the data fit the tool” route from the chapter on distribution shape).
The caution is about what they must not become. Because winsorising and trimming change the data by rule, wholesale, with no one looking at an individual provider, they are the wrong instrument for deciding which providers are unusual – the investigate-first work of this chapter. Used to stabilise an estimate or meet a method’s assumptions, declared and justified: helpful. Used to quietly tidy away the extreme providers you are meant to be finding: the same trap as automatic removal. So reach for them deliberately, not by reflex; and if a winsorising or trimming choice could move a published or actioned conclusion, reach out to the Guild or the Practice Lead to help you weigh it.

title: 6  Unusual Observations
text:
6.6 Where Unusual Values Lead
This chapter has been deliberately conceptual: it gives you a way of thinking about values that stand out, not a button to press. That framework – no universal definition, different origins to tell apart, a line you choose and must defend, investigation before action, and keeping over removing – is the foundation the rest of the book builds the machinery on.
The computational tools come next door, in outlier detection methods, which turn the lenses of this chapter into reproducible rules – z-scores, modified z-scores, Tukey’s fences applied at scale – and combine them so that agreement across methods, rather than any single rule, prioritises what to investigate. When the task is comparing many providers against a benchmark, z-scoring and its funnel plots put the small-provider precision trap on a proper footing. When the task is watching a single provider over time, statistical process control separates a genuine shift – a special cause – from ordinary fluctuation. The deeper question of when an unusual value undermines a method’s assumptions – rather than merely needing a note – opens out in statistical inference and runs through every test that follows. And the discipline that holds all of it together – recording which rule you used, what you found, what you decided, and what you would have found under other choices – is the business of QA principles.
The habit underneath them all is the one this part of the book keeps returning to: look at the value in its context, ask why it is there, and decide on the evidence – never reach for the delete key because a number is inconvenient.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
7.1 From a Sample to a Population
A care home in your region has recorded 30 per cent more falls than the national average over the past six months. That is not a number to wave away. Six months of consistently elevated falls is a real signal, not the sort of blip a single bad week produces, and it deserves attention. But a signal is not yet a verdict. Counts of comparatively uncommon events bounce around from month to month; small homes bounce around more than large ones; and “the national average” is itself only an estimate. What you want to know is the home’s true underlying falls rate – the rate that would show through if the month-to-month noise settled – and what you actually hold is six months of counts. Reasoning carefully from the one to the other, and being honest about how far the data let you go, is the work of statistical inference.
That reasoning has a standard shape, and it is worth naming its parts precisely, because they are easy to run together. The population is the complete collection of units you want to draw a conclusion about: every care home in England, say, or – when the unit is a stretch of time – every month this home runs under its current management. A parameter is a fixed numerical feature of that population: the average falls rate across all homes, or this one home’s true underlying rate. The parameter is what you actually care about, and it is unknown. The sample is the slice you observe: the 200 homes that returned data, or this home’s six months of counts. From the sample you compute an estimate – a number meant to stand in for the parameter. Population and parameter are the reality you are after; sample and estimate are what reach you; inference is the bridge. Keeping the population (a set of units) distinct from the parameter (a number describing them) is the first discipline of the subject: a great deal of confused statistics comes from sliding between the two.
This is also where a change of notation pays off, and it is worth saying plainly where it comes from. Some quantities you could compute exactly only if you held the whole population at once – full information. Others are what a sample actually gives you – partial information, a realisation that would have come out differently from a different sample. To keep the two apart, statistics writes them with different symbols. The population mean and standard deviation, the theoretical quantities full information would settle, are written \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\). The mean and standard deviation you compute from the data in hand are written \(\bar{x}\) and \(s\). The chapter on describing data used \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) throughout, because there the data in hand were the whole of what it set out to describe. The moment you treat your data instead as a sample standing in for something larger, \(\bar{x}\) and \(s\) become estimates of the unknown \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) – and it is that move, from a number you have to a claim about one you do not, that this chapter is about.
It is worth being concrete about what the population and sample really are in regulatory work, because the honest answer is rarely the tidy one the textbooks assume. The cleanest case is a genuine random sample from a fixed population, and CQC does work with data of this kind: national patient and staff surveys, for instance, draw from a defined population by design. But surveys bring their own difficulty. People choose whether to respond, and those who respond can differ systematically from those who do not, so what returns is often not a random sample of the population but a self-selected slice of it. Other data are administrative rather than sampled: registration records, statutory notifications, and inspection histories that in principle cover every provider of a type. Even there, “the whole population” is more aspiration than fact – records lag reality, changes go unrecorded, providers fail to report, and definitions drift over the years, so completeness is never guaranteed. Naming the population first, and asking honestly how the data in hand came to be the data in hand, is the part of inference the formulae cannot do for you. A standard error answers exactly one question – how much an estimate would wobble from one random sample to the next – and it answers that well; it says nothing about a population that was never randomly sampled, nor about the providers whose data never arrived.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
Note🤔 Rethinking: when inference does not apply



The formal machinery of this chapter is most secure when your data arose from a known random mechanism – because a standard error is defined as the spread of an estimate across repeated random samples, and that definition only cashes out precisely when you can describe exactly how the sampling was done. But variability does not disappear just because sampling was not strictly random. A purposive sample – chosen to hit particular groups or criteria – will still differ from one collection occasion to the next, producing a scatter of estimates if the exercise were repeated. What becomes harder is formally defining the shape of that scatter and giving a precise meaning to the probabilistic statements that come with a confidence interval or a p-value. The formal machinery presupposes a model for how the data were generated; the less clearly that model holds, the more cautiously the numbers should be read.
This matters because the temptation to report the interval anyway is strong. Faced with a self-selected survey, a convenience sample, or the subset of providers that happened to return a form, it is easy to run the same formulae and report a confidence interval as though the sampling had been clean. It had not, and the interval inherits the flaw silently: it will look just as tidy whether or not the responders resemble the population. Where the departure from random sampling is understood, it can sometimes be modelled and corrected; where it cannot, the honest course is to treat the figures as a description of the data you hold and to resist pushing them further.
The simplest version of the principle is this: if you genuinely held the full population – every provider, every record, complete and correct – there would be nothing to infer. Statistical inference exists because complete information is almost never available. Where information is close to complete, the residual uncertainty is usually not “which providers did we happen to pick?” but “are the figures complete, comparable, and free of recording gaps?” – and that question is one the standard error cannot reach.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
7.2 What Makes an Estimate Good
Most inference rests on an estimation procedure, and to judge one well it helps to separate three things a single sentence usually blurs. The estimand is the unknown quantity you are actually after – the home’s true falls rate, the proportion of all homes that are genuinely good. It is a fixed feature of the population, and you never see it directly. The estimator is the recipe you apply to the sample to approximate the estimand – “take the mean falls rate of the homes that reported,” say. It is a procedure, fixed before you see any particular data. The estimate is the number that recipe returns when you feed this sample in: 45 falls per thousand bed-days. Change the sample and the estimand stays put, the estimator stays put, and only the estimate moves. A point estimate is one such number – your single best guess. But a guess with no sense of its own quality can mislead as easily as inform, and the deepest habit in this chapter is judging that quality, for which there is no better picture than archery.
Picture the estimand as the bullseye and each estimate as an arrow loosed at it. Two quite different things can go wrong, and keeping them apart is the single most useful distinction in the subject. Accuracy – or its absence, bias – is whether your arrows are centred on the gold or pulled systematically to one side. Precision is whether they land close together or scatter widely, regardless of where their centre sits. The two are independent: you can have a tight cluster sitting well off the mark (precise but biased, confidently wrong), a loose spray that happens to centre on the target (accurate but imprecise), or any combination, as the four targets below lay out.








Figure 7.1: Accuracy and precision are different virtues. Top-left is the ideal: arrows centred on the bullseye and tightly grouped. Top-right is accurate but imprecise – the group centres on the target but sprays widely, so any single shot may land far out. Bottom-left is precise but biased – a tight group, but consistently off to one side, confidently wrong. Bottom-right is both biased and imprecise. A narrow grouping tells you the aim is steady; it says nothing about whether it points at the right target.





Real archery has a feature regulatory work lacks: the archer sees where each arrow lands and can correct. Inference is archery at a target you cannot see. You loose your one arrow and the gold stays hidden, so you never learn directly how far off you were – and the target is in a different place for every provider, because each has its own unknown true rate. This is why the two failures matter so unequally. Imprecision is the honest one: a scattered group openly admits it is unsure, and more data pulls the arrows together. Bias is the dangerous one, because nothing in the data announces it. A tight group looks authoritative whether or not it sits on the gold, and gathering more data only tightens a group that is aimed at the wrong place. So when you are deciding whether to trust an estimate, “could this be biased?” is the sharper question than “how precise is it?” Precision you can see and improve; bias you have to reason about.
If you cannot see where a single arrow lands, how can you say anything about precision at all? The answer is the quiet engine of frequentist inference: you reason about the shots you would take. Imagine loosing your arrow not once but again and again, under the same conditions – drawing a fresh sample each time and recomputing the estimate. Each arrow in this extended picture is a fresh sample; the bullseye is the unknown parameter; and the pattern the arrows make – how tightly they cluster, and whether their centre sits on the gold – is what statisticians call the sampling distribution. All the probabilities of this chapter attach to that imagined pattern, not to the single arrow in front of you. The remarkable part, which the next section turns on, is that you can describe that whole pattern from the one sample you actually hold.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
section: 7.3 From One Sample to Many: the Standard Error
text:
7.3 From One Sample to Many: the Standard Error
Imagine, then, drawing your sample again and again from the same population and computing the estimate each time. Those estimates would not agree; they would scatter into a distribution of their own – the sampling distribution of the estimate. Its spread is the precision of your method: a narrow sampling distribution means the estimate barely moves from sample to sample, a wide one means it is at the mercy of which sample you happened to get. The standard deviation of that imagined scatter has its own name, the standard error. For a mean it works out as the standard deviation of your single sample divided by the square root of the sample size – and that is the whole trick: both ingredients, the spread of the data and the size of the sample, are things one sample already gives you, so you never have to draw the rest. The picture below shows the imagined scatter for one estimate, the mean.








Figure 7.2: Estimates scatter around the truth in a predictable way. Each of a thousand fresh samples of the same size yields its own sample mean; collected together, those means pile up tightly around the true population value (dashed line). The spread of that pile – measured by the standard error – can be estimated from a single sample, without ever drawing the rest. A larger sample would make the pile narrower; a smaller one, wider.





Two properties of the standard error are worth holding on to. First, it shrinks as the sample grows – but slowly, in proportion to the square root of the sample size, so that quartering the uncertainty takes sixteen times the data. This is the arithmetic behind a recurring regulatory fact: large providers can be pinned down precisely, while small ones cannot, however carefully you analyse them. Second, the standard error is a measure of precision only – a particular summary of the spread of the sampling distribution, not the full picture of its shape. It says nothing about whether that distribution is centred on the truth. In the archery image, it is how tightly the arrows group, not how far the group sits from the unseen gold. If your sample is unrepresentative – the silent providers differ from the responding ones – the whole sampling distribution slides off the bullseye, and the standard error, computed from the sample you have, will not breathe a word of it. Precision is visible; bias is not.
The deeper point behind the sampling distribution is what makes frequentist inference work at all. For many common estimators, the shape and spread of the sampling distribution can be described mathematically – under stated assumptions, and often without needing any sample beyond the one you hold. Bias and precision are therefore properties of the procedure, not of any particular result: if the recipe is unbiased, its estimates cluster around the truth on average across all the samples it might ever receive, regardless of which one you drew. The stated assumptions are not decorative. The standard formula \(s/\sqrt{n}\) for the standard error of a mean assumes, among other things, that each observation is drawn independently. Cluster the providers, stratify the sample, or introduce unequal selection weights, and the formula changes – use the simple version on a complex design and the interval will look narrower than it should be. Naming the sampling design at the outset, before computing anything, is part of the work of inference, not an optional preliminary.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
7.4 Confidence Intervals
A point estimate plus a standard error is already an honest statement: here is my best guess, and here is how much it would wobble. A confidence interval packages the two into a range, and it is the form most worth reporting, because it carries the uncertainty on its face.
Before reading one, it pays to defuse a piece of cargo cult. Intervals are very often quoted at “95 per cent,” and the figure is so familiar that it can seem to come from the mathematics itself. It does not. The level is a choice, a dial you set, and 95 per cent is merely a convention with no special claim on the truth. To keep that visible, this chapter quotes an 89 per cent interval instead – a deliberately unround number, chosen for no reason except that it makes the arbitrariness impossible to forget. An 89 per cent confidence interval for the home’s falls rate of, say, 39 to 51 per thousand bed-days says: the estimate sits around the middle of this band, and values across the band are all guesses for the unknown parameter that are reasonably consistent with what we observed.
What the “89 per cent” means deserves care, because the natural reading is the wrong one. It is tempting to say “there is an 89 per cent probability the true rate is between 39 and 51.” That is not what a confidence interval claims. The true rate is a fixed number; it is either in this particular interval or it is not. The 89 per cent is a property of the method, not of this one interval: across the imagined long run of fresh samples, roughly 9 out of 10 of the intervals the procedure produces would contain the true value. The figure below makes the distinction concrete by doing exactly that.








Figure 7.3: A confidence interval is a property of the method, not of any single interval. Twenty samples are drawn from the same population, each yielding its own 89 per cent confidence interval for the mean. Most capture the true value (dashed line); a minority miss it entirely, and those misses look no different from the hits. Over many repetitions about one interval in nine misses by design – which is why ‘89 per cent’ describes the procedure across samples, not the chance that this particular interval is right.





Two practical points follow. The width of the interval is set by the standard error, so the same forces apply: more data and less variability both narrow it, and a small provider carries a wide interval no matter what. That width is a feature, not a defect – it is the analysis telling you the truth about how little it knows. The mistake is to read a wide interval as “no signal” and quietly drop the provider. A small home with a high estimated falls rate and a wide interval has not been cleared; it has been flagged with uncertainty, and from a regulatory standpoint potential harm that cannot be ruled out is a reason to look, not to look away. Statistical certainty and regulatory priority are not the same axis.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: the shape of an interval



Almost every confidence interval you meet has the same shape: an estimate, plus or minus a margin. The margin is some critical value times the standard error – for a simple mean, roughly “the estimate, give or take about two standard errors” at the conventional level. That tidy form is worth unpacking, because it shows what the interval is made of and where it can break.
The estimate is the signal; the standard error is the noise; the critical value is just the coverage you asked for, translated into how many standard errors wide to draw the band. So the width is not free – it is inherited from the estimator and the sampling design together. A mean from a simple random sample has the clean \(s/\sqrt{n}\) – the sample’s standard deviation over the square root of its size. The moment the design is more involved – stratified, clustered, weighted to correct for unequal selection – the standard error gains terms for those features, and an interval built with the simple formula will be the wrong width, usually too narrow. For some estimators there is no neat formula at all, and the margin has to be found by other means, such as resampling the data many times over.
The plus-or-minus shape also assumes the sampling distribution is roughly symmetric. For quantities pinned near a boundary – a rate close to zero, a proportion close to one – it is not, and a symmetric band can stray below zero or above one, which is nonsense. There honest intervals are asymmetric. The lesson is that “estimate ± uncertainty” is a genuinely useful habit precisely because it keeps signal and noise visibly apart, but the harder the estimator-and-design combination, the more care the second half of that expression demands.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
7.5 Hypothesis Testing
Estimation answers “how large, and how sure?” A hypothesis test answers a narrower question: “how convincingly is what we are seeing more than noise?” It is a more limited tool than estimation, and once you have an interval you have often answered the test already – but its vocabulary is everywhere in practice, so it pays to understand it precisely.
In its most common form, hypothesis testing is a contest between two stated possibilities. The null hypothesis is the position you provisionally adopt: typically the ordinary state of affairs – business as usual, the world as it would be if nothing had changed. This home’s true falls rate equals the benchmark – the national reference rate used as the comparator; the new staffing model made no difference; the two regions score alike. The alternative hypothesis is the possibility under consideration: that the rate differs, that the model helped. You then ask: would we expect to see data like this, if the null hypothesis were true? The p-value gives one answer – it is the probability, under the null hypothesis, of observing data as extreme as yours or more so. A small p-value means the data sit awkwardly with the null: they would be a surprising coincidence if business as usual were the whole story, so the sample carries some evidence that something may be going on. Not proof. A large p-value means the data are unremarkable under the null; it does not follow that the null is true, only that these data cannot rule it out.
To reach a decision from a p-value, you compare it against a pre-chosen significance level – written α (alpha) – that you fix before looking at the data. If the p-value falls below α, you reject the null; if it falls above, you do not. The most familiar threshold is α = 0.05 (5 per cent), but this carries no special mathematical authority; in regulatory settings other values are often more defensible. The choice of α is a prior judgement about the maximum false-positive rate you are prepared to accept, and it should be stated and justified, not silently inherited from a textbook.
Nothing in this procedure delivers certainty in either direction, and the most common way to misread a test is to imagine that it does.
Because a test ends in a decision – treat this as noise, or act on it – it can be wrong in two ways, and naming them now, rather than as an afterthought, is the only honest way to use it. A false positive (known as a Type I error in this setting) raises the alarm on a provider that is in fact fine. A false negative (a Type II error) misses a provider that genuinely has a problem. The threshold you set for “surprising enough to act” trades one against the other: tighten it to cut false alarms and more real problems slip through; loosen it to catch more real problems and more false alarms follow. There is no setting that escapes the trade-off, only a choice about which error you would rather make – and, as the box below argues, that choice is not a statistical one.






Note🤔 Rethinking: two kinds of mistake, and the one a regulator fears most



A smoke alarm is a useful model for the two kinds of mistake. It can fail in two ways: it sounds when there is no fire (a false positive – waking you at 3am for burnt toast) or it stays silent when there is a fire (a false negative – the one with real consequences). Making it hair-trigger catches every fire at the cost of repeated nuisance alarms that cause people to stop trusting it; making it insensitive stops the nuisance alarms but means a real fire may go undetected. The sensitivity dial is the threshold – and who should set it is whoever is most exposed to the consequences of each kind of failure.
Often, academic research and regulation differ in how they set their thresholds, and the reason is a difference in what the two errors cost. Research tends to guard against the false positive – a wrong claim that something is working might send others down blind alleys – and so adopts strict thresholds, demanding strong evidence before accepting an effect as real.
A regulator weighs the consequences instead. A false negative can mean a failing service left unexamined and people left at risk; a false positive means an inspection that finds nothing, costing time and a little reputational friction but, usually, no direct harm. Those costs are not symmetric. For high-stakes safety indicators it is often right to accept a generous false-positive rate – a looser threshold, more providers flagged for a closer look – precisely because missing a real problem is the graver failure. For lower-stakes indicators, or where the consequence for a provider is severe (such as enforcement), the balance tips back towards caution.
The probabilities of committing the two errors are intertwined: tightening the threshold to reduce false positives directly raises the probability of false negatives, and vice versa. For a given estimator and sample size there is no setting that escapes this trade-off; choosing α is always a choice about which error you would rather make more often. The only path to reducing both at once is more data, which raises power – the probability of correctly detecting a real effect when one exists. Power is not a fixed number: it rises as the true effect grows larger, so a test has higher power to detect a severe problem than a marginal one. A high-powered design rarely misses a genuine signal; a low-powered one frequently does, especially for modest effects. Both the threshold and the target power should be stated and justified before the analysis, never adjusted after seeing the results.
This is where small providers bite hardest. Thin data typically means low power: even a genuine problem may not produce a statistically clear signal, simply because there is too little information. A small provider that is flagged despite thin data may be showing a genuinely strong signal, detected against the odds. But a small provider that is not flagged has not been shown to be safe – only that the data were too sparse to detect a problem if one existed. Absence of a flag is not a clean bill of health, and reading it as one is how thin data quietly becomes false reassurance.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
7.6 Inferential Honesty
Lead with the estimate and its interval; reach for the test as a supplement, not the headline. An interval of 41 to 57 against a benchmark of 40 signals that the data are little compatible with the benchmark at the chosen level – while the range itself reveals the genuine uncertainty, since the true rate could plausibly sit barely above 40 or well above 50. A bare “the result is significant” tells you only that the computed interval excludes 40, and hides everything the range itself shows.
There is a subtler point worth holding: the interval’s edge is not a wall. By construction the interval marks a floor on compatibility – values within it are those the data most readily entertain at the stated confidence level, with values around the point estimate itself the most compatible of all, and compatibility declining continuously as you move outward from it. What moves here is the interval, not the benchmark: the benchmark is a fixed reference value – in the running example, the national rate of 40 – that the null hypothesis sets the parameter equal to; the interval is a random quantity whose realisation depends on the sample. Compatibility does not cliff-drop at the interval’s edge. Had the benchmark been set at 41 rather than 40 – just enough to sit inside this particular interval – the data would carry essentially the same information, yet the verdict would flip from significant to not significant. Any null value excluded by the narrowest margin is only marginally less compatible with the data than one that would sit just inside; the boundary simply marks where compatibility crosses the chosen floor. The interval therefore describes a region of greater plausibility, not a cage of acceptable values. And that makes the confidence level a choice as arbitrary as the 0.05 threshold: widen it from 89 to 99 per cent and the interval expands – a benchmark excluded at 89 per cent coverage may be covered at 99; narrow it and the reverse might occur. Adjusting the level after seeing the data to stretch the interval around – or away from – the benchmark is the same move as raising the p-value cutoff because the result just missed: it is shopping for a conclusion, not conducting an analysis.
Moving beyond interval estimation, once a test is in play the word significant arrives with it, and it carries more trouble than any other term in applied statistics.
Part of the trouble is the typical threshold. The 5 per cent line – p below 0.05 – is a convention, popularised by Ronald Fisher in the 1920s for agricultural field trials as a rough rule of thumb, not a law of nature and not a point where evidence switches on. Its persistence owes something to the era before computers: p-values then had to be read from printed tables that listed only a handful of critical values, and the round threshold of 0.05 was simply “just there”. Now that software produces exact p-values for almost any procedure, the rational basis for a fixed cutoff is even weaker than before. A p-value of 0.049 and one of 0.051 carry fundamentally the same weight; treating one as a triumph and the other as a failure reads a continuous measure of surprise as if it were a switch. Even the originators of significance testing knew this: as Edgeworth put it as early as 1885, a result that passes the test “is not certainly important,” and one that fails it “is not certainly unimportant.” Report the actual value and let it weigh in proportion.






Note🤔 Rethinking: what a p-value is not



The p-value is the most widely misread number in statistics, and the misreadings nearly all run the same way – towards overclaiming. Five are worth stating flatly, because each is a step from evidence towards a certainty the number cannot supply. Each is widespread enough to have prompted repeated calls for reform from professional statistical bodies; each still appears routinely in regulatory and research contexts alike.

It is not the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It is the probability of the data given the null – \(P(\text{data}\mid\text{null})\) – not the probability of the null given the data, \(P(\text{null}\mid\text{data})\). “If the home were average, how odd is this?” is a different question from “given this, how likely is the home average?”. Unfortunately, often it really is the second question that is the interesting one.
It is not the probability that your result is “due to chance.”
It is not a measure of how large or important an effect is. A microscopic, meaningless difference earns a tiny p-value given enough data.
A non-significant result is not evidence that the effect is absent. Failing to detect something is not the same as showing it is not there – especially with a small provider, where power is low.
A threshold crossing is not a conclusion. Whether p lands just above or just below a line is, on its own, no basis for a regulatory decision; and the difference between a significant result and a non-significant one is not itself a reliable signal.
