The constructive version of all this is simple: report estimates with their uncertainty rather than verdicts, be deliberate about what the data can and cannot support, and stay modest about what any single analysis establishes.


The deeper trouble is confusing statistical significance with practical significance. They answer different questions. Statistical significance asks whether an effect is distinguishable from noise; practical significance asks whether it is large enough to matter. Sample size drives a wedge between them. With a very large dataset, an effect far too small to act on – a fraction of a percentage point – will be statistically significant, because the standard error is tiny and even a trivial difference clears the bar. With a small dataset, an effect large enough to demand attention can fail to reach significance, because the standard error is wide. So significance never settles whether to act. The estimate and its interval do: look at the size of the effect and the range of sizes still consistent with the data, and ask whether anything in that range would change what you do.
Beneath all of this sits a quieter but more fundamental concern: the validity of every confidence interval and p-value depends on whether the assumptions of the procedure hold. These are not bureaucratic formalities; they are what makes the stated probabilities meaningful. An interval computed from a clustering design using a simple-random-sample formula does not have 89 per cent coverage – its true coverage is unknown, often narrower than advertised. A test applied to data that violates its independence assumption can produce p-values that catastrophically understate uncertainty. The habit to build is: name the assumptions before running the analysis, check what you can, and flag what you cannot. Inference that glosses over failed assumptions is not cautious inference – it is confident inference pointing in an unknown direction.
A final caution belongs to how CQC analysis is actually done. The formal guarantees of a test assume the question and the analysis were fixed in advance. For many analyses, it is the opposite: you look at the data, notice something, try another cut, exclude an odd record, follow a hunch. That flexibility is how you learn from data nobody designed, but it quietly inflates apparent significance – every data-driven choice is a fork the statistics cannot see, so p-values come out too small and intervals too narrow. The practical consequence is the posture of this whole chapter: treat a significant result as a reason to look, not as proof of what you will find. The strongest evidence at CQC is rarely a single significant number; it is the same signal showing up across sources that we would not believe to be all wrong in the same way.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: the Bayesian alternative



Everything covered so far in this chapter is the frequentist approach to inference. It treats the population parameter as a fixed but unknown number – a single true value the data try to reveal – and asks how estimates would behave across hypothetical repetitions of the sampling. That is why a p-value cannot be the probability the null is true, and why a confidence interval is a statement about the method rather than about any one interval. It also explains a quiet frustration: the framework will not answer the question people most naturally have, which is “given what we have seen, how likely is it that this provider is genuinely worse than average?”
Bayesian inference answers that question head-on, at the price of a different starting commitment. The parameter is not treated as a fixed unknown, but as a random variable – something that can be assigned a probability distribution. This shift is precisely what unlocks direct probabilistic statements about the parameter, rather than about hypothetical repeated samples. You begin with a prior – a distribution capturing what you believed about the provider before seeing this data, often drawn from how providers of its type typically behave. The data then update the prior, through their likelihood, into a posterior: a full probability distribution for the provider’s true rate given everything you now know. From the posterior you can read a credible interval, which really does mean “an 89 per cent probability the true value lies in this range” – the reading people wrongly attach to confidence intervals – and you can answer the regulator’s question directly.
The same machinery yields a feature that matters for small providers. When many providers are modelled together, each one’s estimate is informed by the others: a small provider’s noisy figure is pulled towards the typical pattern of its peers, by an amount the data themselves decide – heavily when its own data are thin, barely at all when they are rich. This pooling effect, or shrinkage, is not a fudge that hides bad data; it is principled regularisation, and it tends to predict an individual provider’s future better than its own raw estimate. You might have seen its frequentist counterpart in the shrinkage adjustments part of the z-scores workflow - the aim is the same.
Sounds like a dream, right? Well, unfortunately there is no free lunch, and the catch here is the choice of prior (alongside some additional computational complexity): results depend on it, so it must be stated openly and justified, never smuggled in. Doing this well is specialist work and should always be informed by domain expertise and accompanied by careful computational checks.

title: 7  Statistical Inference
text:
7.7 Where This Leads
This chapter has stayed deliberately conceptual, because the ideas here – sample and population, the aim and steadiness of an estimate, the honest expression of uncertainty, the gap between significance and importance – are the foundation the methods chapters build on, each applying them to a particular tool.

Z-scoring turns the distance of a provider’s estimate from the benchmark into a flag, with everything in this chapter standing behind the word “significant.”
Statistical process control reads a sequence over time, treating a point beyond the control limits as a signal much as a test treats a small p-value.
Comparing groups with t-tests applies hypothesis testing and confidence intervals directly, and is where the effect-size measures that formalise “how large?” – and the corrections needed when many hypotheses are tested at once – are set out.
Correlation carries the same logic over to linear relationships between variables.
Handling missing data builds on the bias-versus-precision distinction drawn here: an inference is only as trustworthy as the assumption that what is missing has not shifted the aim.
The chapter on quality assurance sets out how to document the estimates, intervals, and threshold choices a defensible analysis rests on.

The thread tying them together is the posture of this chapter: in regulatory work, inference points you towards where to look. It is the start of an investigation, not the end of an argument.

title: 8  Handling Missing Data: Foundations
text:
8.1 When the Value Isn’t There
Some missing data is waiting in every real dataset, and regulatory data is no exception. Providers do not submit returns; surveys go unanswered; a system fails to record a field; a member of staff never fills one in. The chapters so far have quietly assumed the numbers were all present and correct – a histogram drawn, a standard deviation calculated, an unusual value investigated, and most recently an estimate carried from a sample to a conclusion with its uncertainty attached. This chapter takes away that assumption and asks what to do when part of the picture is simply not there.
The instinct, faced with a gap, is to reach for something to fill it – an average, a last known value, a plausible guess. Resist it. The single most important thing to understand about missing data is that the gap itself carries information, and the first job is to read it. Why is this value absent? A figure missing because a server rebooted at 3 a.m. is a different problem from a figure missing because the provider it belongs to would rather you did not see it. The two gaps look identical in the spreadsheet – an empty cell, an NA – and call for opposite responses. Everything in this chapter follows from telling them apart.
So the question is not “do I have missing data?” – you do – but “does it matter for the question I am asking?” Sometimes a handful of absent values changes nothing of consequence. Sometimes a pattern of absence is the most important thing in the dataset. The work of this chapter is learning to tell which situation you are in before you decide what to do, and to be honest in your reporting about what the gaps allow you to claim. The computational machinery – visualising patterns at scale, multiple imputation, sensitivity analysis with code – is referred to a later chapter on computational methods for missing data. Here the work is conceptual: a way of thinking that keeps you from the two cardinal errors, filling a gap you should have read and ignoring one you should have filled.

title: 8  Handling Missing Data: Foundations
text:
8.2 Why Data Goes Missing
Not all missingness is equal, and the differences are not about how much is missing but about what the missingness depends on. In 1976 Donald Rubin set out a classification that has organised the field ever since: three missingness mechanisms, distinguished by whether the chance of a value being absent is tied to nothing, to things you can see, or to the very thing you cannot. The names are a regrettable piece of statistical jargon – two of the three contain the word “random” and mean quite different things by it – so hold on to the underlying question in each case rather than the label.
A value is missing completely at random (MCAR) when its absence has nothing to do with anything – not with the value itself, not with any other variable, observed or not. A server drops two per cent of records at moments unrelated to which records they are; a monitor fails on whichever patient happens to be next; you deliberately survey a randomly chosen half of providers and leave the other half blank by design. Under MCAR the values you do have are a fair, if smaller, sample of the values you wanted: the providers with complete data look, on average, just like the ones without. This is the benign case, and it is also the rare one. True randomness is uncommon in administrative data, because absence almost always relates to something – an older system, a smaller team, a busier week. Note the qualifier on the survey example: missingness by design is MCAR only when the selection is genuinely random. The moment a design leaves data blank according to a provider characteristic – send the long form only to large providers, sample more heavily in one region – the missingness depends on something you can see, and it is no longer MCAR. Treat MCAR as a useful idealisation to measure reality against, not a description you should expect to fit.
A value is missing at random (MAR) when its absence depends on things you have observed, but not – once those are accounted for – on the missing value itself. This is the mechanism whose name does the most damage, because MAR data is precisely not missing at random in the everyday sense. Smaller providers might be less likely to return a particular metric; older respondents less likely to finish a survey; certain regions habitually late. In each case the gaps are patterned, but the pattern runs through something you can see – size, age, region – and within each group the missingness is as good as random. The practical promise of MAR is that the information needed to correct for the gaps is sitting in your own dataset: if you account for the variables the missingness leans on, you can recover an unbiased picture. The practical catch is that you can never be quite sure you have caught them all.
A value is missing not at random (MNAR) when its absence depends on the missing value itself, even after you have accounted for everything you can observe. The provider with a high incident count is the one that does not submit incident data; the patient with the poor experience is the one who abandons the satisfaction survey; the service with the awkward cost figure is the one that withholds it. Here the gap is informative in a way you cannot reach – the reason a value is missing is bound up with how large or small or troubling it would have been, and no amount of looking at the data you have can tell you by how much. MNAR is the mechanism that should worry a regulator most, and it is exactly the one that regulation is most likely to produce, because the incentive to not report is often strongest where the unreported number is worst.
Three mechanisms, then, with a clean logical ladder between them: MCAR depends on nothing, MAR on what you can see, MNAR on what you cannot. Two cautions keep this from hardening into something it is not. The first is that the data itself can carry you only partway up that ladder, a limit worth being precise about. The second is that these three labels, useful as they are, compress a richer story into a single word – a shorthand it pays to look behind once the stakes of the distinction are clear.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: what the data can and cannot tell you about the mechanism



Rubin’s mechanisms have an exact definition that the prose above smooths over. Let \(R\) be a response indicator for some variable – \(R = 1\) when the value is recorded, \(R = 0\) when it is missing – and split the data you wanted into the part you observed, \(X\), and the part that went missing, \(Y\). The three mechanisms are statements about what the probability of being missing, \(P(R = 0)\), is allowed to depend on:

MCAR: \(P(R = 0 \mid X, Y) = P(R = 0)\) – the missingness depends on neither \(X\) nor \(Y\).
MAR: \(P(R = 0 \mid X, Y) = P(R = 0 \mid X)\) – it may depend on the observed \(X\), but not further on the missing \(Y\).
MNAR: \(P(R = 0)\) depends on \(Y\) even after conditioning on \(X\).

Written this way, one awkward fact becomes plain. You can test whether missingness depends on \(X\), because you hold \(X\) for everyone: compare the observed characteristics of the cases with and without the missing value, and if they differ, the data is not MCAR. But you cannot test the step that matters most – whether, among cases identical on everything you observed, missingness still depends on the unseen \(Y\) – because testing it would require the very values that are missing. MAR and MNAR are not distinguishable from the data alone. Choosing between them is a judgement, grounded in how the data came to be and what the people who collected it know, not a hypothesis the numbers can decide. Rubin’s term for the comfortable case – MAR together with a mild technical condition – is that the mechanism is ignorable, meaning you may analyse the data without modelling the missingness process itself. The word is a promise about convenience, not about safety: a mechanism is ignorable only if MAR genuinely holds, and whether it does is something you assume rather than confirm – which is why Schafer and Graham, in their widely read review of the field, treat the choice of mechanism as a matter of judgement rather than a question the data can answer.


These categories are not academic taxonomy. Each carries a different consequence for your analysis, and the consequences come in two kinds that are worth keeping firmly apart.

title: 8  Handling Missing Data: Foundations
section: 8.3 Two Kinds of Harm: Bias and Precision
text:
8.3 Two Kinds of Harm: Bias and Precision
Missing data can hurt an analysis in two distinct ways, and confusing them is one of the commonest mistakes in this whole area. One harm is a loss of precision; the other is bias. They are not degrees of the same thing – they are different failures, with different cures, and a single estimate can suffer one, the other, or both.
The cleanest way to hold them apart is to picture estimation as archery. Building on the inference chapter’s estimates and confidence intervals, think of an estimate as an arrow loosed at a target, with the true value as the bullseye. Precision is how tightly your shots group: a precise estimator lands its arrows close together, an imprecise one scatters them. Bias is whether the group is centred on the bullseye or pulled systematically to one side. The two can be independent. You can have a tight group well off-centre, or a loose group centred on the gold. Missing data can do either – widen the scatter, shift the aim, or both – and the cure differs in each case.
Losing precision means your estimate becomes more uncertain. Throw away the incomplete records and you are working with a smaller dataset; a smaller dataset gives a wider confidence interval, less power to detect a real difference, a blurrier picture. The aim is still true – the arrows still cluster around the bullseye – it is simply a less steady hand. Every kind of missingness costs you precision, MCAR included, for the unavoidable reason that you have less data than you set out to collect. The cost is honest, in the sense that the wider interval openly admits how much less you now know.
Bias is the more dangerous harm, because it is dishonest: the estimate becomes systematically wrong – consistently too high or too low – while often looking perfectly confident about it. If the providers who fail to report incidents are the ones with the most incidents, then the average you compute from those who did report is not a slightly fuzzy estimate of the true average; it is a precise estimate of the wrong quantity, the average among the well-behaved. And here is the trap that catches people: a biased estimate does not announce itself. Its interval is an ordinary width – set by how many records survived, not by how far the aim has slipped – so it looks no different from a sound one; it simply sits in the wrong place and fails to cover the truth. The hallmark of bias is an estimate that is confidently mistaken, and the width of the interval cannot expose it: width tells you how steady the aim is, never whether it is pointed at the right target.
So the two harms map cleanly onto what the mechanisms do – though it is worth being careful with the verb, because a mechanism does not cause anything on its own; what does the damage, or avoids it, is the handling you choose in its light. Under MCAR there is no intrinsic risk of bias to begin with, because the cases you keep are a fair sample; the only cost is precision. Under MAR, bias appears if you ignore the observed variables the missingness leans on, and disappears if you account for them – the bias is real but removable, and removing it is your job. Under MNAR, bias survives every routine method, because the correction would need the missing values themselves; the most an honest analysis can do is bound it or report it. The figure below puts all three on one scale.








Figure 8.1: Three estimates of the same true average provider satisfaction (dashed line, the value you would get if every provider reported). Each shows a point estimate with its 95 per cent confidence interval. With near-complete returns the aim is true and the interval tight. Dropping incomplete records at random (MCAR) keeps the aim true but widens the interval – precision lost, no bias. Dropping records because lower-scoring providers stayed silent (MNAR) slides the estimate above the truth: the interval is an unremarkable width, set by how many cases survived, yet it never reaches the true value. Precision is how tight the grouping is; bias is whether it is centred on the target. An interval’s width tells you nothing about whether it is in the right place.





Carry the distinction with you, because it shapes every decision that follows. When you reach for a method to handle missing data, ask which harm you are trying to undo. If the missingness is MCAR and your only loss is precision, there is no bias to fix and the honest move may simply be to proceed with the smaller dataset and report the wider uncertainty. If there is bias, precision is the lesser worry: a sophisticated method that recovers a little precision while leaving the bias untouched has polished the wrong problem. And the regulatory stakes attach to both. Lost precision makes a genuinely failing provider harder to detect – it raises the false negative rate, the graver kind of regulatory mistake. Bias does something worse: it can make a whole sector look better, or worse, than it is, and quietly mislead the decisions that rest on the analysis.






Note🤔 Rethinking: the three labels are a summary, not the whole story



Rubin’s three mechanisms are the shared language of this field, and you will not get far without them. But be honest about what they are: MCAR, MAR and MNAR compress the whole story of why a value is absent into a single word, and a single word is sometimes too blunt for the awkward cases real data throws up. The label is a shorthand for the mechanism, never a substitute for understanding it.
That gap between the label and the story is exactly where regulation bites. When the stakes are low, the shorthand is enough. When they are high, go back to the story itself: what, in the real world, decided whether each value was recorded, and could that reason be tied to the very thing you are trying to judge? That is a question about how the data came to be – answered from knowledge of the service and the people who collected it, not read off the dataset – and a confident “it’s MAR” can close an argument before anyone has checked whether it is true. The harder you look, the more you are forced to say out loud what you are assuming about the gaps; those assumptions are no more testable than the MAR-versus-MNAR call itself, but at least they are now visible. What that honesty buys is not certainty but clarity about exactly what you have assumed – which is why, for anything load-bearing, the mechanism behind the gaps is a deeper conversation waiting to happen, not a label to assign alone.

title: 8  Handling Missing Data: Foundations
text:
8.4 Investigating Missingness
Because the mechanism cannot be read off a percentage, there is no shortcut threshold to lean on. “More than ten per cent missing, drop the variable” is the kind of rule that feels rigorous and decides nothing that matters: five per cent of MNAR gaps can bias an analysis badly, while thirty per cent of genuine MCAR gaps costs only precision. So the work is investigation, and it runs in four movements, from what the data shows you to what only a human can tell you. The thread that ties them together is a single posture: stop treating the gaps as nuisance to be cleared away and start looking at them, the same way you would look at the values themselves.
Quantify it first. How much is missing, and where? Not just the headline proportion but its distribution: which variables carry the most gaps, how many records are missing several fields at once, and – the question that earns its keep in regulation – whether certain kinds of provider are missing more than others. A single overall figure hides everything interesting. Missingness concentrated in one variable, or one type of provider, is the first hint that you are not in the benign MCAR case. (One caution that the methods chapter takes up in earnest: software often disguises missingness as a code rather than a blank – a 9, a 999, an “unknown” category – so a dataset that reports zero missing values on real-world data deserves suspicion, not relief.)
Look at the pattern. Numbers summarise; a picture reveals. The plainest picture is a map of the gaps: providers down one side, the metrics they should return across the other, every absent value shaded in. Scattered evenly, the way MCAR would leave it? Or clustered – in particular records, particular periods, particular providers?








Figure 8.2: A missingness map of 36 providers (rows, sorted by how many fields they are missing) against six required returns. Shaded cells are absent. The gaps are not scattered evenly: a band of providers along the top is missing several fields at once, and the incident-reports column is the most affected. This is the visual signature of non-random missingness – exactly what a fair MCAR sample would not produce.





Do certain variables always go missing together, hinting at a shared cause such as a single unfiled return? Counting how often each combination of fields is absent answers that directly, and the answer is usually that gaps travel in bundles rather than singly.








Figure 8.3: The same 36 providers, counted by which combination of fields they are missing. Most providers are complete, but the gaps that do occur arrive in bundles: staffing, incidents and satisfaction go missing together far more often than any field goes missing alone. Co-missing fields point to a shared cause – here, a strained provider failing to file several returns at once – which a single overall percentage would have hidden entirely.





These shapes – the clustered map, the bundled patterns – are the visual fingerprints of non-random mechanisms. A bar chart of co-missing fields like the one above is the simple cousin of the upset plot that the chapter on computational methods for missing data will draw at scale; the same chapter shows how to produce these maps and pattern summaries straight from a dataset, in R and Python. The point of meeting them here is to fix the habit before the tooling: a percentage tells you how much is missing, but only a picture tells you whether the missingness has a shape worth worrying about.
Relate it to what you can see. This is the step that tests, as far as anything can, where on the mechanism ladder you stand. Mark each record as missing or not on the variable of interest, and compare the two groups on everything else you do hold. Do the providers with missing satisfaction scores differ from the rest in size, region, sector, or rating? If they look the same on every observed characteristic, MCAR becomes more plausible. If they differ – smaller providers, say, far more likely to have gaps – then the missingness is at least MAR, and you have just identified the variable you will need to account for. And if the gaps cluster among providers with known risk factors – open complaints, past enforcement, a poor previous rating – then the warning light for MNAR is on, because the missingness is tracking the very thing you were trying to measure. But be clear about where this step stops. It can show that missingness depends on what you observed; it can never show that it depends only on what you observed. The last leg of the judgement always rests on assumptions and domain knowledge, because the defining feature of MNAR is precisely that the information you would need to detect it is the information you do not have.
Ask why. No statistic completes this step; you complete it by talking to the people who own the data. A gap can mean a system migration, a changed reporting requirement, a metric that simply does not apply to this provider type (which is not missing data at all, but a category to handle separately), a return that exists but has not yet arrived – or a deliberate choice not to submit. These causes sit at different points on the mechanism ladder, and you usually cannot tell which you are facing from the numbers. Sometimes the right response is to augment the data – link other records, pull a related field, bring in an external source that plausibly explains the gaps and shifts a suspected MNAR problem onto observed ground where you can handle it. That is often exactly the right move, and worth real effort. But it has a limit, and past it sits a judgement call no dataset can make for you: at some point you have to ask the people who know the service what the silence most likely means, and write down the answer as an assumption. Metadata, data managers, and the regulatory context – is submission mandatory, and what follows from not submitting? – are what turn a guess about the mechanism into a defensible judgement. The discipline of writing that judgement down, with its reasons, is part of the quality assurance that any proper analysis owes its readers.
What the four movements give you is not a verdict the data has signed off on, but an honest, evidence-backed reading of the mechanism – and a reading is often enough to choose proportionately what to do next.

title: 8  Handling Missing Data: Foundations
text:
8.5 Deciding What to Do
The mechanism you have argued for, together with how much is missing and how high the stakes are, points to one of a small number of responses. They escalate, from the light-touch default to the move that reframes the whole analysis. The table sets them side by side; the prose that follows walks the escalation.








Strategy
When it is reasonable
What it costs or risks




Complete case analysis (listwise deletion)
The missing slice is small and plausibly MCAR; complete cases resemble the full set on everything you can see
Loses precision; introduces bias under MAR or MNAR; silently changes the sample behind each table unless you report it


Single imputation (mean, median, mode, or last value carried forward)
A trivial gap, or a rough exploratory pass you will not act on
Treats a guess as a fact: shrinks the standard error, overstates confidence, and distorts the distribution and the relationships between variables


Stratify, adjust, or weight on observed variables
Missingness looks MAR and the variables it leans on are ones you hold and can put in the model
Only as good as the MAR assumption and the variables you chose; any residual MNAR bias survives untouched


Multiple imputation
MAR with useful auxiliary information; a high-stakes estimate worth doing properly
Needs the right model and care; done casually it misleads as confidently as single imputation; computational – see the methods chapter and reach out to the Guild


Maximum likelihood (model-based)
The same MAR territory; a principled alternative to imputation
Specialist tooling; tied to a specific model of the data


Sensitivity analysis / treat the pattern as the finding
Substantial or MNAR missingness; whenever the mechanism cannot be settled
Yields a range or a referral rather than one tidy number – and that honesty is the point



When the missingness is minor and plausibly MCAR – a few per cent, scattered, with the missing and complete cases indistinguishable on everything you observed – the honest response is usually the simplest. Complete case analysis – also called listwise deletion – means working only with the records that have no gaps, and under MCAR it is unbiased: you lose a little precision and nothing else. The price of admission is transparency. Report the sample size before and after exclusion, state why you judge the missingness benign, and check quickly that the complete cases really do resemble the full set on the characteristics you can see. Done openly, this is not a fudge; it is the right tool for a small, well-behaved problem. What it is not is a default to apply silently, which is how it usually gets used: the most common failing in published work is not a bad imputation but a complete-case analysis nobody mentioned (and the fact that most statistical packages have this behaviour implemented as a default for certain analyses also does not help).
When the missingness is moderate and looks MAR – patterned, but through variables you hold – the task is to use those variables so the pattern stops biasing the result. You might analyse the affected groups separately and combine them, include the variables the missingness leans on as adjustments in your model, weight the complete cases to stand in for the missing ones, or impute the gaps with a method that draws on the observed information. The first three are within reach of routine analysis; the fourth, multiple imputation done properly, is the substance of the computational methods chapter and, for a high-stakes result, a good moment to involve the Guild or the Practice Lead. Whatever you choose rests on the assumption that the missingness really is MAR – that the variables you adjusted for were the ones that mattered. State that assumption plainly, because if the truth is MNAR the bias survives every one of these methods.
One thing this stage rarely makes obvious is that the same missingness, left unhandled, can do very different damage depending on the method you feed the data into. A handful of gaps that barely shift a simple average can pull hard on a model that leans on the joint behaviour of many variables at once – a regression with several predictors, a latent-variable or item-response model, a time series. As analysis moves onto that more advanced terrain, do not reach for a general rule of thumb; check instead whether the specialist literature has studied your particular combination of method and missingness, because for many of them it has, and the findings rarely transfer. Beyond the few standard cases, there is no substitute for patience, domain expertise, and a serious attempt at sensitivity analysis.
When the missingness is substantial, or tangled up with the outcome itself – a large fraction gone, concentrated among providers you have other reasons to worry about – the right response is to stop reaching for a statistical patch. There is no method that recovers an unbiased estimate from MNAR data without assumptions you cannot check, and pretending otherwise launders a serious problem into a clean-looking number. The honest path forks. You can bound the answer with a sensitivity analysis, asking what your conclusion would be under the best and worst plausible stories about the missing values, and reporting the range rather than a false point. And you can take the step that, in regulation, is often the most important of all: stop treating the missingness as an obstacle to the finding, and recognise that it is the finding.






Note🤔 Rethinking: in regulation, who doesn’t report is often the point



Statistics textbooks present missing data as an obstacle: a nuisance standing between you and the unbiased estimate you wanted. In a regulatory setting that framing is frequently backwards.
A researcher estimating average blood pressure has no interest in the missingness for its own sake; the gaps are noise to be overcome. But CQC is often not trying to estimate a typical value at all – it is trying to find the providers that warrant attention. And when the providers least likely to submit are the ones most likely to be failing, the pattern of non-reporting carries the very signal the analysis exists to detect. Forty per cent of providers not returning incident data is not merely a hole in your denominator; it is forty per cent of providers failing a reporting requirement, concentrated – your investigation showed – among those with open complaints and prior enforcement. The estimate you could squeeze from the sixty per cent who did report is the less important number on the page.
So the question flips. Not “how do I get around the missing data to estimate performance?” but “what does the pattern of who is missing tell me about provider behaviour?” The non-reporters become a list to investigate in their own right, not because their imputed incident rate looks high, but because the silence itself is a finding – a possible failure of governance, of compliance, or of candour. A missing value, after all, is still an observation; it simply has a very special value, and in a system built to find exceptions that value can be more revealing than any number it conceals. The statistical handling still matters for whatever estimate you do report, and you should caveat it honestly. But read the gap before you fill it: the absence may be the answer.


Across all four responses runs one constant: document what you found and what you did. For any analysis that will be acted on, record how much was missing and where, what mechanism you judged most likely and on what evidence, which handling you chose and why, and – the part most often skipped – what it all means for the confidence a reader should place in your conclusions. That record is not bureaucratic overhead; it is what lets the next person see the choices you made and trust, or challenge, the result. Missing data is one of the clearest places where a defensible analysis and an indefensible one differ not in their methods but in their honesty about the gaps.

title: 8  Handling Missing Data: Foundations
text:
8.6 Where Missing Data Leads
This chapter has stayed deliberately conceptual: it offers a way of thinking about absent values, not a procedure to run. The frame – read the gap before filling it, tell the three mechanisms apart while remembering they summarise a richer process, separate bias from lost precision, investigate before handling, and in regulation treat systematic absence as a finding – is the foundation the rest of the book builds the machinery on.
It sits where it does for a reason. The inference chapter taught you to estimate a quantity from a sample and to carry honest uncertainty alongside it; this chapter took away the comfortable assumption underneath that work, that the sample was the one you meant to collect. The two ideas combine in the warning that runs through everything above: a single filled-in guess quietly lies about your uncertainty, shrinking the very confidence interval that was supposed to tell the reader how much to trust the number. That is why imputation has to be done carefully rather than casually, and why the honest goal is a valid estimate with honest error bars, not an accurate prediction of values that are gone for good.
The machinery is next door, in the chapter on computational methods for missing data, which turns these ideas into reproducible work: making disguised missingness visible, drawing the maps and patterns at scale, and carrying out the multiple imputation and sensitivity analyses sketched here in R and Python. The discipline of recording the assumptions and choices that missing-data handling forces on you belongs to QA principles, the next chapter, which makes the documentation this chapter keeps asking for into a standard you can hold an analysis to. And the habit underneath all of it is one this part of the book keeps returning to, whether the subject is an unusual value or an absent one: look at what the data is doing, ask why it is doing it, and decide on the evidence – never reach for the convenient fix because a gap is inconvenient.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
text:
9.1 Credibility and the Analytical Obligation
The 2024 independent review of CQC’s operational effectiveness, led by Dame Penelope Dash, raised concerns about inconsistency in how analytical methods were described and how evidence categories were applied. Those concerns were not merely technical. They went to whether CQC’s regulatory conclusions could be traced back to the analysis claimed to support them, and whether that analysis could survive scrutiny – from providers who might challenge ratings, from Parliament, or from the courts. When methods are not documented, chosen without justification, or used to produce conclusions more certain than the evidence warrants, the analysis becomes impossible to defend and difficult to improve.
Quality assurance is the professional response to that risk. The Code of Practice for Statistics, maintained by the UK Statistics Authority, frames the obligation in terms of three pillars: Trustworthiness, Quality, and Value. Trustworthiness concerns the governance, independence, and integrity of the people and organisations producing statistics. Quality concerns the rigour of the methods, the accuracy of the outputs, and the assurance processes that test them – the QASQA framework addresses this pillar directly through Standard 6 (Be rigorous: use suitable data sources and sound methods) and Standard 7 (Be open about quality: prominently explain strengths, limitations, and uncertainty in estimates). Value concerns whether statistics serve the decisions they are meant to serve, communicated so they are easy to use and understandable (Standard 9: Be clear) and accessible (Standard 10). All three pillars apply to quantitative work at CQC; it is Quality and Value that the QASQA framework most directly operationalises.
Quality assurance is not bureaucracy and it is not a final sign-off ritual. It is a discipline of thinking, built into the analytical process from the first conversation with a commissioner to the moment analysis is accepted and signed off. This chapter explains the governing logic of the QASQA framework. Some helpful checklists and method-specific requirements are in the individual methods chapters; the full framework document can be found here.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
text:
Note🤔 Rethinking: The Code of Practice and Why It Applies to Us



The Code of Practice for Statistics is maintained by the Office for Statistics Regulation (OSR), the regulatory arm of the UK Statistics Authority. It sets the standards that producers of official statistics – government departments, regulators, and arm’s-length bodies – must meet to deserve public trust. The OSR assesses organisations against the Code and, where standards are not met, can withdraw accreditation – a serious reputational and regulatory consequence.
CQC holds a portfolio of accredited official statistics – the highest designation for official statistics in the UK. Accreditation is not a fixed status: it is maintained by ongoing compliance with the Code, and the OSR monitors compliance actively. The requirement is therefore a live professional obligation for everyone involved in producing those statistics, not a one-time certification.
Beyond the formal requirement, the Code provides a genuinely useful framework for good practice. Its insistence on transparency about limitations, consistency in methods, and honest communication of uncertainty are the same qualities that make statistics defensible in regulatory proceedings. Producers who treat the Code as a checklist miss the point; those who internalise its logic produce better statistics and are better placed to explain and defend them.
The QASQA Framework is CQC’s primary instrument for meeting the Quality pillar of the Code at the level of individual analytical projects. It does not operate in isolation: Trustworthiness is addressed through governance structures, and Value through how analytical work is communicated – both covered in this chapter.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
section: 9.2 The Analytical Lifecycle and the Five Key Areas
text:
9.2 The Analytical Lifecycle and the Five Key Areas
The AQuA Book – the UK Government’s standing guidance on analytical quality assurance – frames analytical quality as a property of the whole cycle, not a single gate at the end. CQC’s QASQA Framework maps its five key areas directly onto this cycle. The alignment is deliberate: the AQuA Book represents the settled cross-government consensus on what analytical quality assurance requires, built from experience across departments with widely differing analytical contexts. Aligning CQC’s framework to it means our practice can be compared against, and if necessary defended against, that common standard; it also means CQC analysts can draw on a growing body of guidance and case studies developed around the same framework.
Problems originate at specific stages: a vague brief cannot be rescued by careful execution; a method chosen without examining its assumptions will produce misleading results regardless of how correctly it is coded; conclusions overstated at delivery cannot be quietly corrected in the technical annex. Each stage sets up the next, and quality built in at each stage costs far less than quality inspected in at the end.
The framework is also a practical template: teams complete it by mapping their existing processes and documentation to each key area, so the assurer has a coherent view of the work without having to trace evidence across unfamiliar materials – increasing transparency and creating an audit trail that supports accountability when findings are challenged.
The five key areas are not a checklist to be ticked once the analysis is done; they are a frame for thinking at each stage of the work. Each area has an exemplifying question and a set of indicators that aid assessment, giving concrete form to what addressing the area requires. All five must be addressed for every piece of quantitative analysis that informs a regulatory decision. Which indicators within each area apply, and how thoroughly they are documented, depends on the risk and complexity of the work – that calibration is the subject of the proportionality section below.







Key area
Exemplifying question




Engagement and Scoping
Has the requirement been properly understood and the analytical approach specified?


Design
Is the analytical design defensible – method suited to both data and decision?


Analysis
Has the work been executed correctly, verified, validated, and documented?


Delivery and Communication
Have methods, results, and limitations been communicated clearly and appropriately to their audience?


Fitness for Purpose
Does the finished analysis deliver what was needed, supported by sound methods and credible evidence?

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
text:
9.3 Roles and Responsibilities
Quality assurance requires people to carry distinct accountabilities across the analytical cycle. The framework assigns four roles, adapted from the AQuA Book, whose responsibilities must be agreed before work begins. These roles are common across analytical quality frameworks; what varies between frameworks is how the specific standards within each role are defined. Understanding the roles before engaging with the five key areas is important, because the indicators and exemplifying questions within each area are framed in terms of what analyst, assurer, and approver must each do at every stage.
The key structural principle is the independence of the assurer from the analyst: these two roles must be held by different people, because the assurer’s protective function exists only where they are genuinely separate from the work they are reviewing.
The commissioner defines the need. They set out the analytical requirements, agree that the proposed approach will meet those requirements before work starts, and accept the analysis and assurance when the work is complete. At CQC, commissioners often act on behalf of end-users – a director commissioning analysis to support inspectors carries responsibility for representing end-user needs in the brief, not just their own. Vague or shifting requirements from commissioners are a leading source of analytical failure; the scoping stage exists precisely to pin these down before they can cascade into the analysis.
The analyst designs and executes the analysis. Design includes the assurance plan: from the outset, the analyst is responsible for thinking through how the work will be verified and validated, not treating QA as something to arrange after the findings are ready. The analyst also self-assures their own work – checking their code, verifying key calculations, assessing whether the results make substantive sense – before the work goes to the assurer. Self-assurance is the first line of quality control, not an optional extra – but it cannot be the only one. The assurer exists precisely because even careful analysts have blind spots, and because independence creates a form of accountability that self-review cannot.
The assurer independently reviews the analyst’s work. Independent means not involved in the analysis: a colleague who sat in on analytical discussions, helped with a code section, or informally reviewed a draft during its development does not occupy the assurer role as the framework defines it. The assurer reviews the analyst’s self-assurance, undertakes further verification and validation as appropriate, reports errors and areas for improvement back to the analyst, and confirms to the approver that the work is sound. This confirmation carries weight only because the assurer is genuinely independent.
The approver provides final accountability. They scrutinise the work of analyst and assurer together and confirm that it has been appropriately assured. This is a substantive review, not a ceremonial one; the approver’s sign-off carries organisational accountability for the quality of the output.
Full independence – where the assurer has had no involvement in the analytical work – is the ideal, and for high-stakes work it must be pursued seriously. In practice, capacity constraints, the niche nature of some analyses, and other external pressures mean complete independence is not always achievable. The response is proportionate: the simpler the work and the lower the stakes, the looser the independence requirement can reasonably be; the more complex and high-stakes, the stricter it must be. What is never appropriate is treating self-assurance alone as sufficient for work where an error could have material regulatory consequences.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
text:
9.4 The Five Key Areas
The sections below take each key area in turn, describing the thinking it demands, the practical work it calls for, and the warning signs that suggest a stage has been skipped or rushed.

Engagement and Scoping
The first key area covers how well the requirements have been understood and the analytical approach specified. Most analytical problems begin here, at the moment of first contact between a commissioner’s need and an analyst’s capabilities, and most serious failures can be traced to this stage being rushed or left implicit.
Good scoping is not just writing down a research question. It means arriving at a shared, precise understanding of what decision the analysis will inform, which providers, populations, or time periods are in scope, which data sources will be used, and what level of QA is appropriate for the risk involved. Assumptions and limitations belong in the scoping conversation, not the conclusion: if a method requires a data quality that may not be achievable, that is not a problem to disclose at delivery – it is a constraint to surface before the analytical plan is agreed. Commissioners who understand the method’s assumptions can make informed decisions about whether to proceed; commissioners who discover limitations at the end cannot.
The scoping stage also establishes the assurance plan itself: who will review, at what points, and with what documentation. QA is not a stage that follows analysis; it is designed in at the start. Commissioners and analysts must agree on what is being delivered before work begins. This is the moment that surfaces misaligned expectations before they propagate into the work.
Red flags: an analytical question too vague to operationalise; no discussion of limitations or key assumptions before work begins; the analyst uncertain about how findings will be used; no agreement on QA level, timeline, or review responsibilities.


Design
The second key area covers how defensible the analytical design is. Design is where method meets question, and the AQuA Book’s standard is that the method must suit both the data and the decision – not be merely available, not be merely familiar, but genuinely appropriate. Every statistical technique makes assumptions about the data to which it is applied: a method that assumes independence when records are clustered by provider, or normality when the distribution is heavily skewed, produces results that might appear technically correct while being substantively misleading.
Surfacing and examining those assumptions is design work, not something to be deferred until after the analysis is run. The same applies to the data quality questions that affect method choice: what missing data exists and why, whether unusual observations are errors or genuine extremes, whether data sources are comparable across the time period in question. These are not details – they are the conditions under which the method’s assumptions are either met or violated, and they must be assessed before the design is finalised.
Reproducibility is also a design decision. If code will not be version-controlled, if analytical decisions will not be logged, if the data extraction will not be documented, it will prove difficult, if at all possible, to independently replicate the analysis or defend it if challenged. High-risk work in particular – where findings may be scrutinised, where methods are novel, or where the same analysis will be run repeatedly – must be designed with an audit trail from the outset, not retrofitted with one when review arrives.






Note🤔 Rethinking: Researcher Degrees of Freedom



Every analysis involves decisions the design does not fully settle: which variables to include, how to handle unusual observations, what transformations to apply, which model to use when more than one is defensible. These researcher degrees of freedom are not a methodological failure – they are an inevitable feature of applied statistical work. The problem is not the choices themselves but their invisibility.
Invisible choices create two problems. Different analysts making different but defensible choices from the same data can reach different conclusions. And invisibility creates conditions for unconscious bias: the natural tendency to find a path that produces an expected, hoped-for, or convenient result is checked only by the requirement to justify decisions in writing as they are made, not after the results are known.
The remedy is documentation: record each choice as you make it, not after the analysis is complete; justify it explicitly; and for choices that materially affect the findings, test sensitivity to alternatives and report the results. For high-stakes work, pre-specifying the analytical approach before examining the outcome data limits the scope for those degrees of freedom to be exercised unconsciously. Peer review of analytical choices – separate from code review – catches justifications that look reasonable in isolation but would not survive fresh scrutiny.


Red flags: method chosen by habit rather than by fit to the question and data; assumptions not examined; no plan for missing data or unusual observations; no design provision for reproducibility.


Analysis
The third key area covers how well the quantitative analysis has been executed and assured. The core discipline is straightforward: implement as designed, verify the calculations, validate that the results make sense, and document any departures from the plan. In practice, verification and validation address different failure modes and both are needed.
Verification asks whether the calculation is correct. It catches technical errors – miscoded variables, copy-and-paste mistakes, software bugs, formula errors. For scripted analysis in R or Python, code review by a colleague who was not involved in writing the code provides a second pair of eyes; automated testing provides systematic checks; replicating key results by an independent route provides the strongest form of assurance. Verification is not optional for high-stakes work even when the analyst is confident: confidence is not evidence of correctness.
Validation asks whether the result makes sense. It catches substantive errors – results that look technically correct but are implausible, that contradict previous findings without explanation, or that fail basic consistency checks. Comparing findings with previous estimates, with results from a simpler approximation, or with qualitative knowledge from the inspection record are all forms of validation. A technically perfect calculation of the wrong quantity passes verification and fails validation; both checks are needed precisely because they guard against different kinds of failure.
Every analysis involves choices that the design does not fully settle: which observations to exclude, how to handle an unexpected pattern of missing data, what to do when an assumption is borderline rather than clearly met. These choices are legitimate, but they are also exposure points – different defensible choices can lead to different conclusions from the same data. The response is not to eliminate judgement but to document it: record what was decided and why, and for choices that materially affect the findings, test sensitivity to alternatives and report the results.
Red flags: no code review or independent calculation check; results not compared with prior estimates or domain expectations; assumptions violated but not addressed; departures from the analytical plan unrecorded.






Note🤔 Rethinking: What the Penny Dash Review Was Really About



The 2024 independent review of CQC’s operational effectiveness raised concerns about how analytical methods were described, how evidence standards were applied across teams, and whether analytical decisions were adequately documented. Each concern maps directly onto a stage of the framework.
Unclear method description is a design and delivery problem: if methods cannot be explained, they probably were not examined carefully enough to be defensible. Inconsistent evidence standards are a scoping and fitness-for-purpose problem: if the standard varies without reason across teams or analyses, the regulatory conclusions built on them are not comparable. Inadequate documentation is an analysis and reproducibility problem: if decisions are not recorded, the analysis cannot be reproduced, reviewed, or improved.
The QASQA Framework addresses all three – not by adding paperwork, but by embedding the questions at each stage of the analytical cycle rather than asking them retrospectively. Document as you go, not as an afterthought: it is faster, more accurate, and far easier to defend.




Delivery and Communication
The fourth key area covers how clearly and appropriately methods, results, and limitations are communicated. Good analysis that is not understood by its audience is not good analysis, and the Code of Practice’s Value pillar treats accessibility as a professional standard, not an aspiration.
The practical challenge in CQC’s regulatory context is that audiences vary – and that variation demands genuine adaptation, not just simpler sentences. Inspectors making rating decisions, Board members assessing organisational risk, and providers receiving public-facing reports all need different registers, different levels of technical detail, and different entry points into the uncertainty. Communicating statistical uncertainty across different levels of statistical literacy is a distinct analytical skill, and one worth developing deliberately. The language of confidence intervals, p-values, and sensitivity analyses makes no contact with a reader who lacks statistical training; badly translated, it actively misleads. An inspector needs to understand what the evidence does and cannot support; a Board member needs a different framing of the same uncertainty; a provider needs plain language they can act on. None of these requires hiding the uncertainty – but all require expressing it in terms the reader can use. Investing in this translation is not a soft skill; it is a condition of the analysis doing its job.
In each case, the obligation is to communicate honestly – which means communicating uncertainty alongside point estimates, limitations alongside conclusions, and what the analysis cannot tell you alongside what it can. Confidence intervals, caveats about missing data, qualifications about causal interpretation – these are not failures to be buried in technical appendices but necessary context for any reader trying to act on findings responsibly.
Equally, uncertainty should not be communicated in a way that implies the analysis is worthless. The task is calibrated honesty: match the language of confidence to the strength of the evidence, neither overstating certainty nor manufacturing doubt. “We observe a consistent pattern across three years of data, though we cannot rule out confounding from changes in recording practice” is more useful than either a bald assertion or a long list of qualifications that leaves the reader with nothing actionable.
Visualisation is part of communication, and the same standards apply. Charts should clarify rather than obscure, should not imply more precision than the data supports, and should be accessible to readers with colour-vision differences. The principles in Data Visualisation develop the detailed guidance for CQC contexts.
Delivery is also the moment at which the original question, agreed at scoping, is answered. If scope drifted during the analysis, or if the question evolved, the gap between what was commissioned and what is being delivered must be acknowledged rather than papered over with an answer to a related but different question.
Red flags: methods described in jargon without explanation appropriate to the audience; uncertainty communicated identically regardless of the audience’s statistical literacy; limitations omitted or buried at the end; findings presented as more certain than the methods justify.


Fitness for Purpose
The final key area steps back to assess whether the analysis does what it set out to do: whether the findings meet the objectives, are supported by the methods and data, are coherent with other available knowledge, and are caveated in proportion to the strength of the evidence. This is not a formality. It is the check that catches accumulated drift – where every individual decision along the way was reasonable but the overall product has moved away from what was needed, or where correct findings are presented with stronger conclusions than the evidence supports.
Credibility here has a specific meaning. Findings should be supported not just by the analysis at hand but by other available evidence, by expert judgement, and by consistency with what is already known. Internal coherence – do the pieces fit together into a consistent picture? – is a minimum. Coherence with other evidence – do related indicators tell the same story? do inspection findings align? – is the stronger test. A result that is internally coherent but contradicts inspection experience without explanation is not yet credible in the regulatory sense, and a regulatory decision built on it is exposed.
Reproducibility has its final check at this stage: is the documentation sufficient for another analyst to replicate the work? Does the audit trail record the decisions taken, including those that did not work out? This is what makes learning from the analysis possible, and what makes a credible defence of it available if the analysis is challenged.
The strength of conclusions must be matched to the strength of evidence. The language of delivery – “this suggests”, “we observe a consistent pattern”, “we cannot rule out” – should reflect what the analysis actually supports, not what stakeholders might prefer to hear.
Red flags: findings have drifted from the original question; results inconsistent with other evidence without explanation; conclusions stronger than the evidence supports; documentation insufficient for independent replication.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
text:
9.5 Proportionality: Matching QA to Risk
Not all analysis needs the same depth of assurance. A one-off descriptive summary prepared for an internal meeting and a risk model used to target enforcement decisions are both quantitative analyses, but they differ enormously in their potential consequences if wrong. The AQuA Book is explicit that the level of assurance and documentation should be proportionate to complexity and risk, and the QASQA framework applies this logic directly.
Risk assessment should be agreed by the analytical team – analyst, assurer, and commissioner – at project outset, and reviewed at key milestones. The factors that bear on it include the business criticality of the decision the analysis informs; whether quantitative analysis is the primary driver of that decision or one input among many; the statistical complexity of the methods; whether the approach is novel at CQC; whether the analysis will be used repeatedly or adapted to new purposes; the precision the decision requires; and whether findings will be public-facing or may be challenged externally.
Three broad risk levels organise these factors for practical purposes. Low risk covers routine descriptive statistics, internal data queries, and exploratory analysis by experienced teams. QA at this level is lightweight – brief analytical plans, verbal feedback, summary notes – though all five questions must still be addressed. Medium risk covers routine but consequential reporting: inspection outcome analysis, cross-functional benchmarking, established survey methods for decision support. Standard documentation is expected, including specification documents, QA logs, and code review records where scripted analysis is involved. High risk covers analysis that informs policy decisions, will be publicly scrutinised, uses advanced or novel methods, involves sensitive data, or sets methodological precedents. Comprehensive documentation is required across all stages, with a full audit trail; State of Care analysis, risk models for inspection targeting, and first uses of novel techniques all sit here.
When there is doubt, err toward the higher category. The cost of additional assurance is time; the cost of insufficient assurance on a high-stakes analysis is a regulatory decision that cannot be defended.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
section: 9.6 A Discipline of Checks Before Analysis Begins
text:
9.6 A Discipline of Checks Before Analysis Begins
Before applying any statistical method, a brief structured review catches problems that propagate expensively if missed. The five checks below run in sequence – they follow the order in which information is gathered and assumptions are built – and together cover the commonest sources of downstream failure.
To make them concrete, consider a running example: you are about to analyse quarterly waiting times for community mental health referrals across providers, with the aim of identifying those where waits have lengthened materially over the past year.
Data provenance comes first, before any other work. Where did the data come from? When was it extracted, from which system, and by whom? Which version is being used, and is access authorised? For the waiting times analysis: confirm the extraction covers the full twelve-month period and that data were pulled from the same system across all quarters. Using data extracted at different points from systems that were updated between extractions will produce apparent trends that are artefacts of the extraction, not of actual waits. This check takes minutes and prevents hours of debugging when results look implausible.
Data structure follows once provenance is confirmed. What does each row represent? What period does the dataset cover? Are duplicates present, and if so, are they legitimate? For the waiting times analysis: confirm that each row is one referral, not one patient – a patient with multiple referrals should appear multiple times. Confusing the unit of observation – analysing at referral level when the question is about provider-level average waits – produces results that are logically wrong in a way that neither verification nor validation reliably catches, because the code runs and the results look plausible to anyone who does not know the subject matter.
Variable definitions follow once structure is understood. What does each column actually measure? What do the codes mean? How were derived variables calculated? For the waiting times analysis: establish exactly how the waiting time is defined – is it calendar days from referral receipt to first appointment, or working days? Is a rejected and re-referred patient’s clock restarted? Misunderstanding a variable’s definition leads to misinterpretation that neither verification nor validation reliably catches: verification confirms the code is correct, validation confirms the result is plausible, but neither checks that the variable was understood correctly in the first place.
Data quality follows once definitions are clear. Are values within expected ranges? How much is missing, and is the missingness patterned in a way that suggests a problem? For the waiting times analysis: check for negative waits, implausibly long waits that likely reflect data entry errors, and for any quarters where a large proportion of referrals have no recorded outcome date. Stop and investigate when you see: values impossible by definition (negative counts, percentages above 100, future dates in historical records); suspicious uniformity (all round numbers, implausibly many zeros, a distribution too neat to be genuine); internal inconsistencies (numerators larger than denominators, fields that should agree but do not); abrupt distributional shifts between periods not explained by a known event; results that perfectly match expectations (genuine data is noisy, and perfect fit is a warning sign, not a reassurance). Each of these is more likely to indicate a data quality problem than a genuine empirical finding. The Handling Missing Data: Foundations chapter provides the conceptual framework for assessing missingness; Unusual Observations covers investigating extreme values.
Analytical appropriateness is the final check, building on all the preceding ones. Is the question defined precisely enough to answer? Does the method suit the data and the question? Are its assumptions understood and assessable from the available data? Is there enough data to support the level of precision the decision requires? For the waiting times analysis: if some providers have fewer than ten referrals per quarter, changes in their average wait will be so imprecise as to be uninformative, and the analysis should plan in advance how to handle them – excluding very small providers, aggregating to half-year periods, or presenting results with explicit precision caveats.
These questions take minutes to ask before analysis begins. Answering them after an analysis is complete, under time pressure, with the mental commitment already invested in the approach, is far more difficult and far less reliable.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
text:
9.7 Multiple Lines of Evidence
A single analysis, however carefully executed, is a single perspective. It can be affected by data quality issues specific to one period or source, by analytical choices, by chance patterns in that particular dataset, or by unmeasured confounding. In a regulatory context, building a case from several independent lines of evidence is what turns a suggestive finding into a defensible conclusion.
In CQC practice, triangulating across independent lines means asking whether a pattern holds up across:

time – does it persist across months, quarters, or years, or is it specific to one period?
data sources – do different indicators or datasets point the same way, or contradict each other?
providers – is it a broad pattern across many providers, or an isolated case?
methods – do different analytical approaches reach similar conclusions, or is the result sensitive to which method is chosen?
qualitative evidence – do inspection findings and stakeholder intelligence align with the quantitative picture?

Returning to the waiting times example: if z-scoring flags a provider as a statistical outlier on wait times, that flag is a prompt for investigation, not a verdict. Check whether the pattern persists over multiple quarters – the Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics chapter provides tools for this – whether related indicators point the same way, whether the inspection record aligns, and whether the pattern could be a data-quality artefact. A flag corroborated across several of these lines is far stronger evidence than any single result.
Replication and consistency across multiple lines of evidence support a conclusion more strongly than statistical significance in a single analysis. Use statistics to guide investigation, not to settle it. Where different lines of evidence genuinely conflict, or you are unsure how to weight them, raise the question with the Guild.

title: 9  Quality Assurance Principles
10  Analytical Tooling at CQC
text:
10.1 The Computational Context
Analytical work unfolds across many media. It happens in your own reasoning, in conversations with colleagues, in organisational processes and review meetings, on paper, in word processors and spreadsheets, and in computational environments where code runs against data. Statistical thinking is not reducible to keyboard and mouse. But the parts of an analysis that have to be reproduced, audited, and defended eventually need to be captured somewhere that supports those properties – and in practice that means a scripted, computational environment. This chapter sets out the tools available to CQC analysts, explains why D&I is moving towards cloud-based, scripted analysis, and describes what you need to get started.
Note that nothing here requires being a software engineer. The aim is the generation of analysis that can be reproduced, reviewed, and defended – three things that matter in a regulatory context where findings inform decisions about providers and need to be able to withstand scrutiny. The shift towards scripted, version-controlled, cloud-based work is the shift that makes those properties achievable at scale. The tools described below make it practical; the culture described at the end of this chapter is what actually gets you there.
Two boundaries are worth naming early. Data engineers in Analytical Content handle data ingestion, schema design, and pipeline infrastructure in CQC’s Enterprise Data Platform (EDP) – that is not the work this chapter describes. Data scientists work primarily in Azure Machine Learning and Databricks for machine learning and AI work at scale. This chapter is for quantitative aand mixed-methods analysts working on the analyses this book covers.

title: 10  Analytical Tooling at CQC
text:
10.2 The Tool Landscape
Analysts across Data & Insight (D&I) work with several tools, each suited to different tasks. Two of them – R and Python – are the languages this book adopts, and the ones you will spend most time in for the analyses it covers. The others you will meet around them: a query language for getting at data, and a couple of tools for presenting or exploring it that the book does not use but you should know exist. All of them run in CQC’s cloud environment, Microsoft Fabric, introduced in the next section; both R and Python come ready to use there, with nothing to install.
R is a statistical computing environment with a well-established role in CQC quantitative work. It was built for statistics first, and that shows: its defaults, its data structures, and the ecosystem around it are all oriented towards analytical work, from data preparation and descriptive statistics through modelling and significance testing to visualisation and reporting. For most statistical tasks in this book, R does what you need without much ceremony.
Python is a general-purpose language with an analytical ecosystem just as capable. It covers the same full range of quantitative work in D&I – data wrangling, descriptive statistics, significance testing, statistical modelling, process control, visualisation, and reporting – and reaches comfortably beyond statistics into automation, data engineering, and machine learning. Where R is a specialist that happens to be excellent at analysis, Python is a generalist that analysis is one part of. For the work in this book the two are interchangeable; which you reach for is mostly a matter of what you and your team already know.
Both R and Python extend their built-in capabilities through packages (also called libraries): bundles of additional, ready-made functions that someone has written, tested, and published for others to reuse. Coding proficiency in both languages is among the skills the D&I Analytical Competency Framework describes, at a range of levels. The code examples throughout the book are written in both languages, so you can follow along in whichever you are developing; they lean on base language – the functions built into R and Python themselves – wherever it achieves the same result as a package, more on this below.
SQL is the query language for structured data, including CQC’s data estate in the EDP. It is not a substitute for R or Python – it retrieves and filters data rather than analysing it – but some SQL is a useful skill when interrogating data held on the EDP, and knowing enough to get at and filter what you need helps on most projects that draw on EDP sources. Work is underway to provide connections and integrations that will make hand-written SQL less necessary for routine analytical work over time.
Power BI is Microsoft’s business-intelligence and dashboarding tool, and there is a substantial body of Power BI work at CQC – interactive dashboards and reporting in particular. It is worth knowing it exists and is widely used, but it sits outside the analytical work this book covers, and the book does not touch on it.
Excel has genuine uses: building simple outputs, quick one-off look-ups, and interim calculations that do not constitute final findings. What it is not suited to is analysis that will be repeated, shared, or reviewed. Formula chains across worksheets are difficult to audit; manual steps cannot be verified without repeating them; the file format records no version history. For any analysis that contributes to a regulatory decision, the expectation is scripted code in R or Python.






Note🤔 Rethinking: Why Scripted Code Rather Than a Spreadsheet?



The case for scripted analysis is not about complexity or prestige, and it does not need a bespoke argument: government already sets one out. The Quality Assurance of Code for Analysis and Research guidance – the “Duck Book”, the companion to the Aqua Book on quality analysis for government – frames it as three properties that stack on one another.
Reproducible comes first: someone else, starting from the same data, can produce the same output by running your documented code. The Duck Book calls this the one thing you can guarantee about an analysis. In a spreadsheet, the “steps” are cell edits made by a person in an order no one recorded; a formula can be correct, but if a row was filtered before it ran and there is no record of that filter, the result cannot be reproduced.
Auditable builds on it: there is a trail of who made which analytical choice, when, and on what evidence. A version-controlled script carries that trail; a spreadsheet named “Final FINAL v3” does not. The Duck Book’s underlying point is that manual and semi-manual processes are slow and error-prone precisely because the inputs and the outputs are not connected directly – code with version control connects them and leaves an audit trail automatically.
Assured sits on top: a second person can read the logic and check it, and the depth of that checking is matched to the risk of the work – the Aqua Book’s proportionality principle. Reviewing interconnected formulas across worksheets is far harder than reviewing a script.
None of this means Excel is useless, however it falls short when it comes to reproducibility, auditability, and assurance, as well as when advanced analytics are concerned.
