If you are unsure whether a task calls for code, a simple rule of thumb is: if it will be repeated, reviewed, or defended, it calls for code.

title: 10  Analytical Tooling at CQC
text:
10.3 Working in Microsoft Fabric
Microsoft Fabric is CQC’s cloud environment for analytical computing. It provides R and Python notebooks that run in your browser, storage connected to CQC’s data estate, and built-in integration with Azure DevOps for version control. There is no local software installation required.
Analysts use Fabric in two ways: sandbox workspaces for learning and experimentation, and project workspaces for real analytical work.

Sandbox workspaces
Every D&I function has a dedicated sandbox workspace on Fabric. These are available to all analysts in a function as soon as permissions are in place and staff records are current with the function lead. Sandboxes are the right place to learn Fabric, experiment with code, work through examples in this book, and build skills before applying them on real projects.
Sandboxes hold no EDP data by default, but you can load your own data. They are isolated learning environments, and they are the right starting point for anyone new to Fabric or to scripted analysis.


Project workspaces
When you have a specific analytical use case – work that needs EDP data, will be collaborated on by a team, or will produce a regulatory output – you can request a project workspace. The intention is that project workspaces will increasingly support not just notebook-based analysis but automated analytical pipelines, moving the function towards the reproducibility standards the AQuA Book recommends.
Project workspaces can also be used for work that is better kept compartmentalised – work not accessible to the whole function. The workspace configuration, including data access and permission groups, is agreed before the work starts. Discuss requirements with the Practice Lead before raising a request; the Platform and Solutions team will review the specification and advise on the appropriate configuration.
Data access in project workspaces is set up through shortcuts to relevant EDP sources. Shortcuts make data available without creating copies, which keeps data governance clean and avoids version drift between the source and your analysis.


Getting access
Fabric access requires two steps: a permissions set (raise an IT ticket for “Amend existing Power BI group” – the Power BI label is legacy and grants Fabric access) and, for project workspaces, a separate ticket for “New Workspace Request”. For the sandbox, no workspace ticket is needed: function workspaces already exist and are accessible once permissions are in place.
Discuss access with the Practice Lead before raising either ticket, so the right configuration is agreed upfront.

title: 10  Analytical Tooling at CQC
text:
10.4 Version Control and Code Review
Version control is the practice of tracking every change made to a piece of code, with a record of who made the change, when, and what the intent was. Azure Repos – the repository service within Azure DevOps – provides this at CQC, and can be linked directly to a Fabric workspace so notebooks and scripts are version-controlled without leaving the Fabric environment.
Adopting version control is not optional for serious analytical work. It is what makes the following possible:

an assurer can review the code and see what changed between the version they reviewed and the version that produced the final output;
a future analyst can understand why a choice was made, not only what was chosen;
you can recover a previous version of the analysis if a change turns out to be wrong;
the history of an analysis is available as evidence if findings are challenged.

Connecting a repository to a Fabric workspace requires a brief setup – contact the Platform and Solutions team, who will configure the link. Guidance materials are available via the Guild channels. Once connected, commits, branches, and pull requests are managed from within Fabric.
Code review – where someone not involved in writing the analysis reads the code to check for errors, ambiguity, and departures from documented decisions – is part of the assurance process described in the Quality Assurance Principles chapter. For code review to be practical, the code must be in a repository the reviewer can access. Without version control, code review is not systematically possible.
D&I does not yet have a formal code review process. Developing that is part of building the analytical infrastructure that makes work fully auditable. In the meantime, the expectation is that scripted analysis is in a repository and that a second pair of eyes on the code is sought before high-stakes outputs are signed off. The Practice Lead can help identify reviewers and facilitate peer review.






Note🤔 Rethinking: What “Base Code” Means in Practice



The code examples in this book use base R and base Python wherever they achieve the same result as a package. This is not minimalism for its own sake – it is about four practical considerations.
First, base language is readable without knowing a package’s conventions. An analyst unfamiliar with a specific package can follow logic written in base code; they may not be able to follow the same logic written in a package’s domain-specific syntax, which creates a barrier to code review.
Second, Fabric’s pre-installed environment already covers the full analytical range of D&I work without additional packages. Where a package genuinely adds clarity – ggplot2 in R, pandas or matplotlib in Python – the examples use it and say so. Where base language achieves the same result clearly, that is what the examples show.
Third, reducing dependency keeps analyses portable. An analysis that imports ten packages may not run in a future environment where the same versions are not present; base language has no such fragility.
Fourth, every package is third-party code running inside CQC’s environment, and each one is a dependency you are trusting and a part of the analysis’s attack surface. Fewer dependencies means less exposure to supply-chain risk and less to vet. Packages should come from the approved, trusted sources available in Fabric rather than being pulled in ad hoc; when a regulator’s analysis rests on a library, where that library came from is a fair question.
When this guidance uses a package, it will always name it and explain why.

title: 10  Analytical Tooling at CQC
text:
10.5 Towards Reproducible Analytical Pipelines
Reproducible Analytical Pipelines (RAP) is the standard recommended by the AQuA Book and the Government Analytical Function for statistical outputs in the public sector. The core requirement is straightforward: someone else, starting from the same data, can produce the same output by running your documented code. No manual steps, no copy-and-paste between applications, no “you had to be there.”
Building a complete RAP pipeline for every analysis is not the expectation today. CQC is building towards it, and there are a lot of moving parts: data infrastructure, version control adoption, shared coding environments, capability development across the function. That work takes time.
The expectation, from the first day of every project, is a culture of reproducibility:

Write code rather than manual steps, whenever possible.
Record every analytical decision as you make it – in comments in the code, or in an accompanying log – not after the analysis is complete.
Use version control so the history of changes is never lost.
Write code that can be run again from scratch, not code that produces a result only when started from a particular saved state.
When a step is automated, document the automation.

These habits cost almost nothing when built in from the start and a great deal when retrofitted. Every analysis set up this way is a building block; the function’s analytical infrastructure grows precisely because individual projects are structured correctly from the outset.
The Government Analytical Function’s RAP strategy describes a progression: Minimum RAP (open-source tooling, version control, peer review, no copy-and-paste between tools) leads to Silver RAP (functions, unit tests, documentation) and eventually Gold RAP (automated deployment and testing). Most work in D&I will be working towards Minimum RAP. The tools described in this chapter – Fabric notebooks, Azure Repos, the scripted examples throughout this book – make Minimum RAP reachable from a first project.

title: 10  Analytical Tooling at CQC
11  Generative AI in Analytical Work
text:
11.1 A Genuinely Useful Tool, and the Catch
Generative AI – the family of tools that produce text, code, and images on demand, and that most people first met through chatbots – has arrived in analytical work, and it is genuinely useful. What sets these tools apart from anything analysts have had before is how you work with them: you instruct them in ordinary language, and they take in unstructured material – notes, documents, a half-formed question – rather than only neatly arranged data. That is the real change. CQC analysts have access to an enterprise version of Microsoft Copilot, and Microsoft Fabric offers AI features alongside the notebooks described in the chapter on analytical tooling. These tools can draft code in seconds, explain an unfamiliar function, suggest an approach you had not considered, and talk through a problem at any hour. Many analysts will be tempted to lean on them, and for good reason: used well, they remove friction from the parts of the work that are mechanical rather than thoughtful.
This chapter is about using them well, because the same fluency that makes them useful makes them dangerous. CQC does not yet have an official internal policy on generative AI in analytical work; what follows is professional guidance grounded in the national picture, not an internal rulebook. The catch is simple to state and easy to forget: these tools are confident whether or not they are correct, and confidence is a signal we tend to read as competence. An analysis that runs without errors and sounds right can still be wrong, and a tool that helps you produce one quickly has not helped you at all.
The rest of this chapter rests on a single idea, so it is worth stating plainly before going further: a generative AI tool is itself a statistical model, and likely the most persuasive one you will ever work with. Everything this guidance asks you to do with a statistical method – understand its assumptions, check that they hold, and never trust an output you cannot defend – applies here, and applies more sharply, because this model is extremely complex under the bonnet, and built to sound very, very convincing.
crumbs: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work

title: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work
text:
11.2 A Model That Sounds Right
It helps to be precise about what these tools are. A large language model (LLM) is the engine inside most AI-powered applications, think for example of an AI chatbot like Copilot, or Claude. It is built by training a statistical model on an enormous quantity of data until it becomes very good at one narrow task: given a stretch of text, predict the next word in the sequence. A response is produced by doing this over and over – predict a word, add it, predict the next – which is why the output reads as fluent prose. The UK government’s own AI Playbook puts it without euphemism: these systems “generate content by returning the most likely output for a given input based on patterns learnt from training data, without actual understanding.”
Two consequences follow, and both matter for analytical work. First, the model is optimising for a plausible-sounding answer, not a true one – and most of the time, on common questions, plausible and true coincide, which is precisely what lulls you. Second, when they do not coincide, the model has no way to tell you. It cannot signal that it is guessing, because from the inside every answer is the same kind of object: a likely continuation. This is why a chatbot will, with the same even confidence, give you a correct formula and then invent a citation to support it. The fabrication – often called a hallucination – is not a malfunction. It is the machinery working exactly as designed, on a question where the most likely-sounding continuation happens not to be true.
The AI Task Force of the Royal Statistical Society makes this the centre of its argument in AI is Statistics: these tools are “fluent, confident and persuasive – even when they are completely wrong,” and they have “no natural way to express uncertainty or to signal that they are operating outside their zone of competence.” The remedy proposed is the habit this whole book is trying to build – a statistical mindset that asks, of any result: what is this based on? how reliable is it? does it square with what we already know? how much would it change if the inputs changed slightly? what assumptions sit underneath it? You would ask these questions of an analytical output. The argument of this chapter is that you must ask them of a chatbot too.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: What an LLM Is, Statistically



It is worth seeing why “predicts the next word” produces something that feels like thought. During training, the model is shown vast amounts of text with words hidden, and its internal settings are adjusted, billions of times, until its predictions of the hidden words match the real ones as closely as possible. What it learns in the process is a fantastically detailed map of which words tend to follow which – a map detailed enough to encode grammar, facts, argument structure, and the cadence of human writing, all as statistical regularities.
When you send a prompt - a set of instructions, typically expressed in natural language - the model does not look anything up and does not reason in the way a person does. It computes, for its entire vocabulary, how likely each word is to come next given everything so far, and samples from that distribution – then repeats. There is no separate store of “things that are true” being consulted (unless a bespoke one is built for this explicit purpose); truth and falsehood are both just patterns in the text it was trained on. A further wrinkle: these models are calibrated using human feedback, and people tend to rate agreeable, confident-sounding answers more highly. The result is a measurable tendency, documented in Anthropic’s research on the topic, towards sycophancy – telling you what you seem to want to hear. A tool that is both fluent and eager to agree is a poor sceptic, which matters the moment you try to use it as one.
crumbs: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work

title: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work
text:
11.3 What It Is Good For
None of this means the tools are off-limits. It means their useful range is the work where a wrong answer is cheap and easy to catch, and their dangerous range is the work where a wrong answer is expensive and hard to spot. Within the safe range, the gains are real. Brainstorming is a natural fit: ask for five ways to approach a problem and you get a quick map of the territory, from which you choose, for reasons you can state. Suggesting methods is similar – a starting point for your own reading, not a verdict. Explaining unfamiliar code or an error message can turn an hour of searching into a minute. Drafting boilerplate code – the repetitive scaffolding around the real analysis – is often faster to check than to write.
Notice what these uses share: in every one, the analyst remains the person who decides, and the AI’s output is an input to your judgement, not a substitute for it. The verification is built in because you treat the answer as a suggestion to be weighed, not an instruction to be followed. The danger arrives when that relationship quietly inverts – when the suggestion becomes the decision because checking it felt like unnecessary friction.
Red-teaming – asking the tool to find the flaws in your reasoning – deserves a particular mention. It sounds like the perfect safeguard, and it can surface things you missed or steer you to consider alternatives that you might have overlooked. But a tool that tends towards agreement is a weak adversary: it may reassure you precisely where you most need challenge. Use it to generate candidate objections, then test them yourself; do not mistake “the AI couldn’t find a problem” for “there is no problem.” Agreement from a model built to be agreeable is not evidence.

The Errors That Don’t Announce Themselves
There are two ways the help a tool gives you can be wrong, and they are not equally dangerous. One is loud: say you ask for some code, and what comes back simply does not work – it breaks, or refuses to run, and you know at once that something is off. That kind of error is almost harmless, precisely because it announces itself. The dangerous kind is silent: the work runs through smoothly, produces a clean and convincing result, and gives no hint that it has answered a different question from the one you asked.
Picture asking a tool for the overall performance score across a set of services of very different sizes – a few large, many small. Back comes a tidy figure, sensibly rounded, stated with quiet confidence. Nothing about it looks wrong: it is an entirely ordinary-looking number, sitting in the range you expected. But suppose it has taken a plain average of each service’s percentage, giving a service with a handful of responses the same weight as one with thousands, when the question called for an average weighted by how many people each score was based on. The two answers can differ enough to matter, and the wrong one carries no warning label. Only someone who understands what the average is meant to represent will notice that a perfectly clean result has answered the wrong question.
The lesson is not “do not use these tools.” It is that accepting an output means owning it: understanding what it actually did, and confirming it did what the analysis needed – the verification and validation the quality assurance principles chapter sets out, applied with extra care precisely because the result arrived looking finished. The answer that breaks is the one you can trust to tell you something is wrong; the answer that runs is the one to check.


When It Invents the Method
The same confidence extends to methods and to the literature. Ask a chatbot how to compare satisfaction scores across three providers and it may recommend a one-way analysis of variance, report how to read the result, and cite a methods paper that looks authoritative – author, journal, year, all present. Two things can be wrong at once. If the satisfaction scores are an ordinal scale rather than genuine quantities, analysis of variance is not the appropriate tool, and the chatbot has not paused to ask which kind of data you have, because it cannot. And the citation may simply not exist: a plausible-sounding combination of a real-looking author and a real-looking title that was never written. An analyst who knows the levels-of-measurement ladder catches the first problem; an analyst who checks the reference catches the second. An analyst who trusts the fluent answer inherits both.
crumbs: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work

title: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work
section: 11.4 The Line You Do Not Cross
text:
11.4 The Line You Do Not Cross
There is one boundary that does not move with the stakes or the verification: never let a generative AI tool run the analysis for you. It can help you think, draft, and check; it must not be the thing that produces the finding. If you take code from it, take the code – then verify every line and run it yourself, on your data, in your environment, until you understand and trust each step. The output that goes into a piece of CQC analysis must be one you can explain and defend as though you had written it from scratch.
This is the principle the AI Playbook calls “meaningful human control at the right stages,” and it is the same principle behind the assurance roles in the quality assurance principles chapter: a human being is accountable for the analysis, and accountability cannot be delegated to a tool that does not understand what it produced. The temptation runs the other way – the more capable the tool feels, the more it invites you to hand over not just the typing but the thinking. Handing over the typing is efficiency. Handing over the thinking is the failure this chapter exists to warn against, and it compounds: an unexamined output becomes an input to the next step, and a small error you never understood propagates into a result you cannot account for.
crumbs: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work

title: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work
text:
11.5 Keeping Data Safe
In this context, it is tempting to picture data-governance considerations as simply being about feeding datasets into a tool. In practice you will rarely do that – partly because you should not be letting the tool run the analysis, so it has no need of your data, and partly because these tools have practical limits on how much they can take in at once. The more realistic, and more useful, case is sharing context to guide the work: an analysis plan, a data dictionary or code book, a description of what the variables mean, a draft write-up you want a second read of. That is where these tools genuinely help, and it is subject to exactly the same rules.
Those rules are simple to state. CQC’s enterprise Microsoft Copilot and the AI features within Fabric are configured to keep information inside CQC’s environment; public, consumer chatbots are not, and anything typed into them may leave CQC’s control entirely. So CQC information – provider data, unpublished findings, internal plans and codebooks, anything not already public – goes only into the approved, enterprise tools, and never into an external AI application. Some enterprise features may depend on specific licences; whether a particular tool is cleared to hold a particular kind of information is exactly the question to settle before you use it, with the Practice Lead and the Platform and Solutions team, not after. Pasting sensitive material into a chat box may feel weightless, but it is not.






Note🤔 Rethinking: Whose Judgement Was It?



If a tool shaped a piece of analysis, that should be visible in the record – and the part that needs particular care is not what was decided but why. An audit trail that shows a particular method was chosen is defensible when the reason is “the analyst chose it because it fits the data and the question in this and this other ways”, and not when the honest reason is “the chatbot suggested it and we went with it.” The two can potentially produce the identical result, yet one is professional judgement and the other is its absence.
The distinction is not that AI may not be consulted – it may. It is that a suggestion becomes a sound analytical decision only once an analyst has understood it, tested it against what they know, and made it their own. At that point the choice is genuinely theirs, and the record should say so: a sentence noting where and what a tool was used for – “an initial method shortlist came from a chatbot and was checked against this guidance”; “Copilot drafted code that was then reviewed and tested” – is part of being transparent about how the work was done, which is one of the standards under the Code of Practice for Statistics’ Trustworthiness pillar. Owning your choices and recording them honestly is what lets you use these tools without quietly outsourcing the thing you are accountable for.
crumbs: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work

title: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work
text:
11.6 Where This Leads
The chapters that follow are where these tools will tempt you most, because each method involves choices an AI will happily make on your behalf. The argument of this chapter is worth carrying forward intact: treat a generative AI tool as you would any statistical method in this guidance – something to understand before you rely on it, and whose output you check rather than trust on sight. It is a tool to think with, never one to decide for you or to do the work for you.
What the tool cannot supply is the thing this guidance advocates as irrenunciable: the judgement to know what a result means, whether it can be believed, and how to defend it. That judgement is what makes someone an analyst rather than a conduit for a machine’s most likely answer. Generative AI can make a good analyst faster. It cannot make anyone an analyst, and it can make a careless one quietly, fluently wrong.
crumbs: 11  Generative AI in Analytical Work

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.1 What a Z-score Measures
A z-score answers one question: how far does this provider’s value sit from what you would expect, relative to the spread of values around that expectation? The general formula, in the notation of CQC’s own z-scoring guidance, is
\[z = \frac{y - t}{s}\]
where \(y\) is the provider’s observed value, \(t\) is the expected value of the indicator – usually the national mean across all providers, occasionally an externally derived target – and \(s\) is the standard deviation of the values around that expectation. Subtract the expected value from the provider’s value, then divide by the standard deviation: the result is the provider’s distance from expectation, expressed in standard deviations rather than in the indicator’s original units.
That change of units is the whole point, and it buys you two things. First, a common scale: waiting times in hours, incident rates per thousand bed-days, and screening percentages all become directly comparable once each is expressed as standardised distance, which is why one set of bands can serve hundreds of indicators. Second, a sense of how unusual a value is: z-scores have mean 0 and standard deviation 1 by construction, and when the underlying data are roughly normal, the familiar landmarks of the normal distribution apply – about 68 per cent of providers within ±1, about 95 per cent within ±2, almost all within ±3. A provider at \(z = +2.5\) is not just “above average”; if nothing truly distinguished the providers, fewer than 1 in 100 would sit this far above the mean by chance.
It is worth being precise about what kind of statement that is. At its heart a z-score is descriptive: it locates a provider within the spread of all providers, nothing more. Whether the distance is statistically significant, and whether it matters in practice, are two further questions the z-score does not answer on its own – the section on distance, significance, and importance keeps the three apart. CQC has used z-scoring for provider comparison since the early 2010s, and it has endured for practical rather than aesthetic reasons: it is simple, explainable (“three standard deviations from the mean” lands with stakeholders in a way model output does not), it scales to thousands of providers, and a good-enough method consistently applied and widely understood usually beats a perfect method inconsistently used. CQC has also learned where it falls short – hence the minimum requirements and Working z-scores covered below.
Figure 12.1: Most providers land near the middle by chance alone. On a normal distribution, 68.2% of z-scores fall within ±1, another 27.2% between 1 and 2 standard deviations out, 4.2% between 2 and 3, and only 0.3% beyond ±3. The bands describe standardised distance from the expected value: beyond ±2 is well into the tail, beyond ±3 markedly so.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
The bands CQC uses
CQC reads z-scores through bands. The labels describe how far a provider sits from the expected value – they are a descriptive scale of distance, not a graduated scale of statistical confidence:

CQC’s z-score bands and the evidential weight each carries







Z-score range
Band label
What the evidence supports




z ≤ −3
Markedly below average
Strong evidence of a real difference; a value this far out would very rarely arise by chance if the provider were truly at the expected value


−3 < z ≤ −2
Below average
Evidence of a difference, though denominator size and overdispersion should also be weighed


−2 < z ≤ −1
Somewhat below average
Weak evidence – a difference this size could plausibly arise from random variation


−1 < z < +1
In line with average
Any difference from the expected value could plausibly be random variation


+1 ≤ z < +2
Somewhat above average
Weak evidence – a difference this size could plausibly arise from random variation


+2 ≤ z < +3
Above average
Evidence of a difference, though denominator size and overdispersion should also be weighed


z ≥ +3
Markedly above average
Strong evidence of a real difference; a value this far out would very rarely arise by chance if the provider were truly at the expected value



Why “Markedly”, not “Significantly”? Because “significantly” is too easily read as “statistically significant”, and the outer bands describe a large standardised distance, which is not the same claim. The wording keeps the descriptive and inferential meanings apart.
A note of caution on the middle bands. If every provider were performing identically, the normal distribution still predicts roughly 32 per cent landing outside ±1, about 4.6 per cent outside ±2, and 0.3 per cent outside ±3 – purely by chance. With 150 providers you would expect around 48 in the “somewhat” bands even if nothing distinguished them, so landing there is weak evidence of anything. This is why the bands carry evidential weight, not confidence percentages.
Direction also matters. For some indicators high values are concerning (incident rates, mortality); for others low values are (screening uptake, percentage seen within target). Decide which direction signals risk before you read any bands.






Note🤔 Rethinking: what a z-score does not mean



A natural way to describe a flagged provider is: “We can be 95% confident this provider is genuinely different from the average.” It is the most common thing people say about z-scores, and it is wrong.
The probability behind a z-score answers “how likely is data this extreme, if the provider were truly at the expected value?” The tempting interpretation reverses the conditional and answers a different question – “how likely is the provider truly different, given the data?” These are not the same quantity, and converting one into the other requires information that z-scoring never supplies. Treating “z beyond ±2” as “95% sure there is a real difference” is the inverse probability fallacy, the same misreading of p-values that the chapter on statistical inference unpacks – and it is exactly the misreading CQC’s own banding review set out to correct.
What you can say is conditional and evidential: “If this provider were truly performing at the expected value, a result this extreme would be very unlikely.” That is a statement about the data under an assumption – not a confidence level attached to the provider.
Two further traps follow from the same root. Significance is dichotomous, not graduated: a result is beyond your chosen threshold or it is not, and a banding scheme can describe distance in graduated steps but must not attach graduated confidence to them. And significance is not importance: a z of 2.5 can be statistically clear yet operationally trivial if the absolute difference is tiny, while a z of 1.5 at a small provider can hide a difference that matters. Always read the absolute numbers alongside the z-score.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.2 Before You Start
Data requirements. You need provider-level data with, depending on the indicator: a numerator and denominator (proportions, ratios), observed and expected counts (standardised ratios), or observed values directly (continuous measures, percentages without underlying counts). What type of data you hold determines which version of the method applies, so settle that first. Complete your data-quality checks before scoring anything – the chapter on quality assurance sets out the pre-analysis questions – and remove missing or invalid values rather than scoring them.
Comparability. Providers in one scoring run must be reasonably comparable: the same indicator definition, and a population similar enough that one expected value makes sense for all of them. Where the sector splits into genuinely different groups – nursing versus residential care homes, say – score each benchmark group separately rather than forcing one comparison.
How many providers? There is no fixed minimum number of providers for z-scoring, but with few providers and an expected value computed from those same few, the method will be conservative in flagging anything. What does have firm minimums is the size of each provider’s own numbers – the minimum requirements described in the step-by-step guide below.
When z-scoring is not the right tool. Categorical outcomes need different methods entirely. Indicators tracked over time for a single provider belong with statistical process control. And where providers serve fundamentally different populations, adjust for case-mix first or the z-scores will measure the case-mix, not the quality.
QA framework alignment. Z-scoring most directly supports QA question 3 (Analysis – is the method appropriate and correctly applied?) and question 5 (Fitness for Purpose – does the indicator measure what the regulatory question requires?). Document your choice of standard versus Working z-scores, banding thresholds, and exclusion criteria as part of the question 3 record.
Worked examples for this chapter. Example 1: GP Access Analysis applies z-scoring to survey data with missing values and small denominators; Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times deals with heavily skewed data, transformation, and Working z-scores.
The code below uses pseudo-randomly generated data. The Python tabs read the R-generated dataset via r.gp_data so both languages show identical numbers; commented-out code shows how to generate the data in Python independently.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.3 Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Pin down the data type
Identify whether your indicator is a raw count, a continuous measure, a percentage (no underlying numerator/denominator), a proportion \(r/n\), a standardised ratio \(O/E\), or a ratio of counts \(O_1/O_2\). Everything downstream – the expected value, the transformation, the minimum requirements – follows from this classification. If you are unsure, ask the Guild before proceeding.


Step 2: Clean the data
Remove missing and invalid values. Do not z-score a blank, and do not silently zero-fill one – the chapter on missing data foundations explains why the difference between “zero” and “missing” matters.


Step 3: Calculate the expected value from all providers
Compute the expected value \(t\) – usually the national mean – using all available data, even from providers whose own numbers are too small to score reliably. Small providers individually contribute little, but excluding them shifts the benchmark; the expected value should reflect the whole national picture. (Providers that fail the minimum requirements are excluded later, from the scoring – not from this calculation.) For proportions the expected value is the sum of numerators over the sum of denominators, not the mean of the individual proportions; for continuous data and percentages it is the plain mean of the values.
Occasionally an externally derived target replaces the national mean – a NICE-recommended level, for instance. The justification needs to be strong, and the target changes some later calculations; the Overthinking box on the method’s machinery says where.


Step 4: Check the minimum requirements
Standard z-scoring leans on the normal distribution, and small numbers break that approximation: a care home with 15 beds can only produce percentages in jumps of 6.7 points, and no bell curve describes that. CQC’s guidance therefore sets minimum requirements per data type before the standard method applies:

CQC minimum requirements for the standard z-scoring method






Data type
Minimum before standard z-scoring




Raw counts
every count ≥ 50


Proportions, expected value between 0.10 and 0.90
each denominator ≥ 30


Proportions, expected value 0.05–0.10 or 0.90–0.95
each denominator ≥ 100


Proportions, expected value below 0.05 or above 0.95
each denominator ≥ 500


Ratios of counts
numerator and denominator both ≥ 10


Standardised ratios
expected count E ≥ 5


Percentages and continuous data
no count minimums – but the data (or a transformation of it) must be approximately normal



For proportions, percentages and ratios, more than half the numerator values must also be greater than zero. If a small minority of providers fail the requirements (fewer than about 10 per cent), you may exclude them and apply the standard method to the rest; if more fail, switch to Working z-scores for the whole indicator. And do not be tempted to rescale the units – multiplying days by 24 to get hours does not make discrete data any less discrete; the gaps just move.


Step 5: Calculate the z-scores
For continuous data and percentages that pass a normality check, the calculation is the plain formula: subtract the expected value from each provider’s value and divide by the standard deviation of the values around it. For proportions, ratios of counts, and standardised ratios, the standard method first transforms the data towards normality and then uses a provider-specific standard deviation that reflects each provider’s own denominator – the technical box below sets out the exact recipes. Either way the output reads the same: distance from expectation in standardised units.


Step 6: Assign bands and sanity-check the distribution
Cut the z-scores at ±1, ±2, ±3 into the bands above, then check the result looks plausible: roughly the expected share in each band, no impossible values, no obvious skew. If far more providers land outside ±2 than the ~5 per cent chance predicts, suspect overdispersion – more provider-to-provider variation than the model expects – and read the Overthinking box before flagging half the sector.


Step 7: Visualise
Plot the distribution of z-scores, and present provider-level results as a funnel plot – covered in its own section below, because it answers a question the bands cannot.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: the standard method’s machinery



The plain formula hides three pieces of machinery that CQC’s implementation (and the iHub) applies for you. Knowing they exist saves you from “correcting” results that are already correct.
Transformations towards normality. For percentages and continuous data that fail a normality check (the guidance uses a Kolmogorov–Smirnov test, accepting p ≥ 0.01), the method searches a cascade of transformations – cube, square, inverse, log, square root, cube root, the same for 100 − Y, arcsine for percentages, then Box–Cox – and standard-scores the best-fitting transformed values. Some transformations reverse the relationship between raw values and z-scores, in which case the signs are flipped back. A percentage that resists all transformation can still be scored untransformed if its mean sits at least 3 standard deviations clear of the 0 and 100 boundaries.
Provider-specific standard deviations. For proportions, ratios of counts, and standardised ratios, each provider’s z-score uses its own standard deviation, driven by its own denominator:

proportions: \(Y_i = \arcsin\!\left(\sqrt{r_i/n_i}\right)\), with \(s_i = 1/(2\sqrt{n_i})\);
ratios of counts: \(Y_i = \log_e\!\left(\frac{O_{i,1}+0.5}{O_{i,2}+0.5}\right)\) (the 0.5 handles zero counts), with \(s_i = \sqrt{\frac{O_{i,1}}{(O_{i,1}+0.5)^2} + \frac{O_{i,2}}{(O_{i,2}+0.5)^2}}\);
standardised ratios: \(Y_i = \sqrt{O_i/E_i}\), with \(s_i = 1/(2\sqrt{E_i})\).

In each case \(z_i = (Y_i - T)/s_i\), where \(T\) is the transformed expected value. The transformation stabilises the distribution; the provider-specific \(s_i\) makes a given distance harder to reach for small providers, which is exactly as it should be – their estimates are noisier.
Winsorisation and overdispersion. Provider-specific standard deviations capture within-provider sampling noise, but providers also genuinely differ from one another, so the resulting z-scores often spread wider than the standard normal predicts – overdispersion. Left unadjusted, it flags providers for being part of ordinary between-provider variation. The standard method therefore estimates the overdispersion and, where present, widens the effective standard deviation: first winsorise the unadjusted z-scores (shrink the most extreme 10 per cent at each end to the 10th and 90th percentile values, so genuine outliers do not inflate the estimate), then estimate the overdispersion factor as the mean of the squared winsorised z-scores, test it, and if it is real, recompute each z-score with the between-provider variance added to its denominator: \(z_i^* = (Y_i - t)/\sqrt{s_i^2 + \hat{\tau}^2}\). This follows Spiegelhalter et al. (2012), and it applies only to proportions, standardised ratios, and ratios of counts. If you are using an externally derived target, the overdispersion is estimated against the data’s own mean first, and the target enters only at this final step.
Validated implementations of all of this exist. Do not re-implement it from scratch – use the established scripts, and raise anything unclear with the Guild.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
section: 12.4 Worked Example: GP Cancer Referral Rates
text:
12.4 Worked Example: GP Cancer Referral Rates
Scenario: you are analysing two-week-wait cancer referral rates across 150 GP practices and want to identify practices whose referral behaviour sits unusually far from the national picture – in either direction, since both under- and over-referral matter.
The referral rate per 1,000 patients is a ratio, but with list sizes in the thousands its denominator is large enough that treating the rate as a continuous measure is defensible – which keeps this example on the plain formula. (With small denominators you would be in minimum-requirements territory and the calculation would change; see the step-by-step guide.)
Because the data are simulated, we know the right answer in advance – a luxury real analysis never has, and exactly what makes a simulation useful for judging a method. Most practices share one true referral rate of 5.6 per 1,000, and three are planted as genuinely different: two referring well above that rate, one well below. Watch which of them the method finds.

Set up the data

RPython



# Simulated data for illustration (seed for reproducibility)
set.seed(42)
list_size <- round(rnorm(150, mean = 8000, sd = 2000))  # registered patients

# True underlying referral rates per 1,000: common rate 5.6, with three
# genuinely divergent practices planted (two high, one low)
true_rate <- rep(5.6, 150)
true_rate[c(12, 87)] <- c(9.5, 8.2)
true_rate[140] <- 2.4

gp_data <- data.frame(
  practice_id = paste0("GP", 1:150),
  list_size   = list_size,
  referrals   = rpois(150, lambda = list_size * true_rate / 1000)
)

# Observed referral rate per 1,000 registered patients
gp_data$referral_rate <- (gp_data$referrals / gp_data$list_size) * 1000

head(gp_data, 3)

  practice_id list_size referrals referral_rate
1         GP1     10742        59      5.492460
2         GP2      6871        28      4.075098
3         GP3      8726        59      6.761403





import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Use the R-generated data so both tabs show identical numbers
gp_data = r.gp_data

# To generate independently in Python instead:
# rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# list_size = np.round(rng.normal(8000, 2000, 150))
# true_rate = np.full(150, 5.6)          # per 1,000
# true_rate[[11, 86]] = [9.5, 8.2]       # planted high (0-indexed)
# true_rate[139] = 2.4                   # planted low
# gp_data = pd.DataFrame({
#     "practice_id": [f"GP{i}" for i in range(1, 151)],
#     "list_size": list_size,
#     "referrals": rng.poisson(list_size * true_rate / 1000),
# })
# gp_data["referral_rate"] = gp_data["referrals"] / gp_data["list_size"] * 1000

gp_data.head(3)

  practice_id  list_size  referrals  referral_rate
0         GP1    10742.0         59       5.492460
1         GP2     6871.0         28       4.075098
2         GP3     8726.0         59       6.761403







Calculate the expected value and standard deviation

RPython



national_mean <- mean(gp_data$referral_rate)
national_sd   <- sd(gp_data$referral_rate)

cat("National mean:", round(national_mean, 2), "per 1,000\n")

National mean: 5.55 per 1,000

cat("Standard deviation:", round(national_sd, 2), "\n")

Standard deviation: 1 





national_mean = gp_data["referral_rate"].mean()
national_sd = gp_data["referral_rate"].std()  # ddof=1, matching R's sd()

print(f"National mean: {national_mean:.2f} per 1,000")

National mean: 5.55 per 1,000

print(f"Standard deviation: {national_sd:.2f}")

Standard deviation: 1.00







Calculate z-scores and assign bands

RPython



gp_data$z_score <- (gp_data$referral_rate - national_mean) / national_sd

gp_data$band <- cut(
  gp_data$z_score,
  breaks = c(-Inf, -3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3, Inf),
  labels = c("Markedly below", "Below", "Somewhat below", "In line",
             "Somewhat above", "Above", "Markedly above")
)

table(gp_data$band)


Markedly below          Below Somewhat below        In line Somewhat above 
             1              1             19            111             15 
         Above Markedly above 
             2              1 





gp_data["z_score"] = (gp_data["referral_rate"] - national_mean) / national_sd

gp_data["band"] = pd.cut(
    gp_data["z_score"],
    bins=[-np.inf, -3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3, np.inf],
    labels=["Markedly below", "Below", "Somewhat below", "In line",
            "Somewhat above", "Above", "Markedly above"],
)

gp_data["band"].value_counts().sort_index()

band
Markedly below      1
Below               1
Somewhat below     19
In line           111
Somewhat above     15
Above               2
Markedly above      1
Name: count, dtype: int64





Check the band counts against chance expectations before reading anything into them: with 150 practices, the normal distribution predicts roughly 100 in line, around 41 in the two “somewhat” bands, about 6 beyond ±2, and none or one beyond ±3. Here the middle bands hold about what chance predicts – but one practice sits beyond +3 and another beyond −3, where chance would put nobody. Those two are the signal.


Inspect the distribution

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(gp_data, aes(z_score)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.25, fill = "#99C9F7", colour = "white",
                 linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-3, -2, 2, 3), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = cqc_ink, linewidth = 0.4) +
  labs(x = "Z-score", y = "Number of practices") +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 12.2: Two practices stand clear of the pack. The bulk of the distribution is bell-shaped around zero, as chance alone would produce – but one practice sits beyond +3 and one beyond −3, distances that 150 practices should essentially never reach by chance.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK

apply_cqc_style()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.hist(gp_data["z_score"], bins=24, color="#99C9F7", edgecolor="white")
for ref in (-3, -2, 2, 3):
    ax.axvline(ref, linestyle="--", color=CQC_INK, linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("Z-score")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of practices")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 12.3: Two practices stand clear of the pack. The bulk of the distribution is bell-shaped around zero, as chance alone would produce – but one practice sits beyond +3 and one beyond −3, distances that 150 practices should essentially never reach by chance.










List the practices to look at

RPython



# Practices beyond +/-2: candidates for a closer look, in either direction
flagged <- gp_data[abs(gp_data$z_score) >= 2,
                   c("practice_id", "referral_rate", "z_score", "band")]
flagged[order(flagged$z_score), ]

    practice_id referral_rate   z_score           band
140       GP140      1.646612 -3.889167 Markedly below
18         GP18      2.977298 -2.562553          Below
112       GP112      7.667965  2.113761          Above
59         GP59      7.944389  2.389339          Above
12         GP12     10.021475  4.460069 Markedly above





# Practices beyond +/-2: candidates for a closer look, in either direction
flagged = gp_data.loc[
    gp_data["z_score"].abs() >= 2,
    ["practice_id", "referral_rate", "z_score", "band"],
]
flagged.sort_values("z_score")

    practice_id  referral_rate   z_score            band
139       GP140       1.646612 -3.889167  Markedly below
17         GP18       2.977298 -2.562553           Below
111       GP112       7.667965  2.113761           Above
58         GP59       7.944389  2.389339           Above
11         GP12      10.021475  4.460069  Markedly above





Five practices clear the ±2 threshold, and the two planted extremes – GP12 referring far above the national rate, GP140 far below – head the list with z-scores beyond ±3. But notice what else the list teaches. Three practices beyond ±2 are chance passengers (the simulation made them ordinary), and the third planted practice, GP87, is not on the list at all: its genuinely elevated rate of 8.2 per 1,000 was masked by an unlucky draw. Detection is probabilistic – a clean threshold catches the gross cases, misses some real ones, and sweeps in some innocents. A list like this is where the statistics stop and the regulatory judgement starts: the next section is about reading it honestly.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.5 Interpreting Results

Distance, significance, and importance: three different questions
A z-score is routinely asked to carry three distinct meanings that are easily conflated. Keeping them apart is the single most important skill in interpreting z-scored indicators.
1. Observed distance (descriptive). How many standard deviations is this provider from the expected value? This is all a z-score measures directly. A z of +1.8 says a provider sits 1.8 standard deviations above the mean – nothing more.
2. Statistical significance (inferential). Is the difference larger than random variation would plausibly produce, given how precisely this provider can be measured? This depends on precision, which is driven by the size of the provider’s denominator – not on distance alone. Two providers can have the same rate but very different significance: a large practice (8,000 patients) just 3 percentage points above the mean can be a clear signal, because its estimate is precise, while a small practice (120 patients) 13 percentage points above the mean may not be, because its estimate is noisy. This is why the cross-provider band and the funnel-plot limits below are not interchangeable: the band standardises by the spread across providers, while significance depends on each provider’s own precision. The funnel plot is the tool that handles this correctly.
3. Practical importance (contextual). Even a clearly significant difference may not matter operationally – and a non-significant one may still warrant attention. A 3-percentage-point difference might be real but immaterial; a small provider that misses significance for lack of data has not been shown to be fine. Only domain knowledge answers this question; no z-score can.
The distinction is not a technicality. It is the difference between using z-scores as one input to a judgement and treating them as the judgement itself.


Reading the bands for regulatory action
In line with average (|z| < 1): no action needed on this indicator.
Somewhat above/below (1 ≤ |z| < 2): weak evidence – roughly a third of providers land here even when nothing distinguishes them. Monitor; do not investigate on this alone.
Above/below (2 ≤ |z| < 3): evidence of a difference. Whether it earns investigation depends on the practical size of the difference, the provider’s denominator (is the estimate precise?), the provider’s history and other indicators, and available inspection resource.
Markedly above/below (|z| ≥ 3): strong evidence of a difference, and a strong candidate for investigation unless there is a clear explanation – a specialist service, a data error, an unadjusted case-mix difference. Investigation determines why; the z-score only establishes that.


Many indicators at once: when chance looks like a signal
CQC routinely scores dozens of indicators across thousands of providers, and the arithmetic of chance scales with you: at a ±2 threshold, expect roughly one flag in twenty to be noise. Run 100 indicators and a handful of borderline flags are pure coincidence. So a provider flagged once, marginally, on one indicator among many is thin evidence – especially with nothing else corroborating. A provider flagged on three indicators simultaneously is a different matter: coincidences multiply fast, and the probability that all three are chance is small. Weight evidence across sources, raise the bar for single-indicator action (require |z| > 3, or corroboration), and treat profiling as screening rather than confirmatory inference. Formal multiple-testing corrections exist and matter in formal evaluations – the chapter on comparing groups covers them – but in routine profiling the practical defence is corroboration, not correction.






Note🤔 Rethinking: regression to the mean



When a care home is flagged well above average this year, expect it to look better next year – even if nothing about its quality changes. This is regression to the mean, and it is a statistical certainty, not a mystery.
A provider’s z-score reflects both its true underlying performance and random variation. A provider in the flagged zone has usually experienced both worse-than-average true performance and unlucky noise; next period the noise averages out, and the z-score drifts back towards zero. Three regulatory consequences follow. Do not read a flagged provider “recovering” into the expected band as proof that an intervention worked – some of that recovery was coming anyway. Do not dismiss a flag on the grounds that it will regress – the true-performance component is still there and still warrants attention. And trust repeated flags far more than single ones: a provider consistently beyond ±2 across periods is showing you signal, because noise does not repeat on schedule.
The practical test, where you have several years of data: plot each provider’s z-score over time. Persistent problems produce persistently high z-scores; random extremes wander back.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.6 Funnel Plots: Visualising Provider Variation
Funnel plots are the natural visual companion to z-scoring. Where z-scores give you a number for systematic flagging, funnel plots give you the picture – every provider’s performance against statistically derived limits that widen for smaller providers, exactly because their estimates are noisier.
A funnel plot places each provider as a point, with the denominator (practice list size, bed-days) on the x-axis and the indicator on the y-axis. Control limits drawn around the expected value mark the range of variation a provider of that size would produce by chance; small providers get wide limits, large providers narrow ones, and the limits trace the characteristic funnel. Two pairs are conventional:

Conventional funnel plot control limits








Limit
Width
Equivalent z-score
Reading




Inner
±1.96 standard errors
|z| > 1.96
screening threshold – worth monitoring


Outer
±3.09 standard errors
|z| > 3.09
strong signal – worth investigating



A provider outside the outer limits has a precision-based z-score – computed against its own standard error – beyond ±3.09. The funnel makes this visually obvious, and also shows immediately why small providers are harder to flag: their own limits, set by their own denominators, simply extend further. That is not lenience; it is statistical fairness.

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

theta <- national_mean  # national mean referral rate per 1,000

# Control limits across the range of list sizes
# (Poisson approximation: SE of the rate = sqrt(theta * 1000 / n));
# lower limits clamped at zero - a rate cannot be negative
n_seq  <- seq(min(gp_data$list_size) * 0.9, max(gp_data$list_size) * 1.05,
              length.out = 300)
se_seq <- sqrt(theta * 1000 / n_seq)
limits <- data.frame(
  n      = n_seq,
  ll_95  = pmax(0, theta - 1.96 * se_seq), ul_95  = theta + 1.96 * se_seq,
  ll_998 = pmax(0, theta - 3.09 * se_seq), ul_998 = theta + 3.09 * se_seq
)

# Classify each practice against its own limits
se_prov <- sqrt(theta * 1000 / gp_data$list_size)
dist    <- abs(gp_data$referral_rate - theta)
gp_data$signal <- ifelse(dist > 3.09 * se_prov, "Outside 99.8% - investigate",
                  ifelse(dist > 1.96 * se_prov, "Outside 95% - monitor",
                         "Within limits"))

ggplot(gp_data, aes(list_size, referral_rate)) +
  geom_line(data = limits, aes(n, ul_95),  linetype = "dashed", colour = "#4289B3") +
  geom_line(data = limits, aes(n, ll_95),  linetype = "dashed", colour = "#4289B3") +
  geom_line(data = limits, aes(n, ul_998), linetype = "dotted", colour = "#A83DA4") +
  geom_line(data = limits, aes(n, ll_998), linetype = "dotted", colour = "#A83DA4") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = theta, colour = cqc_ink, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = signal, shape = signal), size = 2.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Within limits"                = "#6D6D6D",
                                 "Outside 95% - monitor"        = "#A57703",
                                 "Outside 99.8% - investigate"  = "#A83DA4"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Within limits"               = 16,
                                "Outside 95% - monitor"       = 17,
                                "Outside 99.8% - investigate" = 15),
                     name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Practice list size (registered patients)",
       y = "Referral rate per 1,000 patients") +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")







Figure 12.4: The funnel finds exactly the two planted extremes. Both practices outside the outer (99.8%) limits are the genuinely divergent ones; the six drifting past the inner limits are roughly the false-alarm count chance predicts – and the funnel’s widening mouth shows why a small practice needs a much larger rate difference than a large one to earn the same flag.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK

apply_cqc_style()

theta = national_mean  # national mean referral rate per 1,000

# Control limits across the range of list sizes
# (Poisson approximation: SE of the rate = sqrt(theta * 1000 / n));
# lower limits clamped at zero - a rate cannot be negative
n_seq = np.linspace(gp_data["list_size"].min() * 0.9,
                    gp_data["list_size"].max() * 1.05, 300)
se_seq = np.sqrt(theta * 1000 / n_seq)

# Classify each practice against its own limits
se_prov = np.sqrt(theta * 1000 / gp_data["list_size"])
dist = (gp_data["referral_rate"] - theta).abs()
outer = dist > 3.09 * se_prov
inner = (dist > 1.96 * se_prov) & ~outer
within = ~inner & ~outer

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.plot(n_seq, theta + 1.96 * se_seq, "--", color="#4289B3", linewidth=1)
ax.plot(n_seq, np.maximum(0, theta - 1.96 * se_seq), "--", color="#4289B3",
        linewidth=1)
ax.plot(n_seq, theta + 3.09 * se_seq, ":", color="#A83DA4", linewidth=1)
ax.plot(n_seq, np.maximum(0, theta - 3.09 * se_seq), ":", color="#A83DA4",
        linewidth=1)
ax.axhline(theta, color=CQC_INK, linewidth=1)

ax.scatter(gp_data.loc[within, "list_size"], gp_data.loc[within, "referral_rate"],
           color="#6D6D6D", marker="o", s=25, alpha=0.85, label="Within limits")
ax.scatter(gp_data.loc[inner, "list_size"], gp_data.loc[inner, "referral_rate"],
           color="#A57703", marker="^", s=45, label="Outside 95% - monitor")
ax.scatter(gp_data.loc[outer, "list_size"], gp_data.loc[outer, "referral_rate"],
           color="#A83DA4", marker="s", s=45, label="Outside 99.8% - investigate")

ax.set_xlabel("Practice list size (registered patients)")
ax.set_ylabel("Referral rate per 1,000 patients")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 12.5: The funnel finds exactly the two planted extremes. Both practices outside the outer (99.8%) limits are the genuinely divergent ones; the six drifting past the inner limits are roughly the false-alarm count chance predicts – and the funnel’s widening mouth shows why a small practice needs a much larger rate difference than a large one to earn the same flag.








Reading it. A provider beyond the outer limits warrants investigation; beyond the inner limits, monitoring. The funnel’s shape carries the fairness argument for you: when a small provider with a high rate is not flagged, the plot shows the reason – its wide limits mean the evidence is not yet strong enough, not that the concern has been dismissed.
Compare the funnel’s list with the banded list from the worked example and you will notice they overlap without coinciding: both catch the two planted extremes, but each flags a different supporting cast. That is not a bug in either tool. The cross-provider band standardises by the spread across practices, the funnel by each practice’s own precision – the distinction from the three-questions section made visible. The two only agree exactly when providers are equally precise; where denominators vary, trust the funnel for flagging.
Communicating with it. Funnel plots convey uncertainty in a way tables of z-scores cannot, which is why they are the form to reach for in reports, board papers, and publications; keep the z-scores themselves for internal analysis, dashboards, and automated flagging.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Working z-scores – when the standard method will not do



When data fail the minimum requirements – counts below 50, denominators below the thresholds, percentages that no transformation makes normal – the standard method’s normality assumption is unsafe, and CQC’s guidance switches to Working z-scores. The name is the clue to the construction: rather than measuring distance directly, the method works backwards from a probability. First obtain, for each provider, the probability of a value as extreme as theirs; then convert that probability into the z-score that would carry the same probability under a standard normal distribution, \(Z_w = \Phi^{-1}(1 - p)\). The result reads exactly like any other z-score – +2.0 still means “as unusual as 2 standard deviations above the mean” – but the probability underneath it came from a distribution that actually fits the data.
The probability is obtained one of two ways:

Model-based, for counts (with or without covariates), proportions, and standardised ratios with integer numerators: fit candidate distributions suited to the data type – Poisson then negative binomial then their zero-inflated versions for counts; Beta for proportions – choosing the best fit by AIC, simplest first, and read each provider’s p-value from the fitted model.
Empirical, for percentages, continuous data, and ratios with non-integer numerators: rank the providers and take \(p = \text{rank}/(n+1)\). Simple and robust – though note it uses only the order of providers, so it deliberately discards how far apart they are.

Working z-scores need no winsorisation or overdispersion adjustment – the fitted distribution absorbs what the normal approximation could not. Externally derived targets cannot be inserted directly (there is no \(t\) in a rank); instead the whole set of scores is shifted by an adjustment factor computed from where the target would have ranked.
In practice you should not be implementing any of this by hand: the iHub applies the working method automatically whenever a provider fails the minimum requirements, and validated R scripts exist for manual work. Use them, and bring questions of implementation or interpretation to the Guild – this is precisely the machinery the Quant Clinic exists for.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.7 Documenting Your Analysis
For QA purposes, record: the indicator definition and time period; the number of providers before and after exclusions, and the exclusion criteria; the expected value and standard deviation (or the transformation and model, if the working method applied); whether you used standard or Working z-scores and why; the banding thresholds; the share of providers in each band and whether the distribution looked plausible; and the providers flagged, with recommended follow-up. Transparent documentation of method choices and assumptions is what the Code of Practice for Statistics asks of sound methods and assured quality – and it is what makes the analysis defensible when a provider challenges it.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.8 Related Approaches

Monitoring one provider over time → statistical process control reads a sequence of values where z-scoring reads a cross-section.
Comparing two groups with no national benchmark → t-tests.
Investigating the flagged providers → unusual observations for the investigate-first discipline, and outlier detection methods for the toolkit.
Foundations → variation and uncertainty for why distance alone is not evidence; distribution shape for normality and transformations; statistical inference for significance, p-values, and their honest reading.
Complete workflows → Example 1: GP Access Analysis and Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
section: 12.9 When to Seek Support from the Guild
text:
12.9 When to Seek Support from the Guild
Raise your analysis with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) at a Quant Clinic session when: the distribution stays extremely skewed despite transformation; you need Working z-scores and are unsure of implementation or interpretation; the data show clear overdispersion; you are scoring many indicators at once and need a multiple-testing strategy; small numbers meet high-stakes decisions; a stakeholder challenges the methodology; or you are developing a new indicator and z-scoring’s appropriateness is itself the question.

title: 12  Comparing Provider Performance: Z-scoring
text:
12.10 Further Reading

Internal CQC resources

Methodological Note: Z-Score Bandings – What They Tell Us and What They Don’t (Internal, 2026): the source for the band labels and interpretation used in this chapter; sets out the inverse probability fallacy and the distance/significance/importance distinction.
Guidance on z-scoring indicators, V5 (Internal, 2020): the authoritative technical specification for the standard and Working z-score methods, minimum requirements, winsorisation, overdispersion, and aggregation.
The QA Framework sets the documentation standards for z-scoring analyses; see also How to Use This Book for how to access support.



Interpreting significance and uncertainty

Wasserstein, R.L. & Lazar, N.A. (2016). “The ASA’s statement on p-values: context, process, and purpose.” The American Statistician, 70(2), 129–133.
Goodman, S. (2008). “A dirty dozen: twelve p-value misconceptions.” Seminars in Hematology, 45(3), 135–140.
Office for Statistics Regulation. How to communicate uncertainty in statistics.



Provider comparison and funnel plots

Spiegelhalter, D.J. (2005). “Funnel plots for comparing institutional performance.” Statistics in Medicine, 24(8), 1185–1202.
Spiegelhalter, D.J. et al. (2012). “Statistical methods for healthcare regulation: rating, screening and surveillance.” Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series A, 175(1), 1–47.
NHS England, Summary Hospital-level Mortality Indicator (SHMI): neutral “higher/as/lower than expected” banding language with overdispersion-adjusted limits.
Funnel plots explained – Nuffield Department of Primary Care Health Sciences, Oxford.

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.1 Two Kinds of Variation
Every measure that matters to a regulator moves from month to month. Falls counts, waiting times, complaint volumes – none of them sits still, and most of the movement means nothing. The discipline SPC brings is a vocabulary for that fact, inherited from Walter Shewhart’s work in 1920s manufacturing and now standard across healthcare improvement. Common cause variation is the routine, expected fluctuation a stable process produces on its own: no single month’s wobble has an explanation, because the explanation is the process – many small influences, summing differently each month, within a predictable range. Special cause variation is different in kind: something identifiable has changed – a new manager, an outbreak, a rota collapse, a data error – and the measure has moved outside, or patterned itself against, its own routine. The first kind of variation is to be expected and tolerated; the second is to be found and explained.
The distinction matters because each mistake about it has a cost. Read common cause as special – chase every up-tick, demand an explanation for every bad month – and you waste investigative effort on noise, erode trust in the data, and pressure providers into explaining randomness, which teaches them to invent stories. Acting on noise even has a name in the SPC tradition, tampering, and Deming’s demonstration that it makes a process more variable rather than less is one of the field’s founding results. Read special cause as common – wave away a real deterioration as “just variation” – and a problem runs on unexamined. You cannot eliminate both mistakes; what a control chart does is set an explicit, statistically grounded boundary that balances them, instead of leaving each month’s judgement to whoever is reading the dashboard.
The everyday alternative is worse than it looks. Comparing this month to last month – the logic of every up/down arrow and most red-amber-green ratings – is a two-point comparison, and two points from a stable process can easily differ by half their value. The picture below makes the point with simulated data from a process in which nothing changes at all.
Figure 13.1: A process in which nothing changes still produces a 75% month-on-month rise. Twenty-four months of incident counts simulated from one stable process: a two-point comparison finds a 56% fall and a 75% surge, yet every point sits comfortably inside the control limits and no SPC rule fires. Month-on-month arrows read noise as news; the chart reads it as a stable process.
A control chart is the tool that operationalises the distinction. It plots the measure in time order around a centre line (the process mean) and two control limits, conventionally set three standard deviations of the process’s own short-term variation above and below the centre. While the process is stable, almost every point – about 99.7 per cent, when the data are roughly normal – will fall inside the limits, so a point outside them is a rare event under stability: not proof that something changed, but strong grounds to investigate. The limits are calculated from the data’s own behaviour, which is the crucial difference from a target or specification line: a control chart tells you what the process is actually doing, not what anyone wishes it were doing.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
Note🤔 Rethinking: a chart is not a test



A control chart looks like statistical inference – limits, thresholds, signals – and the temptation is to read it as a rolling hypothesis test with p-values attached. Resist it. The chapter on statistical inference deals in questions of the form “what is the true value of this fixed quantity?”, answered by estimates and intervals from a defined population. SPC asks a different kind of question – “is this process behaving as it has been behaving?” – about a moving stream of data with no fixed population behind it. Shewhart chose three-standard-deviation limits not from a significance table but as an economic judgement: wide enough that false alarms stay rare, tight enough that real shifts surface quickly. The 0.3 per cent figure is a useful intuition for how rare a false alarm is, not a p-value to report.
The practical consequence is liberating: a control chart needs no null hypothesis, no significance level, and no correction for the fact that you look at it every month. It earns its keep as a decision aid – investigate, or leave alone – applied continuously. What it does not do is explain anything. A signal says “something changed around here, about then”; what changed, and whether it matters, is investigative and regulatory work the chart cannot start. The strongest habit in SPC practice is to treat every signal as the beginning of a question.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.2 Before You Start
Data requirements. You need a measure recorded in consistent time order – the same definition, collected the same way, at reasonably regular intervals (weekly, monthly, quarterly). Control limits calculated from fewer than about 12 points are provisional at best; 15–20 baseline points make them trustworthy. Complete the usual data-quality checks first (the chapter on quality assurance sets out the pre-analysis questions), and be alert to definition changes mid-series: if what “an incident” means changed in April, the chart cannot bridge April.
The question must be longitudinal. SPC follows one process – a provider, a service, a system – through time. To compare many providers against each other at one point in time, use z-scoring and funnel plots; the two methods are complementary, not interchangeable.
When SPC is not the right tool. Very short series (a handful of points) cannot support limits; deeply seasonal or strongly autocorrelated data (daily attendances, say) violate the independence the basic charts assume and need adjustment first; and a one-off before/after comparison with no ongoing stream may be better served by a designed two-group comparison.
QA framework alignment. SPC most directly supports QA question 3 (Analysis – is the method appropriate and correctly applied?) and question 4 (Delivery and Communication – does the chart communicate the finding clearly?). Document the chart type, the baseline period behind the limits, and any recalculations as part of the question 3 record.
Worked examples for this chapter. Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring builds an I-chart around an intervention; Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays handles trend and seasonality in a longer series.
The code below uses pseudo-randomly generated data. The Python tabs read the R-generated dataset via r.falls_data so both languages show identical numbers; commented-out code shows how to generate the data in Python independently.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.3 Choosing the Right Chart
The right chart follows from the type of data you hold, because the limits must reflect how that kind of data naturally varies:

Chart selection by data type







Your measure
Chart
Limits based on




Continuous values, one per period (mean waiting time, average length of stay)
I-chart (individuals chart)
the average month-to-month moving range


Proportions or percentages (% seen within target)
P-chart
the binomial distribution; limits widen when the denominator shrinks


Counts of events, opportunity roughly constant (complaints per quarter)
C-chart
the Poisson distribution


Rates with a varying denominator (falls per 1,000 bed-days when occupancy moves)
U-chart
the Poisson distribution, scaled by each period’s denominator



In practice the I-chart covers more ground than the table suggests. Healthcare SPC tutorials – Mohammed and colleagues’ is the standard reference – recommend it as the robust default even for many proportions and rates, provided denominators are large and reasonably stable, because it estimates variation from the data’s own behaviour rather than from a distributional assumption that real data often violate. If you are unsure, start with the I-chart; switch to P/C/U when small or shifting denominators make period-specific limits genuinely necessary.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.4 Step-by-Step Guide: Building an I-chart

Step 1: Plot the raw series first
Before any limits, plot the data in time order and look. Obvious trends, seasonality, sudden level shifts, and suspect values are all easier to see on a plain run of points than to infer from summary statistics – and each one affects whether, and over what baseline, limits make sense.


Step 2: Choose the baseline period
The limits should describe the process as it routinely behaves, so calculate them from a stretch of data you believe to be stable – the baseline – and not across a known change. If you are assessing an intervention, the baseline is the pre-intervention period; the limits are then frozen and extended forwards, and the post-intervention data are judged against them. Calculating limits across the whole series, change included, smears the change into the limits and hides it.


Step 3: Calculate the centre line and control limits
The centre line is the mean of the baseline observations. The limits need an estimate of the process’s short-term variation, and the I-chart gets it from the moving range – the absolute difference between each pair of consecutive points:
\[\text{UCL} = \bar{x} + 2.66\,\overline{\text{MR}}, \qquad \text{LCL} = \bar{x} - 2.66\,\overline{\text{MR}}\]
where \(\bar{x}\) is the baseline mean and \(\overline{\text{MR}}\) is the mean of the moving ranges. In words: take the average jump between consecutive months, multiply by 2.66, and place the limits that far either side of the mean. (The constant converts an average moving range into three standard deviations’ worth of individual-point variation; it comes from the statistics of ranges, not from anything you tune.) Using consecutive differences is what makes the I-chart robust: a slow trend or a step change inflates the overall standard deviation but barely touches the typical month-to-month jump, so the limits stay honest about routine variation. If the lower limit falls below zero for a measure that cannot be negative, draw it at zero.


Step 4: Apply the detection rules
A point outside the limits is the headline rule, but a process can change without ever breaching them – a modest sustained shift moves every point a little, not one point a lot. Pattern rules catch this. The compact set used across NHS practice:

Outside the limits: any single point beyond the UCL or LCL.
Shift: a run of 8 consecutive points on the same side of the centre line (some implementations use 7).
Trend: 6 or more consecutive points all rising or all falling.
