Each rule is calibrated to be rare under stability, so any of them firing is grounds to investigate. A larger battery of rules exists – the Overthinking box below sets out the trade-off – but these three carry most of the practical value.


Step 5: Interpret, act, and only then recalculate
Random scatter inside the limits with no rule firing means the process is stable: leave it alone, and resist the monthly demand for a narrative about noise. A signal means investigate – find what changed, around when the rule fired. And only once a change is confirmed, explained, and sustained should you recalculate the limits on the new level, so that monitoring continues against the process as it now is. Recalculating routinely, every time a point is added, defeats the chart: the limits exist precisely to stay still while the data move.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: detection rules and the sensitivity trade-off



The full Western Electric rule set adds inner-zone tests to the basic three: two-out-of-three consecutive points beyond two standard deviations on the same side; four-out-of-five beyond one standard deviation on the same side; and fifteen consecutive points hugging the centre within one standard deviation – “too quiet”, which in regulatory data is worth a thought, since processes do not usually become eerily stable on their own; over-smoothed, manually adjusted, or fabricated figures do.
Every added rule buys earlier detection and pays in false alarms. One useful currency for the trade-off is the in-control average run length: with the single outside-the-limits rule, a stable process produces a false alarm roughly once every 370 points; stack on the inner-zone rules and that falls to once every ~90. For a chart watched monthly, once-in-30-years becomes once-in-7 – per chart, so a dashboard of fifty charts will produce several spurious signals a year under the full rule set. Choose by consequence, exactly as the error trade-off in hypothesis testing is chosen: for high-stakes safety indicators, accept more false alarms to catch deterioration sooner; for routine monitoring, the basic three rules keep the signal-to-noise ratio workable.
Two further cautions. The rules assume points are independent; daily or weekly operational data are often autocorrelated (busy days follow busy days), which fires run rules far more often than the nominal rates suggest – this is the most common way SPC quietly misleads in practice, and a reason to monitor at a coarser interval or seek Guild advice. And with very large denominators, P- and U-chart limits computed from binomial or Poisson theory hug the centre line so tightly that everything signals – the same overdispersion met in funnel plots; the pragmatic fix is usually an I-chart of the rates, the principled one an inflation of the limits.

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
section: 13.5 Worked Example: Care Home Falls Programme
text:
13.5 Worked Example: Care Home Falls Programme
Scenario: a 60-bed nursing home has recorded monthly falls for two years. At the start of month 13 it introduced a falls prevention programme – sensor mats, medication review, strengthened night cover. Did the programme work, and is the improvement holding?
Because the data are simulated, we know the answer in advance: the true underlying rate falls from 18 to 13 falls per month at month 13 – a real but undramatic reduction, about 28 per cent. Watch how it shows up on the chart: not as any single alarming point, but as a pattern.

Set up the data

RPython



# Simulated data for illustration (seed for reproducibility).
# True rate: 18 falls/month for months 1-12, 13/month from month 13.
set.seed(42)
falls_data <- data.frame(
  month = 1:24,
  falls = c(rpois(12, lambda = 18), rpois(12, lambda = 13)),
  phase = rep(c("Baseline", "Post-intervention"), each = 12)
)

head(falls_data, 3)

  month falls    phase
1     1    23 Baseline
2     2    15 Baseline
3     3    18 Baseline





import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Use the R-generated data so both tabs show identical numbers
falls_data = r.falls_data

# To generate independently in Python instead:
# rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# falls_data = pd.DataFrame({
#     "month": range(1, 25),
#     "falls": np.concatenate([rng.poisson(18, 12), rng.poisson(13, 12)]),
#     "phase": ["Baseline"] * 12 + ["Post-intervention"] * 12,
# })

falls_data.head(3)

   month  falls     phase
0      1     23  Baseline
1      2     15  Baseline
2      3     18  Baseline







Calculate baseline limits and freeze them
The question is whether the process changed at month 13, so the limits come from the baseline year only, and the post-intervention months are judged against them.

RPython



baseline <- falls_data$falls[falls_data$phase == "Baseline"]

centre <- mean(baseline)
mr_bar <- mean(abs(diff(baseline)))   # average month-to-month jump
ucl    <- centre + 2.66 * mr_bar
lcl    <- max(0, centre - 2.66 * mr_bar)  # falls cannot be negative

cat("Centre line:", round(centre, 1), "falls/month\n")

Centre line: 19.3 falls/month

cat("Control limits:", round(lcl, 1), "to", round(ucl, 1), "\n")

Control limits: 0.7 to 38 





baseline = falls_data.loc[falls_data["phase"] == "Baseline", "falls"]

centre = baseline.mean()
mr_bar = baseline.diff().abs().mean()  # average month-to-month jump
ucl = centre + 2.66 * mr_bar
lcl = max(0, centre - 2.66 * mr_bar)   # falls cannot be negative

print(f"Centre line: {centre:.1f} falls/month")

Centre line: 19.3 falls/month

print(f"Control limits: {lcl:.1f} to {ucl:.1f}")

Control limits: 0.7 to 38.0







Draw the chart

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(falls_data, aes(month, falls)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = centre, colour = cqc_ink, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(ucl, lcl), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#4289B3", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 12.5, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "#A83DA4", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_line(colour = cqc_muted, linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = phase, shape = phase), size = 2.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Baseline" = "#007EF5",
                                 "Post-intervention" = "#A83DA4"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Baseline" = 16, "Post-intervention" = 17),
                     name = NULL) +
  annotate("text", x = 12.7, y = 36, label = "intervention",
           colour = "#A83DA4", size = 3.2, hjust = 0) +
  annotate("text", x = 24, y = ucl + 1.6, label = "upper control limit",
           colour = "#4289B3", size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 24, y = lcl - 1.6, label = "lower control limit",
           colour = "#4289B3", size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2, 24, 2)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2, 40)) +
  labs(x = "Month", y = "Falls") +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")







Figure 13.2: The falls programme shows up as a pattern, not a dramatic point. No month breaches the baseline control limits – but all twelve post-intervention months sit below the centre line, a run far past the shift rule’s eight, signalling a genuine sustained reduction.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK, CQC_MUTED

apply_cqc_style()

base = falls_data[falls_data["phase"] == "Baseline"]
post = falls_data[falls_data["phase"] == "Post-intervention"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
ax.axhline(centre, color=CQC_INK, linewidth=1)
ax.axhline(ucl, color="#4289B3", linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
ax.axhline(lcl, color="#4289B3", linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
ax.axvline(12.5, color="#A83DA4", linestyle=":", linewidth=1.2)
ax.plot(falls_data["month"], falls_data["falls"], color=CQC_MUTED,
        linewidth=0.8, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(base["month"], base["falls"], color="#007EF5", marker="o",
           s=30, label="Baseline", zorder=3)
ax.scatter(post["month"], post["falls"], color="#A83DA4", marker="^",
           s=35, label="Post-intervention", zorder=3)

ax.text(12.7, 36, "intervention", color="#A83DA4", fontsize=9)
ax.text(24, ucl + 1.2, "upper control limit", color="#4289B3",
        fontsize=8, ha="right")
ax.text(24, lcl - 2.4, "lower control limit", color="#4289B3",
        fontsize=8, ha="right")
ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("Falls")
ax.set_ylim(-2, 40)

(-2.0, 40.0)

ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 13.3: The falls programme shows up as a pattern, not a dramatic point. No month breaches the baseline control limits – but all twelve post-intervention months sit below the centre line, a run far past the shift rule’s eight, signalling a genuine sustained reduction.










Read the signal

RPython



post <- falls_data$falls[falls_data$phase == "Post-intervention"]

# Outside-the-limits rule: any post-intervention month beyond the limits?
cat("Months beyond the limits:", sum(post > ucl | post < lcl), "\n")

Months beyond the limits: 0 

# Shift rule: how long is the run below the centre line?
cat("Consecutive months below the centre line:", sum(post < centre), "\n")

Consecutive months below the centre line: 12 

# Size of the change
cat("Baseline mean:", round(centre, 1),
    "- post-intervention mean:", round(mean(post), 1),
    "- reduction:", round(centre - mean(post), 1), "falls/month\n")

Baseline mean: 19.3 - post-intervention mean: 13.7 - reduction: 5.7 falls/month





post = falls_data.loc[falls_data["phase"] == "Post-intervention", "falls"]

# Outside-the-limits rule: any post-intervention month beyond the limits?
print(f"Months beyond the limits: {((post > ucl) | (post < lcl)).sum()}")

Months beyond the limits: 0

# Shift rule: how long is the run below the centre line?
print(f"Consecutive months below the centre line: {(post < centre).sum()}")

Consecutive months below the centre line: 12

# Size of the change
print(f"Baseline mean: {centre:.1f}"
      f" - post-intervention mean: {post.mean():.1f}"
      f" - reduction: {centre - post.mean():.1f} falls/month")

Baseline mean: 19.3 - post-intervention mean: 13.7 - reduction: 5.7 falls/month





The outside-the-limits rule never fires: monthly falls counts are noisy, so the baseline limits are wide, and a 28 per cent reduction is not large enough to push any single month past them. If “points outside the limits” were your only rule, you would conclude nothing had happened – and you would be wrong. The shift rule tells the real story: twelve consecutive months below the centre line, against the eight that stability would essentially never produce. The reduction is genuine, sustained, and roughly five to six falls a month.
Two pieces of honesty belong in the write-up. First, the chart establishes that the process changed at about the right time – it cannot establish that the programme caused the change; a chart is not a designed evaluation, and winter occupancy, a changed resident mix, or recording practice could each move the same line. Corroborate before celebrating. Second, now that the shift is confirmed and explained, recalculate the limits from the post-intervention months and monitor against the new level – the old limits answered “did it change?”; the new ones answer “is it holding?”.






Note🤔 Rethinking: the chart on the boardroom table



The evidence for SPC in healthcare is unusually solid for an analytical method: a systematic review (Thor and colleagues, 2007) found control charts improved how teams distinguished real change from noise, helped clinicians and managers talk about the same numbers without talking past each other, and supported earlier, calmer responses to deterioration. NHS England’s “Making Data Count” programme has pushed the approach into routine board reporting precisely to displace the up/down arrows and red-amber-green summaries that read noise as news.
For a CQC analyst this cuts both ways. It means SPC charts are increasingly what providers themselves bring to the conversation – board packs, quality accounts, improvement reports – so reading them critically is now part of regulatory literacy. Check what period the limits were calculated from, whether limits were quietly recalculated after a bad run, whether a “improvement” is a rule-level signal or two hopeful points, and whether the measure’s definition held still underneath the chart. A provider’s chart done well is good evidence of a monitoring culture; done badly, it can launder noise into narrative. The same standards this chapter sets for your own charts are the standards to read theirs by.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.6 Documenting Your Analysis
For QA purposes, record: what you measured, over what period, and any exclusions; the chart type and why it suits the data type; the baseline period the limits were computed from; the detection rules in use; every signal and what investigation concluded; and every recalculation of the limits, with the confirmed change that justified it. Limits and rules are method choices like any other – the Code of Practice’s sound-methods obligations apply, and a chart whose limits cannot be reproduced is not evidence.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.7 Related Approaches

Comparing many providers at one point in time → z-scoring and funnel plots; the funnel plot is SPC’s cross-sectional sibling, with the same 3-standard-deviation logic.
A one-off before/after question with no ongoing monitoring → comparing two groups, which also supplies the effect-size language for “how big was the change?”.
Investigating what a signal means → unusual observations for the investigate-before-acting discipline.
Foundations → measuring variation for spread; statistical inference for the error trade-off that rule choice re-enacts.
Complete workflows → Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring and Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays.
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
section: 13.8 When to Seek Support from the Guild
text:
13.8 When to Seek Support from the Guild
Raise your analysis with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) at a Quant Clinic session when: the data are autocorrelated or strongly seasonal (daily series especially); denominators are very large and P/U limits flag everything (overdispersion); you need faster detection of small persistent shifts (CUSUM charts, which accumulate deviations from a target, or EWMA charts, which weight recent observations – both more sensitive and harder to read than Shewhart charts); the monitoring needs risk adjustment for case-mix; several changes overlap and you need to date them; or rare events make counts mostly zero (specialised chart types exist).
crumbs: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics

title: 13  Monitoring Trends Over Time: SPC Basics
text:
13.9 Further Reading

Internal CQC resources

The QA Framework sets the documentation standards for SPC analyses; see How to Use This Book for how to access support.



Practical guidance

Mohammed, M.A., Worthington, P. & Woodall, W.H. (2008). “Plotting basic control charts: tutorial notes for healthcare practitioners.” Quality and Safety in Health Care, 17(2), 137–145. (The standard short tutorial; source of the XmR-as-default advice.)
Provost, L.P. & Murray, S.K. (2011). The Health Care Data Guide: Learning from Data for Improvement. Jossey-Bass. (The comprehensive reference for chart selection and improvement use.)
NHS England – Making Data Count and the SPC tool. (Templates and board-reporting practice across the NHS.)



Method and evidence

Thor, J. et al. (2007). “Application of statistical process control in healthcare improvement: systematic review.” Quality and Safety in Health Care, 16(5), 387–399.
Benneyan, J.C. (1998). “Statistical quality control methods in infection control and hospital epidemiology, Part I.” Infection Control and Hospital Epidemiology, 19(3), 194–214.
Sherlaw-Johnson, C. & Bardsley, M. (2016). Monitoring change in health care through statistical process control methods. Nuffield Trust. (SPC for system-level change; overdispersion and CUSUM/VLAD comparisons.)
HM Treasury (2015). The Aqua Book: guidance on producing quality analysis for government.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.1 One Idea, Three Flavours
Strip away the variants and every t-test computes the same quantity:
\[t = \frac{\text{estimate} - \text{null value}}{\text{standard error of the estimate}}\]
The numerator is the signal – how far what you observed sits from business as usual. The denominator is the noise – the standard error, how much that estimate would wobble from sample to sample. Their ratio says how many standard errors of noise the signal spans: a t near zero is unremarkable, a t of 3 means the observed difference is three times the wobble that chance alone produces, which chance rarely manages. Because the noise is estimated from the sample rather than known, the ratio is compared against Student’s t distribution – a bell curve with heavier tails than the normal, the extra tail-weight being the honest price of estimating the spread from limited data. As samples grow the penalty fades and t becomes the normal distribution; with ten observations it matters.
What changes between the flavours is only which estimate goes in the numerator:
Notation here is the sample notation of the chapter on statistical inference – \(\bar{x}\) and \(s\), divisor \(n-1\) – because a t-test is inference through and through: the groups in hand stand for the larger reality you are reasoning about.
The choice between the last two flavours is about design, not preference. Pairing applies when the same units appear in both measurements – the same wards before and after, the same practices in two winters. Differencing within each pair cancels everything stable about the unit (size, case-mix, culture), so the comparison rides only on what changed, which usually buys substantial precision. Treating genuinely paired data as independent throws that precision away; treating independent groups as paired is simply invalid. Match the test to how the data arose.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
The three t-test flavours share one logic









Flavour
Question
Estimate
Standard error
Degrees of freedom




One-sample
Does this group’s mean differ from a target \(\mu_0\)?
\(\bar{x} - \mu_0\)
\(s/\sqrt{n}\)
\(n - 1\)


Two-sample (independent)
Do two separate groups differ?
\(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2\)
combined from both groups’ spreads
from both group sizes


Paired
Did the same units change between two measurements?
\(\bar{d}\), the mean within-pair difference
\(s_d/\sqrt{n}\)
\(n - 1\) pairs






Is a t-test the right tool at all?
At CQC the honest answer is often no – not because t-tests are weak, but because the routine regulatory questions have different shapes. Comparing many providers against a national picture is z-scoring and funnel plot territory: t-tests handle exactly two groups, and running one per provider both asks the wrong question and multiplies false alarms. Watching one provider or system over time belongs to statistical process control. The t-test’s natural CQC habitat is the deliberate two-group question: a pilot evaluated against controls, a before/after comparison with no ongoing stream, a thematic analysis contrasting two defined groups of services. If that is your question, read on.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.2 Before You Start
Data requirements. A continuous outcome (quantitative data, at the top of the measurement ladder); two defined groups, or one group plus a meaningful target; and enough observations to estimate a spread – below about five per group the test technically runs but tells you little. Complete the usual data-quality checks first.
Independence is the assumption that kills. Each observation must be one independent unit. The CQC trap is clustering: if you compare patient-level scores between two groups of practices, the patients within a practice resemble each other, the effective sample is far smaller than the patient count, and the test’s certainty is fictitious. The simple, safe unit for a t-test is the provider: analyse practice-level means and let \(n\) be the number of practices. Patient-level comparisons across clustered groups need multilevel methods – Guild territory.
When a t-test is not appropriate. More than two groups (ANOVA – raise with the Guild); heavily skewed outcomes with small samples (transform first, or use the rank-based alternatives in the Overthinking box); categorical outcomes (different methods entirely); paired and independent structure mixed together (Guild).
QA framework alignment. t-tests most directly support QA question 3 (Analysis – is the method appropriate and correctly applied?) and, in pilot evaluations, question 5 (Fitness for Purpose – does the comparison answer the right question?). Document the flavour chosen and why, the assumption checks, and every comparison you ran – not only the significant ones.
Worked examples for this chapter. Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot is a paired before/after evaluation; Example 5: Regional A&E Performance is a two-sample comparison with assumption checking.
The code below uses small illustrative datasets defined inline, so the R and Python tabs are each fully self-contained.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.3 Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: State the hypotheses before touching the data
The null hypothesis is business as usual, exactly as the chapter on statistical inference frames it: the pilot made no difference, the two regions share one true mean, the trust meets the standard. The alternative is the possibility you are entertaining. State them – and the direction, if you genuinely have one – before looking at the outcomes, because a hypothesis written after peeking inherits the peek’s bias.


Step 2: Check the assumptions, in order of importance
Independence first, because no later step can repair it: one unit, one observation, no clustering, no repeated measures smuggled into an “independent” comparison.
Normality second, with proportion: the test assumes the relevant quantity – the observations, or for a paired test the within-pair differences – is roughly normal. Look at a histogram or Q-Q plot rather than leaning on a formal test (Shapiro–Wilk flags trivial departures in large samples and misses real ones in small samples, which is exactly backwards for your needs). With more than 30 or so observations per group, the Central Limit Theorem makes the test robust to moderate non-normality; with ten, heavy skew or wild outliers genuinely distort it.
Equal variances you can largely retire as a worry: use Welch’s t-test, which estimates each group’s spread separately and adjusts the degrees of freedom. It is R’s default for good reason – it costs almost nothing when the variances happen to be equal and protects you when they do not. Do not run a preliminary variance test to decide which t-test to use; the two-stage routine distorts the very error rates it is meant to guard.


Step 3: Run the test
One line in either language:

R: t.test(group1, group2) – Welch by default; paired = TRUE for paired; mu = target with a single vector for one-sample.
Python: scipy.stats.ttest_ind(group1, group2, equal_var=False); ttest_rel for paired; ttest_1samp for one-sample.



Step 4: Report the estimate first, the verdict second
Lead with the difference in means and its confidence interval, in the indicator’s own units – that is the finding. The t-statistic, degrees of freedom and p-value follow as supporting evidence, with the p-value read as graded surprise, not proof. The interval in the software output is 95 per cent by convention and by default; remember the level is a dial someone chose, not a law of nature. And translate: “scores were 6.8 points higher (95 per cent CI 1.9 to 11.8)” tells a decision-maker something; “p = 0.011” alone does not.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.4 Effect Sizes: Answering “How Large?”
A p-value answers “could this be noise?” and nothing else. The question a regulator actually acts on – is this difference big enough to matter? – needs an effect size: a measure of magnitude that does not change just because you collected more data.
The most useful effect size is usually the plainest: the difference in means, in original units, with its confidence interval. “6.8 points on a 100-point experience score” can be weighed against costs, baselines, and what a point of experience score means for people using services; no further statistics needed.
The standardised companion is Cohen’s d: the difference in means divided by the pooled standard deviation,
\[d = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p}\]
which expresses the gap in units of how much practices ordinarily differ from each other. Its value is comparability – d can be compared across indicators with different units, which is why evaluation literatures speak it. Conventional labels exist (0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large), and they are the most over-trusted numbers in applied statistics: Cohen offered them reluctantly, for use when nothing better was available, and context beats them every time. A d of 0.2 on mortality matters more than a d of 0.8 on a survey item nobody acts on.
Effect sizes also carry the power lesson in reverse. A small pilot can only ever detect large effects – with ten units a group, a real but modest improvement will routinely fail to reach significance. So a non-significant pilot has usually told you “this study could not see effects of plausible size”, not “there is no effect”. If a pilot’s verdict will drive investment, size the study for the smallest effect worth detecting before collecting data – a power calculation, and a conversation for the Guild.






Note🤔 Rethinking: the verdict trap in pilot evaluations



Pilot evaluations invite a binary reading – “did it work, yes or no?” – and the t-test seems to oblige, p-value as gavel. Both halves of that reading mislead.
A significant pilot has shown that something moved the numbers in the pilot sites. Whether it was the intervention is a separate question the test cannot answer: pilot sites are rarely chosen at random, they enjoy attention, enthusiasm, and resources that a rollout will not carry (and improvement driven by being watched has its own name, the Hawthorne effect), and a site selected because it was struggling will drift back towards ordinary regardless – the same regression to the mean that haunts provider flags. A significant t-test is evidence the pilot sites improved; corroboration is what turns it into evidence the programme works.
A non-significant pilot has shown even less. With small samples the test is weak, so “no significant difference” is usually a statement about the pilot’s size, not the programme’s value – absence of evidence, not evidence of absence. The honest summary of a small null pilot is the confidence interval: “the data are consistent with effects anywhere from −1 to +9 points” keeps the genuine uncertainty visible, where “the pilot found no effect” quietly buries it. Decisions can still be made under that uncertainty; they should just be made knowing it is there.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.5 Worked Example: Care Coordination Pilot
Scenario: ten GP practices ran a care coordination pilot for a year; ten comparable practices did not. The outcome is each practice’s mean patient experience score (0–100) from the year-end survey. Did experience improve in the pilot practices?
Note the unit of analysis: the practice, not the patient. Each practice contributes one number – its mean score – so the twenty observations are independent and a t-test is legitimate. Comparing the thousands of underlying patient responses directly would ignore the clustering of patients within practices and overstate the evidence.

Set up and look at the data

RPython



# Practice-level mean experience scores (0-100)
pilot   <- c(80.5, 72.7, 76.5, 77.5, 76.6, 74.6, 81.0, 74.6, 83.1, 74.7)
control <- c(76.2, 80.1, 65.4, 69.9, 70.5, 73.5, 69.9, 60.4, 61.2, 76.3)

scores <- data.frame(
  score = c(pilot, control),
  group = rep(c("Pilot", "Control"), each = 10)
)

aggregate(score ~ group, scores, function(x) c(mean = mean(x), sd = sd(x)))

    group score.mean  score.sd
1 Control  70.340000  6.517191
2   Pilot  77.180000  3.351550





import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Practice-level mean experience scores (0-100)
pilot = np.array([80.5, 72.7, 76.5, 77.5, 76.6, 74.6, 81.0, 74.6, 83.1, 74.7])
control = np.array([76.2, 80.1, 65.4, 69.9, 70.5, 73.5, 69.9, 60.4, 61.2, 76.3])

scores = pd.DataFrame({
    "score": np.concatenate([pilot, control]),
    "group": ["Pilot"] * 10 + ["Control"] * 10,
})

scores.groupby("group")["score"].agg(["mean", "std"]).round(2)

          mean   std
group               
Control  70.34  6.52
Pilot    77.18  3.35





With ten points a group, show every point rather than summarising them away:

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

means <- aggregate(score ~ group, scores, mean)

ggplot(scores, aes(group, score, colour = group)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.06, size = 2.4, alpha = 0.85, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_errorbar(data = means, aes(ymin = score, ymax = score),
                width = 0.3, linewidth = 0.8, colour = cqc_ink) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Control" = "#375071", "Pilot" = "#007EF5")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean patient experience score (0-100)") +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 14.1: Pilot practices score higher on average, but the groups overlap – the question is whether a gap this size survives the noise. Every practice is shown; the horizontal bar marks each group’s mean. Three control practices score above several pilot practices, and the control group is visibly more spread out.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK

apply_cqc_style()

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)  # jitter only, for legibility
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
for i, (name, vals, col) in enumerate(
    [("Control", control, "#375071"), ("Pilot", pilot, "#007EF5")]
):
    x = i + rng.uniform(-0.06, 0.06, len(vals))
    ax.scatter(x, vals, color=col, s=35, alpha=0.85)
    ax.hlines(vals.mean(), i - 0.15, i + 0.15, color=CQC_INK, linewidth=2)

ax.set_xticks([0, 1], ["Control", "Pilot"])
ax.set_ylabel("Mean patient experience score (0-100)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 14.2: Pilot practices score higher on average, but the groups overlap – the question is whether a gap this size survives the noise. Every practice is shown; the horizontal bar marks each group’s mean. Three control practices score above several pilot practices, and the control group is visibly more spread out.








The picture already carries most of the analysis: the pilot group sits higher on average, the groups overlap, and the control group is noticeably more variable – which is precisely why the test should be Welch’s.


Run the test and size the effect

RPython



# Welch two-sample t-test (R's default)
result <- t.test(pilot, control)
result


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  pilot and control
t = 2.9515, df = 13.449, p-value = 0.0109
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
  1.850356 11.829644
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y 
    77.18     70.34 

# Effect size: Cohen's d (pooled standard deviation)
s_pooled <- sqrt((var(pilot) * 9 + var(control) * 9) / 18)
d <- (mean(pilot) - mean(control)) / s_pooled
cat("Difference in means:", round(mean(pilot) - mean(control), 1),
    "points; Cohen's d:", round(d, 2), "\n")

Difference in means: 6.8 points; Cohen's d: 1.32 





from scipy import stats

# Welch two-sample t-test (equal_var=False)
result = stats.ttest_ind(pilot, control, equal_var=False)
ci = result.confidence_interval()
print(f"t = {result.statistic:.2f}, df = {result.df:.1f}, "
      f"p = {result.pvalue:.4f}")

t = 2.95, df = 13.4, p = 0.0109

print(f"95% CI for the difference: {ci.low:.2f} to {ci.high:.2f}")

95% CI for the difference: 1.85 to 11.83

# Effect size: Cohen's d (pooled standard deviation)
s_pooled = np.sqrt((pilot.var(ddof=1) * 9 + control.var(ddof=1) * 9) / 18)
d = (pilot.mean() - control.mean()) / s_pooled
print(f"Difference in means: {pilot.mean() - control.mean():.1f} points; "
      f"Cohen's d: {d:.2f}")

Difference in means: 6.8 points; Cohen's d: 1.32







Interpret
Lead with the estimate: pilot practices scored about 6.8 points higher on average, with a 95 per cent confidence interval from roughly 1.9 to 11.8 points. The interval excludes zero – equivalently, the test is significant (t ≈ 2.95, p ≈ 0.011) – so the difference is unlikely to be noise. But read the interval honestly: the data are consistent with anything from a modest 2-point gap to a substantial 12-point one. Cohen’s d ≈ 1.3 sounds impressive, yet with ten practices a side it is estimated as imprecisely as everything else here.
Then temper the causal reading, as the Rethinking box above argues: this establishes that pilot practices ended the year scoring higher, not that the programme caused it. Were the pilot sites selected at random, or did enthusiastic practices volunteer? Did they differ at baseline? A 6.8-point difference with this much honest context is a good case for a larger, better-designed evaluation – which is usually exactly what a pilot should earn.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.6 Many Comparisons: the Multiplicity Problem
Run one test at the conventional threshold and a true null has a 5 per cent chance of a false alarm. Run twenty – twenty indicators, twenty subgroups, twenty regions – and the chance that at least one comes up “significant” by luck alone is about 64 per cent. Multiplicity is how diligent, well-intentioned analysis manufactures findings out of noise, and two-group comparisons are especially prone to it because each one is so easy to run.
The defences, in the order to reach for them:

Pre-specify. Name the primary comparison before touching the data; everything else is exploratory and labelled as such. This is the cheapest and strongest defence.
Report everything. All comparisons run, not just the survivors – a “significant” result means little without the denominator of attempts behind it.
Correct formally where verdicts matter. The Bonferroni correction divides the significance threshold by the number of tests (20 tests → 0.05/20 = 0.0025 each): simple, defensible, and conservative – with many tests it can drown real effects. The Benjamini–Hochberg procedure controls the false discovery rate instead – the expected share of flagged results that are false – which suits screening contexts where some false alarms are tolerable but a flood is not. Choosing between them, and counting what the “family” of tests even is, is worth a Guild conversation when the stakes are real.

In routine profiling across many indicators, recall the corroboration posture from z-scoring: require signals to agree across sources before acting. Formal corrections are for the designed, verdict-bearing analyses – evaluations, published comparisons – where a stated error rate is part of the claim.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: when the assumptions fail



Non-normal data. With small samples and visible skew, two routes exist. A transformation (log is the workhorse for right-skewed times and rates) often restores enough symmetry, at the price of interpreting results on the transformed scale – “a 15 per cent multiplicative difference” rather than “3 days”. Or switch to the rank-based alternatives: Mann–Whitney U for two independent groups, Wilcoxon signed-rank for paired data. They trade a little power for robustness, and note they test a subtly different hypothesis – whether one group’s values tend to exceed the other’s, not whether means differ – which is often closer to the regulatory question anyway.
Outliers. A single wild value can swamp a ten-observation t-test. Do not delete it to rescue significance: investigate it, report results with and without it, and if the two disagree, say so – that disagreement is the finding.
Clustered data. No alternative test fixes broken independence. Patients within practices, residents within homes, repeated months within a provider – these need the right unit of analysis (aggregate to one value per cluster, as the worked example does) or multilevel models (Guild). A t-test on clustered observations is not conservative or approximate; it is wrong, and wrong in the flattering direction.
Equivalence is not a failed difference test. “No significant difference” never demonstrates that two groups are the same – the absence-of-evidence trap again. If sameness is the actual claim (a cheaper service performs no worse, say), that is equivalence or non-inferiority testing, which reverses the burden of proof and needs its own design. Raise it with the Guild before promising anyone a “no difference” conclusion.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.7 Documenting Your Analysis
For QA purposes, record: the question and the two groups (or target), with the unit of analysis stated; the flavour of test and why; the assumption checks and what they showed; every comparison run, including the non-significant ones; the estimate with its confidence interval, the test statistics, and the effect size; and the practical interpretation, including the limits on any causal reading. The Code of Practice’s sound-methods obligations are satisfied by exactly this trail – and a pilot evaluation that may drive investment decisions deserves nothing less.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.8 Related Approaches

Comparing many providers at once → z-scoring and funnel plots; running a t-test per provider is the multiplicity problem in costume.
Following one provider or system over time → statistical process control; a before/after question with an ongoing data stream is usually better answered by a chart with frozen baseline limits.
Relationships between two continuous variables → correlation.
Foundations → statistical inference for p-values, intervals, and the error trade-off; distribution shape for skewness and transformation.
Complete workflows → Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot (paired) and Example 5: Regional A&E Performance (two-sample).

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
section: 14.9 When to Seek Support from the Guild
text:
14.9 When to Seek Support from the Guild
Raise your analysis with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) at a Quant Clinic session when: you need more than two groups (ANOVA), adjustment for confounders (regression), or repeated measures (multilevel models); the data are clustered and aggregation to one value per cluster loses the question; you need a power calculation before a pilot; the claim is equivalence rather than difference; the comparisons multiply and you need a correction strategy; or assumptions fail and you are unsure which alternative answers the question actually asked.

title: 14  Comparing Two Groups: t-tests
text:
14.10 Further Reading

Internal CQC resources

The QA Framework sets the documentation standards for comparative analyses; see How to Use This Book for how to access support.



Comparison of means and its assumptions

Altman, D.G. (1991). Practical Statistics for Medical Research. Chapman & Hall. (Chapter 9 remains the standard treatment of comparing groups in health data.)
HM Treasury (2015). The Aqua Book: guidance on producing quality analysis for government. (Uncertainty and verification expectations for government analysis.)



Estimation, significance, and multiplicity

Cumming, G. (2014). “The new statistics: why and how.” Psychological Science, 25(1), 7–29. (The estimate-first reporting posture this chapter follows.)
Greenland, S. et al. (2016). “Statistical tests, P values, confidence intervals, and power: a guide to misinterpretations.” European Journal of Epidemiology, 31, 337–350.
Wasserstein, R.L. & Lazar, N.A. (2016). “The ASA’s statement on p-values: context, process, and purpose.” The American Statistician, 70(2), 129–133.
Bland, J.M. & Altman, D.G. (1995). “Multiple significance tests: the Bonferroni method.” BMJ, 310(6973), 170.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.1 Putting a Number on “They Move Together”
The chapter on data visualisation left a promise unpaid: its four scatter plots of identical summary statistics spoke of “the same strength of straight-line relationship” and deferred what that meant. This is where it is made precise. Pearson’s correlation coefficient \(r\) measures how tightly two variables follow a straight line together, on a scale from −1 to +1: at +1 the points lie exactly on a rising line, at −1 exactly on a falling one, and at 0 knowing one variable tells you nothing (linear) about the other. The sign is direction; the absolute value is strength; a correlation of −0.7 is exactly as strong as +0.7.
The construction is worth seeing, because it connects two ideas the book has already built. Convert each variable to z-scores – each value’s distance from its own mean, in its own standard deviations – and \(r\) is simply the average product of the paired z-scores:
\[r = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i} z_{x,i} \, z_{y,i}\]
In words: for each observation, multiply “how unusual it is on x” by “how unusual it is on y”, and average. When high-on-x reliably comes with high-on-y, the products are mostly positive and \(r\) climbs; when the pairings are haphazard, positives and negatives cancel and \(r\) sits near zero. Correlation is standardised co-movement – which is also why it is unitless and why it cannot care about how much access improves per decile of deprivation, only how consistently.
Squaring the coefficient gives the proportion of variation the two variables share: \(r = 0.5\) means \(r^2 = 0.25\), a quarter of the variation in one accounted for by a straight-line relationship with the other – and three-quarters not. Keeping \(r^2\) in view is the quickest cure for over-reading: correlations that sound impressive often share strikingly little.
One number cannot carry a relationship’s shape, though – that was the whole moral of the four scatter plots. The same \(r\) can come from a clean linear trend, a curve, or a single extreme point doing all the work. So the first rule of correlation has nothing to do with formulas: look at the scatter plot first, every time.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
Pearson or Spearman
Spearman’s rank correlation \(\rho\) applies the same machinery to the ranks of the values rather than the values themselves. That single change buys three things: it measures any consistently increasing or decreasing (monotonic) relationship, straight or curved; it shrugs off outliers, since the most extreme value is just “rank 1” however extreme it is; and it is the honest choice for ordinal data – one rung down the measurement ladder – such as CQC ratings or deprivation deciles, where distances between categories are not really quantities.
Choose accordingly. Pearson when both variables are genuinely quantitative, the scatter plot looks linear, and nothing extreme is in play – and whenever the specifically linear relationship is what a downstream calculation needs. Spearman when either variable is ordinal, the relationship is monotonic but bent, or outliers are present. For exploratory work, if in doubt, Spearman: it rarely misleads, which is the property you want while exploring. Reporting both is cheap and itself informative – when they roughly agree, the relationship is close to linear and nobody extreme is steering it; when they diverge, the scatter plot will show you why.
How big is “big”? Treat every published banding of correlation strength with suspicion: what counts as strong is a property of the field, not of the number. In complex social and health data, where outcomes have many causes, an \(r\) of 0.4 between two provider-level measures can be a genuinely substantial finding; in a physical measurement context the same value would suggest broken equipment. The question is never “does it clear 0.5?” but “is a relationship of this size large enough to matter for the decision in front of us?” – the same significance-versus-importance discipline as everywhere else, with the extra step that \(r^2\) converts the coefficient into plain shares of variation.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.2 Before You Start
Have a question, not a fishing rod. Correlation is cheap to compute, which is its main danger: a dataset of twenty variables offers 190 pairs, and at conventional thresholds about nine or ten of them will look “significant” by luck alone. Decide which relationships you have reason to examine – a mechanism you can articulate, a policy question, a claim to check – before computing anything, and treat anything found by browsing as a hypothesis for fresh data, not a finding. The arithmetic of this trap, and the corrections for when verdicts matter, live in the multiplicity section of the t-tests chapter.
Data requirements. Two variables measured on the same units, quantitative or ordinal; a reasonable number of observations (below about 30 the coefficient swings wildly from sample to sample); each unit appearing once (repeated measures of the same providers over time violate independence – Guild territory); and genuine variation in both variables, because a variable that barely varies has nothing to correlate.
When correlation is not the tool. Comparing providers against a benchmark → z-scoring. Monitoring change over time → statistical process control. Two categorical variables (rating by provider type) → chi-squared methods, with the Guild. Predicting one variable from several, or adjusting for confounders → regression, with the Guild.
QA framework alignment. Correlation work most directly supports QA question 2 (Design – is the analytical approach right for the question?) and question 3 (Analysis). Document the method choice, outlier handling, and – above all – the alternative explanations considered.
Worked example for this chapter. Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes runs the full workflow on nurse staffing and pressure ulcer rates, confounders and all.
The code below uses pseudo-randomly generated data. The Python tabs read the R-generated dataset via r.gp_access so both languages show identical numbers; commented-out code shows how to generate the data in Python independently.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.3 Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Plot the scatter, and read it
One variable per axis, one point per unit. Read four things off it before any number: the overall shape (linear, curved, none); outliers (points far from the cloud – investigate them before they steer the coefficient); clusters (two distinct groups may need analysing separately, or may be the finding); and the variation available (a compressed range on either axis will mute any correlation – more on that below).


Step 2: Choose the coefficient
From the shape and the data types, per the guidance above. Note the choice and the reason – it is a method decision like any other.


Step 3: Calculate, with uncertainty
Report the coefficient, the sample size, and a confidence interval – with 100 observations an \(r\) of 0.5 carries an interval of roughly ±0.15, and with 20 observations the same \(r\) is little more than a gesture. Software supplies the interval for Pearson directly; the Overthinking box covers where it comes from and what to do for Spearman.


Step 4: Interpret all four dimensions
Strength (the coefficient, and \(r^2\) for shares of variation); direction (does the sign make sense against domain knowledge? a “wrong-signed” correlation is often the most informative kind); significance (does the interval exclude zero – and remember what that does and does not license); and context (what, other than the tempting causal story, could produce this association?).


Step 5: Report so the reader cannot over-read
A complete report names the method, the units and sample size, the coefficient with its interval, shows the scatter plot, and states in words both what the relationship is and what it is not – at minimum, that association is not causation, and which confounders are live candidates. The worked example below ends with a model paragraph.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
section: 15.4 Worked Example: Deprivation and GP Access
text:
15.4 Worked Example: Deprivation and GP Access
Scenario: you are examining whether GP practices in more deprived areas report worse patient access. For 100 practices you hold the area’s Index of Multiple Deprivation decile (1 = most deprived, 10 = least deprived) and the percentage of patients reporting they could get an appointment when they needed one, from the GP Patient Survey.
This is a contextual question – understanding how access relates to circumstances – not a performance comparison, and that framing matters for what the answer can be used for.

Generate and plot the data

RPython



# Simulated data for illustration (seed for reproducibility).
# Access improves with decile (less deprived = better access) plus noise.
set.seed(42)
n <- 100
gp_access <- data.frame(
  practice_id = sprintf("GP%03d", 1:n),
  imd_decile  = sample(1:10, n, replace = TRUE)
)
gp_access$access_pct <- round(
  70 + 1.5 * gp_access$imd_decile + rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 7), 1
)

head(gp_access, 3)

  practice_id imd_decile access_pct
1       GP001          1       80.6
2       GP002          5       79.9
3       GP003          1       78.8





import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Use the R-generated data so both tabs show identical numbers
gp_access = r.gp_access

# To generate independently in Python instead:
# rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# imd = rng.integers(1, 11, 100)
# gp_access = pd.DataFrame({
#     "practice_id": [f"GP{i:03d}" for i in range(1, 101)],
#     "imd_decile": imd,
#     "access_pct": np.round(70 + 1.5 * imd + rng.normal(0, 7, 100), 1),
# })

gp_access.head(3)

  practice_id  imd_decile  access_pct
0       GP001           1        80.6
1       GP002           5        79.9
2       GP003           1        78.8






RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(gp_access, aes(imd_decile, access_pct)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, colour = "#007EF5", size = 2, alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10) +
  labs(x = "Deprivation decile (1 = most deprived, 10 = least deprived)",
       y = "Patients able to get an appointment (%)") +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 15.1: Access rises with affluence, but deprivation is nowhere near the whole story. Each point is a practice; the cloud climbs from the most deprived areas (decile 1) to the least (decile 10), yet practices spread across roughly twenty percentage points of access at every level of deprivation.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style

apply_cqc_style()

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)  # horizontal jitter only, for legibility
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))
ax.scatter(gp_access["imd_decile"] + rng.uniform(-0.15, 0.15, len(gp_access)),
           gp_access["access_pct"], color="#007EF5", s=25, alpha=0.75)
ax.set_xticks(range(1, 11))
ax.set_xlabel("Deprivation decile (1 = most deprived, 10 = least deprived)")
ax.set_ylabel("Patients able to get an appointment (%)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 15.2: Access rises with affluence, but deprivation is nowhere near the whole story. Each point is a practice; the cloud climbs from the most deprived areas (decile 1) to the least (decile 10), yet practices spread across roughly twenty percentage points of access at every level of deprivation.








The shape is roughly linear, no point stands apart from the cloud, and the spread within each decile is large. Expect a moderate positive coefficient, not a dramatic one.


Calculate both coefficients
The deprivation decile is ordinal, which argues for Spearman; the scatter looks linear and well-behaved, which permits Pearson. Run both – agreement is itself a check.

RPython



pearson  <- cor.test(gp_access$imd_decile, gp_access$access_pct)
spearman <- cor.test(gp_access$imd_decile, gp_access$access_pct,
                     method = "spearman", exact = FALSE)

cat("Pearson r:", round(pearson$estimate, 2),
    " 95% CI:", round(pearson$conf.int[1], 2), "to",
    round(pearson$conf.int[2], 2), "\n")

Pearson r: 0.52  95% CI: 0.36 to 0.65 

cat("Spearman rho:", round(spearman$estimate, 2), "\n")

Spearman rho: 0.51 

cat("Shared variation r^2:", round(pearson$estimate^2, 2), "\n")

Shared variation r^2: 0.27 





from scipy import stats

pearson = stats.pearsonr(gp_access["imd_decile"], gp_access["access_pct"])
ci = pearson.confidence_interval()
rho, _ = stats.spearmanr(gp_access["imd_decile"], gp_access["access_pct"])

print(f"Pearson r: {pearson.statistic:.2f}"
      f"  95% CI: {ci.low:.2f} to {ci.high:.2f}")

Pearson r: 0.52  95% CI: 0.36 to 0.65

print(f"Spearman rho: {rho:.2f}")

Spearman rho: 0.51

print(f"Shared variation r^2: {pearson.statistic**2:.2f}")

Shared variation r^2: 0.27







Interpret and report
The two coefficients agree (r ≈ 0.52, ρ ≈ 0.51), confirming what the plot showed: a roughly linear, moderately strong association not driven by any single point. A reporting paragraph that resists over-reading:

Across 100 GP practices, patient-reported access was moderately associated with area deprivation (Pearson’s r = 0.52, 95% CI 0.36 to 0.65; Spearman’s ρ = 0.51): practices in less deprived areas tended to report better access. Deprivation accounts for about a quarter of the variation in access (r² = 0.27) – practices vary widely at every level of deprivation, so local factors clearly matter at least as much. This is an association in cross-sectional data: it does not show that deprivation causes poor access, and plausible third factors (workforce availability, list pressure, premises) could contribute to both.

Note what the paragraph does for a regulator: it contextualises – access expectations should not be blind to circumstance – without excusing, because the wide scatter within every decile is exactly the evidence that providers in deprived areas can and do achieve good access.






Note🤔 Rethinking: what a correlation licenses a regulator to do



Suppose the analysis above is right: deprivation and access are genuinely associated. What follows for regulatory practice? Less than it seems, and the discipline of saying exactly what is the point of this box.
It does not follow that a poorly-performing practice in a deprived area is excused – the within-decile spread shows good access being achieved in the most deprived areas, and those practices would be wronged by a blanket allowance. It does not follow that deprivation causes poor access, so it does not follow that any particular adjustment or intervention will work. And it certainly does not follow that an individual deprived patient struggles to get an appointment – the analysis lives at practice level, and reading group-level associations as if they described individuals is the ecological fallacy, one of the oldest traps in area-based statistics.
What a robust association legitimately buys is narrower and still valuable: it informs interpretation (a flag concentrated in deprived areas may partly reflect circumstance, so look closer before concluding), it targets support and attention (if access problems cluster with deprivation, so should help), and it generates hypotheses for properly designed investigation. Association evidence steers where to look and how hard; it does not, by itself, justify treating any provider differently. That distinction – context for judgement, never a substitute for it – is the regulatory version of “correlation is not causation”.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.5 The Ways Correlations Lie

A single point can invent one
Pearson’s r is built from products of distances, so one extreme point – extreme on both axes at once – contributes an enormous product and can conjure a relationship out of nothing. The pair of panels below is the same set of forty unrelated measurements; the right panel adds one fabricated extreme value.








Figure 15.3: One extreme point manufactures a correlation. Left: forty unrelated provider measurements, Pearson r = −0.02. Right: the same forty points plus a single extreme value – r jumps to 0.41, while rank-based Spearman barely moves (ρ = 0.12). If the coefficient changes when one point is removed, the point – not the relationship – is the finding to investigate.





The defence is the routine you already have: see the point on the scatter plot, investigate it, and report the correlation with and without it. Spearman’s robustness shows clearly here – which is a reason to check with Spearman, not a licence to skip the investigation: the extreme point may be the most important observation in the dataset.


Restricted ranges mute real relationships
A correlation needs variation to measure. Analyse only the practices in the three most deprived deciles and the deprivation–access correlation largely vanishes – not because the relationship is absent, but because deprivation barely varies in the subset, leaving noise to dominate. Range restriction haunts regulatory data in a specific way: any analysis confined to flagged providers, or to one rating band, has pre-compressed the very variation it then tries to correlate. State the range your data covers, and resist generalising beyond it.


Aggregation can reverse the story
Simpson’s paradox is the unsettling fact that a correlation can hold in every subgroup and reverse when the subgroups are pooled – nursing homes and residential homes might each show no staffing–incidents relationship, while the combined dataset shows a strong one, simply because the two types differ in both staffing levels and incident rates. Whenever the units plausibly belong to different families (provider types, sectors, regions), plot the groups distinguishably and check the within-group pattern before trusting the pooled coefficient.


Hunting finds fool’s gold
The multiplicity arithmetic from the t-tests chapter applies with extra force here, because correlation matrices generate comparisons quadratically. Twenty variables yield 190 coefficients; even pure noise hands you nine or ten “significant” ones. Pre-specify, report the full set examined, and treat browsed discoveries as hypotheses awaiting fresh data.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: the machinery under the coefficient



Where the confidence interval comes from. The sampling distribution of r is awkward – squeezed against the −1/+1 boundaries – so the standard interval works on Fisher’s transformation \(z' = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\frac{1+r}{1-r}\), which is approximately normal with standard error \(1/\sqrt{n-3}\); build the interval there and transform back. The \(\sqrt{n-3}\) is the practical message: halving the interval’s width takes roughly four times the data, and below n ≈ 30 the interval is wide enough that the coefficient’s second decimal is decoration. R’s cor.test does this for Pearson automatically; for Spearman no equally simple formula exists, and bootstrap intervals are the usual answer (Guild, if it matters to the conclusion).
Attenuation: noisy measures dilute every correlation. If x and y are measured with error – and every CQC indicator is a noisy proxy for the thing it tracks – the observed correlation is biased towards zero, in proportion to the measures’ unreliability. Two consequences. A modest observed r between, say, a staffing proxy and a harm proxy may understate the true underlying relationship considerably. And correlations between changes (this year minus last year) are hit hardest, since differencing two noisy numbers compounds the noise – year-on-year change scores correlate weakly with almost everything, a fact regularly misread as “nothing matters”.
Non-linear but real. A U-shaped relationship – risk high at both extremes of, say, occupancy – can produce r ≈ 0 and ρ ≈ 0 while being the most important pattern in the data. No coefficient in this chapter detects it; only the scatter plot does, which is the visualise-first rule earning its keep one final time. Modelling curvature is regression territory, with the Guild.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.6 Documenting Your Analysis
For QA purposes, record: the question and why a relationship analysis answers it; the method (Pearson, Spearman, or both) and the reason; the scatter plot, retained as evidence; outlier handling, with results shown with and without any influential points; the coefficient, interval, and sample size; the full set of correlations examined, not just the reportable ones; and the alternative explanations considered, with the causal claims you are not making stated explicitly. The chapter on quality assurance holds the templates.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.7 Related Approaches

Comparing providers against a benchmark → z-scoring and funnel plots.
Two groups’ means rather than two variables’ co-movement → t-tests.
Relationships among three or more variables, prediction, or confounder adjustment → regression and partial correlation, with the Guild.
Two categorical variables (ratings by provider type) → chi-squared association methods, with the Guild.
Foundations → data visualisation for the scatter plot and the four-clouds warning; data types for the ordinal/quantitative distinction that drives the Pearson/Spearman choice; statistical inference for intervals and significance.
Complete workflow → Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
section: 15.8 When to Seek Support from the Guild
text:
15.8 When to Seek Support from the Guild
Raise your analysis with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) at a Quant Clinic session when: a causal question is actually on the table (programme effects, “does staffing drive harm?”) – observational causal inference is a specialism, not a stretch goal; confounding needs adjusting for rather than acknowledging; the relationship is visibly non-linear; the data are hierarchical (patients in practices, practices in systems) or repeated over time; the outcome is categorical; or a Spearman interval, partial correlation, or correlation-matrix-wide error control would change the conclusion.

title: 15  Exploring Relationships: Correlation
text:
15.9 Further Reading

Internal CQC resources

The QA Framework sets the documentation standards for relationship analyses; see How to Use This Book for how to access support.



Correlation and its interpretation

Altman, D.G. (1991). Practical Statistics for Medical Research. Chapman & Hall. (Chapter 11: the standard treatment of correlation and regression in health data, including Fisher’s transformation.)
Anscombe, F.J. (1973). “Graphs in statistical analysis.” The American Statistician, 27(1), 17–21. (The four datasets behind the visualise-first rule.)
Field, A. (2013). Discovering Statistics Using R. Sage. (Accessible chapter-length coverage of Pearson, Spearman, and their assumptions.)
HM Treasury (2015). The Aqua Book: guidance on producing quality analysis for government.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.1 From Lens to Rule
The chapter on unusual observations did the thinking: “unusual” has no universal definition; a value stands out for one of three reasons (a data error, a genuine extreme, or mixed populations) and each calls for a different response; the line you draw is a choice to declare, not a fact to discover; and the default treatment is to investigate and keep, never to silently delete. This chapter assumes all of that and adds the machinery – the rules that apply those lenses to eight hundred providers as readily as to eight, identically every time they run.
Mechanising the judgement matters for exactly the reasons hand-inspection fails. A rule is reproducible: a colleague running your code flags the same providers, which is what makes a flagged list defensible when a provider asks why they are on it. A rule scales: national datasets cannot be read point by point. And a rule is declarable in advance, which is the discipline that stops threshold-shopping – the researcher degree of freedom ch06 warned about – before it starts. What a rule is not is a judgement: every method below produces candidates for investigation, nothing more.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.2 Before You Start
Read the conceptual chapter first. The methods here are easy to run and easy to misuse; unusual observations supplies the questions that make their output meaningful – above all why is this value unusual?, asked before anything is done about it.
Data requirements. A quantitative variable, one value per unit. Twenty or more observations before any of the rules below is worth much – with fewer, the summary statistics the rules are built on are themselves too uncertain.
When these methods are the wrong tool. Values arriving over time belong with statistical process control, which respects the time order these methods ignore. Comparing providers against a benchmark where natural variation depends on size belongs with z-scoring and funnel plots – a small provider’s wild rate is often precision, not deviance, and the methods below do not know the difference. And a clearly two-humped distribution is usually mixed populations: stratify first, or the rules will flag one whole group as outliers from the other.
QA framework alignment. Detection work most directly supports QA question 3 (Analysis – has the data been appropriately checked?) and question 5 (Fitness for Purpose – are results defensible given data quality?). The documentation section below sets out the record to keep.
Worked example for this chapter. Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation runs the full multi-method workflow with the investigation and treatment decisions documented end to end.
The code below uses pseudo-randomly generated data. The Python tabs read the R-generated dataset via r.care_homes so both languages show identical numbers; commented-out code shows how to generate the data in Python independently.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.3 Three Detectors, Three Personalities
Each rule answers “how far from the middle is too far?” with different definitions of middle and far – and those choices give each a distinct failure mode worth knowing before trusting any of them.
The classic z-score rule flags values more than a chosen number of standard deviations from the mean – \(|x - \bar{x}|/s\) beyond 2.5, or 3 for a stricter line. It is familiar, quantitative, and carries one structural flaw for this particular job: the mean and especially the standard deviation are computed from the very data being screened, so the outliers being hunted inflate the yardstick used to hunt them. One monstrous value can widen \(s\) enough to hide both itself and every lesser extreme – an effect called masking, and not a rare curiosity: the worked example below shows the classic rule missing every impossibly low value in the dataset for exactly this reason.
The modified z-score (Iglewicz and Hoaglin’s robust replacement) swaps in summaries that extremes cannot corrupt: distance from the median, scaled by the median absolute deviation – the median of the absolute distances from the median, a robust stand-in for the standard deviation:
\[M_i = \frac{0.6745\,(x_i - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}\]
where \(\tilde{x}\) is the median and the 0.6745 calibrates \(M\) to read like a z-score when the data are normal. The conventional flag is \(|M| > 3.5\). Because median and MAD barely move when the extremes do, the yardstick stays honest no matter how wild the values being screened – the property the classic rule lacks. (One implementation trap with library MAD functions is real and lurking; the Overthinking box below defuses it.)
Tukey’s fences – the IQR rule, the one box plots apply silently – flags anything below \(Q_1 - 1.5\,\text{IQR}\) or above \(Q_3 + 1.5\,\text{IQR}\). Built entirely from quantiles, it is immune to the extremes by construction; its personality flaws run the other way – it flags generously on skewed data, and the share it flags drifts with sample size, both points the conceptual chapter develops.
No detector is the correct one, so do not choose – run all three and read their agreement. A value flagged by all three is unusual by any reasonable definition and heads the queue; flagged by two, high priority; flagged by one, quite possibly an artefact of that method’s personality – the classic rule’s normality habit, the fences’ generosity with skew. Agreement converts three imperfect rules into one usable triage.






Note🤔 Rethinking: disagreement is information



The instinct, on seeing three methods flag three different lists, is to ask which method is right. The better question is what the disagreement is telling you, because each mismatch has a typical cause and the cause is more useful than the flag.
When the classic z-score flags far fewer values than the robust methods, suspect masking: a handful of severe extremes have inflated the standard deviation and are shielding themselves and others. The robust lists are the ones to trust – and the severe extremes are probably your data errors. When the fences flag far more than both z-rules, look at the shape: on a long-tailed distribution the fences read ordinary tail values as stragglers, and what you may actually need is a stratified comparison or a transformed scale, not a longer investigation list. And when a value is flagged by exactly one method, the flag is usually telling you about the method – which is precisely why a single-rule screening pipeline, however tidy, quietly inherits a single personality’s blind spots.
The regulatory habit this builds: report which methods flagged each value, not just that it was flagged. “Flagged by all three rules” and “flagged only by the fences on a skewed indicator” should start very different conversations – and documenting the difference is what makes the queue defensible when a provider asks why they were on it.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.4 Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Look before you compute
A histogram and a box plot, every time: shape, skew, how many candidates, in which tail, isolated or clustered. The methods below should confirm and order what the picture shows, and surprises in either direction deserve attention.


Step 2: Run all three detectors
Compute the classic z-scores, the modified z-scores, and the fences, with thresholds stated in advance (the conventions – 2.5 or 3, 3.5, 1.5 × IQR – are defensible defaults; just declare which you used).


Step 3: Count the agreement
Sum the flags per unit. Sort descending. That ordering is the investigation queue.


Step 4: Investigate down the queue
For each flagged unit, the three origins from ch06 are the checklist: error? – impossible value, wrong units, missing-data code, wrong provider; genuine extreme? – verify against other periods, related indicators, what is known about the provider; mixed populations? – is this unit being compared with the wrong peers? Stop when the queue’s remaining members were flagged by a single method and the first checks find nothing – screening budgets are finite, which is what the ordering is for.


Step 5: Decide treatment, and show your working
The decisions are ch06’s, applied case by case: keep and document (the default), robust summaries, transformation, removal as the evidenced last resort – with a sensitivity analysis whenever a treatment choice could move the conclusion. Detection adds only one new obligation: because the flags came from declared rules, the non-flags are also a statement, so record the rules and thresholds alongside the decisions.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
section: 16.5 Worked Example: Care Home Staffing Ratios
text:
16.5 Worked Example: Care Home Staffing Ratios
Scenario: 80 care homes have submitted staffing returns – qualified staff per 10 residents – and you need to screen them before the main analysis. Because the data are simulated, we know the truth the methods are trying to recover: 65 typical homes around 3.2 staff per 10 residents; five specialist high-dependency units genuinely staffing around 5.5; three genuinely understaffed homes around 1.5; and seven planted problem values – impossible entries (0, 0.02, 0.05), two figures that look like rates entered per 100 residents rather than per 10 (9.8, 11.2), and two high but genuine specialist returns (7.2, 8.5).

Set up and look

RPython



# Simulated staffing returns (seed for reproducibility).
# Planted truth: 65 typical homes, 5 specialist (high), 3 understaffed (low),
# then 7 problem values - 3 impossible, 2 suspected unit errors, 2 genuine.
set.seed(123)
care_homes <- data.frame(
  home_id = sprintf("CH%03d", 1:80),
  staff_per_10 = pmax(0, c(
    rnorm(65, mean = 3.2, sd = 0.7),
    rnorm(5,  mean = 5.5, sd = 0.3),
    rnorm(3,  mean = 1.5, sd = 0.2),
    c(0.02, 0.05, 9.8, 11.2, 0, 8.5, 7.2)
  ))
)

summary(care_homes$staff_per_10)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  0.000   2.752   3.215   3.481   3.794  11.200 





import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Use the R-generated data so both tabs show identical numbers
care_homes = r.care_homes

# To generate independently in Python instead:
# rng = np.random.default_rng(123)
# vals = np.concatenate([
#     rng.normal(3.2, 0.7, 65), rng.normal(5.5, 0.3, 5),
#     rng.normal(1.5, 0.2, 3),
#     [0.02, 0.05, 9.8, 11.2, 0, 8.5, 7.2],
# ])
# care_homes = pd.DataFrame({
#     "home_id": [f"CH{i:03d}" for i in range(1, 81)],
#     "staff_per_10": np.maximum(0, vals),
# })

care_homes["staff_per_10"].describe().round(2)

count    80.00
mean      3.48
std       1.75
min       0.00
25%       2.75
50%       3.21
75%       3.79
max      11.20
Name: staff_per_10, dtype: float64






RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(care_homes, aes(staff_per_10)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.4, fill = "#99C9F7", colour = "white",
                 linewidth = 0.2, boundary = 0) +
  labs(x = "Qualified staff per 10 residents", y = "Number of homes") +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 16.1: The bulk of homes cluster around three staff per ten residents – and both tails hold values the bulk cannot explain. A group of near-zero returns sits at the far left, a scatter of returns above seven at the far right, with the genuine specialist units in between. The screening methods now have to find what the eye already suspects.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style

apply_cqc_style()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.hist(care_homes["staff_per_10"],
        bins=np.arange(0, 12, 0.4), color="#99C9F7", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("Qualified staff per 10 residents")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of homes")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 16.2: The bulk of homes cluster around three staff per ten residents – and both tails hold values the bulk cannot explain. A group of near-zero returns sits at the far left, a scatter of returns above seven at the far right, with the genuine specialist units in between. The screening methods now have to find what the eye already suspects.










Run the three detectors

RPython



x <- care_homes$staff_per_10

# Classic z-score: mean/standard-deviation distance, flag |z| > 2.5
care_homes$z <- (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
care_homes$flag_z <- abs(care_homes$z) > 2.5

# Modified z-score (Iglewicz & Hoaglin): median/MAD distance, flag |M| > 3.5.
# NOTE: R's mad() is already scaled to mimic the standard deviation
# (constant = 1.4826), so use the raw MAD (constant = 1) with the 0.6745
# multiplier - applying both scalings is a common bug that under-flags.
raw_mad <- mad(x, constant = 1)
care_homes$mod_z <- 0.6745 * (x - median(x)) / raw_mad
care_homes$flag_modz <- abs(care_homes$mod_z) > 3.5

# Tukey's fences: quartiles +/- 1.5 IQR
q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25); q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75); iqr <- q3 - q1
fence_lo <- q1 - 1.5 * iqr
fence_hi <- q3 + 1.5 * iqr
care_homes$flag_iqr <- x < fence_lo | x > fence_hi

cat("Classic z flags:", sum(care_homes$flag_z),
    "- modified z flags:", sum(care_homes$flag_modz),
    "- fences flag:", sum(care_homes$flag_iqr), "\n")

Classic z flags: 3 - modified z flags: 8 - fences flag: 13 

cat("Fences: [", round(fence_lo, 2), ",", round(fence_hi, 2), "]\n")

Fences: [ 1.19 , 5.36 ]





from scipy.stats import median_abs_deviation

x = care_homes["staff_per_10"]

# Classic z-score: mean/standard-deviation distance, flag |z| > 2.5
care_homes["z"] = (x - x.mean()) / x.std()
care_homes["flag_z"] = care_homes["z"].abs() > 2.5

# Modified z-score (Iglewicz & Hoaglin): median/MAD distance, flag |M| > 3.5.
# NOTE: scipy's median_abs_deviation returns the RAW MAD by default,
# so the 0.6745 multiplier is required here (unlike R's pre-scaled mad()).
raw_mad = median_abs_deviation(x)
care_homes["mod_z"] = 0.6745 * (x - x.median()) / raw_mad
care_homes["flag_modz"] = care_homes["mod_z"].abs() > 3.5

# Tukey's fences: quartiles +/- 1.5 IQR
q1, q3 = x.quantile(0.25), x.quantile(0.75)
iqr = q3 - q1
fence_lo, fence_hi = q1 - 1.5 * iqr, q3 + 1.5 * iqr
care_homes["flag_iqr"] = (x < fence_lo) | (x > fence_hi)

print(f"Classic z flags: {care_homes['flag_z'].sum()}"
      f" - modified z flags: {care_homes['flag_modz'].sum()}"
      f" - fences flag: {care_homes['flag_iqr'].sum()}")

Classic z flags: 3 - modified z flags: 8 - fences flag: 13

print(f"Fences: [{fence_lo:.2f}, {fence_hi:.2f}]")

Fences: [1.19, 5.36]





The three counts disagree exactly as their personalities predict – and the classic z-score’s count is the masking effect in action. The planted extremes inflate the standard deviation so much that the rule’s lower threshold falls below zero: a home reporting literally no qualified staff cannot be flagged by the classic rule on this data. The three values it does flag are only the most extreme high returns. The robust methods, their yardsticks untouched by the extremes, see both tails.


Count the agreement and order the queue

RPython



care_homes$n_flags <- care_homes$flag_z + care_homes$flag_modz +
  care_homes$flag_iqr

table(`methods agreeing` = care_homes$n_flags)

methods agreeing
 0  1  2  3 
67  5  5  3 

queue <- care_homes[care_homes$n_flags >= 2,
                    c("home_id", "staff_per_10", "z", "mod_z", "n_flags")]
queue[order(-queue$n_flags, -abs(queue$mod_z)), ]

   home_id staff_per_10         z     mod_z n_flags
77   CH077    11.200000  4.398302 10.199009       3
76   CH076     9.800000  3.600531  8.410900       3
79   CH079     8.500000  2.859743  6.750513       3
80   CH080     7.200000  2.118955  5.090126       2
78   CH078     0.000000 -1.983872 -4.105865       2
74   CH074     0.020000 -1.972475 -4.080320       2
75   CH075     0.050000 -1.955380 -4.042004       2
70   CH070     6.115025  1.500696  3.704373       2





care_homes["n_flags"] = (
    care_homes["flag_z"].astype(int)
    + care_homes["flag_modz"].astype(int)
    + care_homes["flag_iqr"].astype(int)
)

print(care_homes["n_flags"].value_counts().sort_index())

n_flags
