Name: count, dtype: int64

queue = care_homes.loc[care_homes["n_flags"] >= 2,
                       ["home_id", "staff_per_10", "z", "mod_z", "n_flags"]]
queue.sort_values(["n_flags", "mod_z"], key=abs, ascending=False)

   home_id  staff_per_10         z      mod_z  n_flags
76   CH077     11.200000  4.398302  10.199009        3
75   CH076      9.800000  3.600531   8.410900        3
78   CH079      8.500000  2.859743   6.750513        3
79   CH080      7.200000  2.118955   5.090126        2
77   CH078      0.000000 -1.983872  -4.105865        2
73   CH074      0.020000 -1.972475  -4.080320        2
74   CH075      0.050000 -1.955380  -4.042004        2

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")
library(ggplot2)

care_homes$priority <- cut(care_homes$n_flags, c(-Inf, 0, 1, Inf),
                           labels = c("No method", "One method",
                                      "Two or three methods"))

set.seed(4)
ggplot(care_homes, aes(staff_per_10, y = 0)) +
  geom_jitter(aes(colour = priority, shape = priority, size = priority),
              height = 0.3, width = 0) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("No method" = "#6D6D6D",
                                 "One method" = "#A57703",
                                 "Two or three methods" = "#A83DA4"),
                      name = "Flagged by") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("No method" = 16, "One method" = 17,
                                "Two or three methods" = 15),
                     name = "Flagged by") +
  scale_size_manual(values = c("No method" = 1.6, "One method" = 2.4,
                               "Two or three methods" = 2.8),
                    name = "Flagged by") +
  labs(x = "Qualified staff per 10 residents", y = NULL) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "bottom")







Figure 16.3: Method agreement sorts eighty homes into a short, ordered queue. Sixty-seven homes are flagged by no method. Five – four specialist units and one low-staffed home – are flagged by a single method, the generous fences. The eight homes flagged by two or three methods are exactly the planted problem values plus the most extreme specialist: every impossible entry and suspected unit error makes the high-priority list.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style

apply_cqc_style()

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)  # vertical jitter only, for legibility
jitter = rng.uniform(-0.3, 0.3, len(care_homes))

styles = {
    0: dict(color="#6D6D6D", marker="o", s=18, label="No method"),
    1: dict(color="#A57703", marker="^", s=38, label="One method"),
    2: dict(color="#A83DA4", marker="s", s=46, label="Two or three methods"),
}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 3.4))
for level, style in styles.items():
    m = (care_homes["n_flags"] >= 2) if level == 2 else \
        (care_homes["n_flags"] == level)
    ax.scatter(care_homes.loc[m, "staff_per_10"], jitter[m.values],
               alpha=0.9, **style)

ax.set_xlabel("Qualified staff per 10 residents")
ax.set_yticks([])

[]

ax.legend(title="Flagged by", loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 16.4: Method agreement sorts eighty homes into a short, ordered queue. Sixty-seven homes are flagged by no method. Five – four specialist units and one low-staffed home – are flagged by a single method, the generous fences. The eight homes flagged by two or three methods are exactly the planted problem values plus the most extreme specialist: every impossible entry and suspected unit error makes the high-priority list.










Investigate, and close the loop
The queue works through the three origins home by home, and on this simulated data we can mark the screening against the truth. The three impossible entries (0, 0.02, 0.05 staff per 10 residents) are confirmed data errors – a home cannot run with no qualified staff – to be corrected from the source or set aside with a documented note. The two suspected unit errors (9.8, 11.2) go back to the providers; if they were entered per 100 residents, the corrected values rejoin the dataset. The high genuine returns (7.2, 8.5, and the 6.12 specialist) are kept and explained: they are specialist units, and the right long-term fix is a stratified comparison, not a deletion. The five single-method flags – four specialist units around 5.5 and one low-staffed home at 1.04 – were the fences being generous; the first checks find nothing alarming, and they stay, documented, in the dataset.
Notice the two things the screening did not do. It did not decide anything – every action above came from investigation, with the methods only setting the order of the queue. And it did not catch the three genuinely understaffed homes around 1.5 staff per 10 residents: they sit inside every rule’s limits, which is a reminder that statistical unusualness and regulatory concern are different properties – a domain threshold (“fewer than 2 qualified staff per 10 residents warrants a look”) belongs alongside the statistical rules, not instead of them.






Warning⚙️ Overthinking: the robust machinery, and a scaling trap



Why 0.6745, and the trap. For normal data the raw MAD – the median of absolute distances from the median – settles at about 0.6745 of the standard deviation. Multiplying by 0.6745 therefore rescales distance-over-MAD so the modified z-score reads like an ordinary z-score under normality, which is what makes “flag beyond 3.5” a sensible convention. The trap: software disagrees about whose job that rescaling is. R’s mad() already multiplies by 1.4826 (= 1/0.6745) so that its output mimics a standard deviation; scipy’s median_abs_deviation() returns the raw MAD. Apply the 0.6745 formula on top of R’s pre-scaled version and every modified z-score shrinks by a third – the screen silently under-flags, and the two language tabs of an analysis stop agreeing. Use mad(x, constant = 1) with the multiplier (as the code above does), or equivalently (x - median(x)) / mad(x) with none; check any inherited script for this bug before trusting its flags.
Thresholds and variants. The conventions – 2.5 or 3 for z, 3.5 for the modified score, 1.5 × IQR for the fences (3 × IQR for Tukey’s “far out” line) – are defensible defaults, not laws; what matters for QA is declaring them in advance. On skewed data the symmetric fences over-flag the long tail, and skew-adjusted fences exist (widening the fence on the tail side using a robust skewness measure); if an indicator is persistently skewed, though, the better first move is usually a transformed scale or a stratified comparison, which fixes the cause rather than the symptom.
Beyond one variable at a time. Everything in this chapter screens a single indicator. A provider can be unremarkable on every indicator separately and unusual in combination – moderately high incidents and moderately low staffing and moderately high complaints. Multivariate detectors exist for exactly this: Mahalanobis distance (distance from the multivariate centre, accounting for the correlations between indicators), and machine-learning approaches such as Isolation Forest and Local Outlier Factor that find points easy to separate from the crowd. They are powerful, sensitive to their settings, and easy to over-read – raise the need with the Guild rather than reaching for a library default.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.6 Documenting Your Analysis
For QA purposes, record: the methods run, with their thresholds, declared before results were seen; the flag counts per method and the agreement table; the investigation log – which units were examined, what was checked, what was found; every treatment decision with its justification, the dataset sizes before and after any exclusion, and a sensitivity analysis whenever treatment could move the conclusion; and the screening’s known limits – what the rules cannot catch (the understaffed-but-unflagged homes above) and any domain thresholds applied alongside them. The chapter on quality assurance holds the templates; “we removed five outliers” is, on its own, the sentence QA exists to prevent.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.7 Related Approaches

The thinking behind every step here → unusual observations: origins, line-drawing, treatment, the keep-by-default rule.
Size-aware screening of providers against a benchmark → z-scoring and funnel plots, where expected variation depends on the denominator.
Extremes arriving over time → statistical process control.
Outliers distorting a relationship → correlation, where a single point can manufacture an association.
Skew-driven flagging → distribution shape for transformation and what it trades away.
Complete workflow → Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
section: 16.8 When to Seek Support from the Guild
text:
16.8 When to Seek Support from the Guild
Raise your analysis with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) at a Quant Clinic session when: the methods disagree wildly and the data’s shape does not explain why; the screening is multivariate (unusual combinations rather than unusual values); the flagged list is too long to investigate and needs principled pruning; a treatment decision could materially affect regulatory action or faces stakeholder challenge; the data are hierarchical or spatial; or systematic flag patterns suggest a data-quality problem upstream rather than unusual providers downstream.

title: 16  Outlier Detection Methods
text:
16.9 Further Reading

Internal CQC resources

The QA Framework sets the documentation standards for outlier investigation and treatment; see How to Use This Book for how to access support.



Detection methods

Tukey, J.W. (1977). Exploratory Data Analysis. Addison-Wesley. (The box plot, the fences, and the exploratory stance behind them.)
Iglewicz, B. & Hoaglin, D.C. (1993). How to Detect and Handle Outliers. ASQC Quality Press. (The modified z-score and its 3.5 convention.)
Rousseeuw, P.J. & Hubert, M. (2011). “Robust statistics for outlier detection.” WIREs Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery, 1(1), 73–79. (Why detectors must be built from statistics the outliers cannot corrupt.)
NIST/SEMATECH e-Handbook of Statistical Methods, section on outlier detection.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
text:
17.1 From Reading Gaps to Handling Them
The foundations chapter did the thinking, and this chapter assumes all of it: the three mechanisms and the caution that they are a summary rather than a verdict; the two distinct harms, lost precision and bias; the hard fact that MAR versus MNAR cannot be settled from the data, only argued from evidence; and the regulatory inversion – that in CQC’s work the pattern of who is missing is often a finding in its own right, not an obstacle. What remains is the practical question that chapter deliberately deferred: how, concretely, in code, with the uncertainty kept honest.
One sentence from the foundations bears repeating before any code runs, because every mistake in this chapter’s territory traces back to forgetting it: the goal of handling missing data is valid estimates with honest uncertainty, not accurate predictions of the values that are gone. Lost data is lost. Nothing below recovers it; the methods differ only in how openly they admit that.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
text:
17.2 Before You Start
Read the foundations first. The methods here are easy to run and dangerously easy to run on autopilot; the foundations chapter supplies the questions – above all why is this missing? – that decide which method is even admissible.
Data requirements. Any dataset with gaps, which is to say any real dataset. The structured methods (imputation especially) additionally need observed variables that plausibly relate to the missing ones – auxiliary information is the fuel they run on, and without it they idle convincingly.
When these methods are not the point. When missingness is substantial and plausibly tied to the missing values themselves – the struggling providers not reporting – no computation below fixes that; the honest outputs are a sensitivity range and an investigation list, and the foundations chapter’s regulatory inversion is the frame to reach for.
QA framework alignment. Missing-data handling most directly supports QA question 1 (Data – is the data fit for the analysis?) and question 5 (Fitness for Purpose – are limitations declared?). The documentation this chapter ends with is a Code of Practice expectation, not a courtesy.
Worked examples for this chapter. Example 1: GP Access Analysis handles survey missingness inside a z-scoring workflow; the missing-data steps of every other worked example follow the pattern set here.
The code below uses pseudo-randomly generated data. The Python tabs read the R-generated dataset via r.care_homes so both languages show identical numbers; commented-out code shows how to generate the data in Python independently.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
text:
17.3 Step 1: Never Trust “0% Missing”
Before counting gaps, make sure the gaps are visible at all. Real datasets disguise missingness as values: a 9999 where a count should be, a 0 that means “not recorded” rather than “none”, an "Unknown" or "N/A" string, a date of 1900-01-01, a blank that one system exports as an empty string and another as the word NULL. A column that reports itself complete may simply have its gaps in costume – and every summary statistic computed over sentinel codes is corrupted in proportion.
The discipline is short and pays for itself every time: check the data dictionary for declared missing-value codes; tabulate each variable’s most frequent values and inspect anything suspiciously round, repeated, or out of range; check types (numbers arriving as text usually mean stowaway strings); and convert everything that means missing into an explicit NA/NaN before any counting starts. Only then does “how much is missing?” have a trustworthy answer.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
section: 17.4 Worked Example: Care Home Quality Returns
text:
17.4 Worked Example: Care Home Quality Returns
Scenario: 450 care homes should each have submitted three figures – a staffing rate, a resident satisfaction score, and their latest inspection score – alongside their size and region, which you hold for all of them. The returns have gaps. You need to map the missingness, judge its mechanism, and estimate national mean satisfaction without fooling yourself.
Because the data are simulated, the truth is planted and known: satisfaction genuinely rises with home size, and small homes are far more likely to fail to submit their satisfaction and staffing figures (with a little background missingness everywhere). That combination is the trap this chapter exists for – the missing homes differ systematically from the reporting ones, on a variable that also drives the outcome. We also know the true national mean satisfaction across all 450 homes, so every method below can be marked against it.

Set up the data

RPython



# Simulated returns (seed for reproducibility).
# Planted truth: satisfaction rises with size; small homes (< 40 beds) are
# much more likely to fail to submit satisfaction and staffing.
set.seed(123)
n <- 450
size <- sample(20:100, n, replace = TRUE)
satisfaction_full <- 60 + 0.25 * size + rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 8)
true_mean <- mean(satisfaction_full)   # the answer the methods must recover

miss_sat   <- (size < 40 & runif(n) < 0.45) | (runif(n) < 0.05)
miss_staff <- (size < 40 & runif(n) < 0.35) | (runif(n) < 0.04)
miss_insp  <- runif(n) < 0.06

care_homes <- data.frame(
  home_id       = sprintf("CH%03d", 1:n),
  size          = size,
  region        = sample(c("North", "Central", "London", "South"), n,
                         replace = TRUE),
  staffing_rate = ifelse(miss_staff, NA, rnorm(n, 3.2, 0.5)),
  satisfaction  = ifelse(miss_sat, NA, satisfaction_full),
  inspection    = ifelse(miss_insp, NA, rnorm(n, 78, 7))
)

colMeans(is.na(care_homes[, c("staffing_rate", "satisfaction", "inspection")]))

staffing_rate  satisfaction    inspection 
   0.12222222    0.13777778    0.06444444 

cat("True mean satisfaction (all 450 homes):", round(true_mean, 2), "\n")

True mean satisfaction (all 450 homes): 74.47 





import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Use the R-generated data so both tabs show identical numbers
care_homes = r.care_homes
true_mean = r.true_mean

# To generate independently in Python instead:
# rng = np.random.default_rng(123)
# size = rng.integers(20, 101, 450)
# satisfaction_full = 60 + 0.25 * size + rng.normal(0, 8, 450)
# true_mean = satisfaction_full.mean()
# miss_sat = ((size < 40) & (rng.random(450) < 0.45)) | (rng.random(450) < 0.05)
# ... and so on, mirroring the R construction above.

print(care_homes[["staffing_rate", "satisfaction", "inspection"]]
      .isna().mean().round(3))

staffing_rate    0.122
satisfaction     0.138
inspection       0.064
dtype: float64

print(f"True mean satisfaction (all 450 homes): {true_mean:.2f}")

True mean satisfaction (all 450 homes): 74.47





About 14 per cent of satisfaction scores are gone – enough to matter, not enough to be obviously fatal. The percentage alone, though, settles nothing: the next two steps ask where the gaps are and why.


Map the structure
Counting per variable hides the most useful information: which gaps travel together. An upset plot shows, for each combination of missing variables, how many records carry exactly that combination – the natural picture when several variables can each be present or absent.

RPython



library(UpSetR)

missing_flags <- data.frame(
  staffing     = as.integer(is.na(care_homes$staffing_rate)),
  satisfaction = as.integer(is.na(care_homes$satisfaction)),
  inspection   = as.integer(is.na(care_homes$inspection))
)

upset(missing_flags,
      sets = c("staffing", "satisfaction", "inspection"),
      order.by = "freq",
      main.bar.color = "#007EF5",
      sets.bar.color = "#375071",
      matrix.color = "#293241",
      text.scale = 1.1)







Figure 17.1: The gaps travel together. The tallest bars are single-variable gaps – but twenty homes are missing staffing and satisfaction at the same time, nearly three times what independent accidents would produce, which is the signature of a shared cause rather than scattered accidents. The mechanism investigation starts with that cluster.








import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from upsetplot import from_indicators, UpSet
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)

missing_flags = care_homes[
    ["staffing_rate", "satisfaction", "inspection"]
].isna()
missing_flags.columns = ["staffing", "satisfaction", "inspection"]

upset = UpSet(from_indicators(missing_flags), subset_size="count",
              show_counts=False)
axes = upset.plot()
for container in axes["intersections"].containers:
    axes["intersections"].bar_label(container, fmt="%d", padding=2)
plt.show()







Figure 17.2: The gaps travel together. The tallest bars are single-variable gaps – but twenty homes are missing staffing and satisfaction at the same time, nearly three times what independent accidents would produce, which is the signature of a shared cause rather than scattered accidents. The mechanism investigation starts with that cluster.








A scatter of independent accidents would make joint gaps rare; here the staffing-and-satisfaction-together bar is far too tall for coincidence. Some common circumstance is suppressing both returns in the same homes – the map cannot say what, but it says exactly where to dig. (For a record-by-record view, the missingness map from the foundations chapter is the companion picture; the upset plot summarises what that map shows in detail.)


Interrogate the mechanism
The gaps cluster; the question is whether they cluster along variables you hold. Compare what you know about the missing homes with the complete ones:

RPython



miss <- is.na(care_homes$satisfaction)

aggregate(size ~ miss, data = transform(care_homes, miss = miss), mean)

   miss     size
1 FALSE 63.29897
2  TRUE 42.50000

# Welch two-sample comparison of home size by missingness status
t.test(care_homes$size[miss], care_homes$size[!miss])


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  care_homes$size[miss] and care_homes$size[!miss]
t = -6.5292, df = 77.978, p-value = 6.113e-09
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -27.14090 -14.45704
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y 
 42.50000  63.29897 





from scipy import stats

miss = care_homes["satisfaction"].isna()

print(care_homes.groupby(miss)["size"].agg(["mean", "count"]).round(1))

              mean  count
satisfaction             
False         63.3    388
True          42.5     62

# Welch two-sample comparison of home size by missingness status
res = stats.ttest_ind(care_homes.loc[miss, "size"],
                      care_homes.loc[~miss, "size"], equal_var=False)
print(f"t = {res.statistic:.2f}, p = {res.pvalue:.2e}")

t = -6.53, p = 6.11e-09





The non-reporting homes average around 42 beds against 63 for the reporters – a difference far beyond chance. So the missingness is emphatically not MCAR: it leans on size, a variable we hold, which is the MAR signature. The untestable caveat still applies in full: nothing here rules out the gaps also leaning on satisfaction itself (the unhappy small homes staying silent), and that residual doubt is exactly what the sensitivity analysis at the end is for. For now, the working judgement – declared, not hidden – is MAR through size.


Three ways to handle it, marked against the truth
Complete case analysis – analyse the reporters, drop the rest – is the default everyone reaches for and most software performs silently. Under the mechanism we just diagnosed it is predictably biased: the missing homes are small, small homes score lower, so the surviving sample is too cheerful.
Single imputation by the mean – fill every gap with the observed mean – is the classic worse-than-nothing move. It cannot fix the bias (the filled values inherit the cheerful mean), and it actively damages the uncertainty: the sample swells back to 450 with 62 manufactured “observations” of zero variance, so the standard error shrinks while the estimate stays wrong. Confidence rises as validity falls.
Multiple imputation does what single imputation pretends to. Rather than one filled-in guess, it draws many plausible completed datasets – each gap filled from a model of the observed relationships (here, satisfaction’s dependence on size) plus random noise reflecting what the model cannot know – analyses each one, and pools the results so the spread between imputations feeds honestly into the final uncertainty. The version below is hand-rolled in a dozen lines so the machinery is visible; the Overthinking box covers the pooling rules and the production-grade tools.

RPython



obs <- care_homes$satisfaction[!miss]

# 1. Complete case analysis
cc_mean <- mean(obs)
cc_se   <- sd(obs) / sqrt(length(obs))

# 2. Single imputation by the mean
filled  <- ifelse(miss, mean(obs), care_homes$satisfaction)
si_mean <- mean(filled)
si_se   <- sd(filled) / sqrt(n)

# 3. Multiple imputation (m = 20), using the observed satisfaction-size
#    relationship. Each round: bootstrap the reporters (parameter
#    uncertainty), fit satisfaction ~ size, fill the gaps with prediction
#    + residual noise, and record the completed-data estimate.
set.seed(7)
m <- 20
est <- vr <- numeric(m)
reporters <- care_homes[!miss, ]
for (k in 1:m) {
  boot  <- reporters[sample(nrow(reporters), replace = TRUE), ]
  fit   <- lm(satisfaction ~ size, data = boot)
  sigma <- summary(fit)$sigma
  completed <- care_homes$satisfaction
  completed[miss] <- predict(fit, newdata = care_homes[miss, ]) +
    rnorm(sum(miss), 0, sigma)
  est[k] <- mean(completed)
  vr[k]  <- var(completed) / n
}
# Rubin's rules: total variance = within + (1 + 1/m) * between
mi_mean <- mean(est)
mi_se   <- sqrt(mean(vr) + (1 + 1 / m) * var(est))

results <- data.frame(
  approach = c("Complete case", "Mean imputation", "Multiple imputation"),
  estimate = round(c(cc_mean, si_mean, mi_mean), 2),
  std_error = round(c(cc_se, si_se, mi_se), 3)
)
results

             approach estimate std_error
1       Complete case    75.41     0.486
2     Mean imputation    75.41     0.419
3 Multiple imputation    74.67     0.487

cat("True mean:", round(true_mean, 2), "\n")

True mean: 74.47 





rng = np.random.default_rng(7)

obs = care_homes.loc[~miss, "satisfaction"]
n = len(care_homes)

# 1. Complete case analysis
cc_mean, cc_se = obs.mean(), obs.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(len(obs))

# 2. Single imputation by the mean
filled = care_homes["satisfaction"].fillna(obs.mean())
si_mean, si_se = filled.mean(), filled.std(ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n)

# 3. Multiple imputation (m = 20), using the observed satisfaction-size
#    relationship: bootstrap the reporters, fit satisfaction ~ size, fill
#    the gaps with prediction + residual noise, record each estimate.
m = 20
reporters = care_homes.loc[~miss, ["size", "satisfaction"]]
est, vr = np.empty(m), np.empty(m)
for k in range(m):
    boot = reporters.sample(len(reporters), replace=True, random_state=k)
    slope, intercept = np.polyfit(boot["size"], boot["satisfaction"], 1)
    resid = boot["satisfaction"] - (intercept + slope * boot["size"])
    sigma = resid.std(ddof=2)
    completed = care_homes["satisfaction"].copy()
    pred = intercept + slope * care_homes.loc[miss, "size"]
    completed[miss] = pred + rng.normal(0, sigma, miss.sum())
    est[k] = completed.mean()
    vr[k] = completed.var(ddof=1) / n
# Rubin's rules: total variance = within + (1 + 1/m) * between
mi_mean = est.mean()
mi_se = np.sqrt(vr.mean() + (1 + 1 / m) * est.var(ddof=1))

print(pd.DataFrame({
    "approach": ["Complete case", "Mean imputation", "Multiple imputation"],
    "estimate": np.round([cc_mean, si_mean, mi_mean], 2),
    "std_error": np.round([cc_se, si_se, mi_se], 3),
}).to_string(index=False))

           approach  estimate  std_error
      Complete case     75.41      0.486
    Mean imputation     75.41      0.419
Multiple imputation     74.61      0.492

print(f"True mean: {true_mean:.2f}")

True mean: 74.47













Figure 17.3: The method that claims the most certainty is the one that misses. All three approaches are shown with 95% intervals against the true mean (dashed). Complete-case analysis lands a point too high and its interval only just reaches the truth. Mean imputation keeps the same biased estimate and shrinks the interval until it excludes the true value entirely – confidently wrong. Multiple imputation, using the size relationship the mechanism investigation found, recovers the truth with an honestly sized interval.





Read the comparison the way a reviewer would. Complete-case analysis is biased upward by about a point, exactly as the mechanism predicted – and with this much data its interval barely stretches to the truth. Mean imputation keeps the bias and adds false precision; it is the only approach whose interval excludes the right answer, which is the quiet scandal of a method still common in practice. Multiple imputation, fuelled by the size relationship the investigation surfaced, comes within a fifth of a point of the truth with an interval that owns up to the remaining uncertainty. None of this is luck: each result follows from what each method does with information and with doubt.


Stress-test the conclusion
The MAR judgement was an argument, not a proof, so the last step bounds what the unverifiable part could do. Suppose the silent homes are not merely small but unhappy – that their satisfaction runs a full residual standard deviation (8 points) below what the size model predicts; or, in the other direction, that they run 8 points above. Recomputing the national mean under both stories spans roughly 74.3 to 76.5 – about a two-point band. If the decision the estimate feeds would change anywhere inside that band, the missingness genuinely matters and the honest report says so; if not, the conclusion is robust to the assumption you could not test. Either way, the tipping point framing – how bad would the silent homes have to be before the conclusion flips? – converts an untestable assumption into a question a domain expert can actually answer.






Note🤔 Rethinking: imputation is not prediction



The word “imputation” invites a misunderstanding that quietly wrecks analyses: that the job is to predict the missing values, and that a better predictor therefore means better handling. It is not, and it does not.
If prediction were the goal, the ideal imputation would fill each gap with its single most likely value – and that is precisely what single imputation does, and precisely why it fails. Filling every gap with the best guess produces a dataset that is individually plausible and collectively false: too smooth, too certain, its variability flattened and its standard errors shrunk, as the worked example shows. The deliberately noisy fills of multiple imputation – each one a worse prediction than the model’s best guess – produce the estimate that is actually right, with uncertainty that is actually honest. Worse predictions, better statistics: that is not a paradox, it is the whole point. The target was never the individual values (lost data is lost); it was valid estimates of the quantities the analysis is about, with intervals a reader can trust.
The regulatory translation is direct. An imputed satisfaction score for a silent care home is not evidence about that home – it would be indefensible in any conversation about that provider, and no imputation output should ever appear in a provider-level judgement. Imputation exists to stop the national picture being distorted by the silence; what the silence means for the silent home is the other question, and often the more important one.








Warning⚙️ Overthinking: Rubin’s rules, m, and the production tools



Why the pooling works. Each completed dataset yields an estimate \(\hat{q}_k\) and its ordinary variance \(W_k\). Rubin’s rules pool them as \(\bar{q} = \frac{1}{m}\sum \hat{q}_k\) with total variance \(T = \bar{W} + (1 + \tfrac{1}{m})B\), where \(\bar{W}\) is the mean within-imputation variance and \(B\) the variance between the \(m\) estimates. \(B\) is the term single imputation throws away: it measures how much the answer moves when the unknowable values are re-drawn, which is exactly the uncertainty the gaps created. (Significance tests on pooled estimates use a t reference with degrees of freedom that depend on \(B/\bar{W}\) – software handles this.)
Proper imputation needs parameter uncertainty too. Filling gaps with “prediction plus residual noise” from one fitted model still understates \(B\), because it treats the model’s coefficients as known. The bootstrap step in the worked example – refitting the model on a resample before each imputation – is one standard fix; fully Bayesian draws are the other. Library implementations do this correctly by default, which is a reason to prefer them in production.
How many imputations. The old advice of \(m = 5\) dates from when computation was expensive; modern practice scales \(m\) with the fraction of missing information – 20 is a reasonable floor, 50 or more for heavily incomplete data or when the analysis reports variances. The cost is trivial; the stability is not.
The production tools. The hand-rolled loop above is for understanding, not for service. In R, the mice package (van Buuren’s multiple imputation by chained equations) handles many incomplete variables at once, mixed types, and diagnostics; in Python, statsmodels’ MICE implementation and scikit-learn’s IterativeImputer cover similar ground (the latter does not pool – you still own Rubin’s rules). Maximum likelihood (full-information ML) is the equally principled alternative that skips imputation entirely, estimating parameters directly from the observed-data likelihood; for many models it and MI give near-identical answers, and the choice is one of convenience. Chained-equations modelling has real settings to get right – which variables to include (generously: auxiliaries help), how many iterations, convergence checks – so for a high-stakes analysis, involve the Guild rather than accepting defaults.
What none of this touches. Every method in this box assumes MAR given the model’s variables. If the silence depends on the missing values themselves after all that conditioning, MI will be confidently, smoothly wrong – which is why the sensitivity analysis is not an optional extra. Formal MNAR machinery exists (selection models, pattern-mixture models) and rests on assumptions as unverifiable as the problem it addresses; it is Guild territory, and its main practical use is disciplined sensitivity analysis rather than rescue. Survey-style non-response weighting (Laaksonen’s territory) is the other principled MAR route – reweight the reporters to stand for the population – and shares both the strengths and the assumption.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
text:
17.5 Documenting Your Analysis
For QA purposes, record: how much was missing, per variable and overall, after sentinel codes were converted; the structure (the upset plot or missingness map, retained as evidence); the mechanism judgement and the evidence behind it – including the comparisons of missing versus complete cases on observed variables, and an honest statement of what could not be tested; the handling chosen, with the model and settings if imputation was used (and the software’s version – defaults change); the sensitivity analysis and the band it produced; and what the missingness means for confidence in the conclusions, in words a non-specialist reader can weigh. This record is what the Code of Practice’s quality pillar looks like in practice – and for any consequential analysis, it is the difference between a result that survives challenge and one that does not.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
text:
17.6 Related Approaches

The thinking behind every choice here → missing data foundations: mechanisms, bias versus precision, and the missingness-as-finding inversion.
Estimation and honest intervals, which imputation exists to protect → statistical inference.
Non-submission as a provider behaviour to investigate → unusual observations for the investigation discipline.
The documentation standard the record above feeds → QA principles.
Complete workflow with missingness handled in context → Example 1: GP Access Analysis.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
section: 17.7 When to Seek Support from the Guild
text:
17.7 When to Seek Support from the Guild
Raise your analysis with the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) at a Quant Clinic session when: the missingness is substantial and plausibly MNAR – especially when non-submission correlates with risk; a multiple-imputation model is needed in production (variable selection, chained-equations settings, diagnostics); several variables are incomplete at once with structure between them; weighting and imputation are both candidates and the choice matters; the analysis model is complex enough (multilevel, time series, latent variables) that missingness interacts with it in non-obvious ways; or a published or actioned conclusion would move within the sensitivity band.

title: 17  Missing Data: Computational Methods
text:
17.8 Further Reading

Internal CQC resources

The QA Framework sets the documentation standards for data limitations and caveats; see How to Use This Book for how to access support.



The standard references

Little, R.J.A. & Rubin, D.B. (2019). Statistical Analysis with Missing Data (3rd ed.). Wiley. (The canonical theory, from the authors of the mechanism taxonomy.)
van Buuren, S. (2018). Flexible Imputation of Missing Data (2nd ed.). CRC Press. (The practitioner’s reference for multiple imputation and mice; freely readable online.)
Sterne, J.A.C. et al. (2009). “Multiple imputation for missing data in epidemiological and clinical research: potential and pitfalls.” BMJ, 338, b2393. (The best single healthcare treatment, with a reporting checklist.)
Schafer, J.L. & Graham, J.W. (2002). “Missing data: our view of the state of the art.” Psychological Methods, 7(2), 147–177. (Why MI and maximum likelihood are the principled pair; the MAR-name caveat.)



Official-statistics practice

Office for National Statistics, Methodological Research Hub (2024). “How do you solve a problem like missing data?” UK Government Analysis Function. (The official-statistics stance: principled imputation over discarding, contingent on data quality.)
Laaksonen, S. (2018). Survey Methodology and Missing Data. Springer. (Unit versus item non-response and the weighting tradition.)

title: Worked Examples Library
text:
What These Are
The method chapters teach one technique at a time; real analysis never arrives that tidily. Each worked example in this part runs a complete, realistic CQC analysis from raw data to stakeholder summary – data quality checks, assumption checking, the method itself, sensitivity analysis, and the QA documentation that makes the work defensible. The datasets are synthetic but built with realistic problems: missing values, skewed distributions, small denominators, outliers, and confounding.
Use them three ways. As a learning path, work through an example alongside its method chapter and compare your decisions with the documented ones. As a reference, find the example nearest your own analysis and adapt its code and QA templates. As training material, the decision points make natural discussion exercises for peer review practice.

title: Worked Examples Library
text:
Common Workflows
Most CQC analyses follow one of a few recurring shapes. Each weaves several methods together, and the examples show them in full.


Profile provider performance

Check data quality and data types
Explore the distributions, their spread, and their shape
Apply z-scoring
Investigate unusual observations with the detection toolkit
Document methods, assumptions, and limitations



Evaluate an intervention or pilot

Check data quality, data types, and baseline comparability
Ground the comparison in statistical inference
Check assumptions, then apply a paired or two-sample t-test
Report the effect size and confidence interval, not just the p-value
Weigh practical against statistical significance, and document for QA



Monitor trends and detect change

Check data quality, data types, and temporal completeness
Understand variation, and common versus special cause
Build the right control chart from a stable baseline
Read the detection rules; investigate what fires
Recalculate limits only after a confirmed, sustained change

title: Worked Examples Library
text:
The Examples

The worked examples and the methods they exercise








#
Example
Method
Scenario




1
GP Access Analysis
Z-scoring
6,500 practices compared on phone access from survey data, with missing returns and small denominators


2
Care Home Falls Monitoring
SPC
24 months of falls counts around a prevention programme, with baseline limits and an outbreak month


3
Mental Health Wait Times
Z-scoring, skewed data
150 services with heavily right-skewed waits: transformation, and when to escalate to Working z-scores


4
Medication Safety Pilot
Paired t-test
Before/after incident rates in 15 care homes: small sample, one outlier, effect size and power


5
Regional A&E Performance
Two-sample t-test
Two integrated care systems compared on 4-hour performance, with confounders weighed


6
Ambulance Handover Delays
SPC + trend
18 months of handover delays with a rising trend, winter pressure, and multiple signals


7
Infection Rates in Care Homes
Rates and denominators
Comparing infection rates across homes of very different sizes: counts versus rates, small numbers


8
DoLS Application Trends
Panel data
Local-authority application trends around a policy change, with backlogs and population differences


9
Staffing and Quality Outcomes
Correlation
Nurse staffing and pressure ulcer rates across ~800 homes: confounding, outliers, causation discipline


10
Care Home Staffing Investigation
Outlier detection
337 staffing returns screened with multiple methods plus a domain rule; errors separated from genuine extremes



Every example models the same disciplines whatever its method: data quality checks before analysis, missing-data handling made explicit, assumptions checked rather than assumed, sensitivity analysis where decisions could swing, and a QA record an auditor could follow.

title: Worked Examples Library
text:
Running the Examples
Environment. You need R or Python. If you are not yet set up: Microsoft Fabric is CQC’s primary analytics platform (contact your function’s data engineer for access); introductory R and Python courses are available via CQC’s learning platform; and the Guild’s Quant Clinic runs drop-in sessions for hands-on help.
Packages. The R code uses ggplot2 (plus UpSetR where missingness patterns are mapped); the Python code uses pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and scipy (plus upsetplot for the same maps). Install whichever set matches your language:
install.packages(c("ggplot2", "UpSetR"))
pip install pandas numpy matplotlib scipy upsetplot
Data. All datasets are synthetic, pre-generated, and stored in examples/data/:

Datasets used by the examples






File
Example




gp_access_data.csv
1 – GP Access


falls_data.csv
2 – Care Home Falls


mental_health_wait_times.csv
3 – Mental Health Waits


medication_safety_data.csv
4 – Medication Safety


ae_performance_data.csv
5 – Regional A&E


ambulance_handovers.csv
6 – Ambulance Handovers


infection_rates.csv
7 – Infection Rates


dols_applications.csv
8 – DoLS Trends



(Examples 9 and 10 generate their data in the first code chunk, so the generation process is part of the lesson.)
Running. Open an example file and run the chunks in order – later chunks build on earlier ones. Code paths are relative to the examples/ folder (data/filename.csv), which is where Quarto runs each file’s code; if you are running chunks interactively, set your working directory to examples/ first. Each analysis step appears in both languages as tabs – pick one and stay with it; the two produce the same results. If a file cannot be found, check your working directory; if a package is missing, install it from the lists above.
For questions or feedback, contact the Practice Lead for Quantitative Analytics and Statistics.

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.1 Scenario Overview
Method: z-scoring Complexity: Medium Time to complete: 2-3 hours QASQA questions: Q3 (Analysis); Q5 (Fitness for Purpose)

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
NoteMethod Reference



For the z-scoring method itself – assumptions, minimum requirements, banding, and interpretation – see the chapter on comparing provider performance.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.2 Scenario
You are a CQC analyst tasked with identifying GP practices with significantly poor patient access based on the 2025 GP Patient Survey. The analysis will inform inspection targeting and help identify practices that may need support.
Analytical Question: Which GP practices have phone access difficulty rates that are statistically significantly worse than the national average?
Context: The 2025 GP Patient Survey found that 35% of respondents who tried to contact their GP by phone described it as difficult. However, there is substantial variation between practices. CQC wants to identify practices in the worst-performing band for follow-up.

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Handle survey data with varying response rates and sample sizes
Manage missing data and document your decisions
Check z-scoring assumptions and handle violations
Deal with small denominators (practices with few respondents)
Calculate and interpret z-scores in a regulatory context
Assign performance bands and identify outliers
Visualise results effectively for stakeholders
Document QA decisions to meet CQC framework requirements
Communicate findings to non-technical audiences

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: data/gp_access_data.csv
6,500 GP practices with the following variables:









Variable
Description
Type
Notes




practice_code
Unique practice identifier
String
Format: ABC1234


practice_name
Practice name
String
Synthetic names


icb_code
Integrated Care Board code
String
E.g., QOQ


icb_name
ICB name
String
E.g., NHS Devon ICB


region
NHS England region
String
7 regions


list_size
Registered patients
Integer
500 - 25,000


survey_responses
Survey respondents
Integer
30 - 800


phone_difficult_n
Respondents finding phone contact difficult
Integer
0 - 500


imd_quintile
Index of Multiple Deprivation quintile
Integer
1 (most deprived) - 5 (least deprived)


rural_urban
Rural/urban classification
String
Urban / Rural





Data Characteristics
Realistic issues included: - ~5% missing data on phone_difficult_n (non-response) - Wide variation in survey_responses (30 to 800) - Some practices with very small list sizes (<1,000 patients) - Slight positive skew in difficulty rates - Regional variation - Deprivation gradient (more deprived areas have worse access)
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42) for reproducibility). The data matches realistic GP Patient Survey patterns including deprivation gradients and natural variation. Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.5 Step 1: Data Preparation
Purpose: Load, check, clean, and prepare the GP access dataset for analysis.
Key tasks: 1. Import data 2. Check data structure and quality 3. Identify and handle missing data 4. Check for errors and outliers 5. Create analysis dataset


Import Data

RPython



# Load required packages and the CQC figure style
library(ggplot2)
source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

# Set options
options(scipen = 999)  # Avoid scientific notation

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# CQC figure style
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK, CQC_BANDS

apply_cqc_style()

# Set display options
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.width', None)






RPython



# Import GP access data
gp_data_raw <- read.csv("data/gp_access_data.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Display first few rows

Initial observations: - Dataset has 6,500 practices - 10 variables (identifiers, geography, characteristics, survey data) - Mix of character and numeric variables



Data Quality Checks


Check for Duplicates

RPython



# Check for duplicate practice codes
n_duplicates <- sum(duplicated(gp_data_raw$practice_code))
cat("Duplicate practice codes:", n_duplicates, "\n")

Duplicate practice codes: 0 

# Check for duplicate rows
n_dup_rows <- sum(duplicated(gp_data_raw))
cat("Duplicate rows:", n_dup_rows, "\n")

Duplicate rows: 0 





# Check for duplicate practice codes
n_duplicates = gp_data_raw['practice_code'].duplicated().sum()
print(f"Duplicate practice codes: {n_duplicates}")

Duplicate practice codes: 0

# Check for duplicate rows
n_dup_rows = gp_data_raw.duplicated().sum()
print(f"Duplicate rows: {n_dup_rows}")

Duplicate rows: 0





Result: no duplicates found.



Check Missing Data

RPython



# Count missing values by variable
missing_counts <- colSums(is.na(gp_data_raw))
missing_pct <- round(100 * missing_counts / nrow(gp_data_raw), 1)

# Create summary table
missing_summary <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(missing_counts),
  Missing_N = missing_counts,
  Missing_Pct = missing_pct
)

# Display only variables with missing data
missing_summary[missing_summary$Missing_N > 0, ]

                           Variable Missing_N Missing_Pct
phone_difficult_n phone_difficult_n       325           5





# Count missing values
missing_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Variable': gp_data_raw.columns,
    'Missing_N': gp_data_raw.isnull().sum(),
    'Missing_Pct': round(100 * gp_data_raw.isnull().sum() / len(gp_data_raw), 1)
})

# Display only variables with missing data
print(missing_summary[missing_summary['Missing_N'] > 0])

phone_difficult_n  phone_difficult_n        325          5.0





Findings: - phone_difficult_n has ~325 missing values (~5%) - All other variables are complete
Decision needed: How to handle missing phone_difficult_n?



Handle Missing Data
Decision: Exclude practices with missing phone_difficult_n from z-scoring analysis.
Rationale: - Cannot calculate difficulty rate without numerator - Missing appears random (not systematic) - 5% exclusion is acceptable - Document impact in QA log

RPython



# Create analysis dataset excluding missing
gp_data_clean <- gp_data_raw[!is.na(gp_data_raw$phone_difficult_n), ]

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.6 Step 2: Exploratory Analysis
Purpose: Explore the distribution of difficulty rates, check z-scoring assumptions, and identify patterns.


Distribution of Difficulty Rates

RPython



ggplot(gp_data_clean, aes(x = difficulty_rate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#99C9F7", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate),
             color = cqc_ink, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of phone access difficulty rates",
    subtitle = paste("Dashed line: national mean =",
                     round(mean(gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate), 1), "%"),
    x = "Difficulty rate (%)",
    y = "Number of practices"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 19.1: Difficulty rates spread widely around the national mean. The distribution is roughly bell-shaped with a slight right tail – close enough to normal for standard z-scoring, which the Q-Q plot below confirms.








plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.hist(gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'], bins=50, color='#99C9F7', edgecolor='white')
plt.axvline(gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'].mean(), color=CQC_INK, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
plt.xlabel('Difficulty rate (%)')
plt.ylabel('Number of practices')
plt.title(f"Distribution of phone access difficulty rates\nDashed line: national mean = {gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'].mean():.1f}%")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 19.2: Difficulty rates spread widely around the national mean. The distribution is roughly bell-shaped with a slight right tail – close enough to normal for standard z-scoring, which the Q-Q plot below confirms.








Observations: - Approximately normal distribution - Mean around 34.9% - Slight positive skew - Range from ~5% to ~70%



Check Normality

RPython



ggplot(gp_data_clean, aes(sample = difficulty_rate)) +
  stat_qq(colour = "#375071", alpha = 0.5) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#A83DA4") +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q plot: difficulty rates",
    x = "Theoretical quantiles",
    y = "Sample quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()

# Shapiro-Wilk test (on sample if n > 5000)
if (nrow(gp_data_clean) > 5000) {
  sample_data <- sample(gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate, 5000)
  shapiro_result <- shapiro.test(sample_data)
} else {
  shapiro_result <- shapiro.test(gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate)
}

cat("Shapiro-Wilk test p-value:", format.pval(shapiro_result$p.value), "\n")

Shapiro-Wilk test p-value: 0.0032705 








Figure 19.3: The points hug the reference line through the body of the distribution, with only mild drift in the upper tail – the slight right skew seen in the histogram. Normality is a good enough approximation for standard z-scoring here.








from scipy import stats

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))
stats.probplot(gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'], dist="norm", plot=ax)

((array([-3.68973416, -3.45765086, -3.32989911, ...,  3.32989911,
        3.45765086,  3.68973416], shape=(6175,)), array([ 5.40540541,  6.25      ,  6.25      , ..., 66.25766871,
       66.66666667, 70.        ], shape=(6175,))), (np.float64(9.428095457547485), np.float64(34.855534525728345), np.float64(0.9996445474662845)))

ax.get_lines()[0].set_color('#375071')
ax.get_lines()[0].set_alpha(0.5)
ax.get_lines()[1].set_color('#A83DA4')
ax.set_title("Q-Q plot: difficulty rates")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 19.4: The points hug the reference line through the body of the distribution, with only mild drift in the upper tail – the slight right skew seen in the histogram. Normality is a good enough approximation for standard z-scoring here.




# Shapiro-Wilk test (on sample if n > 5000)
if len(gp_data_clean) > 5000:
    sample_data = gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'].sample(5000)
    stat, p_value = stats.shapiro(sample_data)
else:
    stat, p_value = stats.shapiro(gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'])

print(f"Shapiro-Wilk test p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

Shapiro-Wilk test p-value: 0.0021





Assessment: Distribution is approximately normal - suitable for z-scoring.

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.7 Step 3: Z-scoring Analysis
Purpose: Calculate z-scores, assign performance bands, and identify practices for follow-up.


Calculate Z-scores

RPython



# National statistics
national_mean <- mean(gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate)
national_sd <- sd(gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate)

cat("National mean:", round(national_mean, 2), "%\n")

National mean: 34.86 %

cat("National standard deviation:", round(national_sd, 2), "%\n")

National standard deviation: 9.43 %

# Calculate z-scores
gp_data_clean$z_score <- (gp_data_clean$difficulty_rate - national_mean) / national_sd

# Summary
summary(gp_data_clean$z_score)

    Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
-3.12402 -0.70131 -0.01414  0.00000  0.68408  3.72806

national_sd = gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'].std()

print(f"National mean: {national_mean:.2f}%")

National mean: 34.86%

print(f"National standard deviation: {national_sd:.2f}%")

National standard deviation: 9.43%

# Calculate z-scores
gp_data_clean['z_score'] = (gp_data_clean['difficulty_rate'] - national_mean) / national_sd

Assign Performance Bands

RPython



# Assign CQC's descriptive bands (high difficulty = worse access,
# so "above" bands are the concerning ones for this indicator)
gp_data_clean$band <- cut(gp_data_clean$z_score,
                           breaks = c(-Inf, -3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3, Inf),
                           labels = c("Markedly below", "Below", "Somewhat below",
                                      "In line", "Somewhat above", "Above",
                                      "Markedly above"))

483        MCA7978   Practice 484        63.440860  3.032281  Markedly above
1077       JHL5258  Practice 1078        63.333333  3.020875  Markedly above
4212       LZL3090  Practice 4213        63.333333  3.020875  Markedly above
4589       TWE2108  Practice 4590        63.333333  3.020875  Markedly above








Visualise Results

RPython



# CQC bands palette: green = fewer access problems, red = more (labels carry
# the meaning as well as colour)
band_cols <- c("Markedly below" = "#003C00", "Below" = "#167F0E",
               "Somewhat below" = "#5DC54B", "In line" = "#6D6D6D",
               "Somewhat above" = "#FC9E80", "Above" = "#F50D08",
               "Markedly above" = "#8F0000")

ggplot(gp_data_clean, aes(x = z_score, fill = band)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, color = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = band_cols) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = cqc_ink, alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Phone access difficulty: most practices in line, a thin tail to investigate",
    x = "Z-score",
    y = "Number of practices",
    fill = "Band"
  ) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")







Figure 19.5: The vast majority of practices sit in line with the national picture – the regulatory attention belongs to the thin right tail. Bands use the CQC bands palette, paired with labels: for this indicator higher difficulty is worse, so the red bands on the right are the flagged practices.








# CQC bands palette: green = fewer access problems, red = more (labels carry
# the meaning as well as colour)
band_order = ["Markedly below", "Below", "Somewhat below", "In line",
              "Somewhat above", "Above", "Markedly above"]
colors = dict(zip(band_order, CQC_BANDS))

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
bins = np.linspace(gp_data_clean['z_score'].min(), gp_data_clean['z_score'].max(), 51)
for band in band_order:
    data = gp_data_clean[gp_data_clean['band'] == band]['z_score']
    plt.hist(data, bins=bins, label=band, color=colors[band], edgecolor='white')

for x in [-3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3]:
    plt.axvline(x, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5, color=CQC_INK)

plt.xlabel('Z-score')
plt.ylabel('Number of practices')
plt.title('Phone access difficulty: most practices in line, a thin tail to investigate')
plt.legend(title='Band')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 19.6: The vast majority of practices sit in line with the national picture – the regulatory attention belongs to the thin right tail. Bands use the CQC bands palette, paired with labels: for this indicator higher difficulty is worse, so the red bands on the right are the flagged practices.











Save Results

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.8 Step 4: Stakeholder Report

Executive Summary
Analysis: GP Phone Access Difficulty - 2025 Survey
Date: June 12, 2026
Analyst: CQC Data & Insight Team


Key Findings

National average: 34.9% of survey respondents find phone contact difficult
Practices analysed: 6,175 (after excluding 325 with missing data)
Practices flagged (Above / Markedly above): 141 practices (2.3%)
Strongest signals (Markedly above, z > 3): 12 practices (0.2%)



Recommendations

Immediate follow-up: Markedly above practices (z > 3)
Monitoring: Above practices (2 < z ≤ 3)
Context review: Consider practice size, deprivation, rurality




Summary
This example demonstrates a complete z-scoring workflow for CQC regulatory analytics: data quality checks and missing-data handling, exploratory analysis and assumption checking, z-score calculation and performance banding, identification of practices for follow-up, and stakeholder-ready visualisation and reporting. The QA record below closes the loop.

title: 19  Example 1: GP Access Analysis
text:
19.9 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Z-scoring (standard)


QASQA question
Q3: Analysis – was the method appropriate and correctly applied?; Q5: Fitness for Purpose – does the indicator measure what the regulatory question requires?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; C: Data quality and governance; E: Quality assurance and reproducibility


Data source
GP Patient Survey 2025 (synthetic illustrative data – seed 42 for reproducibility)


Key decisions
Complete case analysis for missing data (5% excluded, documented); z ≥ 2 threshold for flagging; national mean and standard deviation calculated from all providers with complete data


Assumptions
Data approximately normally distributed; providers are comparable (same indicator definition); survey non-response is not systematically related to phone access quality


Limitations
Ecological analysis – practice-level rates, not individual patient experiences; no risk adjustment for deprivation or rurality; survey non-response may introduce bias


Sensitivity
Results checked at z ≥ 1.5 and z ≥ 2.5 thresholds – flagged set changes but top-flagged practices are stable


Next steps
Cross-check flagged practices against inspection history and other indicators; confirm data quality for extreme outliers before regulatory action


Sign-off
________ (Analyst) ________ (Peer reviewer) ________ (Date)

NoteMethod Reference



For the SPC method itself – control charts, limits, detection rules, and baseline discipline – see the chapter on monitoring trends over time.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.
crumbs: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.2 Scenario
You are a CQC analyst monitoring falls rates at Oakwood Care Home over 24 months. A falls prevention program was implemented in January 2024 (Month 13). Your task is to determine whether the intervention reduced falls rates and whether the improvement is sustained.
Analytical Question: Did the falls prevention program reduce falls rates at Oakwood Care Home?
Context: Falls are a key quality indicator for care homes. Oakwood Care Home has been tracking monthly falls for 2 years and implemented a comprehensive falls prevention program including staff training, environmental modifications, and enhanced monitoring.
crumbs: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Set up control charts from scratch using I-chart methodology
Calculate control limits correctly (centre line, UCL, LCL)
Identify special causes and decide when to exclude them
Detect intervention effects using SPC detection rules
Apply detection rules (Rule 1: outside limits, Rule 4: runs)
Assess process stability before and after intervention
Evaluate sustainability of improvements
Communicate SPC results to non-technical stakeholders
crumbs: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: data/falls_data.csv
24 months of falls data with the following variables:



Variable
Description
Type
Notes




month
Month number
Integer
1-24


date
Month start date
Date
Jan 2023 - Dec 2024


falls_count
Number of falls
Integer
8-26 per month


phase
Analysis phase
String
Baseline / Post-intervention





Data Characteristics
Realistic patterns included: - Baseline mean: ~16.8 falls/month (excluding the outbreak months) - Special cause event: months 7-8 (flu outbreak, 26 falls in each) - Intervention point: month 13 - Post-intervention mean: ~10.7 falls/month (~37% reduction) - Natural variation throughout
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42) for reproducibility). The data includes a special cause event and demonstrates a sustained intervention effect. Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.5 Step 1: Data Preparation
Purpose: Load and prepare the falls data for SPC analysis.


Setup

RPython



library(ggplot2)

# Import falls data
falls_data <- read.csv("data/falls_data.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Convert date column to Date type
falls_data$date <- as.Date(falls_data$date)

# Display first few rows
head(falls_data)

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Import falls data
falls_data = pd.read_csv("data/falls_data.csv")

# Convert date column to datetime
falls_data['date'] = pd.to_datetime(falls_data['date'])

# Display first few rows

0      1 2023-01-01           21  Baseline

2      3 2023-03-01           22  Baseline

4      5 2023-05-01           12  Baseline








Visualise Time Series

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

ggplot(falls_data, aes(x = month, y = falls_count)) +
  geom_line(color = cqc_muted, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#007EF5") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 12.5, color = "#A83DA4", linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 12.5, y = max(falls_data$falls_count),
           label = "Intervention", angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = -0.5,
           color = "#A83DA4") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 24, 3)) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly falls: lower after the intervention - but is it signal?",
    subtitle = "Oakwood Care Home, 24 months (Jan 2023 - Dec 2024)",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Falls count"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 20.1: Two years of monthly falls, plotted before any limits are drawn. The first year fluctuates in the high teens with a two-month spike to 26 in months 7 and 8; after the intervention line at month 13 the counts sit visibly lower. The control chart will say whether that impression survives the noise.
