title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.6 Step 2: Baseline SPC
Purpose: Establish baseline control limits and assess process stability.


Calculate Control Limits (Including All Data)

RPython



# Baseline data (Months 1-12)
baseline_all <- falls_data[falls_data$month <= 12, ]

# Calculate centre line (mean)
centre_line_all <- mean(baseline_all$falls_count)

# Calculate moving range
mr <- abs(diff(baseline_all$falls_count))
mean_mr <- mean(mr)

# Calculate control limits (±3σ)
ucl_all <- centre_line_all + 2.66 * mean_mr
lcl_all <- centre_line_all - 2.66 * mean_mr

cat("Baseline Control Limits (including all data):\n")

Baseline Control Limits (including all data):

cat("  Centre Line:", round(centre_line_all, 2), "\n")

  Centre Line: 18.33 

cat("  UCL:", round(ucl_all, 2), "\n")

  UCL: 30.67 

cat("  LCL:", round(lcl_all, 2), "\n")

  LCL: 6 





# Baseline data
baseline_all = falls_data[falls_data['month'] <= 12]

# Calculate centre line
centre_line_all = baseline_all['falls_count'].mean()

# Calculate moving range
mr = baseline_all['falls_count'].diff().abs().dropna()
mean_mr = mr.mean()

# Calculate control limits
ucl_all = centre_line_all + 2.66 * mean_mr
lcl_all = centre_line_all - 2.66 * mean_mr

print("Baseline Control Limits (including all data):")

Baseline Control Limits (including all data):

print(f"  Centre Line: {centre_line_all:.2f}")

  Centre Line: 18.33

print(f"  UCL: {ucl_all:.2f}")

  UCL: 30.67

print(f"  LCL: {lcl_all:.2f}")

  LCL: 6.00








Identify Special Causes

RPython



# Check for points outside limits
baseline_all$outside_limits <- baseline_all$falls_count > ucl_all | baseline_all$falls_count < lcl_all

# Display special causes
special_causes <- baseline_all[baseline_all$outside_limits, c("month", "date", "falls_count")]
print(special_causes)

[1] month       date        falls_count
<0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)





# Check for points outside limits
baseline_all['outside_limits'] = (baseline_all['falls_count'] > ucl_all) | (baseline_all['falls_count'] < lcl_all)

<string>:3: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy

# Display special causes
special_causes = baseline_all[baseline_all['outside_limits']][['month', 'date', 'falls_count']]
print(special_causes)

Empty DataFrame
Columns: [month, date, falls_count]
Index: []





Finding: no point breaches the limits when they are calculated from all twelve baseline months – the spike has inflated the very limits used to judge it, the masking problem that haunts limits computed over a known event. But months 7 and 8 (26 falls each) correspond to a documented flu outbreak: investigation, not a rule, identifies them as a special cause.
Decision: exclude months 7-8 from the limit calculation, so the limits describe the process’s routine behaviour. Excluding a point from the limits is not deleting it from the data – both months stay on the chart, labelled.



Recalculate Control Limits (Excluding the Outbreak)

RPython



# Baseline excluding the outbreak months (7-8)
baseline_clean <- falls_data[falls_data$month <= 12 & !(falls_data$month %in% c(7, 8)), ]

# Recalculate
centre_line <- mean(baseline_clean$falls_count)
mr_clean <- abs(diff(baseline_clean$falls_count))
mean_mr_clean <- mean(mr_clean)
ucl <- centre_line + 2.66 * mean_mr_clean
lcl <- centre_line - 2.66 * mean_mr_clean

cat("Final Baseline Control Limits (excluding months 7-8):\n")

Final Baseline Control Limits (excluding months 7-8):

cat("  Centre Line:", round(centre_line, 2), "\n")

  Centre Line: 16.8 

cat("  UCL:", round(ucl, 2), "\n")

  UCL: 26.55 

cat("  LCL:", round(lcl, 2), "\n")

  LCL: 7.05 

# Baseline parameters calculated
baseline_params <- data.frame(
  centre_line = centre_line,
  ucl = ucl,
  lcl = lcl
)




# Baseline excluding the outbreak months (7-8)
baseline_clean = falls_data[(falls_data['month'] <= 12) & (~falls_data['month'].isin([7, 8]))]

# Recalculate
centre_line = baseline_clean['falls_count'].mean()
mr_clean = baseline_clean['falls_count'].diff().abs().dropna()
mean_mr_clean = mr_clean.mean()
ucl = centre_line + 2.66 * mean_mr_clean
lcl = centre_line - 2.66 * mean_mr_clean

print("Final Baseline Control Limits (excluding months 7-8):")

Final Baseline Control Limits (excluding months 7-8):

print(f"  Centre Line: {centre_line:.2f}")

  Centre Line: 16.80

print(f"  UCL: {ucl:.2f}")

  UCL: 26.55

print(f"  LCL: {lcl:.2f}")

  LCL: 7.05


# Baseline parameters calculated
baseline_params = pd.DataFrame({
    'centre_line': [centre_line],
    'ucl': [ucl],
    'lcl': [lcl]
})







Plot Baseline Control Chart

RPython



baseline_all$point_type <- ifelse(baseline_all$month %in% c(7, 8),
                                  "Outbreak (special cause)", "Routine month")

ggplot(baseline_all, aes(x = month, y = falls_count)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = centre_line, color = cqc_ink, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(ucl, lcl), color = "#4289B3",
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_line(color = cqc_muted, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(color = point_type, shape = point_type), size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Routine month" = "#007EF5",
                                "Outbreak (special cause)" = "#A83DA4"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Routine month" = 16,
                                "Outbreak (special cause)" = 17),
                     name = NULL) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = centre_line,
           label = paste("CL =", round(centre_line, 1)),
           hjust = 0, vjust = -0.5, color = cqc_ink, size = 3.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = ucl, label = paste("UCL =", round(ucl, 1)),
           hjust = 0, vjust = -0.5, color = "#4289B3", size = 3.2) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = lcl, label = paste("LCL =", round(lcl, 1)),
           hjust = 0, vjust = 1.5, color = "#4289B3", size = 3.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:12) +
  labs(
    title = "Baseline control chart (months 1-12)",
    subtitle = "Limits calculated excluding the months 7-8 outbreak",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Falls count"
  ) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")







Figure 20.3: With the documented outbreak excluded from the calculation, the limits describe routine months - and the outbreak months stand visibly apart, just under the upper limit, labelled as the known special cause they are. The remaining ten months scatter unremarkably around the centre line: a stable baseline to extend forwards.








plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

routine = baseline_all[~baseline_all['month'].isin([7, 8])]
outbreak = baseline_all[baseline_all['month'].isin([7, 8])]

plt.axhline(centre_line, color=CQC_INK, linewidth=1.2, label=f'CL = {centre_line:.1f}')
plt.axhline(ucl, color='#4289B3', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label=f'UCL = {ucl:.1f}')
plt.axhline(lcl, color='#4289B3', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label=f'LCL = {lcl:.1f}')
plt.plot(baseline_all['month'], baseline_all['falls_count'], '-', color=CQC_MUTED, linewidth=0.8)
plt.plot(routine['month'], routine['falls_count'], 'o', color='#007EF5',
         markersize=7, label='Routine month')
plt.plot(outbreak['month'], outbreak['falls_count'], '^', color='#A83DA4',
         markersize=9, label='Outbreak (special cause)')

plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Falls count')
plt.title('Baseline control chart (months 1-12)\nLimits calculated excluding the months 7-8 outbreak')
plt.xticks(range(1, 13))

([<matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6060DFDA0>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C67F1AD4F0>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C60600F6B0>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6061006B0>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6060BE810>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6060BC440>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606026A50>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C60609DD90>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606100560>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C60609C680>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606102630>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606102060>], [Text(1, 0, '1'), Text(2, 0, '2'), Text(3, 0, '3'), Text(4, 0, '4'), Text(5, 0, '5'), Text(6, 0, '6'), Text(7, 0, '7'), Text(8, 0, '8'), Text(9, 0, '9'), Text(10, 0, '10'), Text(11, 0, '11'), Text(12, 0, '12')])

plt.legend(loc='lower left', fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 20.4: With the documented outbreak excluded from the calculation, the limits describe routine months - and the outbreak months stand visibly apart, just under the upper limit, labelled as the known special cause they are. The remaining ten months scatter unremarkably around the centre line: a stable baseline to extend forwards.








Assessment: the baseline is stable once the documented outbreak is accounted for. The limits can be frozen and extended forwards, so the post-intervention months are judged against the process as it behaved before the programme.
crumbs: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.7 Step 3: Intervention Analysis
Purpose: Determine if the falls prevention program reduced falls rates.


Full Control Chart with Intervention

RPython



falls_data$point_type <- "Baseline"
falls_data$point_type[falls_data$month %in% c(7, 8)] <- "Outbreak (special cause)"
falls_data$point_type[falls_data$month >= 13] <- "Post-intervention"

ggplot(falls_data, aes(x = month, y = falls_count)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = centre_line, color = cqc_ink, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(ucl, lcl), color = "#4289B3",
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 12.5, color = "#A83DA4", linetype = "dotted",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(color = cqc_muted, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(color = point_type, shape = point_type), size = 2.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Baseline" = "#007EF5",
                                "Outbreak (special cause)" = "#A83DA4",
                                "Post-intervention" = "#006560"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("Baseline" = 16,
                                "Outbreak (special cause)" = 17,
                                "Post-intervention" = 15),
                     name = NULL) +
  annotate("text", x = 12.4, y = max(falls_data$falls_count),
           label = "Intervention", angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = -0.4,
           color = "#A83DA4") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 24, 3)) +
  labs(
    title = "Every post-intervention month sits below the baseline centre line",
    subtitle = "Baseline limits frozen and extended to the post-intervention period",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Falls count"
  ) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")







Figure 20.5: Judged against the frozen baseline limits, the post-intervention year tells a consistent story: every month sits below the baseline centre line, and two months dip below the lower control limit itself. A run of twelve and two limit breaches, all in the direction of improvement, is the signature of a genuine sustained shift - not a lucky year.








plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

routine = falls_data[(falls_data['month'] <= 12) & (~falls_data['month'].isin([7, 8]))]
outbreak = falls_data[falls_data['month'].isin([7, 8])]
post = falls_data[falls_data['month'] >= 13]

plt.axhline(centre_line, color=CQC_INK, linewidth=1.2)
plt.axhline(ucl, color='#4289B3', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2)
plt.axhline(lcl, color='#4289B3', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2)
plt.axvline(12.5, color='#A83DA4', linestyle=':', linewidth=1.5)
plt.plot(falls_data['month'], falls_data['falls_count'], '-', color=CQC_MUTED, linewidth=0.8)
plt.plot(routine['month'], routine['falls_count'], 'o', color='#007EF5',
         markersize=7, label='Baseline')
plt.plot(outbreak['month'], outbreak['falls_count'], '^', color='#A83DA4',
         markersize=9, label='Outbreak (special cause)')
plt.plot(post['month'], post['falls_count'], 's', color='#006560',
         markersize=7, label='Post-intervention')

plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Falls count')
plt.title('Every post-intervention month sits below the baseline centre line\nBaseline limits frozen and extended to the post-intervention period')
plt.xticks(range(0, 25, 3))

([<matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6060BEF90>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6060DDD60>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C604EE5040>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6061A3D70>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6061A33B0>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606100D40>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606103C20>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C6061B83E0>, <matplotlib.axis.XTick object at 0x000001C606103710>], [Text(0, 0, '0'), Text(3, 0, '3'), Text(6, 0, '6'), Text(9, 0, '9'), Text(12, 0, '12'), Text(15, 0, '15'), Text(18, 0, '18'), Text(21, 0, '21'), Text(24, 0, '24')])

plt.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 20.6: Judged against the frozen baseline limits, the post-intervention year tells a consistent story: every month sits below the baseline centre line, and two months dip below the lower control limit itself. A run of twelve and two limit breaches, all in the direction of improvement, is the signature of a genuine sustained shift - not a lucky year.











Detect Points Outside Limits

RPython



post_intervention <- falls_data[falls_data$month >= 13, ]

outside_lcl <- post_intervention[post_intervention$falls_count < lcl, ]

cat("Post-Intervention Points Below LCL (indicating improvement):\n")

Post-Intervention Points Below LCL (indicating improvement):

print(outside_lcl[, c("month", "date", "falls_count")])

   month       date falls_count
20    20 2024-08-01           6
22    22 2024-10-01           6

cat("\nTotal:", nrow(outside_lcl), "of", nrow(post_intervention), "months\n")


Total: 2 of 12 months





post_intervention = falls_data[falls_data['month'] >= 13]

outside_lcl = post_intervention[post_intervention['falls_count'] < lcl]

print("Post-Intervention Points Below LCL (indicating improvement):")

Post-Intervention Points Below LCL (indicating improvement):

print(outside_lcl[['month', 'date', 'falls_count']])

    month       date  falls_count
19     20 2024-08-01            6
21     22 2024-10-01            6

print(f"\nTotal: {len(outside_lcl)} of {len(post_intervention)} months")


Total: 2 of 12 months








Apply the Shift Rule
Shift rule: a run of 8 or more consecutive points on one side of the centre line signals a process shift, even when no single point breaches the limits.

RPython



post_intervention$below_centre <- post_intervention$falls_count < centre_line

cat("Shift rule: 8+ consecutive points one side of the centre line\n\n")

Shift rule: 8+ consecutive points one side of the centre line

cat("Post-intervention points below baseline centre line:\n")

Post-intervention points below baseline centre line:

cat("  Total:", sum(post_intervention$below_centre), "of", nrow(post_intervention), "\n")

  Total: 12 of 12 

consecutive_below <- rle(post_intervention$below_centre)
max_run <- max(consecutive_below$lengths[consecutive_below$values])

cat("  Longest consecutive run below centre:", max_run, "\n")

  Longest consecutive run below centre: 12 

if (max_run >= 8) {
  cat("\nShift rule fires: the process has moved to a new, lower level\n")
}


Shift rule fires: the process has moved to a new, lower level





post_intervention['below_centre'] = post_intervention['falls_count'] < centre_line

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy

print("Shift rule: 8+ consecutive points one side of the centre line\n")

Shift rule: 8+ consecutive points one side of the centre line

print(f"Post-intervention points below baseline centre line: {post_intervention['below_centre'].sum()} of {len(post_intervention)}")

Post-intervention points below baseline centre line: 12 of 12

from itertools import groupby
runs = [(k, len(list(g))) for k, g in groupby(post_intervention['below_centre'])]
max_run = max([length for value, length in runs if value], default=0)

print(f"Longest consecutive run below centre: {max_run}")

Longest consecutive run below centre: 12

if max_run >= 8:
    print("\nShift rule fires: the process has moved to a new, lower level")


Shift rule fires: the process has moved to a new, lower level








Statistical Comparison

RPython



baseline_data <- falls_data[falls_data$phase == "Baseline" & !(falls_data$month %in% c(7, 8)), ]
post_data <- falls_data[falls_data$phase == "Post-intervention", ]

cat("Statistical comparison:\n\n")

Statistical comparison:

cat("Baseline (excluding the outbreak months):\n")

Baseline (excluding the outbreak months):

cat("  Mean:", round(mean(baseline_data$falls_count), 2), "falls/month\n")

  Mean: 16.8 falls/month

cat("  Standard deviation:", round(sd(baseline_data$falls_count), 2), "\n")

  Standard deviation: 3.68 

cat("\nPost-intervention:\n")


Post-intervention:

cat("  Mean:", round(mean(post_data$falls_count), 2), "falls/month\n")

  Mean: 10.67 falls/month

cat("  Standard deviation:", round(sd(post_data$falls_count), 2), "\n")

  Standard deviation: 2.84 

reduction <- mean(baseline_data$falls_count) - mean(post_data$falls_count)
pct_reduction <- 100 * reduction / mean(baseline_data$falls_count)

cat("\nDifference:\n")


Difference:

cat("  Absolute reduction:", round(reduction, 2), "falls/month\n")

  Absolute reduction: 6.13 falls/month

cat("  Percentage reduction:", round(pct_reduction, 1), "%\n")

  Percentage reduction: 36.5 %

# Welch two-sample t-test (size of the change, with uncertainty)
t_result <- t.test(baseline_data$falls_count, post_data$falls_count)
cat("\nWelch t-test: p =", format.pval(t_result$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")


Welch t-test: p = 0.000485 

cat("95% CI for the reduction:", round(t_result$conf.int[1], 1), "to",
    round(t_result$conf.int[2], 1), "falls/month\n")

95% CI for the reduction: 3.1 to 9.1 falls/month





from scipy import stats

baseline_data = falls_data[(falls_data['phase'] == 'Baseline') & (~falls_data['month'].isin([7, 8]))]
post_data = falls_data[falls_data['phase'] == 'Post-intervention']

print("Statistical comparison:\n")

Statistical comparison:

print(f"Baseline: mean = {baseline_data['falls_count'].mean():.2f}, "
      f"standard deviation = {baseline_data['falls_count'].std():.2f}")

Baseline: mean = 16.80, standard deviation = 3.68

print(f"Post-intervention: mean = {post_data['falls_count'].mean():.2f}, "
      f"standard deviation = {post_data['falls_count'].std():.2f}")

Post-intervention: mean = 10.67, standard deviation = 2.84

reduction = baseline_data['falls_count'].mean() - post_data['falls_count'].mean()
pct_reduction = 100 * reduction / baseline_data['falls_count'].mean()

print(f"\nReduction: {reduction:.2f} falls/month ({pct_reduction:.1f}%)")


Reduction: 6.13 falls/month (36.5%)

# Welch two-sample t-test (size of the change, with uncertainty)
res = stats.ttest_ind(baseline_data['falls_count'], post_data['falls_count'],
                      equal_var=False)
ci = res.confidence_interval()
print(f"Welch t-test: p = {res.pvalue:.4f}")

Welch t-test: p = 0.0005

print(f"95% CI for the reduction: {ci.low:.1f} to {ci.high:.1f} falls/month")

95% CI for the reduction: 3.1 to 9.1 falls/month
crumbs: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.8 Step 4: Summary and Recommendations

Intervention Effect Detected
Evidence:

Two post-intervention months (20 and 22) fall below the baseline lower control limit
Shift rule fires: all 12 post-intervention months sit below the baseline centre line
The size of the change is statistically clear (Welch t-test p < 0.001, 95% CI roughly 3 to 9 fewer falls per month)
The reduction is practically meaningful (~6 falls/month, about 37%)
The improvement is sustained throughout the 12-month post-intervention period

Evidence strength: strong – with the usual caveat that a chart establishes that the process changed at about the right time, not that the programme caused it. Winter occupancy, resident mix, or recording practice could each contribute; corroborate before celebrating.



Recommendations

Continue program: Falls prevention program has demonstrably reduced falls
Ongoing monitoring: Continue monthly SPC charting
Share best practice: Document program components for sector-wide rollout
Investigate further improvements: Can we reduce further?




Limitations

Single care home (generalisability unknown)
No control group (external factors possible)
12-month follow-up (longer-term sustainability unknown)
Months 7-8 special cause excluded from limit calculation (documented)




Summary
This example demonstrates a complete SPC workflow for intervention evaluation: time-series visualisation and data preparation, a baseline control chart with documented special-cause handling, I-chart limit calculation, intervention detection through the limit and shift rules, statistical confirmation of the change’s size, and stakeholder-ready reporting. The QA record below closes the loop. The natural next step in practice: now that the shift is confirmed and explained, recalculate the limits from the post-intervention months and monitor against the new level.
crumbs: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring

title: 20  Example 2: Care Home Falls Monitoring
text:
20.9 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Statistical Process Control – I-chart


QASQA question
Q3: Analysis – was the method appropriate and correctly applied?; Q4: Delivery & Communication – does the chart communicate the finding clearly?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; D: Communication of findings


Data source
Monthly falls counts (synthetic illustrative data – seed 42)


Key decisions
Baseline period months 1–12 used for control limits; months 7–8 (documented flu outbreak) excluded from the limit calculation but retained on the chart; I-chart chosen (one count per period)


Assumptions
Process stable in baseline period; flu outbreak is a known special cause unrelated to underlying care quality; post-intervention period is independent


Limitations
Single care home – cannot generalise; short post-intervention period (12 months) limits sustainability assessment; regression to the mean possible


Sensitivity
Conclusion holds with the outbreak months included in the limits (the shift rule still fires on all 12 post-intervention months); the limit breaches at months 20 and 22 appear only with the outbreak excluded


Next steps
Continue monitoring for at least 12 more months to confirm sustainability; triangulate with inspection findings and resident outcomes


Sign-off
________ (Analyst) ________ (Peer reviewer) ________ (Date)

21.1 Scenario Overview
Method: z-scoring with transformation Complexity: Medium-High Time to complete: 2-3 hours QASQA questions: Q3 (Analysis); Q5 (Fitness for Purpose)
crumbs: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
NoteMethod Reference



For the z-scoring method itself – including the transformation cascade that CQC’s standard method applies to non-normal data, and the Working z-scores that take over when no transformation works – see the chapter on comparing provider performance. For skewness and transformation in general, see distribution shape.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.
crumbs: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.2 Scenario
You are analysing wait times from assessment to treatment across 150 community mental health services using the 2024 Community Mental Health Survey. Most services have wait times under 3 months, but some have waits exceeding 6 months, creating a highly skewed distribution.
Analytical Question: Which mental health services have wait times that are statistically significantly worse than the national average, accounting for the skewed distribution?
Context: Mental health wait times are a key quality indicator. The data shows substantial right skew - most services perform well, but a minority have very long waits. Standard z-scoring assumes normality, which may not hold here.
crumbs: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Identify non-normal distributions through visual and statistical checks
Understand when z-scoring assumptions fail and why it matters
Apply data transformations (log transformation) to reduce skew
Compare approaches: raw z-scores vs transformed z-scores
Interpret results when assumptions are violated
Conduct sensitivity analysis to assess robustness
Communicate findings about skewed data to stakeholders
Document analytical decisions when methods require adaptation
crumbs: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: data/mental_health_wait_times.csv
150 mental health services with the following variables:









Variable
Description
Type
Notes




service_code
Unique service identifier
String
Format: MHS001-MHS150


service_name
Service name
String
Synthetic names


trust_name
NHS Trust
String
50 trusts


region
NHS England region
String
7 regions


wait_time_days
Median wait time (days)
Integer
30-250 days


survey_responses
Survey respondents
Integer
50-300


deprivation_score
Area deprivation (IMD)
Float
10-40





Data Characteristics
Realistic issues included: - Strong positive skew (right tail) - Most services 60-90 days, some >180 days - No missing data (complete survey) - Variation in survey response rates - Deprivation correlation (more deprived = longer waits)
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42) for reproducibility). The data uses log-normal distribution to create realistic right skew. Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.6 Step 2: Assess Distribution
Purpose: Determine if data is suitable for standard z-scoring.


Visualise Distribution

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

ggplot(mh_data, aes(x = wait_time_days)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#99C9F7", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(mh_data$wait_time_days),
             color = cqc_ink, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median(mh_data$wait_time_days),
             color = "#A83DA4", linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Wait times trail off in a long right tail",
    subtitle = paste("Mean (dashed) =", round(mean(mh_data$wait_time_days), 1),
                    "days | Median (dotted) =", round(median(mh_data$wait_time_days), 1), "days"),
    x = "Wait time (days)",
    y = "Number of services"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 21.1: The long right tail is the problem this example exists for. Most services sit between 60 and 90 days, but the tail stretches past 200 - and the mean (dashed) is dragged about eight days above the median (dotted), the classic signature of positive skew.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK

apply_cqc_style()

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.hist(mh_data['wait_time_days'], bins=30, color='#99C9F7', edgecolor='white')
plt.axvline(mh_data['wait_time_days'].mean(), color=CQC_INK, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, label='Mean')
plt.axvline(mh_data['wait_time_days'].median(), color='#A83DA4', linestyle=':', linewidth=1.5, label='Median')
plt.xlabel('Wait time (days)')
plt.ylabel('Number of services')
plt.title(f"Wait times trail off in a long right tail\nMean = {mh_data['wait_time_days'].mean():.1f} days | Median = {mh_data['wait_time_days'].median():.1f} days")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 21.2: The long right tail is the problem this example exists for. Most services sit between 60 and 90 days, but the tail stretches past 200 - and the mean (dashed) is dragged about eight days above the median (dotted), the classic signature of positive skew.








Observations: - Clear positive skew (right tail) - Mean > Median (indicates skewness) - Most services cluster 60-90 days - Long tail extends to 200+ days



Check Skewness Statistically

RPython



# Skewness: average cubed distance from the mean, in standard-deviation units
x <- mh_data$wait_time_days
skewness_value <- mean((x - mean(x))^3) / sd(x)^3
cat("Skewness:", round(skewness_value, 2), "\n")

Skewness: 1.16 

cat("Interpretation:",
    ifelse(abs(skewness_value) < 0.5, "Approximately symmetric",
    ifelse(skewness_value > 1, "Substantially positively skewed",
    ifelse(skewness_value < -1, "Substantially negatively skewed",
           "Moderately skewed"))), "\n")

Interpretation: Substantially positively skewed 

# Q-Q plot
ggplot(mh_data, aes(sample = wait_time_days)) +
  stat_qq(colour = "#375071", alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#A83DA4") +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q plot: the right tail breaks from normality",
    x = "Theoretical quantiles",
    y = "Sample quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 21.3: The upper tail peels away from the reference line - exactly the departure from normality the histogram suggested. The body of the distribution behaves; the right tail does not, and that tail is where flagging decisions live.








from scipy.stats import skew

skewness_value = skew(mh_data['wait_time_days'])
print(f"Skewness: {skewness_value:.2f}")

Skewness: 1.17

if abs(skewness_value) < 0.5:
    interpretation = "Approximately symmetric"
elif skewness_value > 1:
    interpretation = "Substantially positively skewed"
elif skewness_value < -1:
    interpretation = "Substantially negatively skewed"
else:
    interpretation = "Moderately skewed"

print(f"Interpretation: {interpretation}")

Interpretation: Substantially positively skewed

# Q-Q plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))
stats.probplot(mh_data['wait_time_days'], dist="norm", plot=ax)

        81,  82,  82,  82,  83,  83,  83,  86,  86,  87,  88,  91,  92,
        92,  92,  93,  93,  94,  94,  95,  98,  98, 100, 101, 102, 102,
       102, 103, 104, 104, 105, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 110, 113, 116,
       116, 117, 118, 120, 122, 124, 129, 130, 130, 136, 137, 140, 141,
       147, 153, 157, 164, 172, 173, 215])), (np.float64(31.476063258350987), np.float64(79.80666666666667), np.float64(0.9592998035974385)))

ax.get_lines()[0].set_color('#375071')
ax.get_lines()[0].set_alpha(0.6)
ax.get_lines()[1].set_color('#A83DA4')
ax.set_title("Q-Q plot: the right tail breaks from normality")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 21.4: The upper tail peels away from the reference line - exactly the departure from normality the histogram suggested. The body of the distribution behaves; the right tail does not, and that tail is where flagging decisions live.








Assessment: - Skewness > 1 indicates substantial positive skew - Q-Q plot shows departure from normality in right tail - Standard z-scoring assumptions are violated
Decision: Compare three approaches: 1. Raw z-scores (despite skewness) 2. Log-transformed z-scores 3. Rank-based approach (percentiles)

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.7 Step 3: Approach 1 - Raw Z-scores
Purpose: Calculate standard z-scores despite skewness to see the impact.


RPython



# Calculate raw z-scores
mh_data$z_score_raw <- scale(mh_data$wait_time_days)[,1]

# Assign CQC's descriptive bands (longer waits = worse, so the "above"
# bands are the concerning ones for this indicator)
band_breaks <- c(-Inf, -3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3, Inf)
band_labels <- c("Markedly below", "Below", "Somewhat below", "In line",
                 "Somewhat above", "Above", "Markedly above")
mh_data$band_raw <- cut(mh_data$z_score_raw,
                        breaks = band_breaks, labels = band_labels)

# Summary
table(mh_data$band_raw)


Markedly below          Below Somewhat below        In line Somewhat above 
             0              0             18            110             15 
         Above Markedly above 
             6              1 

# Flag services beyond +2 (Above / Markedly above)
flagged_raw <- mh_data[mh_data$z_score_raw >= 2, ]
cat("\nServices flagged (raw z-scores):", nrow(flagged_raw), "\n")


Services flagged (raw z-scores): 7 





# Calculate raw z-scores
mh_data['z_score_raw'] = (mh_data['wait_time_days'] - mh_data['wait_time_days'].mean()) / mh_data['wait_time_days'].std()

# Assign CQC's descriptive bands (longer waits = worse, so the "above"
# bands are the concerning ones for this indicator)
band_bins = [-np.inf, -3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3, np.inf]
band_labels = ["Markedly below", "Below", "Somewhat below", "In line",
               "Somewhat above", "Above", "Markedly above"]
mh_data['band_raw'] = pd.cut(mh_data['z_score_raw'],
                              bins=band_bins, labels=band_labels)

# Summary
print(mh_data['band_raw'].value_counts().sort_index())

band_raw
Markedly below      0
Below               0
Somewhat below     18
In line           110
Somewhat above     15
Above               6
Markedly above      1
Name: count, dtype: int64

# Flag services beyond +2 (Above / Markedly above)
flagged_raw = mh_data[mh_data['z_score_raw'] >= 2]
print(f"\nServices flagged (raw z-scores): {len(flagged_raw)}")


Services flagged (raw z-scores): 7

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.8 Step 4: Approach 2 - Log-Transformed Z-scores
Purpose: Apply log transformation to reduce skew, then calculate z-scores.


RPython



# Log transform wait times
mh_data$log_wait_time <- log(mh_data$wait_time_days)

# Calculate z-scores on log scale
mh_data$z_score_log <- scale(mh_data$log_wait_time)[,1]

# Assign the same CQC bands on the log scale
mh_data$band_log <- cut(mh_data$z_score_log,
                        breaks = band_breaks, labels = band_labels)

# Summary
table(mh_data$band_log)


Markedly below          Below Somewhat below        In line Somewhat above 
             0              5             17            104             20 
         Above Markedly above 
             4              0 

# Flag services beyond +2 (Above / Markedly above)
flagged_log <- mh_data[mh_data$z_score_log >= 2, ]
cat("\nServices flagged (log z-scores):", nrow(flagged_log), "\n")


Services flagged (log z-scores): 4 





# Log transform wait times
mh_data['log_wait_time'] = np.log(mh_data['wait_time_days'])

# Calculate z-scores on log scale
mh_data['z_score_log'] = (mh_data['log_wait_time'] - mh_data['log_wait_time'].mean()) / mh_data['log_wait_time'].std()

# Assign the same CQC bands on the log scale
mh_data['band_log'] = pd.cut(mh_data['z_score_log'],
                              bins=band_bins, labels=band_labels)

# Summary
print(mh_data['band_log'].value_counts().sort_index())

band_log
Markedly below      0
Below               5
Somewhat below     17
In line           104
Somewhat above     20
Above               4
Markedly above      0
Name: count, dtype: int64

# Flag services beyond +2 (Above / Markedly above)
flagged_log = mh_data[mh_data['z_score_log'] >= 2]
print(f"\nServices flagged (log z-scores): {len(flagged_log)}")


Services flagged (log z-scores): 4






RPython



ggplot(mh_data, aes(x = log_wait_time)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#99C9F7", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Log-transformed wait times: the skew is gone",
    x = "log(wait time)",
    y = "Number of services"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 21.5: On the log scale the tail folds back in. The transformed distribution is roughly symmetric - the shape standard z-scoring assumes - so distances measured on this scale treat the two tails even-handedly.








plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.hist(mh_data['log_wait_time'], bins=30, color='#99C9F7', edgecolor='white')
plt.xlabel('log(wait time)')
plt.ylabel('Number of services')
plt.title('Log-transformed wait times: the skew is gone')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 21.6: On the log scale the tail folds back in. The transformed distribution is roughly symmetric - the shape standard z-scoring assumes - so distances measured on this scale treat the two tails even-handedly.








Observations: - The log transformation removes most of the skew; the transformed distribution is close to symmetric - On the raw scale no service can land more than two standard deviations below the mean (the skew compresses the fast side); the log scale recognises genuinely fast services as well as slow ones - The flagged lists differ between scales - the comparison below quantifies how much

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.9 Step 5: Compare Approaches
Purpose: Assess sensitivity of results to methodological choice.


RPython



# Compare flagged services (z >= 2 on each scale)
raw_codes <- mh_data$service_code[mh_data$z_score_raw >= 2]
log_codes <- mh_data$service_code[mh_data$z_score_log >= 2]

# Agreement
agreement <- length(intersect(raw_codes, log_codes))
raw_only <- length(setdiff(raw_codes, log_codes))
log_only <- length(setdiff(log_codes, raw_codes))

cat("Services flagged by both methods:", agreement, "\n")

Services flagged by both methods: 4 

cat("Flagged by raw z-scores only:", raw_only, "\n")

Flagged by raw z-scores only: 3 

cat("Flagged by log z-scores only:", log_only, "\n")

Flagged by log z-scores only: 0 

cat("Agreement rate:", round(100 * agreement / max(length(raw_codes), length(log_codes)), 1), "%\n")

Agreement rate: 57.1 %

# Scatter plot comparing approaches
ggplot(mh_data, aes(x = z_score_raw, y = z_score_log)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, colour = "#375071") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2, color = "#A83DA4", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2, color = "#A83DA4", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "The two scales agree on the worst services and disagree at the margin",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines show the flagging threshold (z >= 2) on each scale",
    x = "Raw z-score",
    y = "Log-transformed z-score"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 21.7: Every service the log scale flags, the raw scale flags too - but not the reverse. The points curve away from the diagonal because the raw scale stretches the right tail: three services clear the raw threshold only by riding the skew, and drop out once the scale treats both tails fairly.








# Compare flagged services (z >= 2 on each scale)
raw_codes = set(mh_data[mh_data['z_score_raw'] >= 2]['service_code'])
log_codes = set(mh_data[mh_data['z_score_log'] >= 2]['service_code'])

# Agreement
agreement = len(raw_codes & log_codes)
raw_only = len(raw_codes - log_codes)
log_only = len(log_codes - raw_codes)

print(f"Services flagged by both methods: {agreement}")

Services flagged by both methods: 4

print(f"Flagged by raw z-scores only: {raw_only}")

Flagged by raw z-scores only: 3

print(f"Flagged by log z-scores only: {log_only}")

Flagged by log z-scores only: 0

print(f"Agreement rate: {100 * agreement / max(len(raw_codes), len(log_codes)):.1f}%")

Agreement rate: 57.1%

# Scatter plot comparing approaches
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 7))
plt.scatter(mh_data['z_score_raw'], mh_data['z_score_log'], alpha=0.6, color='#375071')
plt.axhline(y=2, color='#A83DA4', linestyle='--', label='Flagging threshold (z = 2)')
plt.axvline(x=2, color='#A83DA4', linestyle='--')
plt.xlabel('Raw z-score')
plt.ylabel('Log-transformed z-score')
plt.title('The two scales agree on the worst services and disagree at the margin')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 21.8: Every service the log scale flags, the raw scale flags too - but not the reverse. The points curve away from the diagonal because the raw scale stretches the right tail: three services clear the raw threshold only by riding the skew, and drop out once the scale treats both tails fairly.
crumbs: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.10 Step 6: Recommendation
Purpose: Document analytical decision and rationale.

Decision: use log-transformed z-scores for the final analysis.
Rationale: 1. Assumption validity: the log transformation brings the data close to the normality z-scoring assumes 2. Even-handed tails: the raw scale can neither recognise genuinely fast services nor avoid over-flagging slow ones; the log scale treats both directions fairly 3. Consistency with CQC’s method: the standard z-scoring method itself searches a transformation cascade (including log) for non-normal continuous data, so transforming is not a departure from the guidance - it is the guidance 4. Interpretability: results can be back-transformed to days for reporting
Caveats: - The log transformation changes the metric - differences become multiplicative (“50% longer”) rather than additive (“30 days longer”) - Services near the threshold may flip between methods; document both flagged lists - If no transformation had worked, the next step would be Working z-scores, not forcing the raw scale
Communication: report wait times in the original scale (days) but use the log-scale z-scores for the statistical comparison.

                           "z_score_log", "band_log")]

flagged_final <- flagged_final[order(-flagged_final$z_score_log), ]

144       MHS145  Mental Health Service 145  ...     2.757139     Above

44        MHS045   Mental Health Service 45  ...     2.180183     Above

[4 rows x 5 columns]







Summary
This example demonstrates z-scoring when the data refuse to be normal: check the distribution before computing anything, transform when the shape demands it (as CQC’s own standard method does), compare the flagged lists across approaches as a built-in sensitivity analysis, and report in units stakeholders can act on. The QA record below closes the loop.

title: 21  Example 3: Mental Health Wait Times
text:
21.12 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Z-scoring with log transformation


QASQA question
Q3: Analysis – was the method appropriate and correctly applied?; Q5: Fitness for Purpose – does the method handle the skewed distribution appropriately?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; E: Quality assurance and reproducibility


Data source
Mental health wait times by provider (synthetic illustrative data)


Key decisions
Log transformation applied after confirming positive skew (histogram, skewness statistic, Q-Q plot); Working z-scores noted as the fallback had no transformation worked; results back-transformed for reporting


Assumptions
Log transformation adequately normalises the distribution; providers are comparable (same pathway definition)


Limitations
Transformation changes scale – absolute differences harder to interpret; no zero waits in this data, but any zeros would require an offset before the log (or an alternative method)


Sensitivity
Results compared between untransformed and log-transformed z-scores; top-flagged providers stable across both


Next steps
Validate transformation choice with Guild if used for formal profiling; consider Working z-scores for high-stakes decisions


Sign-off
________ (Analyst) ________ (Peer reviewer) ________ (Date)

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.1 Scenario Overview
Method: paired t-test Complexity: Medium Time to complete: 2-3 hours QASQA questions: Q2 (Design); Q3 (Analysis)

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
NoteMethod Reference



For the t-test method itself – the paired design, assumptions, effect sizes, and the verdict-trap caution for pilot evaluations – see the chapter on comparing two groups.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.2 Scenario
A medication safety intervention was piloted in 15 care homes over 6 months. The intervention included staff training, medication review protocols, and enhanced monitoring. You need to assess whether the intervention reduced medication incidents to inform a decision about wider rollout.
Analytical Question: Did the medication safety intervention significantly reduce medication incident rates in the pilot care homes?
Context: This is a small-scale pilot with before/after measurements in the same care homes (paired design). One care home showed a very large reduction, raising questions about outliers. The decision to roll out the intervention trust-wide depends on demonstrating effectiveness.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Understand paired vs independent designs and when each is appropriate
Handle small samples (N=15) and their implications
Check paired t-test assumptions (normality of differences, no outliers)
Identify and handle outliers in small samples
Calculate and interpret effect sizes (Cohen’s d)
Distinguish statistical vs practical significance
Assess power and risk of Type II error
Communicate findings for decision-making with limited evidence

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: data/medication_safety_data.csv
15 care homes with before/after measurements:









Variable
Description
Type
Notes




home_id
Care home identifier
String
CH01-CH15


home_name
Care home name
String
Synthetic names


beds
Number of beds
Integer
20-80 beds


incidents_before
Incidents per month (baseline)
Float
3-12 incidents


incidents_after
Incidents per month (post-intervention)
Float
2-10 incidents


difference
Change (after - before)
Float
Negative = improvement





Data Characteristics
Realistic patterns: - Small sample (N=15 care homes) - Paired measurements (same homes before/after) - One home with a strikingly large proportional reduction (about 65%) - Most homes show modest improvement - Some variation in home size
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42)). Most homes show reductions of roughly 20-35%; one home (CH03) shows about 65%. Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.6 Step 2: Visualise Paired Data
Purpose: Understand the pattern of change across care homes.


RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

ggplot(med_data) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = incidents_before, xend = incidents_after,
                   y = reorder(home_id, incidents_before),
                   yend = reorder(home_id, incidents_before)),
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")),
               color = "#007EF5", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(x = incidents_before, y = reorder(home_id, incidents_before)),
             color = "#375071", size = 2.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Every home reduced its incident rate",
    subtitle = "From before (circle) to after (arrowhead)",
    x = "Incidents per month",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 22.1: Every one of the fifteen homes reduced its incident rate - all the arrows point the same way. Each home runs from its before value (circle) to its after value (arrowhead); none of the arrows points right.






ggplot(med_data, aes(x = difference)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 8, fill = "#99C9F7", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = cqc_ink, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "All fifteen changes are reductions",
    x = "Change in incidents (after - before)",
    y = "Number of care homes"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 22.2: All fifteen changes are reductions - the entire distribution of differences sits left of zero, clustering between about half an incident and three incidents per month.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK

apply_cqc_style()

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5.5))

# Slope plot
for idx, row in med_data.iterrows():
    ax1.plot([0, 1], [row['incidents_before'], row['incidents_after']],
             'o-', color='#007EF5', alpha=0.6)

ax1.set_xticks([0, 1])
ax1.set_xticklabels(['Before', 'After'])
ax1.set_ylabel('Incidents per month')
ax1.set_title('Every home declines from before to after')

# Histogram of differences
ax2.hist(med_data['difference'], bins=8, color='#99C9F7', edgecolor='white')
ax2.axvline(x=0, color=CQC_INK, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
ax2.set_xlabel('Change in incidents (after - before)')
ax2.set_ylabel('Number of care homes')
ax2.set_title('All fifteen changes are reductions')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 22.3: Every one of the fifteen homes reduced its incident rate - the arrows all point the same way. Most reductions are modest; none of the changes crosses zero. The change column on the right shows the differences clustering between about half an incident and three incidents per month.








Observations: - All homes show reduction (all arrows point left) - One home (CH03) shows much larger reduction than others - Most changes cluster around -2 to -3 incidents

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.7 Step 3: Check Assumptions
Purpose: Verify paired t-test assumptions before proceeding.


Assumption 1: Normality of Differences

RPython



# Q-Q plot of differences (the quantity the paired test assumes is normal)
ggplot(med_data, aes(sample = difference)) +
  stat_qq(colour = "#375071") +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#A83DA4") +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q plot: normality of the differences",
    x = "Theoretical quantiles",
    y = "Sample quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()

# Shapiro-Wilk test
shapiro_result <- shapiro.test(med_data$difference)
cat("Shapiro-Wilk test p-value:", round(shapiro_result$p.value, 3), "\n")

Shapiro-Wilk test p-value: 0.203 

cat("Interpretation:", 
    ifelse(shapiro_result$p.value > 0.05, 
           "No evidence against normality (p > 0.05)",
           "Evidence of non-normality (p < 0.05)"), "\n")

Interpretation: No evidence against normality (p > 0.05) 








Figure 22.4: The fifteen differences track the reference line without dramatic departures - with this few points the plot can only rule out gross non-normality, and it does. The Shapiro-Wilk test agrees.








# Q-Q plot of differences (the quantity the paired test assumes is normal)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))
stats.probplot(med_data['difference'], dist="norm", plot=ax)

((array([-1.69372899, -1.22918025, -0.93620006, -0.70737068, -0.51078428,
       -0.33229293, -0.1638693 ,  0.        ,  0.1638693 ,  0.33229293,
        0.51078428,  0.70737068,  0.93620006,  1.22918025,  1.69372899]), array([-2.9, -2.6, -2.6, -2.2, -1.9, -1.4, -1.4, -1.2, -1. , -1. , -1. ,
       -0.6, -0.6, -0.4, -0.3])), (np.float64(0.874973525712788), np.float64(-1.4066666666666667), np.float64(0.9693362061116836)))

ax.get_lines()[0].set_color('#375071')
ax.get_lines()[1].set_color('#A83DA4')
ax.set_title("Q-Q plot: normality of the differences")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 22.5: The fifteen differences track the reference line without dramatic departures - with this few points the plot can only rule out gross non-normality, and it does. The Shapiro-Wilk test agrees.




# Shapiro-Wilk test
stat, p_value = stats.shapiro(med_data['difference'])
print(f"Shapiro-Wilk test p-value: {p_value:.3f}")

Shapiro-Wilk test p-value: 0.203

if p_value > 0.05:
    print("Interpretation: No evidence against normality (p > 0.05)")
else:
    print("Interpretation: Evidence of non-normality (p < 0.05)")

Interpretation: No evidence against normality (p > 0.05)







Assumption 2: Check for Outliers

RPython



# Boxplot of differences
ggplot(med_data, aes(x = "", y = difference)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#99C9F7", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, size = 2.5, alpha = 0.6, colour = "#375071") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = cqc_ink, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "The differences hold no formal outlier",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Change in incidents (after - before)"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()

# Identify potential outliers (>1.5 IQR from quartiles)
Q1 <- quantile(med_data$difference, 0.25)
Q3 <- quantile(med_data$difference, 0.75)
IQR <- Q3 - Q1
outliers <- med_data[med_data$difference < (Q1 - 1.5*IQR) | 
                     med_data$difference > (Q3 + 1.5*IQR), ]

if (nrow(outliers) > 0) {
  cat("\nPotential outliers detected:\n")
  print(outliers[, c("home_id", "home_name", "incidents_before", 
                     "incidents_after", "difference")])
} else {
  cat("\nNo outliers detected by IQR method\n")
}


No outliers detected by IQR method








Figure 22.6: No formal outlier: the box plot draws no points beyond its whiskers, and the IQR rule below agrees. The largest absolute reductions (around three incidents a month) sit at the whisker’s end, not past it - what makes CH03 unusual is its proportional reduction, which the differences alone cannot show.








# Boxplot of differences
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
ax.boxplot(med_data['difference'], vert=False,
           patch_artist=True,
           boxprops=dict(facecolor='#99C9F7'))

{'whiskers': [<matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x000001EFFFF60AA0>, <matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x000001EFFFEDA0C0>], 'caps': [<matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x000001EFFFFAE900>, <matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x000001EFFFFAE420>], 'boxes': [<matplotlib.patches.PathPatch object at 0x000001EFFFF20C20>], 'medians': [<matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x000001EFFFFAE120>], 'fliers': [<matplotlib.lines.Line2D object at 0x000001EFFFFADCA0>], 'means': []}

ax.scatter(med_data['difference'], [1] * len(med_data),
           alpha=0.6, s=60, color='#375071')
ax.axvline(x=0, color=CQC_INK, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
ax.set_xlabel('Change in incidents (after - before)')
ax.set_title('The differences hold no formal outlier')
ax.set_yticks([])

[]

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 22.7: No formal outlier: the box plot draws no points beyond its whiskers, and the IQR rule below agrees. The largest absolute reductions (around three incidents a month) sit at the whisker’s end, not past it - what makes CH03 unusual is its proportional reduction, which the differences alone cannot show.




# Identify potential outliers
Q1 = med_data['difference'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = med_data['difference'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
outliers = med_data[(med_data['difference'] < (Q1 - 1.5*IQR)) | 
                    (med_data['difference'] > (Q3 + 1.5*IQR))]

if len(outliers) > 0:
    print("\nPotential outliers detected:")
    print(outliers[['home_id', 'home_name', 'incidents_before', 
                    'incidents_after', 'difference']])
else:
    print("\nNo outliers detected by IQR method")


No outliers detected by IQR method





Assessment: the IQR rule on the differences finds no formal outlier – the largest absolute reductions (CH05 at −2.9, CH03 and CH14 at −2.6) sit inside the whiskers. What stands out about CH03 is its proportional reduction: from 4.0 to 1.4 incidents per month is a 65% drop, against 20–35% almost everywhere else. The same value can be ordinary on one scale and striking on another – which scale matters is a judgement, not a rule.
Decision: no exclusion is warranted, but CH03’s influence is worth quantifying – run the analysis with and without it as a robustness check.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.8 Step 4: Paired t-test (All Homes)
Purpose: Test whether the intervention reduced incidents, and estimate by how much.


RPython



# Paired t-test (two-sided, as pre-specified; the CI is the headline)
t_result_with <- t.test(med_data$incidents_after,
                        med_data$incidents_before,
                        paired = TRUE)

print(t_result_with)


    Paired t-test

data:  med_data$incidents_after and med_data$incidents_before
t = -6.4367, df = 14, p-value = 1.557e-05
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -1.8753834 -0.9379499
sample estimates:
mean difference 
      -1.406667 

# Effect size (Cohen's d for paired data)
mean_diff <- mean(med_data$difference)
sd_diff <- sd(med_data$difference)
cohens_d <- mean_diff / sd_diff

cat("\nEffect size (Cohen's d):", round(cohens_d, 2), "\n")


Effect size (Cohen's d): -1.66 

cat("Interpretation:", 
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.2, "Negligible",
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.5, "Small",
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.8, "Medium", "Large"))), "\n")

Interpretation: Large 

# 95% CI for mean difference
ci <- t_result_with$conf.int
cat("\n95% CI for mean reduction: [", round(ci[1], 2), ",", round(ci[2], 2), "] incidents\n")


95% CI for mean reduction: [ -1.88 , -0.94 ] incidents





# Paired t-test (two-sided, as pre-specified; the CI is the headline)
t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_rel(med_data['incidents_after'],
                                  med_data['incidents_before'])

print(f"Paired t-test results:")

Paired t-test results:

print(f"t-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}")

t-statistic: -6.437

print(f"p-value: {p_value:.6f}")

p-value: 0.000016

# Effect size (Cohen's d for paired data)
mean_diff = med_data['difference'].mean()
sd_diff = med_data['difference'].std()
cohens_d = mean_diff / sd_diff

print(f"\nEffect size (Cohen's d): {cohens_d:.2f}")


Effect size (Cohen's d): -1.66

if abs(cohens_d) < 0.2:
    interpretation = "Negligible"
elif abs(cohens_d) < 0.5:
    interpretation = "Small"
elif abs(cohens_d) < 0.8:
    interpretation = "Medium"
else:
    interpretation = "Large"
print(f"Interpretation: {interpretation}")

Interpretation: Large

# 95% CI for mean difference
ci = stats.t.interval(0.95, len(med_data)-1, 
                      loc=mean_diff, 
                      scale=stats.sem(med_data['difference']))
print(f"\n95% CI for mean reduction: [{ci[0]:.2f}, {ci[1]:.2f}] incidents")


95% CI for mean reduction: [-1.88, -0.94] incidents





Results (all homes): the mean reduction is about 1.4 incidents per month per home (95% CI roughly 0.9 to 1.9), with a very small p-value and a large standardised effect (d ≈ 1.7). Lead with the interval: the data are consistent with anything from a modest one-incident reduction to nearly two.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.9 Step 5: Sensitivity Analysis
Purpose: quantify how much the conclusion leans on CH03, and cross-check the parametric result with a rank-based test.


RPython



# Re-run without CH03 (the proportionally extreme home)
med_data_no_ch03 <- med_data[med_data$home_id != "CH03", ]

t_result_without <- t.test(med_data_no_ch03$incidents_after,
                           med_data_no_ch03$incidents_before,
                           paired = TRUE)

# Effect size without CH03
cohens_d_no <- mean(med_data_no_ch03$difference) / sd(med_data_no_ch03$difference)

cat("=== Comparison ===\n")

=== Comparison ===

cat("All homes:    p =", format.pval(t_result_with$p.value),
    "| d =", round(cohens_d, 2), "\n")

All homes:    p = 1.5565e-05 | d = -1.66 

cat("Without CH03: p =", format.pval(t_result_without$p.value),
    "| d =", round(cohens_d_no, 2), "\n")

Without CH03: p = 3.6999e-05 | d = -1.63 

# Rank-based cross-check (no normality assumption)
w_result <- wilcox.test(med_data$incidents_after, med_data$incidents_before,
                        paired = TRUE, exact = FALSE)
cat("\nWilcoxon signed-rank (all homes): p =",
    format.pval(w_result$p.value), "\n")


Wilcoxon signed-rank (all homes): p = 0.00071934 





# Re-run without CH03 (the proportionally extreme home)
med_data_no_ch03 = med_data[med_data['home_id'] != "CH03"]

t_stat_no, p_value_no = stats.ttest_rel(med_data_no_ch03['incidents_after'],
                                        med_data_no_ch03['incidents_before'])

# Effect size without CH03
cohens_d_no = (med_data_no_ch03['difference'].mean()
               / med_data_no_ch03['difference'].std())

print("=== Comparison ===")

=== Comparison ===

print(f"All homes:    p = {p_value:.6f} | d = {cohens_d:.2f}")

All homes:    p = 0.000016 | d = -1.66

print(f"Without CH03: p = {p_value_no:.6f} | d = {cohens_d_no:.2f}")

Without CH03: p = 0.000037 | d = -1.63

# Rank-based cross-check (no normality assumption)
w_stat, w_p = stats.wilcoxon(med_data['incidents_after'],
                             med_data['incidents_before'])
print(f"\nWilcoxon signed-rank (all homes): p = {w_p:.4f}")


Wilcoxon signed-rank (all homes): p = 0.0006





Sensitivity analysis: the conclusion barely moves. Without CH03 the p-value stays far below any conventional threshold and the effect size is essentially unchanged; the rank-based Wilcoxon test, which makes no normality assumption, agrees. The finding does not lean on one home or on one set of assumptions.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.10 Step 6: Conclusion and Recommendation
Statistical conclusion: incidents fell by about 1.4 per month per home (95% CI roughly 0.9 to 1.9; p < 0.001), a large standardised effect (d ≈ 1.7), robust to removing the proportionally extreme home and to swapping in a rank-based test. All 15 homes improved.
Practical significance: at the pilot homes’ baseline of about 4.7 incidents per month, a 1.4-incident reduction is roughly 30% – an improvement worth having. Scaled naively across 100 homes that would be about 140 fewer incidents a month, though scaling is exactly where the caveats below bite.
Limitations – the honest reading from the chapter on comparing two groups applies in full:

No control group: incidents may have fallen anyway (seasonal patterns, attention effects, regression to the mean if pilot homes were chosen for high rates)
Pilot homes were not selected at random, and pilots enjoy enthusiasm a rollout will not carry
Small sample (N=15) and a 6-month window limit generalisability and say nothing about sustainability

Recommendation: the pilot has earned a larger, better-designed evaluation – ideally with comparison homes – alongside any phased rollout. Monitor implementation with control charts per home, investigate what drove CH03’s exceptional proportional improvement, and plan a 12-month follow-up.

title: 22  Example 4: Medication Safety Pilot
text:
22.11 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Paired t-test (before/after within same care homes)


QASQA question
Q2: Design – is the paired design appropriate for this evaluation?; Q3: Analysis – were assumptions checked and correctly handled?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; E: Quality assurance and reproducibility


Data source
Medication incident rates for 15 pilot care homes, 6 months before/after (synthetic illustrative data)


Key decisions
Paired test (same homes measured twice – preserves within-home correlation); two-sided test with the confidence interval as the headline; Shapiro-Wilk on the differences (not the raw values) confirmed normality; CH03 retained (no formal outlier) with its influence quantified


Assumptions
Same homes measured at both time points; no major confounding changes (staffing, case-mix) during the 6-month period; differences approximately normally distributed


Limitations
No control group – cannot rule out temporal trends (regression to the mean, seasonal effects); small sample (n=15) limits generalisability; 6-month follow-up may not capture sustainability


Sensitivity
Conclusion unchanged without CH03; Wilcoxon signed-rank (no normality assumption) agrees


Next steps
Wider rollout with control group design; investigate CH03 outlier; plan 12-month follow-up evaluation

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.1 Scenario Overview
Method: two-sample (independent) t-test Complexity: Medium Time to complete: 2-3 hours QASQA questions: Q2 (Design); Q3 (Analysis)

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
NoteMethod Reference



For the t-test method itself – Welch’s variant as the default, assumptions, and effect sizes – see the chapter on comparing two groups.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.2 Scenario
You are comparing A&E 4-hour wait performance between two Integrated Care Systems (ICS): North Region ICS and South Region ICS. Each ICS contains 100 hospital trusts. Regional leaders want to know if there’s a statistically significant difference in performance to inform resource allocation and best practice sharing.
Analytical Question: Is there a significant difference in A&E 4-hour wait performance between North Region ICS and South Region ICS?
Context: The NHS target is for 95% of A&E patients to be seen within 4 hours. Both regions are below target, but anecdotal evidence suggests North Region performs better. This analysis will inform whether regional differences exist and whether South Region should adopt North Region practices.

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Understand independent samples design vs paired design
Check assumptions for a two-sample t-test, in order of importance
Apply Welch’s t-test as the default, without a preliminary variance test
Interpret confidence intervals for the difference between means
Consider confounding variables (deprivation) before reading the gap causally
Assess practical vs statistical significance
Communicate findings with appropriate caveats

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: data/ae_performance_data.csv
200 hospital trusts (100 per ICS) with the following variables:









Variable
Description
Type
Notes




trust_code
Trust identifier
String
Format: RXX001-RXX200


trust_name
Trust name
String
Synthetic names


ics
Integrated Care System
String
North / South


ae_performance
% seen within 4 hours
Float
60-85%


monthly_attendances
Monthly A&E attendances
Integer
5000-25000


deprivation_score
Area deprivation (IMD)
Float
10-40


beds
Hospital beds
Integer
200-1000





Data Characteristics
Realistic patterns: - North Region: slightly better performance (mean ~75%) - South Region: slightly worse performance (mean ~72%) - Variation within each region - Deprivation correlation (more deprived = worse performance) - Trust size variation
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42)). North Region has 3% better performance on average. Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.
