title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.6 Step 2: Visualise Distributions
Purpose: Compare distributions between regions visually.


RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

# Side-by-side histograms
north_mean <- mean(ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "North"])
south_mean <- mean(ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "South"])

ggplot(ae_data, aes(x = ae_performance, fill = ics)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = north_mean, color = "#007EF5", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = south_mean, color = "#A57703", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("North" = "#007EF5", "South" = "#A57703")) +
  labs(
    title = "North sits about three points right of South, with heavy overlap",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines show regional means",
    x = "% seen within 4 hours",
    y = "Number of trusts",
    fill = "Region"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 23.1: North’s distribution sits about three percentage points to the right of South’s - and the two overlap heavily. The question for the test is whether a gap of this size, against this much overlap, survives the noise.






ggplot(ae_data, aes(x = ics, y = ae_performance, fill = ics)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.3, colour = "#375071") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("North" = "#007EF5", "South" = "#A57703")) +
  labs(
    title = "Offset but interleaved: the regions overlap trust by trust",
    x = "Region",
    y = "% seen within 4 hours"
  ) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")







Figure 23.2: The same comparison as box plots, with every trust shown: the boxes are offset but interleaved, and several South trusts outperform the North median - a reminder that the regional gap says little about any individual trust.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_INK

apply_cqc_style()

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5.5))

# Histograms
north_data = ae_data[ae_data['ics'] == 'North']['ae_performance']
south_data = ae_data[ae_data['ics'] == 'South']['ae_performance']

ax1.hist(north_data, bins=20, alpha=0.6, label='North', color='#007EF5')
ax1.hist(south_data, bins=20, alpha=0.6, label='South', color='#A57703')
ax1.axvline(north_data.mean(), color='#007EF5', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
ax1.axvline(south_data.mean(), color='#A57703', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5)
ax1.set_xlabel('% seen within 4 hours')
ax1.set_ylabel('Number of trusts')
ax1.set_title('Three points apart, heavily overlapping')
ax1.legend()

# Boxplots
bp = ax2.boxplot([north_data, south_data], tick_labels=['North', 'South'],
                 patch_artist=True)
bp['boxes'][0].set_facecolor('#007EF5')
bp['boxes'][1].set_facecolor('#A57703')
ax2.set_ylabel('% seen within 4 hours')
ax2.set_title('Offset but interleaved')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 23.3: North’s distribution sits about three percentage points to the right of South’s - and the two overlap heavily. The box plots make the same point trust by trust: offset but interleaved, with several South trusts above the North median.








Observations: - North Region appears to have higher mean - Distributions look approximately normal - Similar spread (variance) in both regions - Substantial overlap between regions

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.7 Step 3: Check Assumptions
Purpose: Verify two-sample t-test assumptions.


Assumption 1: Normality

RPython



# Shapiro-Wilk test for each region (plots are the primary check)
north_shapiro <- shapiro.test(ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "North"])
south_shapiro <- shapiro.test(ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "South"])

cat("North Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value:", round(north_shapiro$p.value, 3), "\n")

North Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value: 0.237 

cat("South Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value:", round(south_shapiro$p.value, 3), "\n")

South Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value: 0.961 

# Q-Q plots
ggplot(ae_data, aes(sample = ae_performance)) +
  stat_qq(colour = "#375071", alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_qq_line(colour = "#A83DA4") +
  facet_wrap(~ics) +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q plots: both regions consistent with normality",
    x = "Theoretical quantiles",
    y = "Sample quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 23.4: Both regions track their reference lines closely - no visible departure from normality in either, consistent with the Shapiro-Wilk results. With 100 trusts per group the test would be robust to moderate departures anyway.








# Shapiro-Wilk test for each region (plots are the primary check)
north_stat, north_p = stats.shapiro(north_data)
south_stat, south_p = stats.shapiro(south_data)

print(f"North Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value: {north_p:.3f}")

North Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value: 0.237

print(f"South Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value: {south_p:.3f}")

South Region Shapiro-Wilk p-value: 0.961

# Q-Q plots
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

for ax, data, name in [(ax1, north_data, "North"), (ax2, south_data, "South")]:
    stats.probplot(data, dist="norm", plot=ax)
    ax.get_lines()[0].set_color('#375071')
    ax.get_lines()[0].set_alpha(0.6)
    ax.get_lines()[1].set_color('#A83DA4')
    ax.set_title(f"Q-Q plot: {name} region")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 23.5: Both regions track their reference lines closely - no visible departure from normality in either, consistent with the Shapiro-Wilk results. With 100 trusts per group the test would be robust to moderate departures anyway.










Assumption 2: Comparable Spreads (Described, Not Gatekept)

RPython



# Describe the spreads. We use Welch's t-test regardless - running a
# preliminary variance test to choose the t-test distorts the error rates
# the test is supposed to guarantee.
north_perf <- ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "North"]
south_perf <- ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "South"]
north_sd <- sd(north_perf)
south_sd <- sd(south_perf)

cat("North standard deviation:", round(north_sd, 2), "\n")

North standard deviation: 4.08 

cat("South standard deviation:", round(south_sd, 2), "\n")

South standard deviation: 3.99 

cat("Ratio:", round(max(north_sd, south_sd) / min(north_sd, south_sd), 2), "\n")

Ratio: 1.02 





# Describe the spreads. We use Welch's t-test regardless - running a
# preliminary variance test to choose the t-test distorts the error rates
# the test is supposed to guarantee.
north_sd = north_data.std()
south_sd = south_data.std()

print(f"North standard deviation: {north_sd:.2f}")

North standard deviation: 4.08

print(f"South standard deviation: {south_sd:.2f}")

South standard deviation: 3.99

print(f"Ratio: {max(north_sd, south_sd) / min(north_sd, south_sd):.2f}")

Ratio: 1.02





Assessment: both distributions are consistent with normality and the spreads are nearly identical. Welch’s t-test is the right choice either way – it costs essentially nothing when variances are equal (as here) and protects the error rates when they are not, which is why the t-tests chapter recommends it as the default rather than as a fallback after a variance test.

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.8 Step 4: Welch Two-Sample t-test
Purpose: Test whether the regional difference survives the noise, and estimate its size.


RPython



# Welch two-sample t-test (R's default)
t_result <- t.test(ae_performance ~ ics, data = ae_data)
print(t_result)


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  ae_performance by ics
t = 5.2232, df = 197.9, p-value = 4.437e-07
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group North and group South is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 1.853860 4.102808
sample estimates:
mean in group North mean in group South 
           75.01343            72.03510 

# Rank-based cross-check (no normality assumption)
w_result <- wilcox.test(ae_performance ~ ics, data = ae_data, exact = FALSE)
cat("Mann-Whitney U cross-check: p =", format.pval(w_result$p.value), "\n")

Mann-Whitney U cross-check: p = 3.2575e-07 

# Effect size (Cohen's d)
north_mean <- mean(ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "North"])
south_mean <- mean(ae_data$ae_performance[ae_data$ics == "South"])
n_north <- sum(ae_data$ics == "North")
n_south <- sum(ae_data$ics == "South")
pooled_sd <- sqrt(((n_north - 1) * north_sd^2 + (n_south - 1) * south_sd^2) / 
                  (n_north + n_south - 2))
cohens_d <- (north_mean - south_mean) / pooled_sd

cat("\nEffect size (Cohen's d):", round(cohens_d, 2), "\n")


Effect size (Cohen's d): 0.74 

cat("Interpretation:", 
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.2, "Negligible",
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.5, "Small",
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.8, "Medium", "Large"))), "\n")

Interpretation: Medium 

cat("\nMean difference:", round(north_mean - south_mean, 2), "percentage points\n")


Mean difference: 2.98 percentage points

cat("95% CI for difference: [", round(t_result$conf.int[1], 2), ",", 
    round(t_result$conf.int[2], 2), "]\n")

95% CI for difference: [ 1.85 , 4.1 ]





# Welch two-sample t-test (equal_var=False)
res = stats.ttest_ind(north_data, south_data, equal_var=False)
t_stat, p_value = res.statistic, res.pvalue

print(f"Welch two-sample t-test results:")

Welch two-sample t-test results:

print(f"t-statistic: {t_stat:.3f}")

t-statistic: 5.223

print(f"p-value: {p_value:.2e}")

p-value: 4.44e-07

# Rank-based cross-check (no normality assumption)
u_stat, u_p = stats.mannwhitneyu(north_data, south_data)
print(f"Mann-Whitney U cross-check: p = {u_p:.2e}")

Mann-Whitney U cross-check: p = 3.26e-07

# Effect size (Cohen's d)
north_mean = north_data.mean()
south_mean = south_data.mean()
pooled_sd = np.sqrt(((len(north_data) - 1) * north_sd**2 + (len(south_data) - 1) * south_sd**2) / 
                    (len(north_data) + len(south_data) - 2))
cohens_d = (north_mean - south_mean) / pooled_sd

print(f"\nEffect size (Cohen's d): {cohens_d:.2f}")


Effect size (Cohen's d): 0.74

if abs(cohens_d) < 0.2:
    interpretation = "Negligible"
elif abs(cohens_d) < 0.5:
    interpretation = "Small"
elif abs(cohens_d) < 0.8:
    interpretation = "Medium"
else:
    interpretation = "Large"
print(f"Interpretation: {interpretation}")

Interpretation: Medium

print(f"\nMean difference: {north_mean - south_mean:.2f} percentage points")


Mean difference: 2.98 percentage points

# 95% CI for the difference (from the Welch test itself)
ci = res.confidence_interval()
print(f"95% CI for difference: [{ci.low:.2f}, {ci.high:.2f}]")

95% CI for difference: [1.85, 4.10]





Results: North performs about 3 percentage points better (95% CI roughly 1.9 to 4.1), a clearly non-chance difference (p < 0.001, and the rank-based cross-check agrees). Cohen’s d ≈ 0.7 – a medium-to-large standardised effect, though the interval is the more useful number: the data are consistent with a regional gap anywhere from about two points to about four.

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.9 Step 5: Explore Confounding
Purpose: Check if deprivation explains the regional difference.


RPython



# Check if deprivation differs between regions
deprivation_by_region <- aggregate(deprivation_score ~ ics, data = ae_data,
                                   FUN = function(x) round(mean(x), 2))
print(deprivation_by_region)

    ics deprivation_score
1 North             25.69
2 South             25.69

# Correlation between deprivation and performance
cor_test <- cor.test(ae_data$deprivation_score, ae_data$ae_performance)
cat("\nCorrelation between deprivation and performance:",
    round(cor_test$estimate, 2), "\n")


Correlation between deprivation and performance: -0.21 

cat("p-value:", format.pval(cor_test$p.value), "\n")

p-value: 0.0025559 

# Scatter plot
ggplot(ae_data, aes(x = deprivation_score, y = ae_performance, color = ics)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("North" = "#007EF5", "South" = "#A57703")) +
  labs(
    title = "The regional gap persists across the deprivation range",
    x = "Deprivation score (higher = more deprived)",
    y = "% seen within 4 hours",
    color = "Region"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 23.6: Deprivation cannot explain the regional gap: the two regions have essentially identical deprivation profiles, and within each region performance declines only gently with deprivation. The North trend line sits about three points above the South line across the whole deprivation range.








# Check if deprivation differs between regions
deprivation_by_region = ae_data.groupby('ics')['deprivation_score'].mean()
print("Mean deprivation by region:")

Mean deprivation by region:

print(deprivation_by_region)

ics
North    25.694950
South    25.689267
Name: deprivation_score, dtype: float64

# Correlation between deprivation and performance
corr, p_value_corr = stats.pearsonr(ae_data['deprivation_score'], ae_data['ae_performance'])
print(f"\nCorrelation between deprivation and performance: {corr:.2f}")


Correlation between deprivation and performance: -0.21

print(f"p-value: {p_value_corr:.4f}")

p-value: 0.0026

# Scatter plot
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5.5))
for ics_name, color in [('North', '#007EF5'), ('South', '#A57703')]:
    data_subset = ae_data[ae_data['ics'] == ics_name]
    plt.scatter(data_subset['deprivation_score'], data_subset['ae_performance'],
                alpha=0.6, label=ics_name, color=color)
    # Add trend line
    z = np.polyfit(data_subset['deprivation_score'], data_subset['ae_performance'], 1)
    p = np.poly1d(z)
    plt.plot(data_subset['deprivation_score'].sort_values(),
             p(data_subset['deprivation_score'].sort_values()),
             color=color, linestyle='--')

plt.xlabel('Deprivation score (higher = more deprived)')
plt.ylabel('% seen within 4 hours')
plt.title('The regional gap persists across the deprivation range')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 23.7: Deprivation cannot explain the regional gap: the two regions have essentially identical deprivation profiles, and within each region performance declines only gently with deprivation. The North trend line sits about three points above the South line across the whole deprivation range.








Findings: - Deprivation similar in both regions - Negative correlation: more deprived areas have worse performance - Regional difference persists after accounting for deprivation

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.10 Step 6: Conclusion
Statistical conclusion: North performs about 3 percentage points better (95% CI 1.9 to 4.1; p < 0.001; Cohen’s d ≈ 0.7, medium-to-large), the rank-based cross-check agrees, and the gap is not explained by deprivation, which is essentially identical across the two regions.
Practical significance: at a typical trust’s roughly 12,500 monthly attendances, 3 percentage points is about 370 more patients seen within four hours per trust per month – across 100 trusts, tens of thousands of patients. Operationally meaningful by any reading.
Limitations: - Cross-sectional comparison, not causal – “North does something better” is one explanation among several - Other confounders unmeasured (staffing, facilities, case-mix) - The within-region variation dwarfs the between-region gap: many South trusts outperform many North trusts - Both regions sit well below the 95% standard
Recommendations: 1. Investigate what high performers do – in both regions; the within-region spread is the larger signal 2. Treat “adopt North’s practices” as a hypothesis, not a conclusion – the gap is real but its cause is unestablished 3. Consider additional factors – staffing, resources, patient acuity – before any causal story is acted on 4. Monitor the gap over time with control charts – is it widening or narrowing?

title: 23  Example 5: Regional A&E Performance
text:
23.11 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Two-sample independent t-test (Welch’s variant – unequal variances not assumed)


QASQA question
Q2: Design – is the two-group comparison appropriate for this question?; Q3: Analysis – were assumptions checked and correctly handled?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; D: Communication of findings


Data source
A&E 4-hour performance by trust, North and South regions (synthetic illustrative data)


Key decisions
Welch’s t-test used as the default (no preliminary variance test – the two-stage routine distorts error rates); Cohen’s d and the confidence interval reported alongside the p-value; both statistical and practical significance interpreted


Assumptions
Trusts within each region are independent; A&E performance is approximately normally distributed within regions


Limitations
Ecological comparison – between-region averages mask within-region variation; no adjustment for trust-level confounders (size, case-mix, urban/rural); cross-sectional snapshot


Sensitivity
Mann-Whitney U (non-parametric) gives consistent directional result


Next steps
Investigate within-South variation; consider multilevel model accounting for trust-level factors; monitor trends over time using SPC


Sign-off
________ (Analyst) ________ (Peer reviewer) ________ (Date)

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.1 Scenario Overview
Method: statistical process control with trend analysis Complexity: Medium-High Time to complete: 2-3 hours QASQA questions: Q3 (Analysis); Q4 (Delivery & Communication)

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
NoteMethod Reference



For the SPC method itself – and the baseline-stability assumption this example deliberately breaks – see the chapter on monitoring trends over time.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.2 Scenario
A hospital trust is monitoring ambulance handover delays (>30 minutes) over 18 months. National guidance states handovers should occur within 15 minutes, with 30 minutes as the maximum acceptable threshold.
Analytical Question: Are handover delays increasing over time? Are there special causes requiring investigation?
Context: This is operational performance monitoring with clear system pressures. The trust needs to understand whether delays are part of common cause variation or indicate special causes requiring intervention.

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.3 Learning Objectives
By completing this example, you will learn how to:

Recognise when a process is not stable enough for standard control limits
See how a trend masks itself: limits computed across a trend inflate until nothing signals
Quantify a trend with regression, and read its uncertainty honestly
Weigh documented events against formal rules when deciding what to investigate
Handle seasonality (winter pressures) as context, not noise
Communicate deteriorating performance without overclaiming what the chart proves
Know when to escalate to trend-aware monitoring methods

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: data/ambulance_handovers.csv
18 months of handover delay data:

1-18


date
Month start date
Date
Jan 2023 - Jun 2024


delays_over_30min
Number of handovers >30 min
Integer
100-310 per month





Data Characteristics
Realistic patterns: - Increasing trend (~5 delays/month increase) - Winter peaks (Dec-Feb): +40 delays - Special causes: IT failure (Month 8), flu outbreak (Month 15) - Natural variation throughout
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42) for reproducibility). Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
section: 24.7 Step 3: Calculate Control Limits (and Watch Them Fail)
text:
24.7 Step 3: Calculate Control Limits (and Watch Them Fail)
Purpose: demonstrate what happens when standard limits are computed across a trending series – a deliberate misstep, made to show why it misleads.


RPython



# Calculate centre line (mean)
centre_line <- mean(handover_data$delays_over_30min)

# Calculate moving range
mr <- abs(diff(handover_data$delays_over_30min))
mean_mr <- mean(mr)

# Calculate control limits
ucl <- centre_line + 2.66 * mean_mr
lcl <- centre_line - 2.66 * mean_mr

cat("Control Limits (full series):\n")

Control Limits (full series):

cat("  Centre Line:", round(centre_line, 1), "\n")

  Centre Line: 210.6 

cat("  UCL:", round(ucl, 1), "\n")

  UCL: 323.4 

cat("  LCL:", round(lcl, 1), "\n")

  LCL: 97.7 

# Identify points outside limits
handover_data$outside_limits <- handover_data$delays_over_30min > ucl | 
                                 handover_data$delays_over_30min < lcl

cat("\nPoints outside limits:\n")


Points outside limits:

print(handover_data[handover_data$outside_limits, c("month", "date", "delays_over_30min")])

[1] month             date              delays_over_30min
<0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)





# Calculate centre line
centre_line = handover_data['delays_over_30min'].mean()

# Calculate moving range
mr = handover_data['delays_over_30min'].diff().abs().dropna()
mean_mr = mr.mean()

# Calculate control limits
ucl = centre_line + 2.66 * mean_mr
lcl = centre_line - 2.66 * mean_mr

print("Control Limits (full series):")

Control Limits (full series):

print(f"  Centre Line: {centre_line:.1f}")

  Centre Line: 210.6

print(f"  UCL: {ucl:.1f}")

  UCL: 323.4

print(f"  LCL: {lcl:.1f}")

  LCL: 97.7

# Identify points outside limits
handover_data['outside_limits'] = (handover_data['delays_over_30min'] > ucl) | \
                                   (handover_data['delays_over_30min'] < lcl)

print("\nPoints outside limits:")


Points outside limits:

print(handover_data[handover_data['outside_limits']][['month', 'date', 'delays_over_30min']])

Empty DataFrame
Columns: [month, date, delays_over_30min]
Index: []





Finding: nothing is flagged – no point breaches the limits, not even month 15’s spike to 310. That should surprise you, and the reason is instructive. The centre line has been computed across a rising series, so it sits above the early months and below the late ones; and the month-to-month jumps that feed the moving range include the trend and the spikes themselves, inflating the limits to a width (roughly 98 to 323) that nothing short of a catastrophe would breach. A trending process smears its own signal into the limits meant to detect it. The SPC chapter’s baseline discipline – limits from a stable period only – exists precisely to prevent this, and here no stable period exists.

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
section: 24.8 Step 4: See the Failure on the Chart
text:
24.8 Step 4: See the Failure on the Chart
Purpose: visualise why the full-series limits cannot speak – and where the documented events sit.


RPython



handover_data$documented <- handover_data$month %in% c(8, 15)

ggplot(handover_data, aes(x = month, y = delays_over_30min)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = centre_line, color = cqc_ink, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(ucl, lcl), color = "#4289B3",
             linewidth = 0.6, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(color = cqc_muted, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(color = documented, shape = documented), size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#007EF5", "TRUE" = "#A83DA4"),
                     labels = c("Routine month", "Documented event"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("FALSE" = 16, "TRUE" = 17),
                     labels = c("Routine month", "Documented event"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:18) +
  annotate("text", x = 8, y = handover_data$delays_over_30min[8] + 14,
           label = "IT failure", size = 3, colour = "#A83DA4") +
  annotate("text", x = 15, y = handover_data$delays_over_30min[15] + 14,
           label = "Flu outbreak", size = 3, colour = "#A83DA4") +
  labs(
    title = "Limits computed across a trend are too wide to say anything",
    subtitle = "Full-series I-chart limits; the documented events sit well inside them",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Delays >30 min"
  ) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")







Figure 24.3: Limits computed across a trend are too wide to say anything: even the spike to 310 sits comfortably inside them. The two annotated months coincide with documented operational events - an IT failure and a flu outbreak - which is investigation-worthy knowledge the chart cannot supply.








plt.figure(figsize=(11, 6))

# Centre line and limits (full series - deliberately misleading)
plt.axhline(y=centre_line, color=CQC_INK, linewidth=1.2)
plt.axhline(y=ucl, color='#4289B3', linewidth=1.2, linestyle='--')
plt.axhline(y=lcl, color='#4289B3', linewidth=1.2, linestyle='--')

# Plot data, marking the documented events
documented = handover_data['month'].isin([8, 15])
routine = handover_data[~documented]
events = handover_data[documented]

plt.plot(handover_data['month'], handover_data['delays_over_30min'],
         color=CQC_MUTED, linewidth=0.8)
plt.scatter(routine['month'], routine['delays_over_30min'],
            color='#007EF5', s=50, label='Routine month', zorder=3)
plt.scatter(events['month'], events['delays_over_30min'],
            color='#A83DA4', marker='^', s=70, label='Documented event', zorder=3)

plt.annotate('IT failure', xy=(8, 280), xytext=(8, 294),
             ha='center', fontsize=9, color='#A83DA4')
plt.annotate('Flu outbreak', xy=(15, 310), xytext=(15, 324),
             ha='center', fontsize=9, color='#A83DA4')

plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Delays >30 min')
plt.title('Limits computed across a trend are too wide to say anything')
plt.xticks(range(1, 19))

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.9 Step 5: Assess Trend
Purpose: Quantify the increasing trend using regression.


RPython



# Linear regression
trend_model <- lm(delays_over_30min ~ month, data = handover_data)
summary(trend_model)


Call:
lm(formula = delays_over_30min ~ month, data = handover_data)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-45.33 -29.08 -12.43  15.59  76.29 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  167.222     19.665   8.504 2.49e-07 ***
month          4.561      1.817   2.511   0.0232 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 39.99 on 16 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2826,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2378 
F-statistic: 6.304 on 1 and 16 DF,  p-value: 0.02317

# Extract trend
slope <- coef(trend_model)[2]
intercept <- coef(trend_model)[1]

cat("\nTrend Analysis:\n")


Trend Analysis:

cat("  Slope:", round(slope, 2), "delays per month\n")

  Slope: 4.56 delays per month

cat("  Intercept:", round(intercept, 1), "\n")

  Intercept: 167.2 

cat("  R-squared:", round(summary(trend_model)$r.squared, 3), "\n")

  R-squared: 0.283 

# Predict values
handover_data$predicted <- predict(trend_model)




# Linear regression
from scipy.stats import linregress

slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = linregress(
    handover_data['month'], 
    handover_data['delays_over_30min']
)

print("\nTrend Analysis:")


Trend Analysis:

print(f"  Slope: {slope:.2f} delays per month")

  Slope: 4.56 delays per month

print(f"  Intercept: {intercept:.1f}")

  Intercept: 167.2

print(f"  R-squared: {r_value**2:.3f}")

  R-squared: 0.283

print(f"  p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

  p-value: 0.0232


# Predict values
handover_data['predicted'] = intercept + slope * handover_data['month']




Finding: the fitted trend is about 4.6 additional delays per month (p ≈ 0.02, r² ≈ 0.28). Read those honestly: the upward drift is statistically discernible, but the month-to-month noise is large – the trend explains barely a quarter of the variation, and 18 points cannot pin its slope down precisely. What the regression does establish is that the process is not stable, which is exactly the condition standard control limits require.
It also gives the spikes their proper context: months 8 and 15 sit about 75 delays above the fitted trend – roughly twice the typical month’s deviation, and the two largest in the series. Neither breaches a formal 3-sigma rule, even after detrending: with this few points and this much noise, the rules have little power, and the spikes inflate the very moving ranges used to set the residual limits. The case for investigating those months rests on the combination of elevated values and documented operational events (an IT failure in month 8, a flu outbreak in month 15) – which is how it should be: rules order attention; they do not monopolise it.

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.10 Step 6: Conclusion and Recommendations
Statistical conclusion: the headline finding is the trend – handover delays are rising by roughly 4 to 5 a month, and the process is not in statistical control. Months 8 and 15 are the largest deviations from that trend and coincide with documented operational events; no formal SPC rule convicts them, and the write-up should say so rather than borrow the chart’s authority for a judgement that actually rests on operational knowledge.
Operational interpretation: a rising background level points at system capacity, not at single bad months; winter (month 12 is the next-highest value) compounds it; and the documented events add spikes on top of an already-deteriorating process. The distinction matters for action: events get incident reviews, trends get capacity planning.
Recommendations:

Immediate: investigate the two documented-event months; review capacity planning and staffing against the rising baseline; prepare for winter peaks.
Monitoring: standard limits cannot work on this series – monitor deviations from the fitted trend, or escalate to trend-aware methods (CUSUM, a detrended chart with a longer run of data) with the Guild; recalculate conventional limits only if the trend is arrested and a stable baseline emerges.
System: address the capacity constraints behind the trend; strengthen winter resilience.

Limitations: - Only 18 months of data – little power for formal rules, and too short to model seasonality properly - Single trust (generalisability unknown) - External factors (regional demand, ambulance service changes) not captured - The trend may not stay linear

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays
text:
24.11 Summary
This example is a deliberate counter-example: SPC applied where its core assumption – a stable baseline – does not hold. The full-series limits flag nothing, not because nothing is wrong but because the trend inflates the limits; the regression, not the chart, establishes the deterioration; and the months worth investigating earn that status from documented events plus elevated values, not from a rule. When a process trends, say what the chart cannot prove, quantify the trend honestly, and escalate to trend-aware monitoring. The QA record below closes the loop.

title: 24  Example 6: Ambulance Handover Delays

Detail




Method
SPC I-chart with trend analysis; linear regression for trend quantification


QASQA question
Q3: Analysis – is the SPC method appropriate for trending data?; Q4: Delivery & Communication – does the chart make the deterioration visible to stakeholders?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; D: Communication of findings


Data source
Monthly ambulance handover delay rates (synthetic illustrative data)


Key decisions
Full-series I-chart limits computed and shown to be uninformative (trend smears into limits); regression used to quantify the trend; months 8 and 15 referred for investigation on documented events plus elevated values, with the absence of a formal rule breach stated explicitly


Assumptions
Measurement definition consistent throughout; no structural change in data collection; linear trend adequate over this 18-month window


Limitations
No stable baseline exists, so no conventional control limits are valid on this series; 18 points give formal rules little power; trend may not stay linear; single trust – cannot generalise


Sensitivity
Spikes at months 8 and 15 remain the two largest deviations after detrending; trend direction stable when either spike month is excluded from the regression


Next steps

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
NoteMethod Reference



This example compares rates across multiple groups. The underlying data is count data with varying denominators – see data types for why that needs rate-based rather than raw-count comparison. For standardising and benchmarking rates, see z-scoring; for comparing a measure between two groups, see t-tests. Comparisons across three or more groups (ANOVA, or chi-squared for counts) go beyond this guidance – the Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild (the Guild) can advise through the Quant Clinic.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.
crumbs: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.2 Scenario
CQC is analysing infection rates across 80 care homes using Vivaldi study data. Care homes vary in size (20-100 residents) and quality ratings.
Analytical Question: Do infection rates differ by CQC rating? How should we account for varying home sizes?
Context: This involves count data (infections) with varying denominators (residents). Simple comparisons may be misleading if not properly adjusted for exposure.
crumbs: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.3 Learning Objectives
By completing this example, you will learn how to:

Work with count data and rates
Account for varying denominators (exposure adjustment)
Compare rates across groups (CQC ratings)
Respect small groups: what a category of two homes can and cannot tell you
Apply a multi-group test with multiplicity-corrected follow-ups
Resist the tidy story: report the comparison the data actually supports
Communicate findings about quality and safety honestly
crumbs: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes

Data Characteristics
Realistic patterns: - Varying home sizes (20-98 residents) - Most homes rated “Good” (54 of 80); only two rated “Outstanding” - Inadequate homes have genuinely higher infection rates - Small numbers, both of homes in some categories and of infections in some homes - Poisson-distributed counts
Data generation: Synthetic data generated using R (set.seed(42) for reproducibility). Both R and Python load the same CSV file, ensuring identical results.

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.6 Step 2: Visualise Rates by Quality

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

# Order ratings and show group sizes in the labels
infection_data$cqc_rating <- factor(infection_data$cqc_rating,
                                    levels = c("Outstanding", "Good",
                                              "Requires Improvement", "Inadequate"))
n_by_rating <- table(infection_data$cqc_rating)
labels_n <- paste0(names(n_by_rating), "\n(n = ", n_by_rating, ")")

# CQC bands palette, paired with the rating labels
rating_cols <- c("Outstanding" = "#167F0E", "Good" = "#5DC54B",
                 "Requires Improvement" = "#FC9E80", "Inadequate" = "#F50D08")

ggplot(infection_data, aes(x = cqc_rating, y = infection_rate, fill = cqc_rating)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5, size = 2, colour = "#375071") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = rating_cols) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = labels_n) +
  labs(
    title = "Inadequate homes stand clearly higher; everything else overlaps",
    subtitle = "Infection rate per 100 residents, by CQC rating",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Infection rate (per 100 residents)"
  ) +
  theme_cqc() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")







Figure 25.1: Inadequate homes stand clearly higher; everything else overlaps. Good and Requires Improvement homes span almost the same range, and the Outstanding category contains just two homes - too few for its box to mean anything. Group sizes are printed under each label because they change how much each box deserves to be trusted.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style

apply_cqc_style()

# Order ratings and show group sizes in the labels
rating_order = ["Outstanding", "Good", "Requires Improvement", "Inadequate"]
infection_data['cqc_rating'] = pd.Categorical(infection_data['cqc_rating'],
                                               categories=rating_order,
                                               ordered=True)
counts = infection_data['cqc_rating'].value_counts()
labels_n = [f"{r}\n(n = {counts[r]})" for r in rating_order]

# CQC bands palette, paired with the rating labels
rating_cols = ["#167F0E", "#5DC54B", "#FC9E80", "#F50D08"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5.5))
data_by_rating = [infection_data.loc[infection_data['cqc_rating'] == r,
                                     'infection_rate'] for r in rating_order]
bp = ax.boxplot(data_by_rating, tick_labels=labels_n, patch_artist=True)
for box, col in zip(bp['boxes'], rating_cols):
    box.set_facecolor(col)
    box.set_alpha(0.7)

ax.set_ylabel('Infection rate (per 100 residents)')
ax.set_title('Inadequate homes stand clearly higher; everything else overlaps')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()







Figure 25.2: Inadequate homes stand clearly higher; everything else overlaps. Good and Requires Improvement homes span almost the same range, and the Outstanding category contains just two homes - too few for its box to mean anything. Group sizes are printed under each label because they change how much each box deserves to be trusted.








Observations: - Inadequate homes sit visibly higher than every other group - Good and Requires Improvement overlap almost completely - no tidy gradient - Outstanding contains only two homes: its “box” is two data points wearing a costume, and nothing about Outstanding homes in general can be read from it

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.7 Step 3: Statistical Comparison
Purpose: Test if infection rates differ significantly by CQC rating.

RPython



# ANOVA on rates
anova_result <- aov(infection_rate ~ cqc_rating, data = infection_data)
summary(anova_result)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value  Pr(>F)   
cqc_rating   3   69.6  23.193   4.411 0.00649 **
Residuals   76  399.6   5.258                   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

# Pairwise comparisons
pairwise_result <- pairwise.t.test(infection_data$infection_rate, 
                                   infection_data$cqc_rating,
                                   p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
print(pairwise_result)


    Pairwise comparisons using t tests with pooled SD 

data:  infection_data$infection_rate and infection_data$cqc_rating 

                     Outstanding Good   Requires Improvement
Good                 1.0000      -      -                   
Requires Improvement 1.0000      1.0000 -                   
Inadequate           1.0000      0.0035 0.0915              

P value adjustment method: bonferroni 

# Mean rates by rating
cat("\nMean infection rates by CQC rating:\n")


Mean infection rates by CQC rating:

tapply(infection_data$infection_rate, infection_data$cqc_rating, mean)

         Outstanding                 Good Requires Improvement 
            3.440000             2.692407             3.439412 
          Inadequate 
            5.995714 





# ANOVA on rates
from scipy.stats import f_oneway

groups = [infection_data[infection_data['cqc_rating'] == rating]['infection_rate'].values 
          for rating in rating_order]

f_stat, p_value = f_oneway(*groups)
print(f"ANOVA F-statistic: {f_stat:.2f}")

ANOVA F-statistic: 4.41

print(f"p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

p-value: 0.0065

# Mean rates by rating
print("\nMean infection rates by CQC rating:")


Mean infection rates by CQC rating:

print(infection_data.groupby('cqc_rating')['infection_rate'].mean())

<string>:1: FutureWarning: The default of observed=False is deprecated and will be changed to True in a future version of pandas. Pass observed=False to retain current behavior or observed=True to adopt the future default and silence this warning.
cqc_rating
Outstanding             3.440000
Good                    2.692407
Requires Improvement    3.439412
Inadequate              5.995714
Name: infection_rate, dtype: float64





Finding: the overall test says the four groups do not share one mean rate (p ≈ 0.006) – but the Bonferroni-corrected pairwise comparisons show where that difference actually lives: only Inadequate versus Good is individually significant (p ≈ 0.004), with Inadequate versus Requires Improvement suggestive but uncorroborated (p ≈ 0.09) and every other pair indistinguishable. The corrected pairwise step is the multiplicity discipline from the t-tests chapter in action: six comparisons invite false discoveries, and the correction is what keeps the one real finding from being buried among them.
crumbs: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.8 Step 4: Conclusion
Statistical conclusion: Inadequate-rated homes have substantially higher infection rates (mean ≈ 6.0 per 100 residents) than Good-rated homes (≈ 2.7), and that difference survives multiplicity correction. No other pairwise difference does. The data do not show a tidy gradient down the rating scale – Good and Requires Improvement are statistically indistinguishable here, and the Outstanding category (two homes) supports no conclusion in either direction.
Practical significance: roughly 3 extra infections per 100 residents separates Inadequate homes from Good ones – in a 50-resident home, about 1.5 additional infections per period. As a safety signal concentrated in the homes already judged Inadequate, this corroborates the rating rather than revealing something new; its operational use is prioritising infection-control support.
Recommendations: 1. Target infection-control support at Inadequate-rated homes, where the elevated rates are 2. Do not over-read the rest of the scale – this data does not distinguish Good from Requires Improvement on infection rates, and says nothing about Outstanding 3. Monitor rates as a quality indicator, sized fairly by denominators 4. Consider confounders (resident case-mix, staffing levels) before any causal reading
Limitations: - Cross-sectional data (no causality) - Resident case-mix not adjusted - Severely unbalanced groups (2 / 54 / 17 / 7) – the comparison rests almost entirely on Good versus Inadequate - Reporting bias possible (better-run homes may detect and report more infections)
crumbs: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.9 Summary
This example demonstrates analysis of count data with varying denominators – and the discipline of reporting the comparison the data actually supports. Rates, not raw counts, make different-sized homes comparable; a multi-group test plus corrected pairwise comparisons locates the real difference; and the honest summary names what cannot be concluded (a rating gradient, anything about a two-home category) as plainly as what can. The QA record below closes the loop.
crumbs: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes

title: 25  Example 7: Infection Rates in Care Homes
text:
25.10 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Rate calculation and multi-group comparison: ANOVA with Bonferroni-corrected pairwise t-tests


QASQA question
Q2: Design – is rate-based comparison appropriate given varying denominators?; Q3: Analysis – were small groups and multiplicity handled correctly?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; C: Data quality and governance


Data source
Infection counts and resident numbers by care home, with CQC ratings (synthetic illustrative data)


Key decisions
Rates per 100 residents used (not raw counts) to account for varying denominators; pairwise comparisons Bonferroni-corrected; the two-home Outstanding category reported as uninformative rather than summarised


Assumptions
Resident count is an adequate exposure denominator for this period; infection definition consistent across homes; homes within a rating are comparable


Limitations
Severely unbalanced groups (2/54/17/7); no risk adjustment for resident complexity or case-mix; reporting bias possible (better homes may detect and report more); cross-sectional design


Sensitivity
Conclusion (Inadequate vs Good difference) stable to the choice of correction; count-based alternatives (Poisson regression) would need Guild support and were not run


Next steps
Validate rate differences against inspection narrative; consider risk-adjusted (case-mix) analysis with Guild support

26.1 Scenario Overview
Method: Trend and level-shift (interrupted time series) analysis of panel data Complexity: High Time to complete: 3-4 hours QASQA questions: Q3 (Analysis); Q4 (Delivery & Communication)

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
NoteMethod Reference



This example separates a pre-existing trend from a policy-driven step change – the same separation-of-signals discipline as monitoring trends over time, applied with a simple regression. For the cross-sectional outlier step, see z-scoring. Full interrupted time series modelling (autocorrelation, seasonality, controls) is Guild territory.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.2 Scenario
CQC is monitoring Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) application trends across 150 local authorities over 3 years (12 quarters). A policy change in Q7 (2023 Q3) may have affected application rates.
Analytical Question: Are DoLS application rates increasing? Did the policy change affect application patterns? Which local authorities show unusual trends?
Context: This involves panel data (multiple authorities over time) with a known policy intervention point.

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.3 Learning Objectives
By completing this example, you will learn how to:

Analyse panel data (multiple units over time)
Separate a pre-existing trend from a policy step change - and see why a naive before/after comparison conflates them
Identify outlier authorities with unusual patterns
Account for population size in rate calculations
Visualise trends across multiple units
Interpret policy effects in regulatory context
Communicate findings about system-wide changes

Data Characteristics

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.6 Step 2: Visualise National Trend
Purpose: Show overall trend and policy change impact.

RPython



source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

# Calculate national average by quarter
national_trend <- aggregate(rate_per_10k ~ quarter + date,
                           data = dols_data,
                           FUN = mean)

ggplot(national_trend, aes(x = quarter, y = rate_per_10k)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 6.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "#A83DA4",
             linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_line(color = cqc_muted, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 2.6, color = "#007EF5") +
  annotate("text", x = 6.4, y = max(national_trend$rate_per_10k),
           label = "Policy change", hjust = 1, color = "#A83DA4") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:12) +
  labs(
    title = "A steady climb, then a thirty-point step at the policy change",
    subtitle = "Mean rate per 10,000 population 65+ across all local authorities",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "Rate per 10,000 pop 65+"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 26.1: Two signals share one chart: a steady underlying climb of about two applications per 10,000 per quarter, and a step jump of about thirty at the policy change. Rates rise gently from 50 to 61 over the first six quarters, leap to 93 at quarter 7, then resume the same gentle climb on the new level.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style, CQC_MUTED

apply_cqc_style()

# Calculate national average by quarter
national_trend = dols_data.groupby('quarter')['rate_per_10k'].mean().reset_index()

plt.figure(figsize=(11, 5.5))
plt.axvline(x=6.5, color='#A83DA4', linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5,
            label='Policy change')
plt.plot(national_trend['quarter'], national_trend['rate_per_10k'],
         '-', color=CQC_MUTED, linewidth=1)
plt.plot(national_trend['quarter'], national_trend['rate_per_10k'],
         'o', color='#007EF5', markersize=7)
plt.xlabel('Quarter')
plt.ylabel('Rate per 10,000 pop 65+')
plt.title('A steady climb, then a thirty-point step at the policy change')
plt.xticks(range(1, 13))

Figure 26.2: Two signals share one chart: a steady underlying climb of about two applications per 10,000 per quarter, and a step jump of about thirty at the policy change. Rates rise gently from 50 to 61 over the first six quarters, leap to 93 at quarter 7, then resume the same gentle climb on the new level.








Observations: - A steady underlying climb across the whole period - A sharp step at quarter 7, coinciding with the policy change - The post-change quarters continue climbing at about the pre-change pace - a level shift, not an acceleration

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.7 Step 3: Analyse Policy Impact
Purpose: quantify the policy effect without crediting it with the underlying trend.
The tempting analysis is a before/after comparison of means – and on trending data it is wrong in a predictable direction: the rates were rising anyway, so part of any before/after difference is trend dressed up as policy effect. Compute both, and watch the gap.

RPython



# Naive before/after comparison (shown to expose its flaw)
pre_policy <- national_trend$rate_per_10k[national_trend$quarter < 7]
post_policy <- national_trend$rate_per_10k[national_trend$quarter >= 7]

cat("Naive before/after difference:",
    round(mean(post_policy) - mean(pre_policy), 1), "per 10,000\n\n")

Naive before/after difference: 41.8 per 10,000

# Segmented regression: underlying trend + level shift at the policy change.
# (A slope-change term was also checked and is indistinguishable from zero.)
national_trend$post <- as.integer(national_trend$quarter >= 7)
its_model <- lm(rate_per_10k ~ quarter + post, data = national_trend)
summary(its_model)


Call:
lm(formula = rate_per_10k ~ quarter + post, data = national_trend)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-1.2672 -0.6049  0.1747  0.7142  1.1440 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  48.6220     0.6953   69.93 1.26e-13 ***
quarter       1.8363     0.1635   11.23 1.35e-06 ***
post         30.7728     1.1289   27.26 5.83e-10 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.9673 on 9 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9984,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9981 
F-statistic:  2863 on 2 and 9 DF,  p-value: 2.404e-13

cat("\nUnderlying trend:", round(coef(its_model)["quarter"], 2),
    "per 10,000 per quarter\n")


Underlying trend: 1.84 per 10,000 per quarter

cat("Level shift at policy change:", round(coef(its_model)["post"], 1),
    "per 10,000\n")

Level shift at policy change: 30.8 per 10,000





# Naive before/after comparison (shown to expose its flaw)
pre_policy = national_trend[national_trend['quarter'] < 7]['rate_per_10k']
post_policy = national_trend[national_trend['quarter'] >= 7]['rate_per_10k']

print(f"Naive before/after difference: "
      f"{post_policy.mean() - pre_policy.mean():.1f} per 10,000\n")

Naive before/after difference: 41.8 per 10,000

# Segmented regression: underlying trend + level shift at the policy change.
# (A slope-change term was also checked and is indistinguishable from zero.)
import numpy as np

X = np.column_stack([
    np.ones(len(national_trend)),
    national_trend['quarter'],
    (national_trend['quarter'] >= 7).astype(int),
])
coef, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(X, national_trend['rate_per_10k'], rcond=None)

print(f"Underlying trend: {coef[1]:.2f} per 10,000 per quarter")

Underlying trend: 1.84 per 10,000 per quarter

print(f"Level shift at policy change: {coef[2]:.1f} per 10,000")

Level shift at policy change: 30.8 per 10,000





Finding: the naive before/after difference is about 42 per 10,000 – but roughly a quarter of that is the underlying trend, which was lifting rates by about 1.8 per 10,000 each quarter regardless of any policy. The segmented regression separates the two: an underlying climb of ~1.8 per quarter, plus a level shift of ~31 per 10,000 at the policy change, with no detectable change in the slope afterwards. The 31, not the 42, is the honest estimate of the policy’s impact. (For a published analysis, a full interrupted time series model – handling the autocorrelation between quarters, seasonality, and possible controls – is Guild territory; the segmented fit here is the right shape of answer, not the final word.)

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.8 Step 4: Identify Outlier Authorities
Purpose: Find authorities with unusual patterns.

RPython



# Calculate mean rate per LA
la_summary <- aggregate(rate_per_10k ~ la_code + la_name, 
                       data = dols_data, 
                       FUN = mean)

# Identify outliers (>2 SD from mean)
overall_mean <- mean(la_summary$rate_per_10k)
overall_sd <- sd(la_summary$rate_per_10k)

la_summary$z_score <- (la_summary$rate_per_10k - overall_mean) / overall_sd
la_summary$outlier <- abs(la_summary$z_score) > 2

cat("Outlier authorities (|z| > 2):\n")

Outlier authorities (|z| > 2):

print(la_summary[la_summary$outlier, c("la_code", "la_name", "rate_per_10k", "z_score")])

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.9 Step 5: Conclusion
Statistical conclusion: DoLS application rates were already rising by about 1.8 per 10,000 each quarter before the policy change; the change added a level shift of roughly 31 per 10,000, sustained through the following six quarters with no detectable change in the underlying slope. Seven authorities (of 150) sit more than two standard deviations from the cross-authority mean and warrant a closer look.
Operational interpretation: the step change is consistent with the policy raising awareness or lowering the application threshold – an association at the right moment, not a proven cause, since anything else that changed in the same quarter would produce the same step. The continuing underlying climb predates the policy and will continue to raise demand regardless of it.
Recommendations:

Monitor capacity: the level shift plus the ongoing trend together describe the demand trajectory assessment capacity must match
Investigate the outlier authorities: high and low – an unusually low rate may signal under-application, which in this context is itself a safeguarding concern
Continue monitoring: confirm the post-change slope stays parallel rather than accelerating
Consider confounders: deprivation, demographics, concurrent local policies

Limitations: - The step’s timing implicates the policy but cannot prove it (no control series) - Quarterly national means are autocorrelated – the simple regression’s standard errors are optimistic; a full interrupted time series model is the robust version - Confounders not adjusted; no individual-level data; three years is a short series

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.10 Summary
This example demonstrates panel data with a policy intervention – and the one move that matters most: separating the pre-existing trend from the step change, because a naive before/after comparison hands the policy credit for growth that was happening anyway. Rates standardise the comparison across differently sized authorities; the segmented regression splits trend from shift; and the outlier scan turns 1,800 observations into a short list worth investigating. The QA record below closes the loop.

title: 26  Example 8: DoLS Application Trends
text:
26.11 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Segmented regression (trend + level shift) on quarterly national mean rates per 10,000 population 65+; cross-sectional z-score scan for outlier authorities


QASQA question
Q3: Analysis – does the method isolate the policy effect from the pre-existing trend?; Q4: Delivery & Communication – are the trend and the step communicated as different things?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; D: Communication of findings


Data source
DoLS applications by local authority, quarterly over 12 quarters (synthetic illustrative data)


Key decisions
Rates per 10,000 population 65+ used (not raw counts); naive before/after difference shown and rejected (conflates trend with effect); level shift estimated controlling for trend; slope-change term checked and dropped (indistinguishable from zero)


Assumptions
Policy change date correctly identified; population estimates accurate; no concurrent confounding change in the same quarter; linear trend adequate over 12 quarters


Limitations
Autocorrelated quarterly means make the simple regression’s standard errors optimistic; no control series, so the step’s cause is inferred from timing; ecological analysis only


Sensitivity
Level-shift estimate stable with and without a slope-change term (~31 per 10,000 in both)


Next steps
Validate outlier LAs with operational knowledge; full interrupted time series model (autocorrelation, seasonality) with Guild support; watch for regression to the mean in initially extreme LAs


Sign-off
________ (Analyst) ________ (Peer reviewer) ________ (Date)

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
NoteMethod Reference



For the correlation method itself – Pearson vs Spearman, outlier sensitivity, confounding, and the causation discipline – see the chapter on exploring relationships.








NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

Microsoft Fabric: CQC’s primary analytics platform – contact your function’s data engineer for access
RSS training: Introductory R and Python courses available via CQC’s learning platform
Quant Clinic: Drop-in sessions for hands-on help with code and methods – see the Guild Teams channel for upcoming dates

Both R and Python versions are provided throughout. Use whichever you are more comfortable with.
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.2 Scenario
You are a CQC analyst investigating whether staffing levels are associated with quality outcomes in residential care homes. Recent media coverage has suggested that homes with lower staffing ratios have worse outcomes, but you need to understand the relationship properly before making policy recommendations.
Analytical Question: Is there a relationship between registered nurse staffing ratios and resident safety outcomes (pressure ulcer rates) in residential care homes? If so, how strong is it, and what other factors might explain the relationship?
Context: CQC collects data on staffing levels and safety outcomes from ~800 residential care homes. Stakeholders want evidence about whether staffing levels are associated with outcomes, to inform guidance and inspection focus. However, you know that other factors (home size, resident complexity, location) might confound this relationship.
Why correlation, not z-scoring?: This is a thematic analysis exploring relationships between variables, not comparing individual provider performance. The goal is to understand patterns across the sector, not flag specific homes.
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Formulate clear research questions before analysing
Visualise relationships using scatter plots with annotations
Choose between Pearson and Spearman based on data characteristics
Calculate and interpret correlation coefficients with confidence intervals
Identify and handle outliers appropriately
Consider confounding variables and their impact
Conduct sensitivity analyses to test robustness
Distinguish correlation from causation in reporting
Communicate uncertainty and limitations clearly
Document analytical decisions for QA
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data
This synthetic dataset represents 800 residential care homes with the following variables:









Variable
Description
Type
Range




home_id
Unique care home identifier
Character
CH001-CH800


region
Geographic region
Categorical
North, Midlands, South, London


home_size
Number of registered beds
Discrete
10-120


nurse_ratio
Registered nurses per 50 residents
Continuous
0.5-8.0


pressure_ulcer_rate
Pressure ulcers per 100 resident-months
Continuous
0.2-12.5


resident_complexity
Average dependency score (1-5 scale)
Continuous
1.8-4.5


cqc_rating
Overall CQC rating
Ordinal
1-4 (Inadequate to Outstanding)


urban_rural
Location type
Categorical
Urban, Rural



Data characteristics: - Complete data for all 800 homes (no missing values) - Realistic distributions based on CQC sector knowledge - Includes confounding variables (size, complexity, location) - Contains 3 planted outlier homes with unusual staffing or outcomes
The planted truth (visible in the generation code below, because knowing the right answer is what makes a simulation instructive): better staffing genuinely reduces pressure ulcers in this data – but resident complexity pushes both staffing and ulcer rates up. The confounder therefore partially masks the staffing effect: the correlation you will observe understates the relationship that was built in. Watch for it.
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.5 Step 1: Define Research Question
Primary question: Is nurse staffing ratio associated with pressure ulcer rates?
Hypothesised relationship: Higher nurse ratios should be associated with lower pressure ulcer rates.
Alternative explanations: - Reverse causation: Homes with higher ulcer rates might hire more nurses - Confounding by complexity: Complex residents → higher staffing AND higher ulcers - Confounding by size: Larger homes have different staffing models - Regional variation: Different regions have different norms
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.6 Step 2: Load and Explore Data

RPython



set.seed(42)
n_homes <- 800

# Generate home characteristics
home_data <- data.frame(
  home_id = paste0("CH", sprintf("%03d", 1:n_homes)),
  region = sample(c("North", "Midlands", "South", "London"), n_homes, 
                  replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.25, 0.3, 0.15)),
  home_size = sample(10:120, n_homes, replace = TRUE),
  urban_rural = sample(c("Urban", "Rural"), n_homes, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3))
)

# Generate resident complexity
home_data$resident_complexity <- 2.5 + 
  (home_data$home_size / 100) * 0.8 + 
  ifelse(home_data$urban_rural == "Urban", 0.3, 0) +
  rnorm(n_homes, 0, 0.4)
home_data$resident_complexity <- pmin(pmax(home_data$resident_complexity, 1.8), 4.5)

# Generate nurse ratios
home_data$nurse_ratio <- 3.5 + 
  (home_data$resident_complexity - 2.5) * 1.2 +
  ifelse(home_data$region == "London", 0.8, 0) +
  ifelse(home_data$region == "North", -0.5, 0) +
  (home_data$home_size - 65) / 50 * 0.5 +
  rnorm(n_homes, 0, 1.2)
home_data$nurse_ratio <- pmin(pmax(home_data$nurse_ratio, 0.5), 8.0)

# Generate pressure ulcer rates (negatively correlated with staffing)
home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate <- 8 - 
  (home_data$nurse_ratio - 3.5) * 0.6 +
  (home_data$resident_complexity - 2.5) * 1.5 +
  rnorm(n_homes, 0, 1.8)
home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate <- pmin(pmax(home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate, 0.2), 12.5)

# Add 3 outliers
outlier_indices <- c(50, 250, 600)
home_data$nurse_ratio[outlier_indices[1]] <- 7.5
home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate[outlier_indices[1]] <- 3.0
home_data$nurse_ratio[outlier_indices[2]] <- 1.2
home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate[outlier_indices[2]] <- 11.0
home_data$nurse_ratio[outlier_indices[3]] <- 6.5
home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate[outlier_indices[3]] <- 10.5

# Generate CQC ratings
rating_score <- 2.5 - 
  (home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate - 6) / 4 +
  (home_data$nurse_ratio - 3.5) / 3 +
  rnorm(n_homes, 0, 0.6)
home_data$cqc_rating <- cut(rating_score, 
                             breaks = c(-Inf, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, Inf),
                             labels = c(1, 2, 3, 4))
home_data$cqc_rating <- as.numeric(as.character(home_data$cqc_rating))

27.7 Step 3: Visualise the Relationship

RPython



library(ggplot2)
source("../R/theme_cqc.R")

home_data$planted_outlier <- seq_len(nrow(home_data)) %in% c(50, 250, 600)

ggplot(home_data, aes(nurse_ratio, pressure_ulcer_rate)) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = planted_outlier, colour = planted_outlier,
                 size = planted_outlier), alpha = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#A83DA4",
              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = 16, `TRUE` = 17)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = "#375071", `TRUE` = "#A83DA4")) +
  scale_size_manual(values = c(`FALSE` = 1.6, `TRUE` = 3)) +
  labs(
    title = "More nurses, fewer ulcers - loosely",
    x = "Nurse ratio (per 50 residents)",
    y = "Pressure ulcer rate (per 100 resident-months)"
  ) +
  theme_cqc()







Figure 27.1: A loose downward drift, not a tight law: homes with more nurses tend to have fewer pressure ulcers, but the cloud is wide - homes at every staffing level span most of the outcome range. The three planted outliers (triangles) sit apart from the cloud without driving the trend.








import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "../python")
from cqc_style import apply_cqc_style

apply_cqc_style()

outlier_rows = [49, 249, 599]  # 0-indexed positions of the planted outliers
is_out = home_data_py.index.isin(outlier_rows)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

            home_data_py.loc[~is_out, 'pressure_ulcer_rate'],
            alpha=0.4, s=25, color='#375071')

            home_data_py.loc[is_out, 'pressure_ulcer_rate'],
            marker='^', s=80, color='#A83DA4')

z = np.polyfit(home_data_py['nurse_ratio'],
               home_data_py['pressure_ulcer_rate'], 1)

27.8 Step 4: Calculate Correlation

RPython



pearson_all <- cor.test(home_data$nurse_ratio, 
                        home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate,
                        method = "pearson")

spearman_all <- cor.test(home_data$nurse_ratio, 
                         home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate,
                         method = "spearman")

Warning in cor.test.default(home_data$nurse_ratio,
home_data$pressure_ulcer_rate, : Cannot compute exact p-value with ties

correlation_results <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Pearson", "Spearman"),
  n = c(nrow(home_data), nrow(home_data)),
  Coefficient = c(pearson_all$estimate, spearman_all$estimate),
  CI_Lower = c(pearson_all$conf.int[1], NA),
  CI_Upper = c(pearson_all$conf.int[2], NA),
  P_Value = c(pearson_all$p.value, spearman_all$p.value)
)

correlation_results$Coefficient <- round(correlation_results$Coefficient, 3)
correlation_results$CI_Lower <- round(correlation_results$CI_Lower, 3)
correlation_results$CI_Upper <- round(correlation_results$CI_Upper, 3)
correlation_results$P_Value <- format.pval(correlation_results$P_Value, digits = 3)

knitr::kable(correlation_results, 
             col.names = c("Method", "n", "r/ρ", "95% CI Lower", 
                          "95% CI Upper", "P-Value"),
             caption = "Correlation Results")

pearson_r, pearson_p = stats.pearsonr(home_data_py['nurse_ratio'], 
                                       home_data_py['pressure_ulcer_rate'])

spearman_rho, spearman_p = stats.spearmanr(home_data_py['nurse_ratio'], 
                                            home_data_py['pressure_ulcer_rate'])

n = len(home_data_py)
z = np.arctanh(pearson_r)
se = 1 / np.sqrt(n - 3)
ci_lower = np.tanh(z - 1.96 * se)
ci_upper = np.tanh(z + 1.96 * se)

results_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Method': ["Pearson", "Spearman"],
    'n': [n, n],
    'Coefficient': [pearson_r, spearman_rho],
    'CI_Lower': [ci_lower, np.nan],
    'CI_Upper': [ci_upper, np.nan],
    'P_Value': [pearson_p, spearman_p]
})

results_df['Coefficient'] = results_df['Coefficient'].round(3)
results_df['CI_Lower'] = results_df['CI_Lower'].round(3)
results_df['CI_Upper'] = results_df['CI_Upper'].round(3)

print("\nCorrelation Results")


Correlation Results

print(results_df.to_string(index=False))

  Method   n  Coefficient  CI_Lower  CI_Upper      P_Value
 Pearson 800       -0.267     -0.33    -0.201 1.644146e-14
Spearman 800       -0.254       NaN       NaN 2.905844e-13





Results: Pearson’s r = −0.27 (95% CI −0.33 to −0.20, p < 0.001); Spearman’s ρ = −0.25, confirming the relationship is roughly linear and not an artefact of the outliers. A weak-to-moderate negative association: staffing shares only about 7% of its variation with ulcer rates (r² ≈ 0.07). Before reading that as “staffing barely matters”, recall the planted truth – complexity pushes staffing and ulcers in the same direction, so this marginal correlation understates the staffing effect that was built into the data. That is confounding working in the direction people rarely expect: it can hide real relationships as readily as manufacture spurious ones.
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.9 Step 5: Report Findings

Summary

Analysis of 800 residential care homes shows a weak-to-moderate negative correlation between nurse staffing ratios and pressure ulcer rates (Pearson’s r = −0.27, 95% CI −0.33 to −0.20, p < 0.001; Spearman’s ρ = −0.25). Homes with higher nurse-to-resident ratios tend to have lower pressure ulcer rates, but staffing shares only about 7% of its variation with outcomes – resident complexity, care practices, and environment clearly matter at least as much.
The headline number needs careful handling in both directions. It does not establish that staffing drives outcomes: reverse causation (homes with problems hiring more staff) and confounding remain live explanations. Nor does its modest size establish that staffing barely matters: resident complexity is associated with both higher staffing and higher ulcer rates, and a confounder that pushes the two variables the same way will shrink the observed correlation below the true relationship. Untangling that requires analysis that adjusts for complexity – multivariate work beyond correlation, for which we recommend Guild support.



Recommendations

For policy: Staffing-outcome relationship warrants attention, but avoid simplistic staffing ratio targets
For inspection: Consider staffing in context of resident complexity and other quality indicators
For further investigation: Examine outlier homes (especially high-staffing/high-ulcer homes) to understand why staffing alone doesn’t guarantee good outcomes
For research: Longitudinal study needed to establish causation; qualitative work to understand mechanisms
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.10 QA Documentation
Method justification: Pearson and Spearman both reported – the scatter looks linear and the outliers turn out not to steer the coefficient, so the two agree
Assumptions checked: - Linearity: confirmed via scatter plot, corroborated by Pearson-Spearman agreement - Outliers: three identified on the plot; sensitivity analysis below shows minimal influence - Independence: each home is one observation
Limitations: - Cross-sectional data (cannot infer causation or direction) - Confounding by resident complexity is known to be present and unadjusted – the marginal correlation likely understates the staffing relationship - Ecological analysis (home-level, not resident-level) - Staffing measured at one time point (does not capture variation)
Sensitivity analyses: - Excluding the three outliers: r moves from −0.267 to −0.262 (robust) - Spearman vs Pearson: −0.25 vs −0.27 (consistent; no outlier or non-linearity artefact)
Next steps: investigate CH600 (high staffing and high ulcers – the combination no simple story explains); multivariate analysis adjusting for complexity, with Guild support
crumbs: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.11 Key Learning Points

Always visualise first: the scatter plot revealed the outliers and the overall pattern
Context matters: correlation strength depends on field – in complex health and social care data, an r of −0.27 on a safety outcome is worth attention
Confounding cuts both ways: complexity affects both staffing and outcomes, and here it masks the staffing relationship rather than manufacturing it
Correlation ≠ causation: multiple explanations remain live
Report completely: coefficient, confidence interval, p-value, scatter plot, limitations
Sensitivity analysis: test robustness to outliers and to the choice of coefficient
Know when to escalate: only adjusted, multivariate analysis can separate staffing from complexity

This example demonstrates a complete correlation analysis workflow suitable for CQC thematic work, with appropriate attention to confounding, outliers, and limitations.

title: 27  Example 9: Staffing and Quality Outcomes
text:
27.12 QA Documentation Record







Item
Detail




Method
Pearson’s correlation with Spearman’s rank correlation as cross-check


QASQA question
Q2: Design – is correlation appropriate for this thematic research question?; Q3: Analysis – were method choice, outliers, and confounding documented?


Competencies
A: Statistical analysis and methods; B: Analytical judgement; D: Communication of findings


Data source
Nurse staffing ratio and pressure ulcer rate per 100 resident-months (synthetic illustrative data, n=800 care homes)


Key decisions
Both coefficients reported (agreement confirms linearity and outlier-robustness); the three outliers investigated and retained; the known confounding by resident complexity stated as masking, not merely caveated


Assumptions
Each care home is an independent observation; nurse ratio measured consistently across homes; pressure ulcer rates reliably reported


Limitations
Cross-sectional – cannot establish causation or direction; resident complexity confounds the relationship and is unadjusted; ecological (home-level) analysis


Sensitivity
Excluding the three outliers: r = −0.262 vs −0.267 (stable); Pearson −0.27 and Spearman −0.25 consistent


Next steps
Multivariate analysis with Guild support to adjust for resident complexity; investigate CH600; triangulate with inspection narratives; consider longitudinal design

NoteRunning This Example



These examples require R or Python. If you are not yet set up with a coding environment:

crumbs: 28  Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation

title: 28  Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation
text:
28.2 Scenario
You are a CQC analyst investigating staffing levels at adult social care homes. Recent submissions show some extreme staff-to-resident ratios that warrant investigation. The analysis will support:

Identifying homes requiring immediate inspection (data errors vs genuine understaffing)
Understanding natural variation in staffing models
Informing risk-based targeting

Analytical Question: Which care homes have staffing ratios extreme enough to warrant investigation, and which extreme values are data errors versus genuine operational issues?
Context: Care homes report monthly staffing data. Most homes have 2-4 qualified staff per 10 residents, but you’ve seen values ranging from 0 to 12. Some may be data entry errors (wrong decimal point, missing zeros), others may reflect genuine operational models (specialist dementia units) or problems (understaffing due to recruitment issues).
crumbs: 28  Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation

title: 28  Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation
text:
28.3 Learning Objectives
By working through this example, you will learn to:

Visualise data to understand distribution before detection
Apply multiple detection methods (Z-score, Modified Z-score, IQR)
Compare method results to prioritise investigation
Investigate flagged observations systematically
Distinguish data errors from genuine extremes
Document outlier treatment decisions for QA purposes
Communicate findings to inspection teams
Handle small provider variation appropriately

title: 28  Example 10: Care Home Staffing Investigation
text:
28.4 Dataset Description

Synthetic Data: Generated for this example
337 care homes with the following variables:









Variable
Description
Type
Notes




home_id
Unique home identifier
String
Format: CH001-CH350


home_name
Care home name
String
Synthetic names


region
NHS England region
String
7 regions


beds
Number of beds
Integer
15-120 beds


care_type
Specialisation
String
General, Dementia, Nursing


cqc_rating
Current CQC rating
String
Outstanding to Inadequate


staff_per_10
Qualified staff per 10 residents
Numeric
Ratio (target 2.5-4.0)





Data Characteristics
The planted truth (knowing the right answer is what makes a simulation instructive): - Most homes (300): typical staffing around 3.2 staff per 10 residents (standard deviation 0.8) - Specialist units (15): genuinely higher staffing around 5.5 - Struggling homes (10): genuinely low staffing around 1.5 - Planted problem values (12): impossible entries near zero, suspected wrong-unit entries above 9, and a few high-but-plausible returns - Small homes get extra noise: higher natural variation
